title.rendered: Episode 963: The Power of “Feel, Felt, Found”

content.rendered:
Synopsis

“Feel, Felt, Found” is one of the most powerful communication techniques in sales, leadership, negotiating, and networking. Whether you’re managing a BNI chapter or negotiating with a client, this three-part method helps you win by aligning.

  1. I understand how you feel.
  2. Others felt the same way.
  3. Here’s what they found.

In a world where people are just constantly being sold to and algorithms are trying to predict behavior, human connection stands out more than ever. One of the most powerful ways to connect is to make people feel understood before you try to influence them. When people feel understood, they become open. When they’re open, that’s where transformation happens.

Have you used “Feel, Felt, Found”? Share your experience in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 963

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am on my way to the United Kingdom. I’m leaving Ireland and am on my way to the United Kingdom for their BNI annual conference. It’s been a few years since I’ve been to the UK, so I’m really excited to go back. It’s one of our first countries outside of North America. As a matter of fact, it is the first country outside of North America that we started.

Priscilla:
That joined BNI, do you mean?

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. It was the first country outside the US and Canada. I think they were country number three.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. Well, what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
I have an interesting [topic]… at least I think it’s interesting. I was talking to somebody about this in the way they manage chapters, and I used this phrase, and they’re like, “Wow, I’ve never heard that. And this is really powerful. You need to do a podcast on this”. And I’m like, “Okay, I’ll do it.”

So this is a rhetorical question for those listening. Have you ever had someone disagree with you and instead of pushing back, you actually listened and leaned in? That’s a skill, and today, I want to talk about one of the most powerful communication techniques in sales, in leadership, in negotiating, and in networking, something often called Feel, Felt, Found. Feel comma, Felt comma, Found. Here’s the twist — the real power isn’t in those exact words, the real power is in the structure behind them.

So what is Feel, Felt, Found? In a simple phrase, what it is: I understand how you feel. So-and-so felt the same way until they found out “X.” “I understand how you feel. So and So felt the same way until they found out this.” And at its core, the technique does three things. It acknowledges someone’s concern. It shows that they’re not alone. It gently introduces a new perspective. And it is some variation of that phrase, usually, and it’s used in sales.

And I first heard about it in a seminar I took, I was in my early 20s, on negotiating techniques, and they taught us, “You know, I understand how you feel. Others felt the same way. And here’s what they found.” It’s simple, it’s elegant, it’s effective. But let’s be honest, if you use those exact words too often, it starts to sound scripted. Plus, if you use those words with negotiating experts or with sales experts, they’re going to just say, “You’re doing a feel, felt, found on me.” So you don’t have to use those same words. The last thing you want in a relationship-driven environment, like in networking, is to sound like a script. So let’s talk about why it works, and let’s talk about some other alternatives.

The technique works because it lowers resistance. When people feel heard and understood and not judged, they stop being as defensive and they start listening. So here’s the mistake that most people make in conversations, they try to win the argument. But in networking, in business, and negotiating in sales, you don’t want to win by overpowering, you want to win by aligning. Let me resay that. You don’t want to win by overpowering. You want to win by aligning. This method creates alignment before influence. It says, I’m with you. And now, let me walk you somewhere new. Let me share something interesting with you. The real magic happens in changing the words sometimes. You don’t have to say, “I understand how you feel. Others have felt the same way. Here’s what they found.” You can say it in ways that feel natural and conversational and authentic without using those same words.

So instead of saying, “I understand how you feel,” you can say, “I can see where you’re coming from.” or “That makes sense.” or “I hear what you’re saying.” or “That’s a valid concern.” Don’t say all of those all at once, by the way, pick any one of those. I particularly like, “You know, I see where you’re coming from.”

And then, instead of saying, “Others have felt the same way,” pick from these. “You’re not alone in that.” “I’ve worked with others who thought the same thing.” “A lot of people I’ve met felt exactly like that.” “That comes up more often than you might think.” That’s a phrase you can use. I also say, “You know, I had another chapter that was in the exact same situation.” That’s beautiful in BNI. “I know another chapter.” “I met another chapter.” “I worked with another chapter that felt the same way.” So these are phrases to use instead of saying Feel, Felt, Found.

The third – instead of saying, “Here’s what they found,” you can say, “Here’s what they discovered.” That’s my favorite approach. “Here’s what they discovered.” “What ended up happening was…. ” That’s another approach. Or, “And what they realized over time was….” Or, “The interesting thing is….”

Now it doesn’t sound like a technique, it sounds like a conversation, which it is. It’s a conversation… it’s guiding people based on your expertise and knowledge, and you’re helping them be a better version of themselves in business. But if you get confrontational, “No, don’t do it that way. You’ve got to do it this way.” Or “What are you thinking?” – that just makes people defensive. But the whole concept of feel, felt, found, or some variation of it, “I see where you’re coming from.” “You’re not alone in that.” “Here’s what they discovered,” is incredibly powerful.

And when I was managing chapters, Priscilla, I used this technique almost every time I visited a chapter, and I think this is a great technique for Leadership Teams to use in a chapter. I think this is a great technique for our members. I think it’s a great technique for the Membership Committee to use in chapters. I think it’s a great technique for our members to use with their customers or clients; BNI members working with their customers or clients — it’s a fantastic technique, because it gets people to hear and it also gets people to think, “I’m not alone in this. I’m not the only person that’s experienced this.” And you’re not making it about them, because if you say, “Well, you’re doing this wrong, you need to do it this way instead.” Or, “Let me correct you on that.” Yeah, those are fighting words. So instead, “I’m with you. I get it. I know a group that had the same situation and you know what they discovered? And this is what happened,” and it draws people in.

So let me tie this directly to something I used to do in helping to turn around BNI chapters. I’d often hear something like, “This chapter just isn’t generating enough business.” Now I could argue with them, and I could say, “No, you’re wrong. The system really works.” But that just creates the resistance I’m talking about.

Instead, I’d say something like, “You know, I hear what you’re saying. It can definitely feel that way. Sometimes it doesn’t seem to be working, and you’re not alone. I’ve seen other chapters go through that exact same phase. What they discovered is that when they leaned in deeper into the process, more one-to-ones, stronger weekly presentations, a better follow up, the referrals didn’t just increase, they multiplied. Let me introduce you to a few of the chapter members in some other groups that were where you are now and have moved to where you want to be.”

Now, notice what happened there. I didn’t dismiss their concern. I validated it. I didn’t isolate them. I connected them to others. And I didn’t argue; I guided, I coached. That’s the difference between reacting and leading.

Now I think this matters today more than ever. In a world where people are just constantly being sold to and algorithms are trying to predict behavior, human connection stands out more than ever. And one of the most powerful ways to connect is to make people feel understood before you try to influence them. Because when people feel understood, they become open. When they’re open, that’s where transformation happens.

If you take one thing from today, if you’re listening to this and you take one thing from this today and share it with your fellow members, let it be this. You don’t have to push people to change their mind. You just have to walk with them. Walk with them long enough for them to see that different path. Because the most powerful conversations don’t start with persuasion. They start with understanding. And when you master that, you don’t just change conversations, you change outcomes. Validation lowers defenses, and when defenses go down, conversations go up. I’ll leave you with this, Priscilla. And you want to chat for a minute, I’m happy to do it. You don’t persuade by pressure. You persuade by perspective. And the Feel, Felt, Found technique is all about persuading by perspective and not by pressure. What do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think that’s really good. I wish I could remember how you phrased everything, because it was excellent.

Ivan:
Well, luckily there’s a transcript.

Priscilla:(laughing)
That’s so true.

Ivan:
So for everyone who wants to share this with their chapter, there’s a transcript of this whole thing. And we do the transcript, by the way, so that people can do searches on the topics.

Priscilla:
Yeah, right. And it works so well. I used to print them all the time when I was the Education Coordinator so that everyone could use them.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know, we do these… People have asked me, why do we do them so short? And we do them short because I want members to be able to get through them. I want bite-sized pieces of usable information. But we also do it, and this was in my mind from the very beginning — I wanted Education Coordinators to be able to go back to a chapter and do what you just described — take down some notes, print it out, go to the [BNI] meeting and talk about the key points that they heard that particularly resonate with the chapter. So what you’re describing is exactly what I had in mind when I started doing the podcast decades ago. That’s it for me today, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, all right. Well, thank you so much. That was a great presentation.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

title.rendered: Episode 962: Time and Money (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is rebroadcast of Episode 730, first published on October 27, 2021. Some links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Most visitors who aren’t ready to join aren’t sure they can invest the time and money required to make BNI work for their businesses. Shirley Towne from BNI South Carolina Midlands returns to the podcast to explain why those are exactly the reasons they should join BNI.

Before joining BNI, Shirley was spending $5000 per month on Yellow Pages ads for her ADT dealership…and knocking on a lot of doors. It took more than a year for the business to become profitable. Within two years of joining BNI, she had as much business coming to her from BNI as in her most profitable year of Yellow Pages advertising.

The 90 minutes you spend at your weekly BNI meeting are both more fun and more effective than spending the same amount of time cold calling or knocking on doors. Most members recoup the cost of their membership within the first few months. Referrals and personal introductions are a lot more likely to result in closed business than the leads generated by ads.

When you come right down to it, there’s no better use of your time and money than BNI.

Complete Transcript of Episode 730/962

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still Zooming all over the world doing a lot of events. I’ve done a lot of live podcasts [one in] India. I did a couple of Clubhouse events – Breakfast with Champions I did at Clubhouse. And I popped into a Northern Virginia [BNI] chapter to give an award. They created The Ivan Award, like the Oscars except it’s called The Ivan, and so I had to just drop in and congratulate them on the first winners of The Ivan Award, and that was the Northern Virginia chapter with [BNI Executive Director] Leslie Hutchinson. So I’ve been going around.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
Today, I have back a guest who has been on once before, Shirley Towne. Shirley also in the past has owned and operated an ADT Home Security franchise for 16 years. She joined BNI in 2014 to replace the business that she lost from the Yellow Pages – I want to hear a little about that, Shirley, and she never looked back. Shirley has been an Executive Director in the Midlands of South Carolina for four years and she doubled her region in the first two years. She’s launched 20 chapters; Shirley was named BNI USA Executive Director of the Year in 2020. And this is her second time as a guest on BNI Podcast. Shirley, welcome back.


Shirley:
Thank you. I’m excited to be here.

Ivan:
Tell me about that $5,000 a month you spent on the Yellow Pages.

Shirley:
So we spent $5000 every single month; we did it for probably five years. It took us a while to get to the first page in the Yellow Pages. I was so proud, but it was only profitable after that for like a year. We hung on to it you know but the calls just dwindled, and I needed something to replace it because that’s how we made a living.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
And so you know I heard about BNI and I kind of invited myself, and yeah, I went to the chapter, there were 36 people there. I stood up to speak and my voice cracked like I was five years old. I was so nervous it was nothing but a bunch of suits just a few women in the room. That chapter today is now 150 members, so it is more intimidating now than it was then, for sure. Yeah, fun stuff but it totally replaced—BNI did what I wanted it to do. In two years, I was at the level of what my highest level of what I was doing when I was doing the Yellow Pages.

Ivan:
Well we’re going to talk a little bit about the concept of time and money and how it applies in your BNI membership. But here’s an interesting question I’d like to pose to the listeners, because I think you know the answer, Shirley.

The Yellow Pages cost you $5,000 a month. If a BNI membership cost you $5,000 a month, how would you be participating differently?

Shirley:
Ahh, golly. Can you imagine the intensity?

Ivan:
Yes! And it doesn’t cost that much. So, maybe we should be participating intensely so that we can be making that kind of money, and that’s your topic for today. So let me hand it over to you. What are the major considerations?

Shirley:
You know, Ivan, it is the topic. But when you put it like that, oh my goodness, my intentionality should have been even more than it was. Anyway, I guess on some level I knew it, because obviously I wrote that check every month. But when I think about that, it’s just crazy to think about. But yeah, that’s exactly it.

You know, I started being the visitor [host]. I started sitting in on Visitors Days on the visitor orientation and every time I did, you know, I could tell that hesitancy to join was over two major items: time and money, our limited resources. We all have limited resources when it comes to time and money. And so, you do want to be a good steward of both of those.

It took me maybe six or seven Visitors Days to realize that every time I heard the objection of time or money—it just dawned on me. Isn’t that ironic, because those two reasons are the reason why I do BNI today, six years after that whole Yellow Pages saga, seven years, going on seven years. Those are the reasons I do it, there’s no better use of my time or my money when it comes to BNI.

So when I’m talking to visitors, I say that. I just like to get it right out and say “Most of you are thinking, is this a good use of my time and money? And you know, those are the two hesitations or the considerations that we hear, and I want to address both of those. Because those, you know, seven years later, are the reason why I do BNI. There’s no better use of my time, and there’s no better use of my money.”

What’s the investment of time? So I always want to lay that out and say, “We’ve got this 90 minute meeting. A very smart person once told me that if you treat BNI like your best customer, BNI will become your best customer. Block it off on your schedule and count it as, you know, like the time with your best client.”

So I think that was a big mindset change for me. But you look at that, and you just think about the use of your time. And then obviously, in BNI we like to have what we call One-to-Ones. And, Ivan, as you have said a million times, that’s really where the magic of BNI happens. The meeting is amazing, and I always learn a tidbit here or there. But when I connect with an individual on a one-to-one basis, that’s how I learn how to be a better referral partner. I learn who their golden goose referral partners are, who can I connect them with that will feed them for life. So those are the two major time commitments: the meeting, and the One-to-Ones. And those are the times that I enjoy in my workweek. And those are the most productive times of my workweek.

And then when I look at the investment in BNI, I think Ivan said it well. I mean, you know, it is such a small investment. For most of us, it’s under $1,000 for the year. And it’s just crazy to think about the fact that I was paying $5,000 a month just to get leads. Because that’s what those were, they were leads, you know, they were calling two or three other people out of the Yellow Pages.

But when I get a referral from BNI, it’s a personal introduction, where somebody has already said, “This is the person you need to speak to.” So I would dare say that when you think about the use of your time and your money, there’s no better use than BNI. Like, you’re not gonna find anything that will compare to it. And you just want to think about what you do as a profession, and how many referrals it’s going to take to break even. And, you know, most of our members do that in a month or two.

Ivan:
Yeah, so you know, what do you say to the people, I’ve got a thought on this, but I’d like to hear what you say to people who say, “Yeah, but it’s a 90 minute meeting. And then if it’s in person, I’ve got a drive to it, I’ve got to drive back. And you guys – phone calls, and oh my goodness, then you want me to do One-to-Ones.” What do you say to those people?

Shirley:
Yeah, I say that you’ll learn that there isn’t a better use of your time. Where can you go and knock out a meal, and hang out with like-minded individuals, that are really kinda in the same position that you are?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
It’s that, you know, business can be lonely when you’re an entrepreneur. And when you hang out with other people that are going through what you go through, it’s very uplifting. But also, where else can you hang out with 20 or 30 people or 40 people, whatever your chapter size is, that are going to be out there intentionally looking for opportunities for you? I mean, I think about social media. If somebody asked for, you know, somebody to take down a tree, and you look and there’s nine or ten recommendations, and they’re all BNI people, it’s like we dominate social media when it comes to that kind of stuff.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know, you may be in business alone, but you do not have to be alone in business, if you’re in BNI, a global powerful network.

Shirley:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
I think that’s so important. And I tell people look, you know, no matter what, it’s going to take you time. It takes time to build a business. You’re going to spend time cold calling; you’re going to spend time going out and maybe talking to your existing clients to get referrals. You’re going to do advertising. I’ve got nothing wrong with advertising, but advertising costs money and oh, how do you make the money? Well, you make the money by spending a lot of time doing direct selling. And so to me, networking is so much easier than direct selling. And it’s a great use of your time because it is, it truly is about farming not hunting. And when you’re direct selling, you’re out there hunting.

Shirley:
Yes. 100%.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
I knocked on a few doors in my ADT business. And I will tell you, it was not fun. Coming to BNI was fun.

Ivan:
Yeah, look, I get it. I mean, I started BNI because of that. A lot of people don’t know this, and I think I mentioned it in a podcast many years ago, but one of my initial clients, I was hired to train a marketing department how to cold call.

Shirley:
I remember that.

Ivan:
I know how to cold call. I did it long enough to know, I don’t want to ever have to do it again for the rest of my life. Does it work? Yeah, it can work, although I think it’s getting harder. But I didn’t find it as fun. And I think if someone finds it fun, they have an emotional issue.

Shirley: They do – they’re sick. (chuckling)

Ivan:
There’s something wrong. Any closing thoughts, Shirley, on the time and money aspect?

Shirley:
No, I think that when we’re talking to visitors, and we want them to be a part of our network, we just have to be very upfront with them about those two things. And if we do that, you know, I think that we can definitely get them in our room, and they can be the best referral that you could give your chapter.

Ivan:
And I think it’s good to set expectations, too, and say it’s not a get-rich-quick scheme. It’s about building relationships. So it’s going to take time, but that time pays off over time.

Shirley:
Yes, very well said.

Ivan:
Shirley, thanks for being back on BNI Podcast. I appreciate it. And your region and website is bniofmidlands.com, www.bniofmidlands.com. Thank you so much, Shirley, I appreciate you.

Shirley:
Thank you so much. It’s good to get to talk to you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Thank you both. That was great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com, and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Shirley Towne returns to the podcast to explain why some visitors hesitate to join BNI and how to address their concerns.

title.rendered: Episode 961: Hard Work vs. Luck

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Luck may open the door, but hard work is what gets you through it. The people who succeed over time are not the luckiest people. Hard work builds capability. Hard work builds credibility. Hard work creates opportunity. Without effort, luck is useless.

You don’t accidentally build a business, a great network, or an amazing BNI chapter. The harder you work, the less you need luck, and the more it seems to show up anyway. Hard work doesn’t guarantee success, but without it, success is almost guaranteed not to happen.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 488: The Easy Path Is Harder.
  • Listen to Episode 507: Be a Dog with a Bone.
  • Listen to Episode 933: Effort Drives Outcomes.

Complete Transcript of Episode 961

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m in Austin, but I am on my way to Ireland.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
That’s a really horrible accent, but my I do have family roots, the Duffy and Moore family from Ireland. So I’m going back to my roots in Ireland for a BNI Conference, and I’m really looking forward to it.

Priscilla:
That sounds great. And let me just ask you, have you been there before?

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve been there many times. Beautiful, beautiful country. Yeah.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

Ivan:
It’s called the Emerald Isle for a reason. A lot of green, lot of green.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. Okay, well, what are you sharing with us?

Ivan:
Well, I’m talking about hard work versus luck and what really drives success. So let me start with something that I actually agree with, and that is that luck matters. It does. Being in the right place at the right time, meeting the right person, walking into an opportunity you didn’t expect… that happens. But here is where I part company with a lot of people. Luck may open the door, but hard work is what gets you through it.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
And more importantly, hard work is what keeps you in the room once you get there. So I’ve been around long enough — decades in business, thousands of entrepreneurs that I’ve met, and I’ve seen a lot of patterns. And I can tell you this, the people who succeed over time are not the luckiest people. They’re the ones, as a rule — yeah, sometimes there’s an exception — but as a rule, they’re the ones who show up consistently, do the work, and stay in the game long enough for something to actually happen. And there’s research behind this.

Psychologist Angela Duckworth studied what separates high performers from everyone else, and it wasn’t IQ. It wasn’t talent. It was something she called grit — the ability to stay focused and keep working when things get really difficult. In other words, success isn’t about who starts the fastest, it’s about who refuses to stop. Now that doesn’t mean you never stop, but sometimes the smartest move isn’t necessarily to quit or stop, it’s to adjust. And so you adjust, you adjust your plan and take a look at things slightly differently. You don’t give up the vision, but you may need to change the strategy to get there. Refusing to stop doesn’t mean refusing to think. Sometimes you don’t quit. You pivot.

I have pivoted a lot in my career, trust me. You adjust the path, not the destination. Now some people will say, “Well, I’ve seen talented people fail and average people succeed. That’s luck.”  No, I’d say generally, that’s not the whole picture, because here’s what hard work really does. It does three things that luck can’t. First, hard work builds capability. You don’t accidentally become excellent at something. You don’t “luck” yourself into mastery. Research on deliberate practice shows that expertise comes from doing the work over and over again with intention. That’s not luck. That’s discipline. Second, hard work–

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
You like that? I heard you like that; I do too. Second, hard work builds credibility. In BNI, we talk about the VCP process – Visibility, Credibility, Profitability, and you can’t shortcut credibility. You earn it. How do you earn it? By doing the work consistently over time, not by getting lucky once.

Third, hard work creates opportunity. This is the part most people completely miss. They think luck is something that just kind of happens. But in reality, the harder you work, the more people you meet, the more conversations you have, the more doors you knock on, the more lucky breaks seem to show up. That’s not luck. That’s exposure. That’s activity; that’s momentum.

There’s a concept that I love called earned serendipity. It basically means you create the conditions where good things are more likely to happen. You don’t wait for opportunity. You build a life where opportunity can find you. Now, let’s be fair. There is research that shows that luck plays a role. Some models even suggest that less talented people can outperform more talented people because of timing or randomness. And I don’t agree with that.

I don’t completely disagree with that. Here’s the critical point. Luck might determine which opportunity you get, but hard work determines what you do with it, because without effort, luck is useless. I’ll repeat that. Without effort, luck is useless. You don’t accidentally build a business. You don’t stumble into a great network. You don’t trip and fall into a legacy. You don’t build an amazing BNI chapter by accident. I’ve seen this play over and over again in BNI. Members who stay longer, members who engage more, members who build deeper relationships… they don’t just get lucky. They get results consistently.

There’s always something else going on here that we don’t talk about enough. When people say, “It’s all luck,” what they’re often really saying is: “I don’t have control.” And that’s a dangerous mindset, in my opinion. Because if success is luck, then effort doesn’t matter. Discipline doesn’t matter. Consistency doesn’t matter. And if that’s true, why even try? Why even try?

Because here’s what I believe — and what I’ve seen proven over decades of experience: You may not control every opportunity, but you absolutely control your preparation and your response to potential opportunities. Preparation meets opportunity far more often than people realize. Let me give you a simple way to think about this. And if you’re listening to this, write this down. If you’re in a car, pull over and write this down. Luck is a moment. Hard work is a system. And systems beat moments every time. Let me give you that again. Luck is a moment. Hard work is a system. And systems beat moments every time.

Let me share something personal with all of you. When I started BNI, I didn’t have a road map. There was no playbook for referral marketing. I was figuring it out as I went – trial and error. And believe me, there were plenty of errors. And I know I’ve said this in the podcast before, I was method-acting my way through learning how to do all of this. And there were times when things weren’t working. They just weren’t working the way I thought they should or would, and I had a choice. I could stop, or I could adjust–I could pivot. So I adjusted, I pivoted, I tested, I refined, and over time, those small adjustments turned into a system.

Looking back, people might call the success of BNI “luck.” But I can tell you, it didn’t feel like luck at the time. It felt like work, like hard work. Fun work – I loved what I was doing, but it was work. Luck is unpredictable. Hard work is repeatable. Bet on what you can control. Luck may open the door, but hard work decides whether you walk through it or you watch someone else walk through it. The harder you work, the less you need luck, and the more it seems to show up anyway.

That has been my life, Priscilla. The harder I’ve worked, the less I’ve needed luck, and the more it seemed to just show up anyway. So yes, everyone listening to this podcast, acknowledge luck. Be grateful for it when it shows up. But don’t build your life around waiting for it. Build your life around doing the work, because in the end, hard work doesn’t guarantee success, but without it, success is almost guaranteed not to happen. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. I especially like your last sentence.

Ivan:
Thanks. I’ve had discussions with many people about luck and hard work. And you know, if you look at the studies about people who win lottery tickets and they, you know, become multi-millionaires, it’s an incredibly high percentage of people that lose all the money because they don’t know how to manage it. They’ve not earned it; they haven’t learned how to earn it and save it and work with it.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
They got really, really lucky and they lost it all. On the other hand, a lot of people that earn it, they keep it. And they keep it because they’ve learned how to manage it and maintain it, and that doesn’t mean they don’t take risks. I’ve taken risks, I’ve taken a lot of risks in BNI. I don’t think I’ve ever said this on a podcast, but about 12 years ago, I almost filed bankruptcy because BNI Connect was so much putting me in debt, yet I just kept focusing and working on it. And that’s what ended up… my story of BNI Connect is in [my book] The 3rd Paradigm, which I know I’ve talked about. And so it’s not about luck, it’s about hard work. And that’s my philosophy on the two.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. And if you haven’t done the work, you don’t know how hard it is to actually be successful. But then when you are successful, you can produce, which is the important part.

Ivan:
It’s like when people are just handed things they don’t… it’s oftentimes second, third, fourth generation family members can’t maintain the success and wealth of the family because they didn’t achieve it and earn it.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And I mean, that’s just one small sliver of the issue. But I am talking to BNI members, and these are people who — they’re out there working, and I’m telling all of you listening to this, keep going. When I walk into a room, I may not be the smartest guy in a room. I may not be the busiest guy in a room, but I tell you this, I’m the most persistent guy in a room. I am a dog with a bone, and I learned to work very hard, and that’s how I have achieved success. That, and a great network of people who’ve helped me get there. All right, Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Ivan, that was really great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Without effort, luck is useless. Hard work doesn’t guarantee success, but without it, success is almost guaranteed not to happen.

title.rendered: Episode 960: Why Most Leaders Fail

content.rendered:
Synopsis

As leaders, we have two jobs. Our first job is to set the vision, and our second job is to remove obstacles. You and your team need Kamryn Reynolds’ “3D Fix.”

  • Discernment: knowing what needs to be done when and how to do it.
  • Discipline: doing the work and holding yourself accountable and helping your team do the same.
  • Diligence: making sure the job gets done by helping your team when they get stuck.

The key to the 3D Fix is instilling a sense of ownership in your team. BNI chapters that apply this 3D principle become incredible chapters.

Links

  • Visit BNI Tampa Bay, where Kamryn is an Executive Director.
  • Visit Kamryn’s business website, Articulate Advantage.
  • Watch the Green and Clean video on YouTube.
  • Listen to Episode 892: Infinite Legacy.

Complete Transcript of Episode 960

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am back home in Austin, Texas. I just did one of my Founder’s Education series globally. I love doing those, and I do them at least half a dozen times a year, almost… sometimes every month. So I get around more and more via the internet, which is a little bit easier these days for me.

Priscilla:
No kidding. What do you have for us today?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve got somebody, this is his third time on the show, Kamryn Reynolds, and Kamryn is the youngest Executive Director in BNI serving in the Tampa Bay, Florida region [USA]. He’s launched and led award winning chapters. He’s been featured at various BNI US National Conferences, and he spoke at BNI Global Convention in Sydney, Australia. Kamryn focuses on helping [BNI] members understand referral psychology for their businesses so that growth becomes inevitable. Kamryn, welcome back to the BNI Podcast.

Kamryn:
Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Well, it’s good to have you here. So the full title of your presentation is “Why Most Leaders Fail – The 3D Fix.” Tell me what you’re talking about with that.

Kamryn:
Yeah. So I mean, all of us are leaders in some way in our lives, whether it’s personal, whether it’s in how we practice faith, whether it’s in our business, whether it’s in organizations. And so many of us are trying, but our efforts aren’t being directed in the right way.

I mean, I had a leader in an organization, a service organization I was in. They approached me one time and they said, “Hey, Kam, we would love you to fill this specific position.”

And I was like, “Okay, yeah, cool. What would I be doing?”

And he’s like, “Well, I actually don’t know. I just know that we need somebody in this position.”

And I’m like, “Dude, I can’t commit to something I don’t know what I’m going to do. So once you figure out what those responsibilities are, come back to me,” and I never heard another word about again from him or anybody else.

And then that organization didn’t really do much, and then eventually that leader was switched out to somebody else, and things got moving. And so that’s a great example of what not to do as a leader. So I mean, with that, my favorite example of a story version of this, because I love stories, Green and Clean. Are you familiar with Stephen R. Covey? He’s a very famous author.

Ivan:
Yeah, of course. I know him. And I know his son really, really well, Stephen M.R. Covey is a past member of the Transformational Leadership Council, so I know Stephen, his son, very well.

Kamryn:
Yeah, so it’s Stephen talking about his son, Stephen – Green and Clean. People can look it up on YouTube, just Green and Clean. But this story is absolutely fantastic. The dad, Stephen, he’s talking to his son at a family meeting, and his son agrees to take care of the yard. And so Stephen takes him out. He takes him to his neighbor’s yard. He says, “You see this yard? This is Green and Clean. That’s your job. Green and Clean.” And he goes over to their yard. He says, “See this yard? See our yard? This is not Green and Clean. So what’s Green?”

The son points over the neighbor’s yard.

He says, “What’s Clean?”

His son points over to his neighbor’s yard.

He says, “Perfect. I’m here to help you with whatever you need. You tell me when. You tell me how. I’ll even tell you what I would do if it was me.”

And his son goes, “Well, what would you do?” He says, “Well, I would turn on the sprinklers, which you can pull out a hose. You can do it with buckets. You could spit on it all day long, if you want. But how you do it is up to you. Green and Clean is the goal we’re looking for.” And so they have this whole dialog back and forth. And he says, they got to the end and they had their agreement; it was amazing.

And he did nothing, absolutely nothing. He goes, “I could have excused Saturday and Sunday away. But it was Tuesday evening at this point. Monday and Tuesday doing nothing – that is inexcusable.” He said, “I almost moved to win / lose tactics of “You get over here. You do this. You hear me,” and I love what he says. He says, “But that would have killed effectiveness in turn for efficiency.” Because what happens when he’s not there to tell him what to do?

And so, you know, eventually Tuesday evening comes, and they agreed to meet two times a week to talk about, you know, “How are you doing with your stewardship of the yard?” And they meet and he knows the time’s coming, and he sees his son’s lip quivering. He goes, “Hey son, so how’s the yard? Is it green and clean?” And he starts bawling [crying]. And he goes, “Dad, it’s so hard.”

The dad thought, So hard? You haven’t done a single thing. That is what he wanted to say. That is not what he actually said. Instead, he says, “How can I help you?”

Ivan:
How many times have we seen BNI members do that, by the way? They haven’t even done most of the things that need to be done. All right, we’re halfway through, so you want to wrap up the story. It’s a great story.

Kamryn:
Yeah, absolutely. I mean, that’s the end of the story, right? He helps him get started. And then once this gets started, the son keeps it going. And so he set a vision. He set this is how you can do it. And that really leads back to — As leaders, we have two jobs. Our first job is to set the vision, and our second job is to remove obstacles.

Ivan: 
Yeah, and to coach them as well. Because I think Stephen Sr. was definitely coaching his son. I mean, I’ve heard Stephen M.R. tell this story. And you did a good job, by the way, of telling the story very much the way he does. But I think coaching is also part of it. He was there to help him through the process.

Kamryn:
Yeah, and so, right, that whole thing of set the vision, remove the obstacles and leaning into the 3D fix. Well, first you need to set the….

Ivan:
The 3D fix… talk about the climb: discernment, discipline, and diligence.

Kamryn:
Yeah, absolutely. So the 3D Fix to really streamline your leadership, to really have it take hold – that first D is Discernment. And this is the knowing the what; the knowing the when; the knowing the how. Who should be doing what, and really understanding what should be happening, [or] even if it should be happening.

Then once you understand that, then it’s moving to that Discipline. And the discipline is the doing, whether you feel like it or not. It’s also that holding others and yourself accountable to it. And there’s that quote I love…

Ivan:
Accountability is something you do for someone, not to someone.

Kamryn:
Yes, I love when you say that; that’s [among] my favorite quotes from you. But there’s another quote saying, What you permit, you promote. What you allow, you encourage. What you condone, you own. And What you tolerate, you deserve. So finding out how to help others be accountable.

And then that third one is Diligence. And that’s that whole Brian Tracy quote, “If it’s to be, it’s up to me.” As a leader, it’s up to you to get it done, not necessarily for you to do it. So helping, whether it’s you or the other people who need to be doing it based on your discernment of who should do it, to get it done, get it across the finish line.

And you can see that pretty clearly in that Green and Clean story, the Discernment was, “My son should be doing this, not me.” And then the Discipline was, “We’re going to meet twice a week. This is the vision.” And then that Diligence was following up on it, making sure it got done. So Discernment, Discipline and Diligence.

Ivan:
I like it. So how can the leaders listening to this apply the 3D principle to transform their leadership, particularly in BNI, for sure?

Kamryn:
Absolutely. I think a big piece of it, coming back to the two things as leaders — set the vision, remove the obstacles. Setting the vision is a big piece of that. Because… have you ever heard the tale of the brick layers?

Ivan:
Yes, I think I wrote that one, if it’s the one I’m thinking of, in Infinite Legacy. [Episode 892]

Kamryn:
Yeah, I think it was. But I mean, super basic 30-second version – [about] the brick layers. This man comes walking into a town. He sees three brick layers. He goes to the first and he says, “Hey, man, what are you doing?” He says, “I’m laying bricks.” He’s like, “Oh, cool.” He walks to the second brick layer. He says, “Hey, man, what are you doing?” He goes, “I’m building a wall.” He goes, “Oh, cool.” He walks to the third brick layer, and he says, “Hey, man, what are you doing?” And the brick layer turns to him, stands up, looks at what he’s building. He says, “I’m building a cathedral where men’s hearts will change and they will come together.” They’re all doing the same thing.

Ivan:
They’re all doing the same thing, but one is a master brick layer, and what a difference. Yeah, that version of that story is in my book, Infinite Legacy. So thank you for bringing that up.

Kamryn:
By the way, best opening to any book you have… that dynamite opening is fantastic, right? But with that, how chapter members and how leaders can use it is set a vision that inspires because if your goal is, “Oh, we’re gonna grow the chapter by two members,” not an inspiring vision. Where if it’s, “What we’re gonna do, this is how it’s gonna impact us.” That’s a vision that people can get behind. And then you got to remove the obstacles with that Discernment, with that Discipline, with that Diligence.

Ivan:
Excellent. So you want to talk about the before and after? And we need to wrap up then.

Kamryn:
Yeah. I mean, the biggest piece of this is ownership, and that’s really what that 3D principle is – to instill inside of the members, inside of chapters, is that feeling of ownership. This is your chapter. Me, specifically, I’m a launch specialist. I specialize in launching chapters. I’ve done over 11 in the last two years, multiple Hall of Fame launches. And the chapters where I’ve used this 3D fix, they become incredible chapters. They launch quickly. It’s incredible. And the ones where I haven’t, where I’ve gotten a little lax maybe on the discernment of, you know, just accepting anybody instead of really discerning who we are going to be the right people….

Ivan:
Yeah, discernment is important.

Kamryn:
…or that discipline of not helping people stay accountable, or maybe that diligence of getting complacent and not getting it across the finish line. It changes the game.

Ivan:
Well, Kamryn, you’re a good storyteller. You really are. I’d love to have you back again sometime. Kamryn’s website for BNI is www.BNItampa.com and his website for his coaching business is articulateadvantage.com, www.articulateadvantage.com. Kamryn, thanks again. I’m going to have your father on soon.

Kamryn:
Yes, sir, thank you for having me. He’s excited.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’m excited to have him on. So thank you so much for being on. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both for the great podcast.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: As leaders, we have two jobs: to set the vision and to remove obstacles. Use “The 3D Fix:” Discernment, Discipline, and Diligence.

title.rendered: Episode 959: Make Yourself Referrable

content.rendered:
Synopsis

You can’t refer someone you can’t remember. Speech Slayer Laura Reid shares a simple framework to make yourself referrable with one memorable story.

  • Situation
  • Struggle
  • Solution

Sharing a story that you’ve experienced also reduces the anxiety you feel about speaking. Your experiences become your stories, and your stories become powerful hooks to have people remember what it is that you do.

Share your own memorable story in the comments.

Links

  • Visit Laura’s website, www.thespeechslayer.com.
  • Read Laura’s book, The Public Speaking Horror Show.
  • Check out the Or8tor public speaking app.
  • Listen to Episode 49: Conquer Your Fear of Public Speaking.
  • Listen to Episode 416: Specific Is Terrific for Referrals.

Complete Transcript of Episode 959

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi there, Priscilla. I am doing great. And I just got back from the [BNI] U.S. National Conference in Orlando [Florida], so it’s really good to be back. And I have a guest on today. Her name is Laura Reid. I’m pronouncing your last name correctly, right Laura?

Laura:
Yes, you are.

Ivan:
I’m glad to have Laura on. Laura was at the BNI [Global] Convention in Hawaii recently. She’s a storytelling strategist and public speaking and book coach who helps people turn their stories into impact, which I think is really important. What I thought was interesting in her bio was that she’s a former stutterer who once struggled with severe speaking anxiety, and she transformed her greatest challenge into her life’s work.

I like to call that sometimes “What’s in the way becomes the way.” And I think that’s probably your story, Laura. You’re the bestselling author of The Public Speaking Horror Show. You’re going to mention an app that you got when we’re done. And you’re a lecturer in public speaking at Hawaii Community College. You’ve been a BNI member for eight years, and as I mentioned, you just recently spoke at the BNI [Global] Convention. So welcome to BNI Podcast, Laura.

Laura:
I’m thrilled to be here, Ivan. Thank you.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great to have you. So this podcast, the full title is, Make Yourself Referrable with One Story. So the first question I have for you is, can you explain to me what you mean and what you put together about explaining versus being referable?

Laura:
Absolutely. Well, most people think that if they list and explain everything they do, that they’re really covering more ground and therefore increasing their chances of getting a referral. When in reality, the opposite happens, because their fellow members can’t remember anything specific so they don’t know how to refer them. Now, a short, concrete story gives the brain a hook. If it’s a specific type of client, a clear problem and a visible result, then that’s going to pop into someone’s mind later when they’re talking to a friend, a colleague or a client.

So really, for example, instead of thinking Laura’s a speaking coach who does a bunch of things to help people with public speaking, they think Laura is that person who helped that nervous accountant give a confident presentation and land a big contract. That’s referrable, and that’s a story that can retell word-of-mouth, and that’s what turns introductions into real business.

Ivan:
Yeah, listen, I couldn’t agree with you more. I’ve been preaching this for 40 years — specific is terrific in doing your weekly presentation. And then if you basically shotgun your presentation rather than sharp-shoot it, you’re not giving anyone information that they can remember and that they can share. And that’s basically what you’re saying here, right?

Laura:
Yeah, absolutely. I mean, it’s a common mantra in BNI, right? Be specific, to be terrific. But I would also add: be memorable, to be referable. And now that doesn’t rhyme as well, it would make a good book title. You can have that, Ivan, if you want.

Ivan:
No, you should do that one.

Laura: (laughing)
Okay, thanks.

Ivan:
I’ll do the foreword for you.

Laura:
Okay, all right, I’m gonna hold you to that. But yeah, it really put simply — you can’t refer someone who you don’t remember. And there’s a reason that we remember stories from our childhood, but we may have forgotten the information heavy PowerPoint from yesterday.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, anyone who’s ever seen my PowerPoints, they’re almost all photographs, graphics, rather than written words, because I think you remember the image and you remember the statement more than you remember a laundry list of verbiage. So what’s a simple referral story framework that you recommend?

Laura:
Yeah, I love simple frameworks because it really demystifies stories for business owners, where sometimes we think that we need to be creative or good writers to create good stories. And it’s really not true. It’s very scientific. So here’s a three part structure that’s really easy to remember. I call it The Three S’s – Situation, Struggle and Solution.

So the situation is simply a real client or scenario that you experienced. One that you remember well, because if it comes to your mind clearly, [it is] likely [that] there’s a good story there.

And then two is a struggle. What was going wrong? What was at stake for that client, and especially like, what did they have to risk by maybe not being your client, right?

And then the last is a solution or the result, how you helped and what the member did and that outcome. So can I share an example?

Ivan:
Yeah, please.

Laura:
So here’s one that always comes to mind for me. Once a business coach came to me because she was asked to speak at an international conference. Okay, there is a situation. She was freaking out because she had never spoken at a conference before and lacked speaking confidence, and she knew this was a huge opportunity for her, she didn’t want to blow it. Okay, there’s a struggle. I helped her craft a powerful presentation and find her speaking confidence. There is the solution. After her presentation, she called me and she was crying, and I was like, “Oh no,” but then she said five words I’ll never forget: “You just changed my life.” And her life really did change after that. She went on to take on a leadership role in BNI as president of her chapter, and even spoke at a BNI National Conference. There’s the result.

Ivan:
There you go. That’s outstanding. And if you can get creative like that, you know, it’s amazing, the results that you get. I know I have a podcast. I don’t know what podcast number it is, but with an accountant who was petrified to speak, but I remember asking her, “Would you be willing to give people a test? You don’t have to talk, just read the questions.” And it was at a BNI chapter meeting, and she said, “Yeah, I can give a test.”

That was so funny, because she came up with these 10 questions, and half the people were missing most of the answers about taxes, and they’re like, “Oh my gosh, I’m gonna go to jail. This is horrible.” And she just got everybody so engaged. And what was funny was that she was petrified to speak for the 10 minutes that she had, and she went over time because she started speaking just on her own and extemporaneously, and she was just comfortable. So, look for techniques that will let you know, open up and speak. Great story.

Laura:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
All right, so using stories to reduce nerves and increase referrals. Do you want to talk about that?

Laura:
Yeah, absolutely. I mean, as someone who grew up with extreme public speaking anxiety, I have found that stories work really well to ease your nerves. One way that I really got over my own nerves was I started entering storytelling competitions, and I found that when I was up on stage sharing a real story from my life. I wasn’t thinking so much about every word. I didn’t have to memorize it because I experienced it. And I think BNI members find that too, because, we have to speak as BNI members every week. You know, whether we consider ourselves public speakers or not, we are; that’s what we’re doing. So sharing simple stories that they’re not memorizing, that they’ve experienced, really builds that connection, and it connects them, too, with their own voice, and they’re going to naturally just feel less nervous because they’re just being present and sharing that story.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know, here’s something that I think surprises people when I tell them, is that I still get a little bit nervous before I go up and speak. And I’ve been doing this for decades, and yet I have kind of reframed my mindset that those nerves are my adrenaline, and the adrenaline will help me do a better job. And so I tend to embrace that discomfort and use it, as opposed to be afraid of it. And it takes a little practice to get to that point, but once you get there, it’s, I think, powerful.

Laura:
Yeah, absolutely. I mean, if you’re not a little bit nervous and you may have stopped caring, which is not good.

Ivan:
Yes, not good!

Laura:
But the nervousness feeling is so close to excitement. So I like to just rebrand my nerves that I’m really just super excited, as I did this morning before speaking to you, you know? (chuckling)

Ivan:
That’s funny.

Laura:
Yeah.

Ivan:
All right, so we’re almost out of time. Is there a specific thing that you want to end up chatting about before we wrap and give your website?

Laura:
Yeah, absolutely. What I most want people to know is that their stories matter. And what we find, I think, often, is that the stories that we want to share and that we might be scared to share, but we put them out in there into the world, they end up being such a gift for other people. Because our brains are really hard wired for storytelling. And I mean, as Maya Angelou famously said, people will forget what you said, they will forget what you did, but they will never forget how you made them feel.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s so true. And you know, I meet a lot of people and they talk about writing a book, but [they say] “so many people have done books on that topic,” and I say, “Yeah, but they don’t have your stories.”

Laura:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Your stories are personal to you, and they may resonate with somebody in the book. And I think it’s the same thing when you’re doing a presentation. There are many people who may do what you do, but they don’t have your experiences, and those experiences become your stories, and your stories become powerful, and they become hooks, as you call them, to have people remember what it is that you do, IF you’re specific enough, and the storytelling is the way to do it.

Laura:
Yeah, absolutely.

Ivan: You have a website that we can share with our BNI members and listeners. It is www.thespeechslayer.com.  I love that. (chuckling)

Laura:
I was a Buffy the Vampire Slayer fan back in the day.

Ivan: (laughing)
Okay, that’s good to know. thespeechslayer.com.  You also have an app. Do you want to tell everybody what the app is real quick?

Laura:
Yeah, this is so cool, actually. When I was speaking at the convention in Hawaii, I met two other BNI members from London, and together, we’ve developed a public speaking app that got released recently on the Apple Store, and it’s called Or8tor, and it’s spelled o-r-8-t-o-r, and I’m hoping it’ll really help other members, be able to practice and deliver even better stories.

Ivan: Yeah, so just for the record, before we went on live, I mispronounced it orator, but it’s or8tor with the eight in the middle, which I think is great, and we’ll make sure that it gets in the transcript of this podcast. Listen. thank you so much for being on, Laura. I really appreciate you.

Laura:
My pleasure, Ivan, can’t wait to see you back this side one day.

Ivan:
Alright, I look forward to it, too. Over to you, Priscilla.

Laura: Aloha.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, I think that’s really fascinating. Thank you very much. I think that’s it for this week.

title.rendered: Episode 958: How You Stand Changes Who You Meet (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week’s podcast focuses on a technique for in-person networking, and one that can make your meeting seem more welcoming to visitors.

The key to meeting people at a networking event is how you stand. When you walk into a room full of people, look for the open twos and open threes—groups of people that leave space for another person to join them. In an open two, the two people talking are not directly face-to-face, but stand shoulder-to-shoulder at an angle.

If you are hosting an event, remind people to open up their groups. If you have an open stance, you’re going to meet a lot of people that you may not have had an opportunity to meet in the past.

To help you visualize the difference between open groups and closed groups, we’ve included some helpful illustrations from Dr. Misner’s book Networking Like a Pro, Second Edition. Click the images to enlarge them.

Try changing your stance next time you’re at an in-person event, and share what happens in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 732/958

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Still Zooming around the world. I have gone to a couple of actual locations. I was asked by Jack Canfield to speak at his event not so long ago and had a chance to see Jack again. I’ve done a US C.O.R.E. Zoom meeting – the regions from all over the United States that are run by BNI Global. I’ve been to [BNI] China for a live webinar, Malaysia for the [BNI] National Conference, and in Clubhouse again. I’ve been asked to speak in a number of Clubhouse events; the last one was with Dr. Finance on a great Clubhouse group. So still Zooming around a lot.

Priscilla:
Great. I don’t know what Clubhouse is.

Ivan:
Clubhouse is an online social media platform where people basically come into a room and you can listen to a speaker and raise your hand and ask questions, or you’re invited to be a speaker. And it’s an interesting program. And I do see a lot of BNI people on Clubhouse.

Priscilla:
Interesting. I never heard of it. Okay, great. Well, what are you going to share with us today?

Ivan:
Well, I’m going to share a concept that I have talked about for many years, if you’ve heard me do a presentation, this has been in my talk. And my assistant, Dana, just recently pointed out, “You don’t have a podcast on this.” And it’s a key element of my presentations. And this is aimed at people with an in-person meeting.

So you know, maybe a hybrid where occasionally you’re going in-person, or regular in-person BNI meetings. Or you go to a conference and it’s in-person, or any networking event and it’s in-person. So the technique I’m going to give you today is a technique that I’ve used and talked about in many of my books, about Open Twos and Open Threes. And it is for in-person meetings.

So sometimes you walk into a room full of people, you look around, and you have no idea where to start. None. You think who do I walk up to? Who do I talk to? It’s that moment where some people think, who do I know? And why did I even come here?

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And what’s true is how you stand really does change who you meet. And so what you want to do is look around the room and figure out where do you start. So I want to make it a little bit easier for everyone. Now, I understand that many people listening to this are beyond this point. And I get that a lot of people really understand how to network and they go to networking events where they know a lot of people, but it’s important for you to recognize that there are many people that are presently having the experience I just gave. And if you recognize them, and that situation, you can actually be a better networker yourself, by understanding the concepts of Open Twos and Open Threes.

So let’s go back to the scenario of walking into the room. I’ve attached to this podcast transcript, a graphic to the podcast that you can download. It is from Networking Like a Pro, the book I wrote, Second Edition. And the first slide is Where do I start? Where do I start? And it’s a view from above, looking down. This could be you know, just maybe one small corner of the room where you entered. It could be many, many more people around you than what this shows. But the idea is, you walk in the room and you see from above all of these groups of people talking to each other, mixing and mingling.

Now the second slide is a slide called Closed Twos or Closed Threes. And if you’re listening to this podcast and you don’t have the slides in front of you imagine that you’re, you know, on a balcony looking down on everyone. And by the way, Richard Quest with CNN, I talked to him about this technique. And that’s exactly what he did on his CNN broadcast as he stood on a balcony looking down at the audience, and he said, “Oh, look at that, there’s a Closed Two, there’s an Open Three.”

And so you can see it looking down. But a Closed Two is two people standing perpendicular to each other having a conversation; a Closed Three is three people. So it’s like a triangle, looking down from above. And it’s really hard to break into those conversations, especially if you don’t know any of those people.

Ivan:
And so what I recommend is that if you’re going to a group, you don’t know a lot of people, you want to look for the Open Twos and Open Threes, which is the third diagram that I have in the transcript. And I’ll describe it for those of you listening, who don’t have access to the transcript. An Open Two looks more like a “V”. V as in Victor. There are two people standing askew, which makes it really easy to walk up and introduce yourself. An Open Three looks a little bit like a “U”, there’s an open spot. An Open Four would be like a huddle with an opening. Or a Five or a Six, the more people, just having an open spot is so incredibly important.

 And now if you go back to the first slide, if you look at slide number one, and you look down at it, the bottom left [image] is the Open Two, the top right [image] is the Open Three. Those are the kinds of things that you want to be looking for. And of course, [there are] individuals walking around. But those open groups are what you want to be looking for, because you can walk up and have a conversation with those people very easily.

Now, if you are running a BNI Chapter, or if you’re even a Member of a BNI Chapter, if you really want that group, the visitors who come to that group, at an in-person meeting, you want them to feel that you’re very open, and easy to talk to – always, always, always stand in Open Twos – Open Threes, and Open Groups.

I had a chapter who did a survey of visitors and asked them their honest opinion of the chapter. And the overwhelming opinion from visitors was: “They weren’t real friendly, they really seem to like each other a lot.” What they meant – they didn’t really reach out to the visitors. And so the Visitor [Hosts] are like, well, what can we do to improve that? I said, “Start with this, just do this; we’ll add layers to this down the road, but just do this.”

So the chapter really made an effort to always stand in Open Twos – Open Threes, Open Groups, so that when a visitor came in, and the Visitor Host was walking them around, or if the visitor broke loose from the Visitor Host and was walking around, it was easy to strike up a conversation. And the surveys, then, from the visitors came back significantly different. They had comments like, “You know, I can’t quite put my finger on it. But it was just so easy to talk to people in this group.” “Very, very friendly group. “And they hadn’t done anything else other than do the Open Twos – Open Threes concept.

So if you are new to a group and you walk in, look for those. If it is a group that you’re a member of, always try to make sure to stand like that, to stand in Open Twos – Open Threes. And that’s what I mean when I say, and it was your suggestion – I love the title, How You Stand Changes Who You Meet. Because if you have an open stance, you’re going to meet a lot of people that you may not have had an opportunity to meet in the past.

One time, it’s so funny, I did my whole keynote presentation, Priscilla. And at the end, I was signing books, meeting people. It was about a half hour later I met one member and we’re having a conversation and he looks at me. And he says, and he points down at our feet like someone’s looking from above, and we were standing perpendicular to each other. And he said, “We’re in a Closed Two.” And I’m like, oh yeah, you’re right, okay.” So we opened it up.

Here’s the amazing thing, Priscilla. Immediately, and I mean immediately, a third person came in. And as soon as the third person came in, we looked at each other and we said, “Closed Three” and we opened it up. And immediately a fourth person came in, we opened. A fifth, a sixth, a seventh, an eighth. It was a large group very quickly and all we did was open our stance and be friendly. And it really makes an incredible difference. This is such a subtle but significant technique in embracing a relationship-building component of your networking process. Open Twos – Open Threes. I highly recommend it. I’ll have graphics from Networking Like a Pro in this podcast. What are your thoughts Priscilla?

Priscilla:
It is incredibly subtle, but it obviously works. And you also have to greet them in some way, right? When they’re coming close.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
And entice them into your circle.

Ivan:
Yeah, it helps if you’re actually friendly when they do step into your group. No question about that. Here’s something that’s counter intuitive. You know, oftentimes introverts tell me they have a hard time striking up a conversation. And this is totally counterintuitive, but it works.

The really introverted people, I would recommend they look for the larger open groups, you know, an Open Four, an Open Five, Open Six. And that sounds counterintuitive. Why would someone walk into a larger group? You walk in, for the introverts here, because you are kind of unnoticed. It’s easy to just slide into a larger group. Eventually, someone’s going to say, “Hey, hi, nice to meet you. What’s your name?” and you have a chance to introduce yourself. But it’s not as abrupt as walking into an Open Two, and now you’ve got to speak because there’s only two of you.

Priscilla:
Yeah, right. I see that.

Ivan:
It’s a counterintuitive concept. But trust me on this, it works. And it works extremely well.

Priscilla:
That’s funny.

Ivan:
Well, that’s all I’ve got for today, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds great. Well, thank you. That’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
