title.rendered: Episode 957: Two Beers and a Puppy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Bradley Page from BNI Synergy in Newcastle, Australia shares Ross McCammon’s “Two Beers and a Puppy” test and how it fits in with the concepts in Who’s In Your Room? Looking at all your contacts, ask yourself these questions:

  • Would I have two beers* with this person?
  • Would I allow this person to look after my puppy for a weekend?

If you answer “no” to both questions, this person is an anchor, and should not be in your room–or your BNI chapter.

If you answer “yes” to both questions, this person is an engine, and should definitely be in your room–and your BNI chapter.

*Don’t drink beer? Substitute whatever you drink when relaxing with friends.

Links

  • Read Ross McCammon’s “Two Beers and a Puppy”.
  • Listen to Episode 479: Are the People in Your Life an Engine or an Anchor?
  • Listen to Episode 795: Who’s In Your Room, 2nd Edition.
  • Listen to Episode 952: Positive Attitude Is a Competitive Advantage.

Complete Transcript of Episode 957

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Still enjoying the Austin weather. It hasn’t gotten too hot here yet, so it’s great. I’ve got a guest today. First of all, I love the topic, Two Beers and a Puppy. I think that’s hysterical. I have a guest today. He’s from down under; his name is Bradley Page, and he got up at 0-dark-30 to do this podcast with me. I really appreciate it. Bradley is a licensed real estate agent, an auctioneer, and he’s done it for over 22 years. He’s been a BNI member for 10 years. He picked up his 10-year pin at the [BNI] Global Convention in Sydney in 2025 where we got a Guinness World Record. I did a podcast on that recently for the Largest Speed Networking [event]. Currently, he’s doing his second stint as a BNI member, and he’s the inaugural chapter president for BNI Synergy in Newcastle, Australia, which was launched in July 2025. Bradley, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Bradley:
Thank you very much, Ivan. It’s an absolute honor and pleasure to be invited onto your program.

Ivan:
Well, it’s truly my pleasure. And I think one of the great things about bringing in guests is they come in with some ideas and perspective on the program that’s, you know, interesting and funny, and this is certainly both of those. So talk about the process – Two Beers and a Puppy here. Talk about the process.

Bradley:
Absolutely. This process was actually written by a fellow by the name of Ross McCammon, and it’s two-fold. It’s to figure out someone’s value in your life, along with a helpful test for determining how you feel about someone. So what you actually do is, in order to find out how you actually feel about someone, you’ve got to ask yourself two questions, would you have a beer with this person? And would I allow this person to look after my puppy over a weekend? Now you’re going to get four answers to this particular question. So some people are going to be no and no. These people are to be avoided at all costs.

(both laughing)

Bradley:
All right, they’re your “Negative Nancy’s,” okay, which I know that you’ve covered before. So some people are going to be yes and no. These people are to be cautiously trusted. So they’re sitting in the VCP process where you’re starting to know, like, and trust them. Some people are no and yes. These people are no fun to be around, but they make the world a better place for puppies.

Ivan: (laughing)
Okay.

Bradley:
All right, okay. And finally, right, some people are yes and yes. Now Ivan, these people are wonderful people, and your life and work are better for having them in your life. So seek them out, collaborate with them, and enjoy their company. So that’s the process in a whole.

Ivan:
I love it. You named somebody, and did I read this right? He was a yoga friend. You met him in yoga?

Bradley:
Yes, yes. We were. We were both having some challenging times, and yeah, where we were attracting some yuckiness into our life. And she actually came forward and said, “Look, I’ve been given this process, and since I’ve used it, it’s changed my life.” And I went, “Well, things happen for a reason.” If you believe in the Law of Attraction, you attract things into your life for a reason.

Ivan:
The last BNI Podcast, I talked about the Law of Attraction, so I absolutely talked about it. All right, you have a lot more to cover, so how do you use it with people you meet for the first time?

Bradley:
Oh, absolutely. Okay, observation is power, and judgment is weakness. So what you’ve got to do is observe people’s mannerisms. Observe their dress code, observe how they speak and introduce themselves to you. Are they a loud people, or are they really warm, inviting and calm?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Bradley:
Are they pushing you with what they’re trying to get across? Are they someone that you feel really, really comfortable with and you want to spend some time with them? But more importantly, are they someone that you want to have a beer with? And let them look after your dog on a weekend? So, you know some people are a little bit nervous when you’re meeting them, and some can be really, really overbearing.

Ivan:
Now, for those people who don’t do beer, would a couple of coffees or a couple of glasses of wine be okay?

Bradley:
Absolutely, yes, definitely, yeah. So it’s not just defined to be [just beer]. Absolutely, yeah.

Ivan:
So I think I’ve actually had two beers in my entire life. Both of those were places where they had nothing… I wouldn’t drink the water — it was slightly brown… and they didn’t have anything else. So those are the only times I had a beer. But I’d have a couple of glasses of wine with you, my friend.

Bradley: (laughing)
I certainly would do that with yourself and Jody as well, Ivan.

Ivan:
You added something in what you sent me, which I really appreciated, and you talked about the book I wrote, Who’s In Your Room?, and how that applies to the Two Beers and a Puppy concept. You want to talk about that?

Bradley:
Absolutely, your book, yes, two beers and a puppy. I would read it religiously every three to four months. The writing in that is just phenomenal.

Ivan:
It’s Who’s In Your Room?, not Two Beers and a Puppy,

Bradley:
That’s right, Who’s In Your Room?

Ivan: (laughing)

Bradley:
That’s right, yes. Okay (chuckling) Go through all your social media platforms. So your Facebook, your Instagram, your LinkedIn, see who’s actually in there, who’s in your room currently, and ask that question, “Would you have two beers with this person, and would you have this person look after your dog over a weekend? Go through your phone, same sort of thing is that you’ll be amazed that the contacts that you’ve got in there, and are they people who you really want to resonate with? And who’s going to move you to closer to what you want to be? It’s a phenomenal process, and you’ll be astounded what names you put into what bucket.

Ivan:
Mmm

Bradley:
Yeah, and obviously, what do you do then, with the no and no [people] that you come up with? Well, in Who’s In Your Room? you put a little visualization of a box, and you put the names in the box where you can’t touch, and you put a couple of locks on them so they don’t get out. So that’s where they go.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you had some thoughts on engines and anchors, correct?

Bradley: 
Absolutely. In Who’s In Your Room? you say that there’s two types of people. There is engines. They are the people that… they’re always going, they’re always moving, and inspiring people to what you want to be. Where the anchors, they’re the ones that I want to pull you back. So, in this process, when you’re doing Two Beers and a Puppy, I can guarantee you your yes and yeses will be your engines, and your no and no’s will be your anchors.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Bradley:
And I know that, and Priscilla might be able to dig out the podcast, you also did a podcast on hunters versus farmers, same sort of process.

Ivan:
Yeah, so you also wanted to talk… Was that everything you had on two beers and a puppy, or two coffees, for those of you who don’t…

Bradley:
Two coffees, yes.

Ivan:
Anything else you want to add to that? Because I know you wanted to talk about the BNI Podcasts that you’ve heard over the years.

Bradley:
No, I think that’s pretty well it. It is a process. It’s a very strong process. I encourage everybody to do it, and hopefully they have the success that I’ve had in using the process to be a better person, and obviously attract the people into your life to get the results you want.

Ivan:
Excellent. So talk about your thoughts on the BNI Podcasts that come out each week. I thought that was interesting.

Bradley:
Oh, absolutely to me, and I can only talk about myself, but I see myself having an invisible BNI tool belt on my waist. And as each Podcast comes in, you share lots of wisdoms about Who’s In Your Room? – how to speak when the meeting is boring, hopefully Two Beers and a Puppy, and we’ve all got this invisible tool belt to help us to be a better person. Basically it’s a jigsaw puzzle, so each piece that comes out each week fills the jigsaw puzzle, and it’s another tool that goes into the tool belt. So it basically makes me a better person all around having those tools that I can use and being a better BNI member. So I really do hope that there are other BNI members out there that really look forward to receiving the BNI Podcast weekly and using the way I do.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate that, and it’s amazing to me as I travel around the world, how many BNI members listen to the podcast now. And I always know they listen to it because they say, “Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?” So it’s like, “Okay, here’s somebody who listens to my podcast.”

Bradley:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
And there is a link, and I’ll make sure it goes into the podcast. Is this for Ross’s book, or is in an article, Two Beers and a Puppy?

Bradley:
That will be the direct link to how it actually works, yes.

Ivan:
We will put that link here in the podcast, so anybody that wants more information on it can do so. Any closing thought before we wrap up?

Bradley:
No, Ivan. Love your work. Love listening to you, and love being a BNI member. And I love the way that BNI is changing the way the world does business.

Ivan:
Well, Bradley, I really appreciate you, and I appreciate your kind words, and I hope our paths cross in person again, sometime.

Bradley:
Absolute pleasure being invited onto your podcast, Ivan, and Namaste to you.

Ivan:
Yeah, thank you. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was a lot of fun, Ivan. By the way, I would definitely have two beers and you can take care of my puppy.

Ivan: (chuckling)
Oh, can I? All right, I’m not sure you’d want all the ticks that we have here in Austin, on my on my land.

Priscilla:
What are they?

Ivan:
Ticks. Those little insects that give you Lyme disease. So I’m not sure you would want your puppy running around here.

Priscilla:
Yes, yes, yes, ticks. Okay, well, I’ll skip on that and avoid the ticks.

Ivan: (laughing)


Priscilla:
Okay, here we go.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 956: You Can’t Think Your Way into Transformation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Have you ever known you should do something and not done it? That’s because you can’t think your way into transformation. You have to live your way into transformation. Learn why and how.

  • Insight is not implementation.
  • Transformation begins when behavior changes.
  • Identity follows behavior.
  • Transformation is incremental, not instant.
  • Community accelerates living.

Don’t wait until your mindset is perfect. Pick one behavior to start the process. Transformation doesn’t require a complete life overhaul. It just requires the next step.

Share the step you plan to take this week in the comments.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 46: Small Actions, Big Results.
  • Listen to Episode 236: Behaviors and Credibility.
  • Listen to Episode 744: Networking Is a Marathon, Not a Sprint.
  • Listen to Episode 955: Vision Without Execution Is Wishful Thinking.

Complete Transcript of Episode 956

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’m home in Austin, Texas, and today is the anniversary of me taking my wife out on our first date.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow, that’s very cute.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s pretty cool.

Priscilla:
Yeah, okay. Well, I hope you have a great date for this evening.

Ivan:
We will, absolutely.

Priscilla:
Okay. All right. So help me. What are you going to talk about today?

Ivan:
So my topic today is, You Can’t Think Your Way Into Transformation. And there’s a second piece to the to the title, which isn’t on the title to the podcast, but it’s probably the most important part of it. You have to LIVE your way into transformation. You can’t think your way into transformation. You have to live your way into transformation. Which kind of, you know, in a way, is a great segue from the last, or a great follow up to the last, podcast where we were talking about, you know, Vision versus Execution. So as many of you know, I’ve been a member of TLC, the Transformational Leadership Council for about 20 years, and so today I want to talk about transformation, having been a member of the Transformational Leadership Council for two decades.

So let me start with a question. Have you ever known what to do and still not done it? You knew you should make that call, you knew you should apologize for something, you knew you should follow up with someone, you knew you should exercise. You knew you should ask for a referral. You knew, but knowing didn’t change anything. Here’s the truth, you can’t think your way into transformation. You have to live your way into transformation. And today I want to talk about why that matters, especially inside a BNI Chapter.

So my first point is, Insight is Not Implementation. We live in the information age. We can access more knowledge in one afternoon than most people in history had access to in a lifetime. I don’t know what I would do without Monsieur Google. I mean, I Google so much in conversations with people, so the information is there. But knowledge does not equal change. You can read about leadership. You can study communication. You can attend conferences. You can listen to podcasts, hopefully, including this one. But insight is not implementation. Transformation begins when behavior changes, like I talked about in my last podcast. It’s not just when awareness increases that is the beginning, but that’s not where the rubber meets the road. I’ve seen members who understand perfectly how the referral process works. They can explain it, they can teach it, they can diagram it on a whiteboard, but until they consistently practice the things that it takes like One-to-Ones and follow up intentionally and educating their chapter about their business — nothing changes.

Clarity is step one. Consistency is step two. Transformation only happens with that consistency. So here’s my second point, Identity Follows Behavior. Identity Follows Behavior. Here’s something fascinating. We often think, “When I become more confident, then I’ll act more confident.” But psychology tells us something different. Identity follows behavior. You don’t become confident and then speak up. You speak up repeatedly, and confidence grows. Now, you may speak up just slightly as you develop that confidence.

For me, I didn’t know how to network. I was method acting my way through the first couple years of BNI. But as I did it, more and more, my confidence grew and I was able to speak up more. You don’t become a great networker and then start asking. You start asking – and over time, you become a great networker. In BNI, we talk about Givers Gain®. That’s not just a philosophy. It’s a practice. You don’t think your way into becoming generous. You practice giving. And over time, generosity becomes part of your identity. Transformation is not a light switch. It’s a dimmer switch. It gets brighter as you act.

Here’s my third point. Transformation Is Incremental, Not Instant. Not usually. Most people are waiting for a breakthrough moment. An epiphany, a revelation, a big emotional shift. But transformation usually doesn’t happen that way. It happens in small, disciplined actions repeated over time. It’s like training for a marathon. You cannot think your way into cardiovascular endurance. You cannot visualize your way into stronger legs.  You can’t affirmation your way across 26.2 miles. You train. One mile at a time. Networking works the same way. It’s why we named our last book, Networking is a Marathon, Not a Sprint. It’s one meeting at a time. It’s one One-to-One at a time. It’s one referral at a time. It’s one follow up at a time. It’s not dramatic, but it’s powerful. And over time, those small actions compound. They have a compounding effect.

Here’s my fourth point, Community Accelerates Living. Here’s where this becomes especially important for BNI members. Transformation happens faster in the right environment. Left alone, we rationalize. We procrastinate. We justify. But inside a strong chapter, there’s accountability. There’s encouragement. There’s modeling the right behavior. When you see another member consistently doing weekly One-to-Ones, it challenges you. When you see someone mastering, I mean really mastering, their Weekly Presentation, it pushes you, it nudges you. When you see someone embodying a Positive Attitude, even during challenges, it inspires you. You don’t just think differently. You start acting differently. Community doesn’t just teach transformation. It reinforces it. That’s one of the hidden powers of BNI, is that it reinforces the transformational ideas that you have.

So what does this mean for you this week? It means don’t wait until you “feel ready.” Don’t wait until you “fully understand.” Don’t wait until your mindset is perfect. Pick one behavior to start the process. Just one. Schedule the One-to-One. Make the uncomfortable call. Ask for the referral — clearly. Practice listening more than speaking. Follow up with intention.

Transformation doesn’t require a complete life overhaul. It just requires the next step. And then another. And then another. You don’t think your way into becoming a better leader. You don’t think your way into becoming a better networker. You don’t think your way into leaving a legacy. You live your way into these things. One intentional action. One disciplined habit. One uncomfortable conversation at a time. Because transformation isn’t an idea. It’s a lifestyle. And the good news? You can start living it today by doing the behaviors that I’ve just talked about today. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla: 
I think it’s great that you emphasize that it’s just one step a day, or not even a day, but one step in the process.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
Because that’s not intimidating.

Ivan:
Yeah. No, it’s not. That’s why I like To Do lists. I love to do a To Do list. And, you know, I may come up with a To Do list and it’s got 15 or 20, things on it. It looks overwhelming. But then what I do, and I still do a lot of my To Do lists, my short-term ones, by hand. Because what I like to do then is, as I do it, scratch it off.

Priscilla:
Me too. Oh, me too, Ivan. (laughing)

Ivan:
And at the end of the day, you know, if I had three or four things scratched off my list, it’s like, okay, I got some stuff done today. This was good. And then the next day, and then the next day. You know, I’m a big believer in the Law of Attraction. And I don’t know if I ever said this on a podcast — I was actually filmed for the movie, The Secret. Unfortunately, I ended up on the cutting room floor.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
I did. Yeah, I felt bad that I ended up on the cutting room floor. But here’s one of the things about the Law of Attraction. The word “action” is part of the word “attraction,” and so you can attract as much as you want into your life. You can have this vision for transformation in your life, but if you don’t take action, then that’s not going to lead you to the transformation that you want. The word action is part of the word attraction, and I think that’s really critical.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Yeah.

Ivan:
That’s it for me, and I’d love for [BNI] Education Coordinators to share these ideas within the chapter. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, you’re welcome.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Have you ever known you should do something and not done it? You can’t think your way into transformation: you have to live it.

title.rendered: Episode 955: Vision Without Execution Is Wishful Thinking

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Anyone can have a vision. Very few people build something that lasts. Vision tells you where you want to go, but execution determines how you get there. If you really want results, you need a clear vision, defined systems of execution, and a culture of accountability.

Ask yourself these questions:

  • What is my vision for my chapter, my region, or my business?
  • What specific behaviors support that vision?
  • Where is my execution inconsistent?
  • What existing system could I implement this week to close the gap?

Share your execution success stories in the comments.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 162: BNI Light Just Isn’t Right.
  • Listen to Episode 488: The Hard Path Is Easier.
  • Listen to Episode 728: Coaching a Chapter to Greatness.
  • Listen to Episode 951: The Curse of Complexity.

Complete Transcript of Episode 955

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m still at home in Austin. This has been, you know, probably a little longer than normal, where I haven’t had to get on a plane and fly somewhere. So I’m enjoying my time here. The weather in Austin is great this time of year. It’s really fantastic.

Priscilla:
Oh, good. And so what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, today I want to talk about vision, but more importantly, execution. Vision without execution is really just wishful thinking. And I want to talk about this because I think it sounds inspiring, but it can be dangerously incomplete. Now, don’t get me wrong. Vision matters. In fact, without vision, organizations drift, people drift, chapters drift. But vision alone, that’s just a well-worded dream, and dreams don’t build businesses. Execution builds businesses.

So vision is exciting. It’s emotional. It feels big, it feels strategic. You can stand in front of a room and say, “We’re going to double our chapter, we’re going to increase referrals by 30%!” By the way, if you double your chapter, you’re going to increase your referrals by 150% just for the hard data that we have. Or you can say, “We’re going to become the premier networking chapter in our community.” And everyone nods to that, and they all say, “Yeah, that sounds great.” But what then? Because the distance between vision and results is filled by only one thing, and that’s behavior. And behavior requires systems, discipline, follow-up, accountability. Accountability.

Remember, accountability is not something you do to someone, it’s something you do for someone. So accountability is really important. And a focus on excellence. I’ve often said, “Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option?” Excellence is an option, and it’s all about the vision first and then the behavior. In BNI, we don’t just say Givers Gain®. We really do our best to operationalize it. We don’t just talk about building relationships. We track one-to-ones. We measure referrals; we record Thank You For Closed Business. That’s execution.

Vision is direction. Education is movement. Think about this. A Vision tells you where you want to go, but the execution on that vision determines how you actually get there. You can have the clearest GPS in the world, but if you never put the car in drive, you’re just sitting in the driveway admiring what you believe your destination will be. And I’ve seen this in organizations around the world. Leaders with incredible vision. Brilliant ideas. Inspiring language. But no implementation rhythm. No follow-through cadence. No structure. And eventually the excitement fades, because people don’t stay motivated by potential. They stay motivated by progress. I’m gonna repeat that: People don’t stay motivated by potential. They stay motivated by progress.

So there is something — a dangerous space that I call The Execution Gap. It’s the space between what we say we want and what we consistently do. In BNI terms, that gap shows up when we say relationships matter… but we don’t schedule one-to-ones. We say referrals are important… but we don’t educate our members on how to effectively refer business to us. We say culture matters… but we tolerate negativity. That’s the gap. Those things are the gap between vision and execution. And here’s the hard truth: execution isn’t glamorous. Vision gets applause. Execution builds results. Vision gets headlines. Execution produces habits. Habits are what build success. Go to [BNI Podcast] Episode 728, “Coaching a Chapter to Greatness,” on what it takes to create the right habits to achieve success in a BNI chapter. Accomplishment happens when we move from talking about it to tracking it, because what gets measured, gets managed. And what gets managed improves.

So why does execution fail? Why do people struggle with execution? Let me suggest a few reasons:

1. Complexity.
They add too many features, and I’ve talked about this in a previous episode. It’s the Curse of Complexity. Too many moving parts. Simplicity scales, complexity stalls. Simplicity scales, complexity stalls. Don’t add things to the system. That’s called Feature Creep. Don’t do that. Just follow the program. It’s worked for over 40 years – follow the program.

2. A lack of ownership is number two.
Everyone agrees with the vision. No one owns the process, but you’ve got to own the process. You have to own the behavior that needs to be done to achieve the vision.

3. No cadence.
Execution requires rhythm. Weekly, monthly, quarterly. Without rhythm, even the best strategies dissolve.

4. No accountability.
Vision inspires, but accountability transforms. Accountability transforms. You don’t rise to the level of your intentions; you fall to the level of your systems. You fall to the level of your execution.

So Vision + Systems + Culture is what this is all about. If you really want results, you need three things. You need a clear vision. That’s important. You need to know where you’re going. You need defined systems. And in BNI, we have defined systems. Don’t make up new ones; we have them. So how do you get to that vision? Step by step, that should be your focus in a chapter. And you need a strong culture. You need to hold yourselves and each other accountable for following the culture and the system of the program and moving to the vision that your chapter has built.

In BNI, we built systems around the VCP Process® – Visibility, Credibility, Profitability. Structured weekly meetings. The PALMS reporting. Leadership Team roles. These things aren’t accidental. These are intentional systems of execution, because big results require repeatable behaviors. You know, I’m sure you’ve heard me say before if you’ve listened to my podcast, success is about doing six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times. And so it’s that repeatable behavior. Execution multiplies legacy.

Now let me take it one step further. Anyone can have a vision. Very few people build something that lasts. BNI has been around for over 40 years. Legacy is… and by the way, not only around for over 40 years, we have grown every year, year on year on year for 41 years. And sometimes I hear people, “Oh, I don’t like BNI is now doing this, or BNI is now doing that.” Oftentimes, by the way, when people say, “BNI is now doing that,” it’s because the chapter is actually getting back to basics and doing the things they’re supposed to be doing. And I had somebody say, “Well, BNI has just changed so much. You know, it’s not as successful.” Well, maybe the chapter isn’t, but when you have an organization that has 41 years of consecutive growth, year on year on year, I don’t know of any organization other than BNI that has that.

And so legacy is not built just by ideas. It’s built by disciplined implementation over time. The chapter doesn’t become strong because it has a good launch meeting. It becomes strong because for years, sometimes decades, members consistently… and yes, decades, we have members who have been in BNI for decades. We have some of our original members from the Founding Chapter who are still in BNI, and from chapters that started in the first year that are still in BNI after 40 years. Members consistently… If you want to be successful, invite visitors. Do one-to-ones; educate each other. Expect that members do Member Success Program training; follow the system and the structure. That’s execution. And execution is a force multiplier for success.

It wasn’t the idea of BNI that scaled BNI. It was disciplined execution of the process we developed. The vision gave direction. The systems created results. You can have the clearest GPS in the world, but if you don’t put the car in drive, you’re just admiring the destination that you’re hoping for. Execution is rarely dramatic. It’s consistent. It’s doing the fundamentals when no one is applauding. In BNI, we talk about the one-to-ones, inviting visitors, following the agenda, educating members, tracking Thank You For Closed Business. None of that sounds glamorous. But one of our Core Values in BNI is Positive Attitude. Why? Because culture determines whether execution thrives. Vision inspires. Systems organize. Culture sustains. Culture, when applied consistently and appropriately, sustains.

So here’s my challenge for you as members and Directors. This week, ask yourself:

  • What is my vision for my chapter or my region or my business?
  • What specific behaviors support that vision?
  • Where is my execution inconsistent?
  • What system, existing system, particularly in BNI, what existing system could I implement this week to close the gap?

Don’t adjust your vision downward, raise your execution upward. So dream boldly. Execute relentlessly. Because extraordinary things don’t happen by intention alone. They happen by implementation. And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Very, good. Yes, well, that’s great.

Ivan: Thank you.

Priscilla:
I hope that people get to hear this and follow it.

Ivan:
That’s the key. Good ideas, but you got to follow them. It’s the behavior that counts.

Priscilla:
Exactly. Okay, great. Is there anything else you want to share?

Ivan:
No, that’s it. I But I do invite people to ask themselves those four questions, and please, please, please, just follow the system. Don’t continue to try to reinvent the wheel, and don’t let chapters reinvent the wheel. Focus on the system as it is and just improve on implementation of the existing system. That’s it for today.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Execution turns vision into lasting results. You need a clear vision, defined systems of execution, and a culture of accountability.

title.rendered: Episode 954: Protect Your Network

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When Kameron Thorne left BNI after 5 years, his business was booming. A year later, he’d lost 48% of his business. That experience taught him how important it is to protect your network. Now a regional director, Kameron breaks that process down on today’s podcast.

Find Your Network

You don’t just stumble into a great network. You have to intentionally seek out professionals who share your values and your drive to succeed. A great network multiplies your reach, builds your credibility, and creates a consistent flow of business.

Respect Your Network

If you ask your chapter what respect looks like to them, they’ll tell you “Show up on time, keep your word, and follow through on referrals.” Treat your network the same way you would treat your best client.

Stay with Your Network

Never leave your network. Your network is the one constant through seasons of growth and seasons of struggle. It’s your safety net and your cheering section.

How do you protect your network? Share your responses in the comments.

Links

  • Visit Kameron’s region, BNI Utah North.
  • Listen to Episode 54: Responsibility and Referrals.
  • Listen to Episode 603: Too Busy for BNI.
  • Listen to Episode 622: Don’t Be This Guy.

Complete Transcript of Episode 954

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin, Texas, this week. I’m meeting with a good friend and past CEO, Executive Chair of BNI, Graham Weihmiller. Graham and I are working on a book called Garage to Global, and he’s going to be visiting me here in the next couple of days.

Priscilla:
That’s great. And what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
Well, I have an individual who’s been a Director for a long time, Kameron Thorne. He’s been a part of BNI for 20 years. I’ve known him all of those 20 years. He’s a national trainer and member of the BNI Franchise Advisory Board. He lives in South Jordan, Utah [USA] for the last 23 years, married and has four sons, and it’s his very first time on the BNI Podcast. And Kameron, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Kameron:
Thank you. I’ve listened to almost every podcast. And I always thought, “How do I get on here?” Now I’m here.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan: 
Here you are, and I think you’ve got a really good topic, and that’s to Protect Your Network. So there are a few things… there are three talking points that you’ve got related to this, and the first is, you find your network. So talk to us a little bit about that.

Kameron:
Yeah, you think about finding your network. It’s–everybody wants success, but success really, it doesn’t happen in isolation. You know, any of us that belong to any sort of a network made a choice at some point in our lives to find a community where our relationships and referrals are very much fueled on growth in our own business. And so when you talk about finding your network, you got to be intentional about it. You don’t just stumble into a great network. It doesn’t just happen. You have to intentionally seek it out. You know, align ourselves with professionals that may share the same values, your goals, you know, and your drive to succeed.

So you know, when you find your network, it does a couple of things for you. First of all, it multiplies your reach. We can only reach so many people on our own. You know, a network gives you access to hundreds, and if you’re really good at it, possibly even thousands of relationships through simple introductions.

The second thing it does is it builds credibility faster. You know, when you’re recommended by someone in your own network, you’re literally borrowing their trust. That shortens the time it takes for others to feel confident in doing business with you.

And lastly, it creates consistent business flow. That strong network isn’t just occasional opportunities, but it becomes a reliable source of referrals and helps you grow that over time. And so that’s the first portion of just finding your network.

Ivan:
I think everybody who is in BNI absolutely recognizes what you’re talking about as being so incredibly important. I think you have a real example of that, don’t you?

Kameron:
Absolutely. I mean, for my own self… Just about every BNI member that’s ever been out there has been introduced by somebody else out in BNI. For my own self – my business coach, when I moved, when I quit my years of working in the resort business, I started my own business, she said, “Kameron, you have to join a BNI [chapter].” 

I had no idea what a BNI was. I looked up on the internet – said B.M.I, and I was like, body mass index. I don’t think this is the right place. I honestly don’t. But, she made me join, and I am so glad she did. Her name, Cheryl, out in Indianapolis, a long-time member. And she says, “You have to do this. You have to do this”. And I showed up and the pink pieces of paper flying around. I’m sitting next to an accountant. I go, “What are these pink pieces of paper?” It’s like, “Man, this is money.” That’s all he said.

Ivan: (laughing)

Kameron:
He was on the spot.

Ivan: (chuckling)
I know, for those of you who are just reasonably new to BNI, what we’re talking about are the [paper] referral slips that we used to use much more than we do today because of BNI Connect. But go ahead. I didn’t mean to interrupt you.

Kameron: 
No, no worries. And so once you find your network, the second part about protecting your network, is you got to respect your network. That’s the second portion of this I wanted to just hit on real quick. Finding the network is simply only the beginning. Respecting it is what makes it last. And I always ask the BNI members around me, I said, “What does respect look like for you?” And the things that they say are like, “You got to show up on time.” It’s like, yeah we always show up on time. “You’ve got to keep your word.” That’s just a given. The next one is “follow through on the referrals.” And I’m always surprised by BNI members that receive referrals and don’t even follow up on them.

Ivan:
Isn’t it crazy that that happens? It happened to me just recently, by the way, I gave somebody a referral. I said, “Give me call.” He didn’t call me, and I had to call him. I was like, “Hey, man, you know this is a
on the referral scale.”

Kameron:
Exactly. You follow up on those referrals, and then you protect the reputation of those who trust you enough that they recommended you. And then you got to treat it like it’s your very best client. I always ask BNI members, I present to two or three chapters a week, and I say, “Explain your best client. What does it mean? How do you respect your best client? Would  you leave in the middle of the meeting? Would you take a phone call? Would you be on the internet? Would you be late?”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Kameron:
All these types of things. And they’re like, “Absolutely not. I treat them with the most respect.” If you’re with this BNI long enough, this will be your best client, it will be your best client at some point. Treat it that way.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. Any story to represent that, or are you ready to move on?

Kameron: 
When I’m in the room [with the BNI chapter], I say, “When someone in this room passes you a referral, it’s more than a name, it’s a transfer of trust. If you mishandle that, you’re not just letting down one person, you’re letting down your entire network.”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Kameron:
Treat that referral with excellence, you elevate not only yourself, but also the person who referred you, and so you got to keep that best client happy. So first part is find your network. Second, respect your network.

Ivan:
Yeah. Alright. So the third one is never leave your network. Talk about that.

Kameron:
Are you kidding me? If you spent all that time getting your network. Why would you ever leave your network – the businesses? It’s simply a journey, and it’s not always an easy journey. There are seasons of growth and seasons of struggle, but the one constant is always going to be your network. There’s so many reasons why you wouldn’t want to leave your network. You know, we’ve come through… I was in BNI during 2008, during the market crash. I was there during the covid. It’s your safety net during these challenges. You know, we watch people shift in what they’re doing during these times. If you’re the chiropractor, I can’t help you, because you’re not supposed to touch people during covid. But I got other work for you. Are you willing to do that? So it’s that safety net helps people during those challenges. It’s also your cheering section. When you succeed, you’ve got people behind you, and then, because trust – once it’s built, it compounds over time.

I teach people that your network is financial planning. It is. The longer you are with that, the better it gets. Your first year in financial planning, you’re looking at your portfolio, and, like, I put in $10,000 why is there only $6000 inside of there? Well, everyone had to get paid. But year two and three and four it compounds, and it’s the same in BNI. Your first year, usually the least profitable, but year three and four… if it was up to me, I would only sell five-year memberships.

Ivan: (laughing)

Kameron:
Because it really takes… it was my third year that it finally exploded for me. I did okay my first year, second year, I did five times better than my first year. But my third year, that’s when things exploded for me and in the business that I was in, it finally, you know, after my third year, I never did less than $200,000 [a year] in Thank You For Closed Business in my business after that third year.

Ivan:
It takes time to build trust, and I think that’s where people don’t understand it. We live in this, you know, quick society. Everything is short and fast, and what they don’t understand is that trust takes time. It doesn’t happen quickly, and the longer you stay with a group, the more likely they are to be there when times are tough. During covid, that’s when I came up with the phrase Your BNI chapter is a beacon of hope in a sea of fear, because we were living in crazy times. Yet, I saw people make it through covid successfully because of their fellow members.

Kameron:
It’s crazy. I mean, why you would ever leave is just beyond me. Never, never leave. So if we bring it all back together, find your network. Be intentional about who you surround yourself with. Respect that network – show up, follow through, treat it like it’s your best client. And then, you never leave your network because it’s more than business. It’s community. I teach people network like your business life depends on it, because it truly does. BNI is more than just a group, it’s a foundation for success. You know that.

Ivan:
I do. I’ve been doing it 41 years, and I see it over and over and over again, that when people give it time and they build the relationships, they do really, really well. And when they treat it like hunting versus farming, they just blow through the group and they think, hey, I built relationships with these 30 people. I’m done now. I’m gonna find another 30 people, and that’s just not the way to do it.

Kameron:
Tell you a quick story. I left [BNI] my fifth year thinking I had it made. And I created a golf group to go play golf, and thought those referrals would still come to me. And I didn’t tell my wife, I didn’t tell my business coach, I didn’t tell anyone. And we’re looking at my bottom line in year five, I lost like 48% of my business. And my business coach, Jennifer, she cusses like a sailor, and she sure did when she found out. She goes, “What’s been going on?” I said, “I left BNI,” and she screamed at me. Her name is Jen, and my wife’s named Jen. I’ve had two Jens yelling at me the exact same time on the phone. I immediately got back in; it wasn’t the same chapter, and it immediately just took off again for me.

Ivan:
Well, we’re really glad that you got back in. Give us your website in Utah for BNI.

Kameron:
Our website is BNIUtahnorth.com

Ivan:
BNIUtahnorth.com. Listen Kameron, it was great having you on the BNI Podcast today. I really appreciate it. And with that, let me hand it over to Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. It was really fun.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: A year after leaving BNI, Kameron Thorne lost 48% of his business. Today he shares what he learned about protecting your network.

title.rendered: Episode 953: Growth in Difficult Times (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: this is a rebroadcast of Episode 725, first released on September 22, 2021. Some links and references may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

This week on the podcast, Ivan Misner shares the keys to business growth in difficult times.

  1. You must believe you can pivot your business and that your network can help you find ways to help more people in your profession.
  2. You have to have passion about what you do and how you’ll achieve it–including participation in BNI. Together, commitment and passion produce results.
  3. You need leadership based on mobilizing and inspiring rather than managing and complying. It’s about showing up to help people be a better version of themselves.

Commitment and Passion produce Results. C + P = R.

THAT is what we can be doing right now. And that is how we can grow a business during difficult times. Set a vision that makes a difference to the people around you. Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option?

Complete Transcript of Episode 725/953

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I’ve been all about, Zooming about the world. I’ve done some live radio interviews: Pittsburgh, St. Augustine, I’m doing a lot of global interviews. I did some stuff on Clubhouse, the app. I took a little time off in Galveston and I did a live BNI China event.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
And that was a lot of fun. So I’ve been Zooming around.

Priscilla:
It sounds great. So what are you sharing with us today?

Ivan: 
You know, I was asked to talk to some BNI Directors about growth, because it’s so interesting how some directors are just on fire, and their regions are on fire and their chapters are on fire. And they’re really doing well. And other regions and BNI directors are like, “Whoa, how can you possibly grow during these difficult times?”

Why is it that some people see the opportunity, where other people don’t? You need BNI today, more than EVER. And the last 18 months have been, I think, a great example of understanding that, because it’s been a challenge for most businesses. The truth is, it’s your network of friends and associates that can help you get through times like this. You know, operating more than 10,400 networking groups now in over 70 countries, I’ve learned this firsthand.

I’ve also learned something else. It starts with YOU. It starts with what you believe you can do. First, you must have belief – you must believe you can pivot your business and find ways to help more people in your profession, in your industry. You must believe in what you can do for people in this new normal that we live in.

There’s this old story of two shoe salesmen who were sent to different parts of a developing nation to see if there was a market for their shoes. And after a week, the first salesman wrote back to the company and said, no one wear shoes here. There’s no market for us, send me a return ticket. The second shoe salesman wrote back to the company and said, no one wear shoes here. There’s a HUGE market for our shoes. Send me a large shipment.

It’s all perspective. You have to believe. You have to believe in yourself, you have to believe that you can. You have to believe that you can pivot. You have to believe that your network can help you in this process. I recently had a BNI Director who said “You don’t know, you don’t understand, Ivan, it’s impossible to grow right now.” No, that’s not true. If you believe you can or believe you can’t, you’re going to be right. And if you think it’s impossible, it’ll be impossible.

In BNI, we have over 280,000 people in the organization. And I see people in the exact same professions who believe it’s possible to pivot, and people in the exact same profession who believe that it’s not possible to pivot, during these challenging times. And it may come as a surprise to some, but it doesn’t come as a surprise to me, that many of the people who believe they can pivot are doing so successfully. And ALL of the people who believe they cannot pivot are not doing it successfully. If you think you can or you think you can’t, you’re going to be right. Pivoting during difficult times begins with the belief that you can.

Ivan:
Next you have to have passion, passion about what you do and how you’ll achieve it in the current environment. And that includes your participation in BNI, you’ve got to be passionate about it. But let me be clear about passion. Passion does not produce Commitment.

Commitment produces Passion.

Commitment and Passion produce Results.

It is the CPR for any business.

Commitment and Passion produce Results. C + P = R.

Nothing great in life has ever been done without passion and commitment. And that CPR acronym came from one of our Directors, Glenn (Newlin), who suggested it because I was talking to them about commitment and passion, and that’s what it takes to get results.

The third key is leadership. And leadership is not about managing and complying. It is about mobilizing and inspiring. I want to repeat that. Leadership is not about managing and complying. It’s about mobilizing and inspiring. Over the years, I’ve learned that it is true that people don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care.

It’s about the touchpoints that you have with the people you manage, that you work with, and that you serve. In a BNI context, it’s about staying connected with your fellow Members, connecting with visitors in the organization. In your business it is about connecting with your clientele on a regular basis. It’s all about those touch points and connecting with people. It’s about giving members and clients and customers – it’s about giving them love, care and attention.

Ivan:

It’s about doing six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times. A good friend of mine, the late Chick Gallagher liked to say, he was a BNI Director. And he liked to say “It’s not about doing something so many times that you finally get it right. It’s about doing it so many times that you can’t get it wrong.” But you have to be doing the right things. And the right things involve inspiring people to take action. It’s about those touch points.

In BNI that involves learning how to coach people effectively, and the Leadership Teams are so incredibly important. And of course, the Directors and Director Consultants are important. But even, you know, Members, the Visitor Host, the Education Coordinator, can help guide people and help people day in and day out. It’s about showing up to help people be a better version of themselves. THAT is what we can be doing right now. And that is how we can grow a business during difficult times.

Don’t. Over. Complicate. Things.

I don’t know why people make things so complicated. And I’m not just talking about BNI, I’m talking about people in general. We as a human race just tend to make things complicated or we tend to reinvent the wheel, which also drives me nuts.

But BNI doesn’t have to be complicated. You know, when I started BNI back in 1985, I had one sheet of paper. That’s all I had. I think I’ve talked about this in a podcast before, it’s all I had – one sheet of paper. It was the agenda typed up by me on an IBM Selectric typewriter. And if you’re a millennial, you have no idea what I’m talking about today. It’s just basically an anchor, a big heavy typewriter. And back then I typed “Columbus style”, you know, seek-out-and-discover. And I typed up the agenda and people would ask me, do you have any material? When I was trying to start a chapter, they’d ask, do you any material? I said, No. Do you have any marketing material? No. Well surely you have a brochure. No. What do you have? I’ve got the agenda. And they would look at the agenda. And they said, Well, this doesn’t really tell me a lot. And I’d say no, you’ve got to come to a meeting. You have to come to a meeting and experience it.

And Priscilla, I opened 20 chapters in one year, in one year. And all I had was one sheet of paper. And so when people say to me, oh, you can’t do it, I’m like, really? Seriously, look at all this stuff we’ve got, look how easy we make it for people.

It’s about the belief that you have, it’s about the passion that you have. The commitment is what creates that passion. And it’s about your leadership. In my career, I have seen ordinary people do extraordinary things. I believe in you. I believe in the members. I believe that you can do extraordinary things. Our vision controls our perception, and our perception becomes our reality. Set a vision that makes a difference to the people around you. Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option? Even in difficult times, excellence is an option. I see it all around the world. And that’s my message this week. Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s a great message. I just want to add one thing to it, which is that when we’re operating in our chapters, and we’re inviting guests, the guest and not only the guests, but sometimes the Members, don’t even realize what a part they are of a huge network. You’ve created this incredible business building network all over the world. And when you’re in your little chapter, you kind of forget all that. But it’s really an incredible giant clock of working parts, you know, that’s worldwide. It’s really amazing, Ivan, what you’ve created from one piece of paper.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. And obviously, it was many of us that did it. You know, there are literally, I think now, 4000 or 5000 people who work for the company worldwide. And we have well over 280,000 members worldwide. And so many, many people have contributed to this worldwide phenomenon that BNI has become. But I appreciate your kind words, and I can’t wait to see what BNI continues to become.

Because we’re going to get out of these difficult times. And BNI has grown. We’ve added 600 to 700 chapters during all of this chaotic last year and a half. And we’ve added 600 to 700 chapters. We’ve had 36 years of consecutive growth, very few companies in the world have ever done that. It looks like we’ll have a 37th year of consecutive growth. And I think the reason for that is: Today more than ever you need your network, and BNI is a great way to do it. And you’re right. It’s global. And those people who listen to the podcast, I think, have a better idea of that. Anything else Priscilla?

Priscilla:
That is it, Ivan.

Ivan:
Well, then let me hand it over to you. Thank you so much.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. That’s great information. Well, I’d just like to share that this podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. And that these audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds are going to the BNI foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
