excerpt.rendered: Belief, passion, and leadership are the keys to business growth in difficult times. Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option?

title.rendered: Episode 952: Positive Attitude Is a Competitive Advantage

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Positive attitude is one of the top two characteristics of great networkers. It builds trust faster than credentials. Attitude is a choice, not a personality trait. A positive attitude isn’t optimism. It’s ownership of how you show up in the world.

And in BNI, that kind of ownership matters. You don’t control whether someone passes you a referral this week, but you do control how you show up. You do control how you speak about your chapter and your fellow members. You do control how you react when things don’t go your way.

Positive attitude is realism with hope attached, and it’s a chapter multiplier. It amplifies everything else. Because when attitude is right, everything else has a better chance of going right too.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 656: Hope Is More Powerful Than Fear.
  • Listen to Episode 688: How to Stay Positive When Life Throws You a Curve Ball.
  • Listen to Episode 711: Choose the Lens You See the World Through.

Complete Transcript of Episode 952

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still in Austin, and I am so happy to not have to have gotten on a plane for a little while. So I mean, as of this date, I have 2.6 million miles on American Airlines alone.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan: (chuckling)
I know. So, it’s really nice to just not have to get on an airplane for a little while.

Priscilla:
Yeah, no kidding. Well, tell us what this particular podcast is about.

Ivan:
Well, it’s about positive attitude. So let me start with a question to the BNI members, to the listeners, “When you walk into a BNI meeting, how quickly do you feel the room?” Not what’s on the agenda, not how many visitors are there. Just the energy. Because whether we admit it or not, attitude walks into the room before introductions do.

Years ago, I ran a survey of over 3000 business professionals asking a simple question: What are the characteristics of a great networker? And positive attitude ranked in the top two, right alongside with a good listener. Now that’s not accidental, that’s not soft, that’s not strategic, and that’s why positive attitude isn’t just a nice idea in BNI, it’s actually one of our Core Values. Positive attitude is contagious, for better or worse, and here’s the truth, attitude spreads faster than referrals. One consistently positive member can lift a chapter. One consistently negative member can drain it. That’s why I talk about in my book, Who’s In Your Room? –surround yourself with engines, not anchors, not people that are dragging you down.

I’ve seen chapters with less members out-perform stronger chapters, larger chapters, simply because their culture was healthier. Why? Because people don’t just join BNI for referrals. They join and they stay for how it feels to belong. They stay for the relationships, particularly the positive ones I’ll never forget. And I think I may have said this in a podcast, and we’re up to almost 1000 podcasts, so I don’t remember all the stories, but there was one chapter I went to, and this member was talking about how they have a member in their chapter, and she said, “This guy is a fun sucker. He just sucks the fun right out of the room.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
And she said, “Our best meetings are when he calls in sick. We have great meetings when he’s not there.” Wow, that’s horrible, but that’s accurate. I mean, you know, attitudes make all the difference in the world. Priscilla, you ever worked anywhere where somebody got let go or quit and, like almost all the employees go, “Oh, thank goodness that person is gone.”

Priscilla:
Oh yeah, definitely.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, I’ve seen it in employment. I’ve seen it in BNI chapters. Positive attitude creates psychological safety. It creates trust. It creates a willingness to collaborate. It creates an energy to refer proactively. Negative attitude creates defensive listening, withheld referrals, short-term thinking, exit interviews. Attitude is a choice. It’s not a personality trait. This is where I want to bring in a quote that I’ve loved for years and years, and I gave this to my kids, and I’m happy to say, one of them at least put it up on a wall in their house, and I’m so proud of her for having done it. It’s the Charles Swindoll quote. I’m sure you’ve probably heard it Priscilla, but I’m going to read this quote. It’s kind of long. It’s about attitude.

And Swindoll says, “The longer I live, the more I realize the impact of attitude on life. Attitude, to me, is more than facts. It’s more important than the past, than education, than circumstances, than failures, than successes, than what other people think or say or do. It’s more important than appearance, than giftedness or skill. It will make or break a company, community or home. The remarkable thing is we have a choice every day regarding the attitude we will embrace for that day. We cannot change our past. We cannot change the fact that people will act in a certain way. We cannot change the inevitable. The only thing we can do is play on the one string we have. And this is our attitude.” He said, “I’m convinced that life is 10% what happens to me, and 90% how I react to it. And so it is with you. We are in charge of our attitudes.” I read this years ago, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s a great, wonderful piece of writing.

Ivan:
It’s fantastic, and it’s so true. It is so true. You know, when I was… I hadn’t planned on mentioning this… but when I was diagnosed with cancer, I was horrified. I was shocked. I was surprised. And I had a long drive after I got the diagnosis, almost a two-hour drive to get down to my family, who were staying in San Diego, and I was driving down to them [in stop-and-go traffic], and I thought, okay, okay, all right. This is horrible. What possible positive outcomes could I get from this cancer diagnosis? And I wrote a list of the possible positive outcomes that I could have from a cancer diagnosis, and I can tell you, and I may have done a podcast on this, I think I came up with seven or eight things, and Priscilla all of them, 100%, came true.

Priscilla:
Wow. Do you remember any of them?

Ivan:
Yeah, I do. One was, I was going through a lot of… When BNI Connect was being developed, and, you know, people wanted to draw and quarter me. They were so mad at me. They were just furious with me, and I thought, “Well, they’ll cut me some slack at least. He’s got cancer; we’ll leave him alone.” And it was true. It absolutely happened, people kind of backed off for their anger as BNI Connect was being developed. Another one was, “Hey, I’ll lose that weight that I’ve been gaining over the years,” which I also did. (chuckling) And one of the things was — it will draw me closer to my wife, to Elisabeth, and it did that as well. And so every single one of the things that I wrote down in my car as I was driving down in the parking lot, you know, called the California freeways, every one of them came true, and this was on my mind. Life is 10% what happens to me, and 90% how I react to it. And so I wanted to react to it in a way that had a potential positive spin.

And to me, this isn’t optimism. It’s ownership of how one shows up in the world. And in BNI, that kind of ownership matters. You don’t control the weather, you don’t control the economy, you don’t control whether your visitor shows up or not. You don’t control whether someone passes you a referral this week. But you do control how you show up. You do control how you speak about your chapter and your fellow members. You do control how you react when things don’t go your way.

If you’re listening to this podcast, if you think for one second that things have gone my way most of the time, you’re delusional. I have had many things not go my way, and it’s how I reacted to them that made a difference, not the fact that they didn’t go my way. So positive attitude builds trust faster than credentials, in my opinion.

And here’s something that people rarely talk about – before someone trusts your competence, they assess your emotional reliability. Ask yourself, do people feel better after talking to me, or do they feel heavier? That’s really an important question. Do they feel better when they talk to you? In networking trust is emotional before it’s logical. That’s why, in the survey that we did, and I’ll have the survey posted in this podcast, the survey that we did, positive attitude ranked higher than trustworthiness. Can you believe that? It ranked higher than trustworthiness in networking. It ranked higher than follow up, it ranked higher than expertise; not because those aren’t important, but because attitude amplifies everything else. Attitude amplifies everything else.

Positive Attitude fuels Givers Gain®. Let’s connect to this kind of Givers Gain. Giving is not just about actions. It’s about intent. People give more referrals to people who appreciate them, and who celebrate others; who stay constructive during challenges. Positive attitude signals “I’m safe to invest in,” and referrals are investments. What positive attitude is, and is not, is this. I mean, let’s be clear, positive attitude does not mean pretending problems don’t exist. It doesn’t mean avoiding hard conversations or smiling through dysfunction. Positive attitude means focusing on solutions, not blame. Asking, how do we fix this? instead of who caused this? It’s about believing progress is possible. It’s realism with hope attached. I love this phrase. It’s realism with hope attached. I need to turn that into an Ivanism for the record.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Okay, so Dana, who does all of my… she does all the transcripts. Dana, turn this into an Ivanism: Realism is hope. Positive attitude is realism with hope attached.

It’s the chapter multiplier effect. So here’s a final thought. Every BNI chapter has a tone setter. Sometimes it’s the President, sometimes it’s a veteran member, sometimes it’s the person who speaks right after the Education Coordinator — that person’s attitude multiplies. So ask yourself, “What am I multiplying in my chapter?”

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
Energy or erosion?
Momentum or maintenance?
Positive attitude isn’t about being cheerful. It’s about constructive, resilient and intentional. It’s why the data supports it. It’s why it’s a Core Value in BNI, and it’s why the strongest chapters don’t just track referrals. They protect the culture. Because when attitude is right, everything else has a better chance of going right too. That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I love that. Thank you so much, Ivan. That was great.

Ivan:
So, I went on a different rant this week than last week.

Priscilla:
Well, it’s all useful. It really is.

Ivan:
Good.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much for that great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Positive attitude is one of the top two characteristics of great networkers. Realism with hope attached builds trust faster than credentials.

title.rendered: Episode 951: The Curse of Complexity

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Feature creep results in the curse of complexity: what starts as improvement turns into friction, and what starts as innovation turns into inertia. Organizations add layers and processes and rules and features and tools and things, believing that each addition will improve performance. Individually, each edition might make sense, but collectively, they slow everything down.

Complexity doesn’t scale. Customers don’t want more options. They want better ones. Employees don’t want more tools. They want clearer ones. Leaders don’t need new ideas. They need the existing strong ones to be implemented well. Complexity increases noise, simplicity amplifies signal. Your BNI chapter and your business will thrive if you have the courage to do less, better.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 259: Why Make All the Mistakes?
  • Listen to Episode 551: Announcing One to Ones During Referrals
  • Listen to Episode 563: The Referral Part of the Meeting.
  • Listen to Episode 586: Achieving the Power of One.

Complete Transcript of Episode 951

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still in Austin. I mentioned last time that I’ll be here for a couple of months, which is great to not have to get on a plane for a little while, so I’m happy to be home.

Priscilla:
Great. And what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, I want to talk about the curse of complexity. And you know, it’s sometimes called feature creep. I’ve mentioned feature creep in previous episodes, and feature creep is where things just keep getting added to something until the program is no longer what it was once was, and it becomes too complex. I have come up with the term “The Curse of Complexity,” and we’re using that term in a book I’m working on right now called Garage to Global. And I want to talk about why it really, quietly undermines otherwise successful organizations, because when I’m doing this book, it’s about the success of scaling a company.

So why is complexity a curse? Complexity is a curse, and it occurs when organizations add layers and processes and rules and features and tools and things, believing that each addition will improve performance. Individually, each edition might make sense, but collectively, they slow everything down. That’s what feature creep is. So what starts as improvement really turns into friction, and what starts as innovation turns into inertia. And the dangerous part, it’s almost always done by smart, well-intentioned people.

Let me share something that has stayed with me for years. I’ve had the honor of visiting Sir Richard Branson on Necker Island several times. I’ve talked about it in a number of my podcasts, and during one of our conversations, he summed up a problem that I’ve seen repeatedly in business and in BNI. He said to me… it was over lunch. We were having lunch, and he said, “Any fool can make something complicated. It takes a smart businessperson to keep things simple. Simplicity is crucial for success.” I love that, and I couldn’t agree with it more. That line stuck with me, because it’s not just clever: it’s painfully true.

Why complexity is so seductive, and not just in BNI, but think in terms of your own organization. Why is it so seductive? Here’s why: Complexity feels productive. Adding a new feature feels like progress. Adding a new report feels like control. Adding a new rule feels like protection. But complexity rarely shows up as a crisis. It shows up as slower decisions, longer training, confusing customers, or for us, BNI members, frustrating teams and leaders saying, “Why is this harder than it used to be?” Complexity is actually expensive, but the bill arrives quietly to a company.

So why do smart people create complexity? Well, here’s something important to understand. Complexity is not created by incompetence. It really isn’t. It’s created by high performers trying to optimize something they have energy on. It’s created by specialists adding depth without seeing the whole picture. It’s added by leaders, rewarding addition instead of subtraction. Everyone adds, almost no one removes. It’s about adding something new. Oh, I got to make something new. And what’s really interesting is that sometimes the something new was done 20 years ago and failed, but they don’t know that because they didn’t ask anybody.

So over time, the organization becomes harder to run, not because people are less capable, but because the system is harder to navigate. Now this is true, not just in BNI. I’m talking about your business. If you’re listening to this, this applies to your business as well as to BNI. But for BNI, check out BNI Podcast episode 563 where I talk about how some chapters have added CEU announcements during the referral part of the meeting. It’s a complex discussion. Check out that podcast. It’s the Curse of Complexity 101.  I mean, it really is the curse of complexity. Listen to Episode 563 – it’s stuff added when it was never meant to be part of it. Also listen to [BNI Podcast] Episode 551 which similarly adds yet another thing to the meeting that was never part of the original process. And people with good intentions add it, and it becomes more and more complex. I’ll share some other examples a little later in the podcast.

But why does complexity not scale? Because this whole issue is a big part of my book, Garage to Global. Here’s the paradox of growth. What helps you grow early can hurt you later. Early-stage organizations thrive on flexibility. Scaled organizations, however, thrive on clarity and repeatability. Customers don’t want more options. They want better ones. Employees don’t want more tools. They want clearer ones. Leaders don’t need new ideas. They need the existing strong ones to be implemented well. Complexity increases noise, simplicity amplifies signal. There is a discipline in saying no. Entrepreneurs, myself included, can sometimes be like a squirrel on espresso. Ideas are everywhere. Energy is everywhere. But scale doesn’t come from saying yes to everything. It comes from the judicious use of the word “no.” No to bright shiny objects. No to unnecessary exceptions. No to adding complexity just because something is new, clever or exciting, or you just want to change stuff. Every yes has a cost. Every no preserves focus.

Here are a couple more real life BNI examples. This one blows my mind, Priscilla. This one blows my mind. I just this week, did a webinar for BNI Directors around the world. And it’s not just members who, by the way, are guilty of this, Directors do it. So I did webinar for BNI Directors around the world, and one of them asked me what I thought the one problem was, and has been, that is still an issue today after 41 years. And I said, “Absolutely, it’s feature creep and the curse of complexity.” And we continued the conversation. I explained a little bit of what I just explained here, and we continued the conversation for a while. And towards the end, he said words the effect of, “Oh, oh – by the way, we’ve changed the Power of One.” Now, if you don’t know what the Power of One is, go to [BNI Podcast] episode number 586 and you’ll see what the Power of One is. He said, “Oh, we’ve changed the Power of One to the Power of One Plus (or something similar to that).” And I almost went apoplectic. He asked me the one consistent problem, what it was. And I told him what it was. And later in the conversation, he happily and cluelessly said he added something to the Power of One. Priscilla, we’re gonna hear silence for a moment here, because I have to press the mute button to scream. Are you ready?

Priscilla:
I’m ready.

Ivan:
Okay.

Ivan:
Ahhh. Okay, okay, I feel better now. I feel a little bit better now.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
Here’s a situation that… it literally happened today. I’m going to give you another that one literally happened today. I received an email from a 40-year member of BNI who was working with a local Director to open a new group. So he’s working with the core group to kick off soon. And he was told by the local Director that in his region, they have the core groups meet every other week, not every week. Okay, so I’m pressing the mute button again.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
All right. I’m pressing the thing again so I can’t scream. So here we go.

Priscilla:(laughing) 
Okay.

Ivan:
I feel a little bit better.

Priscilla:
Oh, good.

Ivan:
Priscilla, even our Directors sometimes make things up. Bad word, bad word, bad word, Priscilla. That’s my favorite cursing phrase.

Now, simplicity is a mindset and a culture. Simplification is not a one-time clean up, it’s not a project. It’s a cultural barrier. Discipline. It requires regular pruning. Permission to follow the tried-and-true system, 41 years… 41 years of consecutive growth. How many companies can say they’ve had 41 years of growth, year in and year out every year, including during covid, including during recessions? We’ve grown every year.

Leaders who model restraint and not constant change are the ones that are focusing on the right things. Organizations that win long-term don’t just innovate, they execute the basics tenaciously. Simplicity takes courage. Here’s the uncomfortable truth, complexity is exciting. Simplicity is courageous. Steve Jobs once said, “Simple can be harder than complex. You have to work hard to get your thinking clean to make it simple.” Simplicity often means undergoing bad past changes to clean a system. It means letting go of things that just make things more complex. It means protecting what matters most.

I could give you a lot more examples of what I’ve seen added to make things complicated in BNI. But what I think is more important is to focus on what we do want as an organization, and not what we don’t want. We want to execute the fundamentals flawlessly. And after 41 years of consecutive growth, year after year — continued growth isn’t about clogging the system with untested ideas. It’s about taking the ideas that have worked for 41 years and doing them better than we’ve ever done before. The curse of complexity doesn’t destroy organizations overnight. It slowly suffocates them – one seemingly good idea at a time. The leaders who scale don’t do more. They do less — better. If you want speed, clarity and momentum, your competitive advantage may not be what you add next. It may be what you have the courage to apply better. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. That’s great. That’s great, Ivan, and your new book sounds really good.

Ivan:
This is a piece of it. And I hope people really take this podcast to heart. And the next time somebody says, “In our region, we want to do this,” I hope they refer them to this podcast.

Priscilla:
Yeah, well, I’ve had a lot of experience with that, because our BNI group wanted to be different. We were from Berkeley, and so we were… you know, making up things all along.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, the one thing that I’ve seen all across all cultures everywhere in the world is everyone says “We’re different.” And the one thing that I’ve seen across all cultures, everywhere around the world, is the chapters who are successful follow the system.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
Yeah. Don’t make stuff up.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Or should I say they don’t make bad word, bad word, bad word, things up?

Priscilla:(laughing)

Ivan:
Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, you’re welcome. Thank you, Ivan. Well, that’s it for this week. I hope you enjoyed it. That was some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: This is the curse of complexity: what starts as improvement turns into friction, and what starts as innovation turns into inertia.

title.rendered: Episode 950: The Growth Mindset

content.rendered:
Synopsis

To start growing your business with BNI, you just need to dedicate 15 minutes per day inviting visitors and in following up. John Rivers from BNI Portland Business Advantage explains how he went about personally sponsoring 51 new members into his chapter.

Growing a chapter from 25 members to more than 70 requires a growth mindset.

  1. Prepare your visitors by letting them know what to expect at the meeting.
  2. Have enough room and chairs for the visitors, have name tags, and prepare members to meet specific visitors in their contact sphere.
  3. Call visitors to follow up. Make sure they have the application and ask whether there’s anyone whose information they didn’t get.

Links

  • Visit John’s website, AO IT Consulting.
  • Listen to Episode 177: The Double Triple Effect.
  • Listen to Episode 419: The Law of Reciprocity.
  • Listen to Episode 560: I Hit HIDWAL.

Complete Transcript of Episode 950

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. Sometimes I feel like I’m in a “Young Frankenstein” movie, where at some point in the movie, the main character says, “It’s a miracle!” because I’m home for like, two months in a row, and it feels like a miracle. And I’m happy to be at my home office in Austin, Texas, for a couple of months.

Priscilla:
Oh, I’m glad for you. Well, what do you have to share with us today?

Ivan:
Well, I have a guest, and his name is John Rivers. John is celebrating his 20th year as a member of BNI with his company, AO IT Consulting. As a dedicated [BNI] Chapter Support Director, he is deeply passionate about helping chapters grow and thrive. And when John joined the [BNI] Portland Business Advantage, the chapter had just 25 members, and since then, he has personally sponsored – okay, sit down, everybody – 51 members…

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
…and has helped the chapter expand to more than 70 members at this time. Outside of his IT work, John and his wife, Anna, love traveling, particularly to BNI National and Global Conferences and Conventions, and visiting chapters around the world. John, welcome to BNI Podcast.

John:
Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
It’s good to have you. Our topic today is The Growth Mindset, and so I got a couple of questions, and I’m just going to let you run with it, because I think your content is really solid. How do you create a culture of inviting people for growth in a Chapter?

John:
Yeah. So I’ve been in BNI for 20 years, like you said, and I’ve seen chapters that get stuck at the same size, roughly at the same number of members and roughly the same amount of Thank You For Closed Business. But no matter how hard they try, they stay at that same size. But I also found that about 80% of people don’t invite visitors.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, I call those people: they’re happy in their hole and they don’t want a ladder.

John: (chuckling)
Yes, and so in BNI Podcast #177, The Double Triple Effect you talk about, if you double the chapter size, you triple the number of referrals.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
And when I joined Portland Business Advantage [Chapter], we had about 25 members. We’d go up a couple and down a couple, and we always stayed roughly around $500,000 [TYFCB] for a couple of years. And I knew that I couldn’t ask anyone else to do something I wasn’t going to do myself.

So I learned an inviting method from Dr. Ben Reebs, and it’s really simple. You spend 15 minutes per day, and you put it on your calendar, and you hold it sacred, and you invite and follow up for 15 minutes per day. And the last seven years, we grew from 25 members, and now we’re over 70 members with over $6 million a year in Thank You For Closed Business.

Ivan:
Okay, so to heck with my triple effect, you have gone way over that. And so I try to be conservative in the numbers that I give. But what I love about comments like yours is that I tend to give the low end, and what you’ve just described as the high end of what can happen when you when you double or triple your chapter.

John:
Yeah, and now we have many people in the chapter that regularly bring visitors, and that’s how I think we’ve seen a culture change and the growth mindset of the people that are with us now want to continue to see the chapter grow, because the numbers reflect more than just that double triple effect, like you were saying.

Ivan:
Right. And so before people understand that, they kind of wonder, you know, what’s in it? You want to talk about that?

John:
Yeah. So whenever somebody says to me, “How do I get that same effect in my chapter?” I ask them, “What are you looking for from BNI? Is it to get more money? Is it more connections and more referral partners?” And so in order to start growing your business, you just need to dedicate 15 minutes per day in inviting and in following up. And for the [Chapter] Leadership teams out there, if you see someone doing this, recognize them.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
That is [Recognition] is one of our Core Values, and we want… whatever gets recognized gets repeated.

Ivan:
Yeah, good point. Love that.

John:
So I went to an aquarium locally that had an 18 inch shark, and I said, “That’s the smallest shark I’ve ever seen.” And the employee that was working there said, “That shark is actually full grown in this aquarium, but if we were to release it into the ocean, it would grow to six to eight feet long.” And I feel like so many [BNI] chapters are stuck because they’re in a small aquarium.

My chapter was stuck at 50 members for a couple of years, and the room that we were in was beautiful, but it was cramped. We just didn’t have enough chairs for members, or we had enough chairs for members, but not enough for visitors. We had people standing against the back wall, maybe a chair in a corner. And we switched to a larger venue. It had more room, more chairs, and we almost instantly grew 10 new members.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
So I tell people to be ready for that growth, to have that growth mindset first. Let’s be prepared. Let’s print name badges ahead of time for our visitors.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
Introduce the visitors and say, “You’re not only going to meet me, but let me introduce you to one of my colleagues in the chapter that’s going to be a great referral partner for you.” Run a great meeting. Have enough room. And not just have enough room for your members but have enough room that the visitors feel like there’s room for them to join and still have room to grow.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s really an issue of intention and attraction, isn’t it? I mean, if you have an intention to grow, you need to have a room big enough to grow, and then you have the focus on the attracting visitors, which is your 15 minutes a day.

John:
Exactly. So on Zoom, we plan ahead of time, we look at the Visitor Registration list, and we put our visitors into a breakout room with two members in their contact sphere. Now this also requires our members to show up early, so they’re there for the breakout rooms. But then at the end of the meeting, the visitors say, “What really started this meeting off so great is I got to meet two of my ideal referral partners right away when I got here.”

Ivan:
Now wait a minute now, because you’re talking about Zoom, are you doing online meetings or in person, or a hybrid?

John:
So my [BNI] chapter is hybrid.

Ivan:
Okay, got it. So it’s in person, in part and online in part. By the way, I love hybrid, the concept of hybrid chapters. I’m a supporter.

John:
Yeah, my chapter actually has a member in Boise, Idaho. Our chapter base is in Portland, Oregon. We have people at the at the beach. People drive three to six hours for our meeting, and that’s we have five or six people that drive over an hour to get to the meeting. So hybrid is amazing.

So in person, we do the dance, which this is where a visitor comes up to the visitor registration table. They get their name badge that was pre-printed for them. They get to go set their stuff down, maybe get a cup of coffee. The Visitor Host will then walk them up and introduce them to the President for 60 seconds, then move them down to the Vice President, then the Secretary Treasurer, and then to people in their contact sphere. So 15 minutes of Open Networking, that visitor meets 15 people, which after the meeting, they get to go meet those members that they want to follow up with, and possibly do business with.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
And then the fortune is in the follow up. We’ve heard that all over, but I teach my process for how to invite and follow up, and it’s easy and repeatable, and you just put it on your calendar for 15 minutes per day. Now, I taught one person that he wasn’t getting the same results as I was, and I found out he wasn’t following up.

Ivan:
Mm hmm. Yeah, that’s key, and I want to get to that. But let’s just review what you just said. Have enough room and chairs for the visitors, have name tags, prepare members to meet specific visitors. I love that you introduce them to the Leadership Team. I think that’s great. All right, so engaging with visitors as a follow up. Talk about that.

John:
Yeah. So here’s my follow up: before the meeting, I send an email [to the visitor] what to expect at our meeting. I tell them the time, the location, a few details about the meeting, and I say, “I will meet you at the door,” and I make sure that I’m there early, even though I’m not on the Visitor Host Team or on the executive Leadership Team. And in person, I help with the dance. And on Zoom, I help greet the visitors in breakout rooms.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
So, but the magic sauce behind this whole process is after the meeting, I pick up the phone and I call visitors.

Ivan:
Oh hear, hear.

John:
And I actually ask them three questions, open ended questions. Number one, How was the meeting? Number two, Did you get the application? Do you have any questions on that application? And number three, Is there somebody in the meeting that you didn’t get the information for, but you would like to follow up with them?

Ivan:
Mmm, that’s a great question.

John:
Remember, even if a visitor can’t join the chapter, or doesn’t join the chapter, it doesn’t mean that they can’t do business with the people in the chapter. So make sure you’re making those connections for your members.

Ivan:
Yeah, and now your chapter members earn 6 million dollars a year on average.

John:
Yes.

Ivan:
Amazing.

John:
So with all those things, I use LinkedIn, Facebook, Alignable, Craigslist, Eventbrite; I use all these different websites to go out and invite visitors. I post and tell where our meeting is going to be. I direct invite specific people for the chapter. And then I also ask the question, “Who is the best pet sitter in the area?”

Ivan: (chuckling)

John:
And Sally Sue comes up and says, “Oh, this is my pet sitter.” [I] pick up the phone and call that person and say, “Sally Sue said you’re the best pet sitter in the area. What are you doing next Wednesday morning? Are you able to come to my BNI meeting.”

Ivan:
I love it. Absolutely love it. And what I love about it is, you know, people sometimes will be thinking, I can’t, I don’t have time to do all that. But wait a minute. You know, your chapter went from $500,000 in Thank You For Closed Business to $6 million in Thank You For Closed Business. So yes, you do have the time to do that if you’re going to have a 10x increase on ROI, that is a justification for the time to make connections and relationships. Well, listen, talking about time. We’re almost out of time. What’s the easiest way for a chapter to start building a culture of inviting?

John:
Yeah. So the easiest way is to find one other person that’s an accountability partner in your chapter. If you’re lucky enough, you might even get two. With two to three dedicated members that are sending out regular invites and inviting visitors and following up, then the new members will actually join the chapter and join the inviting team, and they’ll start inviting new members and following up, and pretty soon, you’ll have a culture of inviting.

Ivan:
Yeah.

John:
In the past seven years [since I joined BNI] I started out as a solopreneur [and] I now have nine full time employees, and if you listen to the BNI Podcast #419, The Law of Reciprocity, it says, the more that you give to the people around you, the more they’re going to want to give back to you. And I think bringing visitors is one of the biggest ways to give to the people around you.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. And it ends up in generating more and more business for everybody in the room. And that’s the key. That’s what I want. I want everyone to just get a boatload of referrals, and what you’ve just described is a fantastic way to do that. John, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast. John Rivers, and John’s website is www.aoitconsulting.com. Thanks for being here, John. I appreciate it.

John:
Thanks so much.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. Thank you, Ivan. Well, that was wonderful, John, thanks so much. I think that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: John Rivers explains how to apply a growth mindset to your BNI chapter by dedicating 15 minutes a day to inviting visitors and following up.

title.rendered: Episode 949: Coaching a Chapter to Greatness (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: this is a rebroadcast of Episode 728, first published on October 13, 2021. Some links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Mark Breves, the chapter president of the Founder Chapter of BNI, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week. When Mark became the president, the chapter was down to 20 members and struggling. After some coaching from Dr. Misner, Mark and his chapter implemented the following suggestions to grow the chapter back to 35 members (and counting).

  • Talk to people you know who have had success in BNI.
  • Interview every member of your chapter.
  • Focus on member recognition and accountability.
  • Use BNI’s training materials and meeting stimulants.

Look for more podcasts later this year about the recommendations that Dr. Misner made to help the Founder Chapter grow.

Chapter Coaching Materials

Download the BNI Founder Chapter Coaching Materials (PDF).

Complete Transcript of Episode 728/949

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am about to head to Santa Barbara [California] for a training. Jack Canfield has asked me to be part of a training program that he’s got going in Santa Barbara and I’m really excited to be there and to work with Jack and many other people, helping to talk about how to be successful in business. So I’m really excited.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
I’m also incredibly excited to have a guest on the podcast, Mark Breves. And Mark is the chapter President of the Founder’s Chapter of BNI. And when I say Founder’s Chapter, I mean The. Number. One. Chapter. I think it’s 10,363 chapters ago; it was the very, very first chapter of BNI. Let me just talk a little bit about Mark. Mark spent seven years in Thailand. His family traveled a lot. He returned to California when he was 14. He graduated high school in West Covina. I graduated from Azusa Unified so very close to you, Mark.

Mark:
Yes.

Ivan:
You went to Cal Poly Pomona to study electrical engineering. I went to Cal Poly and I taught at Cal Poly. And Mark quickly found out that he wasn’t real fond of physics, which I can totally get. So he took an entrepreneurial leap and opened a small custom t-shirt shop in a mall in Pomona. And I bet you I know which mall that is in Pomona that you opened up in.

Mark:
Which one?

Ivan:
Is it that walk-in mall that there’s like in the streets?

Mark:
Yes.

Ivan:
I remember it. Well, I remember it being redeveloped many, many years ago when my grandmother lived there. Yeah. So anyway, despite some initial difficulties, like he says he didn’t know how to run a business, he did make a success of it. And today his company is here some 35 years later and making some absolute, unforgettable, customized apparel in Irwindale [California]. And of course, I know where Irwindale is, as well. Mark, it’s such a pleasure to have you on this podcast. And you asked me in a note, if I recalled our meeting at Walters Restaurant in Claremont way back. And yes, I do. Do you want to give a quick review of what happened at that meeting?

Mark:
Well, that’s amazing. I mean, we found out you were going to be in Claremont and I couldn’t wait to go and check things out and see you in person and hear you talk. And I was very impressed with the way you worked the room, you went around and met everybody and shook hands and introduced yourself. And you showed everybody how to network, and I thought it was great. I was very impressed. And there was a great Q&A session afterwards, and I left kind of like, “Wow! What can a chapter be if we follow some of these strategies?” It was great.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I really enjoyed doing it and I love meeting BNI members. And I remember this event very clearly because it wasn’t huge; I think there was like 30 people, maybe 40 people at this.

Mark:
Something like that.

Ivan:
Yeah. And it was a blast. So you reached out to me afterwards and asked when you became chapter President, if we could talk and if I could help in any way. And I think I actually even made the offer at that meeting that I’d be glad to help because I know the Founding Chapter was struggling. And by the time we spoke, you had about 20 members. It’s now 35 members. And you asked if I would help and I said yes. And so I coached the Founding Chapter because I really want this chapter to continue to do well. And so what we’re going to do today, Mark, is talk about some of the things that you implemented into your chapter to turn it into a 35 member chapter. So, the first one was talking to people who you know. You want to talk about that?

Mark:
Yes, one of the strategies was to realize that we don’t know anything or everything and it would be better to talk to people who’ve had success in BNI and find out what their strategies were. So my Vice President coming in, Marc Vigil, and I did that. We talked to different chapter leaders, and we had you helping us and our Executive Director, Mike Adray, and the wonderful Christine Bond in Southern California.

Ivan:
Love Christine.

Mark:
She’s great, yeah, she was very helpful. So we just asked questions, you know: What are some of the things we need to do? How can we do them? How do you get people to buy in? That kind of thing.

Ivan:
And love you too, Mike; both great people. Mike Adray. What is the next thing that you did?

Mark:
Well, what we wanted to do is, in order to get a feel for where the chapter was with the Members, we interviewed every single Member, and we asked some specific questions to just get a general idea. What do you like most about the chapter? What do you like least about the chapter? Where would you like to see the chapter in six months? In a year?

And the answers were similar. And it was just like, well, there’s not enough members. There’s not enough referrals. We want more Members. We want more Thank You For Closed Business. And I stole one of Christine Bond’s signature questions, which was, “If your chapter membership cost you $100,000 a year, what would you be doing differently?” And they would say things like, “I would do more One-to-Ones.” or “I prepare my presentations a little better.” And I would say, “Well, why aren’t you doing that now?”

Ivan:
That’s a great question. I didn’t know she asked that. The next time I see her…

Mark: 
Isn’t that a great question? I love that question.

Ivan:
It is, it absolutely is because it makes you think “Well, yeah, I’d be working a lot harder.” Why aren’t we now?

Mark:
Exactly.

Ivan:
Great, great question. All right. Give me your next point about helping members realize the chapter’s potential.

Mark:
Yeah, what we wanted to do is basically focus on the core values of BNI, specifically the member recognition and accountability, which are two of my favorites. I mean, there’s so many that are my favorites, but I thought that one might be a good one. So what we started to do was we did a weekly best speaker trophy award where we would award that to the member who gave the best weekly presentation, they would hold on to it for a week, come back in a week, and then they would give it to the next speaker. So that was a way to encourage people to up their presentation skills a little bit.

Ivan:
Love it. Love it.

Mark:
Yeah, we also wanted to continue with the notable networker theme, what we did was we created almost like a certificate, like a diploma from a university, where we got a simple frame, framed it, put their name in it in Old English letters, so it looked real official. We brought them up to the front of the room said, “How were you able to get that many One-to-Ones and that much [Thank You For] Closed Business?” and gave them a chance to shine. So the goal was, they could be recognized because nobody ever recognizes entrepreneurs, and they could hopefully inspire some other Member to do the same.

Ivan:
You know, the Notable Networker awards were started in your chapter. And we actually in the very early days, had a small award, a standing award, and it would sit in front of the member who won the notable networker award for the month, and it would sit in front of them for the month. Over time, they just kind of grew legs and disappeared. So we went to certificates. But that concept of Notable Networker started in your chapter, as did many, many other things.

Mark:
Nice.

Ivan:
Talk about the top achievers. Is that what you just covered?

Mark:
Sort of, yeah; and we also did a perpetual plaque that we sat in front of the visitor table. So whenever anybody came in, there was this nice walnut framed plaque and it had everybody’s name for each month, you know, in terms of Thank You For Closed Business, referrals, CEUs, etc. So they were all recognized as well. So it gave a little extra I think, and it seemed to work.

Ivan:
You mentioned recognition and accountability. Is there anything else you want to say on that? Or do you want to talk about education?

Mark:
Accountability was going to be a little bit challenging. So we implemented, we started basically following BNI rules a little more closely, and started talking about tardiness and why you want to be on time. And despite a little initial resistance, everybody kind of fell in line and people were showing up on time and doing the right things.

Ivan:
It’s amazing how that effort yields such great results, and people are really hesitant to do it.

Mark:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Education and meeting stimulants. You want to talk about those real briefly?

Mark:
Sure, sure. We basically wanted to make sure that our Members were well trained because well trained Members pass more referrals and ultimately everybody wins when they do that. So we had some chapter in-house educational themes we had, we tried to encourage people to go to trainings, and ultimately more people participated and the results – it’s kind of a no-brainer. BNI has prepared the roadmap, all you have to do is follow it. The other thing we did in the meetings were stimulants, where we tried to make the meetings a little more fun. One of the last ones we did in-person was tie your weekly ask [presentation] to a Beatles song. And it was fun to watch people’s faces; and the tax guy did Tax Man. And it was funny, it was really good.

Ivan:
And just for clarity, he said “weekly ask” – ask with a K. And yeah, that is a fun meeting stimulant. And we’ve got a lot; if you don’t know what we’re talking about, we’re not talking about good drugs. We’re talking about these meetings stimulants that have been developed by BNI over the years. And you can find them, the Leadership Teams have access to them. And that’s a fun one. I wouldn’t do a meeting stimulant every week, but doing them you know, once every month or six weeks, I think works great.

Mark:
It was once every other month.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think that’s great. Yeah. You wanted to talk about COVID briefly, hit upon that.

Mark:
Yes. When COVID came, it sort of changed everything. We really didn’t know what was going to be happening. But fortunately, BNI came through; we just missed one week. And the initial challenge, once the tech issues were resolved, was getting people to focus on the meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mark:
One of our members, Josh Fischer, sent me a PowerPoint wheel that we use in the meetings to this day. It basically is a round wheel, it has everybody’s name on it. Everybody writes down, everybody’s ask. We spin the wheel, the first person has to recite is up on deck. And then they have to recite the ask of another member. If they are able to do that, they get another ask. So everybody’s focused, and it works really, really well.

Ivan:
And Mark, you’ve been stable or grown during COVID, right?

Mark:
Yes, absolutely.

Ivan:
All right. I want to say this to all the listeners, the Members: I recently just had a BNI Director tell me, “What are you thinking, Ivan? You can’t possibly grow; everything is going to go backwards during COVID.” And I had to tell him, if you think you can, or you think you can’t, you’ll be right. And people, COVID is not the problem. COVID is a problem. But people need BNI more than ever. It can be an opportunity, you can in fact, grow during COVID. We’ve opened more than 700 chapters. I don’t know if you knew that, Mark, during COVID!

Mark:
Amazing.

Ivan:
We’ve opened net 700. Our membership has gone up. It’s all about belief. And what I love about you, Mark, is that you believed.  When you and I talked at Walters Restaurant, you believed it was possible. And you helped other people in your chapter believe it was possible. And I’m just really proud of the Founders Chapter. And I’m proud of you. And I’m so pleased to have you on BNI Podcast today.

Mark: Thank you, it’s a privilege.

Ivan:
Well, before I leave, I know the question that chapters are going to ask is, “Hey, can you do this for our group, too?”  (Chuckling) With, you know, 10,400 groups worldwide, I can’t. However, what I’m going to do is take all of the communication that I had with you, Mark, and with your permission, I’m going to do podcasts before the end of the year, it’ll probably take about three podcasts to go through all of the different recommendations that I made to the chapter. And so you’ll get the same coaching, basically, that I gave to the Founders Chapter. And, Mark, thank you so much. Again, it’s a pleasure having you on.

Mark:
Thank you. I appreciate the opportunity. And ‘Founders’ rules!

Ivan:
Founders rules – absolutely. Well done. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you both, that was just great. Well, I’d like to tell you that this podcast is sponsored by wwwmisneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs are going to provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All the proceeds are going to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 948: How to Be a Friend

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The missing link between networking in friendship, according to Barnet Bain, is a sense of safety. Networking remains transactional until people feel safe enough to begin to trust you. Referrals go to members who are steady, grounded and consistent, not the ones who work the room the hardest.

Barnet calls this “friend-working,” and it works best when people are not tracking favors or positioning for advantage, and they are treating each other as equals whose trust and reputation matter. Reliability builds trust faster than charisma ever will. Listen, reflect, and build rapport. Show curiosity instead of judgment. Follow through consistently. Friendship in networking is not about being close. It’s about being safe.

Links

  • Visit Barnet’s website, barnetbain.com, to learn more about his book, How to Be a Friend (in an Unfriendly World).
  • Listen to Episode 150: Relationships are Currency.
  • Listen to Episode 304: Building Referral Relationships.
  • Listen to Episode 742: Building Relationships at Home, at Work, and in Community

Complete Transcript of Episode 948

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, two weeks in a row in Austin! I am very happy to be home here in Austin, and I’m really happy to have as a guest today a long-time friend of mine. I definitely consider him to be a close friend, Barnett Bain. He’s an award-winning filmmaker, author, teacher. He’s known for exploring themes of creativity and connection to human spirit. His film credits include The Academy Award winning movie, “What Dreams May Come,” Outstanding TV Movie Emmy [Award] nominee for “Homeless to Harvard”, Eckhart Tolle’s “Milton’s Secret,” and many others which have been translated into over 2000 languages. He’s often cited as one of the most… some of his films are often cited as the most widely seen in film history. And he has a new book, and his book is “How to Be a Friend (in an Unfriendly World),” which grew out of a Columbia University Master’s course he created for psychologists. My friend Barnet, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Barnet:
I’m thrilled to be with you. I’m thrilled, but I know we have a short window of time, so I want to maximize it and dive right in.

Ivan:
All right. So talk about friendship. What’s missing in friendship and networking?

Barnet:
Well, most networking focuses on how to introduce oneself, how to ask for referrals, how to follow up. What it doesn’t stress is how to be in a relationship. So I make the distinction between networking and friend-working. And friend-working is the skill of creating safety. When people feel safe with you, emotionally, especially reputationally, they chill, they stop guarding, and that’s when trust begins. So without that, networking stays mechanical. You can meet a lot of folks, but very few relationships or referrals happen. That only happens when trust is there, when you’re understood to be dependable and safe. Safety means you won’t disappoint me, embarrass me, let me down, injure my reputation. These are all qualities of friendship, and they are earned.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you talk about networking not working when it’s transactional, which is a message that I have said many, many, many times; it’s got to be relational, not transactional. You want to talk about that at all?

Barnet:
Yeah. Well, you know, over 2000 years ago, Cicero, Marcus Tullius Cicero, wrote the first book that I’m aware of about friendship, and he said that friends don’t seek to profit or gain advantage from one another. So when we talk about friendship in its purest sense, we’re talking about a relationship that is not transactional. It’s not about gaining advantage over another person. Benefits can happen, but it’s not the reason you’re there. Now that doesn’t sound like it belongs in networking, but it actually does.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Barnet:
Relationship is built around what I can get once the ask is made or missed, the energy just drains out of it. There’s nothing left to grow. So the problem is transactional thinking. Now I’ve been a friend of yours and BNI for many, many, many years. You don’t have to tell me that the core philosophy is Givers Gain®. And even that is sometimes misunderstood; it does not mean I give, so you give back to me. That means I give without keeping score.

Ivan:
Right.

Barnet:
And what’s below the water line of that, is that one trusts that the relationship and the community builds over time, and it does. So friend-working, which is the distinction that I make, it works best when people are not tracking favors or positioning for advantage, and they are treating each other as equals, whose trust and reputation matter. The business happens, but it’s the outcome, not the motive. So if I was going to put it into like some kind of a slogan that I could print on my [coffee] mug, it would say, friendship and networking are not opposites, transactional thinking and networking are.

Ivan:
Yeah, so, you know, one of the things I’ve talked about with Givers Gain is that it doesn’t have to be, you know, you may give me a referral, I may not be able to give you a referral back, but I can support you in some way. I can give you advice and give you suggestions. I can maybe make connections that will help you. There’s a lot of ways to give back in a relationship, besides some kind of reciprocal referral situation, which is obviously what BNI talks about a lot, but we also try to make it clear that it isn’t transactional, it’s relational, and there’s a lot of ways to build a relationship.

Barnet:
Yeah, I mean, you know, referrals come from a relationship, they don’t come from performance. You know, you and I have talked for years about what I call the “Hollywood schmooze or lose” model. When I refer someone, you refer someone, I’m attaching my reputation to them. So I’m not asking, “Are they impressive?” I’m asking, “Do I feel safe putting my name next to theirs?” Now in the schmooze or lose model, that’s an approach that encourages people to be on, to perform, to be memorable and polished, visible, but that performance never, ever, ever, did I say ‘never’?, creates safety. Presence does. And people will only refer those who are steady, grounded and consistent, not the people that work the room the hardest. So you know, friendship inside that kind of a model has certain kind of values. Number one, it’s about generating… that you’re safe, that there’s no pressure here. People relax when they don’t feel evaluated or pressured. Follow through is currency. You do what you say you’ll do, especially when it’s small and unglamorous.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Barnet:
Reliability builds trust faster than charisma ever will. You listen. When somebody shares a challenge, you don’t jump in with solutions.

Ivan:
Right.

Barnet:
You reflect on what you heard first. “That sounds frustrating,” you know, that kind of thing. “It seems like that really matters to you.” You’re building rapport. You’re not performing it. And you have a consistency. You most people try to stand out, and friend-working is built by being steady, by being, you know, you tend it the way you tend a garden. Consistency over time beats a strong impression every time. And then, you know, the most important thing to me, is that you protect other people’s reputation. You never put someone — a friend or a business associate or somebody you want to do business with, you never put them in an awkward position. You never over ask. People only refer people who are careful with their social capital, not those who spend it recklessly.

Ivan:
Yeah, I like to say when you give a referral, you give your reputation away. It could enhance your reputation, or it could hurt your reputation.

Barnet:
Yeah, if you’re undiscerning about it, you can lose your reputation forever.

Ivan:
Quickly. Yeah. And getting that reputation back takes time, you know, and people try and talk their way out of, you know, problems that they got into. And a friend of ours, Stephen Covey says, “You can’t talk your way out of something you behaved your way into.”

Barnet:
Actually, you’re digging yourself into a deeper hole.

Ivan:
Yeah, you have to behave your way out of it. And that’s all part of creating and then maintaining that friendship. Yeah.

Barnet:
Exactly, exactly. So, you know, there are certain kind of hallmarks of what a friendship might look like inside a networking organization like BNI. It’s as I say, it’s the same thing as a large family gathering. It’s not about over-sharing or being informal. I believe it’s about relational reliability, it’s how you show up when nothing is being asked for. So it looks like you’re listening without scanning for opportunity. You’re present with people. You’re not looking over their shoulder. You’re not running through and re-arming your next thought or your next idea. You’re listening with them. You put down your devices. You look them in the eye, and you listen. You show curiosity instead of judgment. You follow through consistently. You demonstrate being reliable more than being impressive.

Ivan:
You know that last phrase you said, it reminds me of the Ted Lasso episode. I think he was quoting Walt Whitman that you have to live a life of curiosity rather than judgment and being judgmental.

Barnet:
Well, trust you to pick up on the same cues that I pick up on. Yeah, that’s exactly…

Both: (laughing)

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s a great episode, absolutely a great episode.

Barnet:
It is. And Walt Whitman’s not bad either.

Ivan: (laughing)
No, he is not. Listen, you know, friendship, we have not really referred business back and forth, but I consider you a really close friend, and it’s because of the things that you have done to support me as a friend. You have done a couple of things related to the BNI Foundation. You, at one of our [BNI] Conferences, came to a [BNI] Foundation event, which Elisabeth and I really appreciated. When Elisabeth was sick, you reached out to me multiple times to see how she was doing. When I had cancer and repercussions from cancer, you reached out multiple times to see how I was doing. None of that, absolutely none of that was transactional. It was all relational. And to me, that’s a great example of you walking the talk in terms of your book, How to Be a Friend.

Barnet:
Well, I appreciate that. You know, it gets to a point… it gets to a place where what starts off as training wheels, what starts off as a lane, you know as a practice, like going to the gym, it eventually becomes embodied, and it becomes automatic, and at that point you know you become safe. If other people don’t feel safe with you, you can forget the rest, nothing, nothing comes after that.

Ivan:
100% Well, we’re about out of time, and for those of you interested in the book, it is available on Amazon. I’m sure it’s also available almost everywhere. Or you can go to BarnetBain.com,

Barnet: b, a, r, n, e, t, Bain: b, a, i, n .com, where I’m sure there’s a link to the book. Barnet, any last thought before we turn it over to Priscilla?

Barnet:
Oh, I always have at least one or two. How about: friendship in networking is not about being close. It’s about being safe; safe to refer, safe to recommend, safe to attach your name to. And with that, thank you so much, Ivan, for inviting me. It’s always a pleasure to be with you, either online or at the dinner table.

Ivan:
Or at the TLC events. It’s always great to see you there.

Barnet:
Yeah, I look forward to seeing.

Ivan:
Alright, my friend. Thank you so much. Barnet. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great. Thank you so much to both of you.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Reliability builds trust faster than charisma ever will. Follow through consistently. Friendship in networking is all about being safe.

title.rendered: Episode 947: Guinness Networking World Record

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Thanks to National Director of BNI Australia Frederick Marcoux and his team, BNI Australia now holds the Guinness World Record for the most people attending a speed networking event. As part of the BNI Global Convention in Sydney, 1108 BNI members each went through 20+ rounds of speed networking. That’s right: 20,000 rounds of One-to-Ones in 90 minutes.

Complete Transcript of Episode 947

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I’m actually home this week in Austin, Texas. I have to remind myself what my place looks like, because I have been gone so much.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Oh, wow. Okay, and what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
Well, I have a guest, a really interesting guest with a great story, Frederick Marcoux is the owner and the National Director of BNI Australia. It’s part of BNI, the world’s largest business networking operation. Since acquiring BNI Australia in 2007, almost 20 years now, he’s grown the community to 7000 members nationwide. They generate about three quarters of a billion dollars annually in new business through referrals in Australia. Frederick is a certified Franchise Executive Director, former partner in RSM Australia, which is an accounting firm, as I recall, and Frederick co-authored The 29% Solution, Australian version, with myself. And together with Frederick’s wife, Pauline, he founded the BNI Foundation Australia, which also supports education for underprivileged children like the BNI Foundation. He’s an avid surfer, crazy surfing out there in Australia with all the great white sharks, but he is an avid surfer and adventurer. Frederick has surfed all over the world and has journeyed to Everest base camp and to Antarctica. You’re the only other person in BNI that I know has gone to Antarctica other than me, Frederick. Welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Frederick:
Thank you so much, Ivan. It’s a privilege to be here. It’s good to be with you and Priscilla today.

Priscilla:
Yes.

Ivan:
Well it’s great to have you here. So give me the official title, because now BNI is an official Guinness World Record holder. Give us the official title of what we won.

Frederick:
It’s called The Most People Attending a Business Speed Networking Event. And yeah, we achieved 1108 in Sydney, New South Wales [Australia] on the 20th of November 2025. That’s what it says on the official certificate.

Ivan:
Yeah, and so this is official — BNI is a Guinness World Record holding company. And I thought you were crazy (laughing) when you told me you were going to do this; I thought you were absolutely crazy. And let me just officially say I was wrong, you were right, and well done, Frederick. I’m very impressed.

Frederick:
Thank you. It was a thrill. It was, you know, a big thrill to achieve that. Because, as you know, Ivan, nothing worthwhile is easy.

Ivan:
That’s for sure. Yeah. So why did you want to go after the Guinness World Record?

Frederick:
Well, I was with my marketing director, and we were going over her focus areas and my focus areas. And as you know, Ivan, BNI is sometimes the world’s best known marketing secret. And you know, we wanted to make the most of everyone coming to Australia for the Global Convention, and we were just brainstorming. And I said to Heather, “Heather, you know, it would be great if we could do a Guinness World Record or something.” But for some reason, I thought that Guinness didn’t like it when this happened in conjunction with a conference of some kind, and she challenged me on that. And next thing you know, we were seriously considering it.

But the reason, I guess, other than brand recognition and all that, as you know, Ivan, a lot of people in the world today don’t know what networking is. I mean, believe it or not, a lot of people still think networking is selling, and that bothers me. And we really wanted to get the message out there, especially in the context of today, where there’s AI everywhere, people are afraid, it’s a big change.

Ivan:
The human connection is more important than ever, isn’t it?

Frederick: 
Absolutely, I feel that, you know, human connection is amplified by networking and therefore I feel that BNI in the future is going to be even more important, because people are going to value human connection even more when there’s going to be all this AI around us.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, I read a book many, many, many years ago, Mega Trends by John Nesbit. He contributed to a book I wrote called Masters of Networking. And he said, we’re moving into a high-tech, high-touch society, that the more technologically advanced we become, the more important it is to have a connection with people. And we’ve arrived. We are absolutely there. He wrote that in the 1980s, and he…

Frederick:
Wow.

Ivan:
…he had crystal ball when he said that one. So let me ask you a question. Did anything happen during the [world record] attempt that surprised you or was unexpected?

Frederick:
Okay. Well, everything that we…. as you can imagine, we sat down and we thought, okay, what can go wrong? And everything that we thought of that could go wrong, went wrong. And it wasn’t just wrong. It was just like the worst possible scenario materialized.

Ivan:
Just for the record, I didn’t witness that, and I was there, so you covered it up really well.

Frederick: 
Well, so the first thing that happened, that was really unexpected, was that the room looked empty, and in that Brian, the adjudicator from Guinness World Records, said to me, “Frederick, I’m a little bit nervous.” And I went, “Okay, yeah, me too.” But what happened then was teamwork. I have an amazing team of Executive Directors. They had their Directors lined up. They moved the room and their seats. We had 18 zones organized, groups of four rows with 20+ people. Because you see, the challenge was that we needed to do 20+ rounds; every single person needed to do 20+ rounds of three minutes of speed networking. And that was a big challenge to make sure that everybody could fit that in, in the time that we had. And it worked.

Ivan:
It worked, but you ran into some problems, which, here’s the lesson, I think, that we can share is Murphy’s Law: what can go wrong, will go wrong, and the more you’re prepared for what can go wrong, the more likely you are to be successful. Have you ever heard Misner ‘s Corollary to Murphy’s Law?

Frederick:
Tell me.

Ivan:
It’s a two-part corollary. First, part A is: Murphy was an optimist. Part B is: What can’t go wrong will go wrong. And I’ve tried to live by that to prepare myself for what can go wrong or can’t go wrong, and you guys clearly did both, because it came together, despite all of the challenges that you talked about there.

Frederick: 
Absolutely came together. We did over 20,000 rounds of One-to-Ones in 90 minutes.

Ivan:
That’s amazing, just amazing. All right, so you’ve accomplished this world record. What now? What do you want to do next?

Frederick:
Well, look, it’s clear; it’s clear that speed networking is popular. Okay, we’ve been doing speed networking events face-to-face. We’ve been doing them online. It’s a big thing, and now that we are the Guinness World Records holder, we can get even more people interested in us helping them enhance their events.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Frederick:
And so every single Executive Director in Australia has a world record certificate, and you know, they’re going to go out to business associations, to our alliance partners. We have over 200 organizations in Australia, Ivan, that have three or more members of BNI in our community. And so we’re going to work with those people and enhance their conferences. You know, speed networking is actually very powerful, and the way that we do it, using the G.A.I.N.S. Exchange is the secret sauce.

Ivan:
Yeah. I’ve got a little speed networking G.A.I.N.S. Exchange card I’m happy to send you if you’d like.

Frederick:
Oh, please do.

Ivan:
And I know you can get copies of the award, the Guinness award. When we’re offline, I need to find out how to get one, because I want to put one up in my office. Even though I said I thought you were crazy for doing this, I’m gone on record saying I was wrong. You were crazy smart for doing this.

Frederick:
Well, thank you. And one’s on its way, Ivan, don’t worry about that. There’s one on its way it. I’m excited to share that. And I want everyone to know, everyone in the world to know. I mean, look, Guinness World Records is a very well-known brand, as you know. If you’re a Guinness World Record holder, you’re the best. Well, guess what? We are the best in networking, that’s it. And we need to associate ourselves with this brand that people recognize to help us with brand recognition.

Ivan:
Well, everyone listening to this podcast, just know that we, BNI, because of Frederick and his team, are now a Guinness World Record holding organization at networking. And Frederick, I appreciate you so much. Any last thought before we wrap up here?

Frederick:
Well, let’s get out there and do more networking. The world needs networking more today than ever before. And I’m really proud of my team and everyone. Wendy Lloyd Curley was the MC together with Alvin, and without them and Heather and Avi Sequeira, we would never have been able to accomplish this. So I thank them very, very much.

Ivan:
Well, I appreciate them. I appreciate you, and I’m really excited that we got this. Thank you so much. I look forward to seeing you at the next International Convention, my friend, and almost 20 years now, right?

Frederick:
Absolutely, Ivan. In Panama in 2027 I’ll be there collecting my 20-year anniversary pin. I can’t wait.

Ivan:
Well, I want to be the one to give it to you. So you tell the [awards] team, “Ivan wants to give this to me.”

Frederick:
Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Got it. All right. Thank you, Frederick. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was just great, Frederick. Well, I think that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
