title.rendered: Episode 911: Why I Invited My Competition

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Would you invite a competitor to visit your BNI chapter? Jessica Korff from the BNI Sunrise Success Chapter in Arizona did, and explains why you should, too.

Clarity, connection, and collaboration are what build successful businesses today.

  • Clarity creates abundance. Focus on your specific niche.
  • Connection between members with different specialties in the same profession creates a mini Power Team.
  • Collaboration builds momentum and accountability. “Competitors” with different specialties refer business to each other.

When we give space for others to come in and work together, everybody wins.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 477: The Classification Cowboy.
  • Find out more about Jessica’s branding and boudoir photography at https://www.jessicakorff.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 911

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am really pleased to say that I am in Santa Barbara, California, [with] Jack Canfield. Jack has been a friend of mine for almost 20 years. He is doing his very last Mastermind event, and I’m co-facilitating it. I’m honored to be co-facilitating his very last Mastermind in Santa Barbara.

Priscilla:
Oh, great.

Ivan:
He’s retiring from his work.

Priscilla:
Really? That’s wonderful. Lucky him.

Ivan: (laughing)
Well, I think he’ll miss it, but you know, at some point we all have to be put to pasture.

Priscilla: (chuckling)
I know.

Ivan:
So, my day is coming, but hopefully not anytime soon. I have a guest here today. Her name is Jessica Korff and Jessica, her topic is Why I Invited My Competition, and there’s more to the title: “And you might want to, also.” Jessica is a portrait photographer and educator in the photo industry. She’s the owner of Jessica Korff Studios, and she specializes in branding and boudoir photography. We all love boudoir photography, whoever we are. She’s a proud member of the BNI Sunrise Success Chapter, and she’s been part of BNI SoZona for over eight years and has served as a Support Director for the past five. She is passionate about intentional leadership, collaboration over competition, and guiding others to lead with what they love in life and in business. Jessica, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Jessica:
Oh, thank you for having me. I’m so excited and grateful to be here.

Ivan:
Well, I’m glad you’re here, and I love this topic. And it’s almost completely counter-intuitive to what the average businessperson thinks, but I am 100% convinced that it is what helps make many professions successful — this mindset of abundance, which is the first thing I want to talk about. Talk about the scarcity mindset around competition, and how you thought about that with your group.

Jessica:
Yeah, so I really wanted to start by just naming the elephant in the room, which you just did, right? Why would anyone invite their competition into their BNI chapter?

Ivan:
Right.

Jessica:
It does go against what we’ve been generally taught in business regarding our spaces, protecting our client base, being cautious about anything that might take our potential clients away. But I do think that scarcity mindset is a super outdated practice. What actually works today, I believe, and builds thriving businesses and [BNI] chapters is going to be clarity, connection, and collaboration. I’ve really seen it firsthand that when you lean into that collaboration instead of competition, you really don’t lose business, you gain momentum.

Ivan:
I agree. Can I add one word to it? I would say collaboration and focusing on your specialty.

Jessica:
Yes, definitely. I think that for me, clarity and focus kind of go hand-in-hand. So when I use the term clarity, it’s definitely pushing towards that focus.

Ivan:
Yeah, I have found businesses that really want to be an expert in a niche in whatever field, tend to do much, much better. So talk to me about the clarity and how that creates abundance in your mind.

Jessica:
So I think that there’s this myth of loss that goes with it, and I have three main reasons that I actually think it’s a game. So is this where I can go into those?

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely.

Jessica:
Perfect. Okay, so number one is, I think clarity creates abundance. So focus, same thing, right? Creates the abundance. And when you niche down, you don’t get less. You actually get more of what you love. For me, that clarity was focusing on branding and boudoir photography because they aligned with how I wanted to empower women. That’s my flame. In our chapter, we now have other photographers who lead with their flames. So we have weddings and events, we have family and newborn sessions, and we’re currently looking to add real estate, pet, and even birthing photographers.

Ivan:
Wait, wait. I want everyone to hear this. You already have two, three, and you’re adding another?

Jessica: 
Yes, we have three currently, and we already have one visiting next week for real estate. We definitely want to continue to grow our niches within that photography category.

Ivan:
Yeah. Now I want everyone to hear this. Here’s a chapter that’s going to have three or four photographers in the chapter, all focusing on their specific classification, and I’m guessing many of them, the other photographers, are referring you for your category, and you’re referring them for their category. Is that accurate?

Jessica:
Yes, yes, that’s absolutely true.

Ivan:
Totally counter-intuitive, but incredibly impressive. Sorry, didn’t mean to cut you off. Go ahead.

Jessica:
Oh no, that’s okay, absolutely. We all could do different things, right? That could even potentially cross over, but we really try and focus on leading with our flame, because that’s what’s going to make us unforgettable.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. And really, you get a reputation for being an expert in that category, in that field. And a lot of people don’t understand that. In some of the notes you sent me, you talk about generalists. Tell us your thoughts on that.

Jessica:
Yes. So, you know, we say in BNI all the time, specific is terrific, right? So, and I think that generalists may find themselves blending in a little bit when we do all of the things, while specialists tend to stay top of mind. As an example, our way to think about it, you wouldn’t think to go to a general doctor for brain surgery, even if you could do it. You want the right specialist, because it’s clear what it is they do, and that they do it really well. And I think it’s the same in business. So I know chiropractors who focus on women’s health and others who specialize in pediatric care; I think they could apply this, too. Honestly. I think this idea could work in most categories, because clarity, to me, always creates more opportunity if you’re willing to invite it in.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. But you’ve got to be willing to invite it in, you’re gonna be able to see past the potential competition and look at the collaboration. Dana, my assistant, talked about a chapter that she was involved in that had two landscapers showed up at a meeting, and they said, “Sorry, we can only pick one.” And the two of them walked out for a moment, had a conversation, came back in and said, “Well, I’ll do commercial landscape work, and he’s going to do residential. Would that be okay?” And they said, “Yes.” And these two landscape people joined and referred business to each other. It’s exactly the kind of thing you’re talking about.

Jessica:
Yes, I love that so much. I think that when we have that abundant mindset like that, everybody gets to win.

Ivan:
Yeah. So talk about collaboration being protective, not risky.

Jessica:
So I think that a lot of people worry that when they bring someone else into a similar category, they’re gonna lose clients. I think that’s a big fear they have.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Jessica:
And I really have found the opposite to be true. When I have a trusted colleague that I can refer for things that don’t light me up, kind of like the landscapers, right? Not only do I serve the client better, but I keep them in the room. They get to stay inside the trusted circle. It really creates a stronger chapter ecosystem. For me personally, it also has taken away some of the loneliness that comes with being a solo photographer, right? I have somebody who understands my world, we can brainstorm, support each other, and hold each other accountable. It’s kind of like a mini Power Team.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Jessica:
And also, I say this with love, I think you can feel when someone’s operating from fear. Desperately trying to hold on to everything, I think, really repels people. But if you show up from a place of trust and generosity, that becomes magnetic. I’ve had people ask if I photograph weddings, and I don’t, so I refer my wedding photographer, and the result is the client trusted me more. That kind of trust sticks. That referral gets to stay in our chapter, and we get to protect that client by keeping them within that trusted circle.

Ivan:
So I’ve seen the example of what you just talked about. You referring the photographer, who does, I think you said weddings. I’ve seen this in a couple of chapters. One was in New York that had five attorneys, and they basically said the same thing that they refer another attorney with that specialty. And correct me if I’m wrong, Priscilla, but didn’t one of your chapters that you were in have like seven attorneys at one time?

Priscilla:
Oh, I don’t think seven, but I think we had four.

Ivan:
Four. So, when I attended years ago that chapter, they told me that they got, on a per capita basis, they got most of their referrals from the other attorneys because of what you’re talking about, Jessica. They know the language, they know the profession, and they also know what they want to specialize in, and they’re happy to refer the business to other people.

Jessica:
Yep, absolutely.

Ivan:
All right, so collaboration builds momentum and accountability. I love the fact that it reduces isolation. That’s not something I had considered before, but I love that, and I think it’s really, really quite accurate. So one of the things with BNI Connect, and I don’t know if you’ve got on BNI Connect and look for photography groups, photographer groups, but I’m sure there’s every kind of profession imaginable on BNI Connect that has created groups. And I would urge anyone listening to this podcast to get on and look for your profession there. Because it helps to reduce that isolation as a solopreneur if you’re talking to other people in your business. So I’ve got one other question for you, Jessica. How did you come to the decision to invite more photographers into your chapter? And what would you say to someone who’s afraid of losing business by doing that?

Jessica:
Well, it definitely started with clarity. I realized that I wanted to focus on what I truly love, and inviting others who love different parts of photography, didn’t threaten that and supported it. So even though there were a few things that may cross over, we each lead with our flame, like I said. Our chapter members are more clear. They’ve all told me, it gives them more confidence on exactly how to refer us, and it keeps clients in the trusted hands within our room. So I’d say start with some important questions. Ask yourself, What do I love most about what I do? What’s my flame? What type of clients in my work light me up? And then ask, what might shift if I gave myself permission to lead with that? In BNI, we talk about building a network we can trust, and this is one more way to do that, by bringing more of who we are to the table and trusting that the right people will meet us there.

Ivan:
I love what you’re saying, and I just gotta say, I’m really, really proud of your group and of you and your fellow photographers for getting the big picture of how collaboration can effectively work. I want to mention a podcast that I did years ago, episode 477 which is a classic, and it talks about classification cowboys, how sometimes people try to take multiple classifications. What we’re talking about here is the exact opposite of what you’ll see on episode 477. I’m seeing more and more chapters understanding what you’re describing, and I’m just proud as heck of your group, and I’d love for you to pass that on to them. And if you’re listening to this podcast, really consider it strongly. Well, any last thought before we run, Jessica?

Jessica:
No, that’s really the best part. I think that you’ve already wrapped it up well, that when we give space for others to come in and work together, everybody wins. Everybody gets more clarity. And I really think the referrals come faster, stronger and better.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. Jessica, thank you so much. For anyone interested in Jessica’s photography, her website is www.JessicaKorff.com, JessicaKorrf.com. Korff is spelled K, O, R, F, F, Jessica, J, E, S, S, I, C, A. Jessica, thank you so very much.

Jessica:
Oh, thank you for having me. This was fabulous.

Ivan:
Thanks. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much. That was some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Would you invite a competitor to visit your BNI chapter? Jessica Korff did, and explains why you should, too.

title.rendered: Episode 910: Reflections on BNI’s Journey, Part 3

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In the third and final episode in this series, Dr. Misner answers three questions about the future of BNI.

How can the organization today honor BNI’s legacy while driving its future?

The more people that really live the core values, the stronger their organization is going to be. If our core values remain consistent, we can work through the technological changes that are taking place throughout the world.

What does this 40th anniversary mean to you, personally and professionally?

Some milestones that stand out are

  • Visiting a meeting in Sweden and knowing exactly what was going on even without knowing Swedish.
  • Making it to 100,000 members.
  • Making it to 10,000 chapters, which I’d predicted in 1986 and we achieved in 2020.
  • Achieving world-wide brand recognition.
  • Passing $25 billion worth of business between members in one year.

What are your hopes for BNI’s next 40 years?

I think we can have millions of members. BNI has never been about what is — it has always been about what can be. So ask yourself, what could BNI look like if you thought beyond your current beliefs? Think big. Think bold. But most of all, think beyond.

Complete Transcript of Episode 910

Priscilla:  
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you? 

Ivan:
I am at my home in Austin, Texas, this week, and this is the best season to be in Austin. The weather is usually great. It’s warm. It’s pool weather for sure.

Priscilla:
Okay, good. And so what is this that you’re going to tell us for part three?

Ivan:
Well, we’re going to do part three of what we view for the future, really, of BNI. And we’ve already done two parts, and I don’t think we’ve ever done a podcast that was a three part podcast, but there was a lot that I had to say. And this, as I recall, comes from a series of questions that I was asked by somebody, and I thought it would be great to have you ask me these same or similar questions and let me respond a little bit about what I see as the future of the BNI organization. So you’ve got some questions in front of you. Let me hand it over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. So the first question that I have is, how can the organization today honor BNI’s legacy while driving its future?

Ivan:
So its legacy really rests on the core values of the organization and the implementation of the core values. And what I find interesting is sometimes people say, “Well, you know, so and so doesn’t live the core values.” Okay, well, not everybody’s going to but it can start with you. And so the more people that really live the core values, the stronger their organization is going to be.

And our principal core value is, of course, Givers Gain®. And what I love about that is you can go any place, any place in the world, any BNI chapter, anywhere in the world and ask what our principal core value or philosophy is, and they’ll tell you Givers Gain. And Givers Gain is more than a phrase. To me, it’s a way of living one’s life. It’s a perspective to view and interact with the world. It’s an attitude, not an expectation, and when it’s applied properly, it will change your life.

And when it changes enough lives, it’ll change the world. And that’s what we’re trying to do in BNI, because I really believe that culture eats strategy for breakfast. Culture is the secret sauce. It’s the DNA of a successful organization. And so I think that driving forward, you know, technology has changed. Many things have changed in 40 years, but if our core values remain consistent, we can work through the technological changes that are taking place throughout the world.

Priscilla:
Great. Second question is, what does this 40th anniversary mean to you, personally and professionally?

Ivan:
Well, you know, BNI has been relevant now for the majority of my life. We went international back in about 1995 when we opened in Canada, the first country outside of the United States. And then, you know, we’ve kind of, it’s interesting… When you’re building a business based on word-of-mouth, it’s not linear. It’s sort of a hopscotch. So if you’re looking, if you were to look at a map of the world, it wouldn’t be like… other than the US and Canada, which are side-by-side, most of the other countries, we hopped around, we opened in the UK, and then Sweden, and then Australia. And so it, it doesn’t look linear. And the reason for that is that word-of-mouth comes from people who happen to visit a chapter or hear about a chapter in a country, and they want to open [a chapter] somewhere. And so one of our first countries outside the US, besides Canada and UK, was Sweden. And Sweden was the first place that I went to and saw a BNI meeting in another language. And it was a visitor’s day, and it was all in Swedish. And, you know, if you’re an American, you know, a few words in French. I grew up in Southern California, so I know some Spanish, but Swedish is just different.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
You know, it has this great sing song kind of tone, and it’s not…. very few people in the US know Swedish unless they’re from Sweden or have family members who are Swedish. And I remember the first meeting I went to in another language was in Sweden, Stockholm I believe. And one of the Directors leaned over to me and said, “You have no idea what they’re saying right now, do you?” And I said, “No, they’re introducing the referral part of the meeting, and they’re explaining that if you don’t have a referral, this is where you give a testimonial.” And the person said, “Wow, you know Swedish?” I said, “No, I wrote the agenda. That’s what happens right now.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
And he laughed. And he’s like, “You’re right, that’s exactly what he’s saying.” And I said, “I know.” That’s what happens in you know… we have now, at this moment, over 11,300 chapters worldwide, and you could go to any good chapter in any country, and you’ll know exactly what’s happening in the meeting, even if you don’t know the language. That’s one of the amazing things about BNI. And you know, there are all these different cultures, but what people forget about is the culture of entrepreneurism, and how, you know, we overlay this culture of entrepreneurism on top of the individual culture of that nation, and we provide a system and a structure within the cultural context of that country, not outside the cultural context. So in terms of milestones for me, opening in another country, knowing what was going on in a different language, hitting our first 100,000 members kind of blew my mind. And then, of course, when we hit 10,000 chapters, that was a huge milestone for me, because I had predicted in 1986 that someday we would have 10,000 chapters. And I’ve told that story.

I had a friend Bruce, and Bruce said, “How many chapters do you have now, Ivan?” and I said, “30.” He’s like, “And you think you can have 10,000?”  I said, “Yeah, I absolutely think we can have 10,000.” I remember him saying, “Well, it’s good to have goals, Ivan. Very good to have goals.” And I’m thinking, no, no, it’s gonna happen. And sure enough, in 2020 we surpassed the 10,000 chapter mark. And I think, you know, along the way, early on, probably by 1986 when I realized how big it could be, I realized that I was part of something bigger than myself and that BNI was going to become a brand.

And a brand begins to have name recognition when a significant portion of the target audience can readily identify the brand by its name, logo or other visual cues, meaning they can recognize it upon seeing it, without needing extensive prompting. And that’s usually achieved through consistent exposure to marketing, branding efforts across various channels.

It’s one of the reasons why this podcast is now 18 years old. We’ve been doing this for 18 years. I remember one of our first podcasts was, “I’m a Baby Boomer, What’s a Podcast?” because nobody knew what podcasts were when I started doing these. And, of course, very active in social media. We want to see the organization grow, because with a few 100,000 members, you’re not going to have brand recognition like you will with a million members. Plus, with a million members, the amount of business that we can generate… I’ve said this before – last year, we generated 25 billion with a “B”, 25 billion US Dollars worth of business for our members. And that, to me, is just mind boggling. So that’s some of my milestones, Priscilla. I know you have one other question, and we’re running out of time, and we’re not going to do four episodes on this.

Priscilla:
Okay. So here we go. Vision of the future. What are your hopes for BNI’s next 40 years?

Ivan:
I think we can have millions of members. As of this date, we have about 340,000 members worldwide. I think we can have millions of members because we can make an even bigger impact in the world. A brand begins to have name recognition when a significant portion of the audience really identifies with that brand, and that happens when you have millions of clients. And so, I believe that we have to think beyond where we are now. And these were some of the closing comments that I had at a recent BNI Conference, and I’m going to share them with you now and it’s our theme for this year, which is Think Beyond.

Think Beyond what you see in front of you, beyond what your business is today, beyond what networking has meant to you so far. And this message is not only to you as a BNI member, but to your business as well, because beyond is where growth happens. Beyond is where possibility lives. Now, I know some of you are thinking, when it comes to BNI, Ivan, it’s been 40 years. Can’t we just think about sitting down, taking a break, or taking our networking legs off duty?

You know, fair enough, 40 years is a milestone worth celebrating. However, BNI is not over the hill at 40, we’re picking up speed. What we built over the last four decades is just the foundation. If you’re coasting for long, it’s because you’re going downhill. I want chapters to hear that, especially smaller chapters. You know, if you’re in a Chapter saying, “Oh yeah, we’re doing okay, you know, things are moving along okay.” If you’re coasting for long, it’s because you’re going downhill. BNI as an organization is not coasting. We are not resting on our past. We are reaching for the future, because BNI has never been about what is – it has always been about what can be. We’re not slowing down. We’re scaling up.

BNI began with a vision, not just for referrals, but for relationships. Not just for business, but for belonging. It started with a handful of people in one room who believed in thinking differently, who believed in thinking beyond themselves, and four decades later, that belief has turned into a global movement. Not because we stayed the same, but because we kept thinking beyond what was and leaned into what could be. The future does not belong to those who settle in comfort zones. It belongs to those who think beyond them. It belongs to leaders who think beyond transactions and build transformational networks. It belongs to professionals who think beyond scarcity and embrace abundance and collaboration. It belongs to you, to those of you listening to this podcast if you choose to think beyond your current limits.

So ask yourself, what could BNI look like if you thought beyond your current beliefs? What could your network become if you thought beyond your own results? What legacy could you leave if you thought beyond success and into significance? That’s a legacy that you can achieve. Let’s remember that what we build together doesn’t end at a BNI meeting. It doesn’t end this year. It may not even end this generation. We’re part of a movement, a global community, that’s rewriting what it means to do business with heart, with purpose, and with vision. I love being part of this organization, and I appreciate all of you members who are listening to this all around the world. I appreciate you very much. And as you finish listening to this podcast, carry this with you. Think big. Think bold. But most of all, think beyond. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla: 
That’s great. I loved it. Wonderful. Thank you. Okay, well, I think that that’s it for this week, Dr Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: In the third and final episode in this series, Dr. Misner answers three questions about the future of BNI in the next 40 years.

title.rendered: Episode 909: Reflections on BNI’s Journey, Part 2

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In Part 2 of this three-part series, Dr. Misner answers questions about the evolution of BNI and shares lessons learned over the past 40 years.

How has the concept of networking evolved since BNI’s inception?

One of the biggest things in the evolution of the organization is the implementation of technology like this podcast and BNI Connect. Technology has flattened the ability for us to communicate and made it affordable.

What role has BNI played in shaping the global networking culture?

In 1985, networking was face-to-face cold-calling. Implementing Givers Gain® and trademarking it helped to shape the global culture of networking to a very large extent.

What innovations or practices have been most impactful in keeping BNI relevant over the decades?

Moving online in 2020 when COVID hit saved thousands of member businesses and BNI as an organization.

What are the key lessons that you’ve learned as a leader during BNI’s journey?

  • Ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice.
  • Stick to the fundamentals.
  • Work in your flame and not in your wax.

Don’t miss the final episode in the series next week!

Links

  • Episode 65: Networking Is Simple, but Not Easy
  • Episode 307: Culture Eats Strategy for Breakfast
  • Episode 393: The 7 Things I Learned from Starting a Business
  • Episode 641: Garage to Global®

Complete Transcript of Episode 909

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Southern California this week. I’m here to support my daughter, who’s got a little surgery. So I’m taking the week off, and here to support my daughter.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. I hope she does well.

Ivan:
Yeah, she’s been a trouper.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
So we’re following up on last week’s episode, which is Part One of Reflections on BNI’s Journey, because this is our 40th anniversary. And the way we did this was I had someone who I did a radio interview or podcast where they had some really great questions, and so I shared the questions with you. You’re going to run them by me, and I’m going to, actually, I’m elaborating, have an opportunity to elaborate even more on these podcasts. So let’s see if we can do it in two episodes. Otherwise, we’ll be doing a third.

Priscilla:
Yeah, okay, let’s see. But it’s great information, so even if it spills over into a third, it’s still great. Okay, so the next section is called the evolution of BNI, and the first question was, how has the concept of networking evolved since BNI’s inception?

Ivan:
So the meetings are very much the same, or really similar. If you went to a meeting in 1985 and a meeting in 2025, you’d recognize it. Although the name changed from… originally, it was called The Network, but we needed a trademark, so we changed the name to BNI [Business Network International] for the trademark. So other than the name, you would recognize the organization. However, we have added to it over the years, and probably one of the biggest things in the evolution of the organization is the implementation of technology. BNI has been an early adopter to technology, and that surprises people, because we’re an in-person, mostly in-person. We have online chapters and hybrid chapters, but still quite a bit of in-person [meetings], but technology has flattened the communication hierarchy.

Things like this podcast. When I started BNI in 1985 this would have been unheard of — podcasts like this. I think we started this in around 2007 and it’s enabled me to communicate directly with members and I read all of the comments that are posted up on BNI Podcast [website]. And you know, the technology has really flattened the ability for us to communicate, and it’s also made it affordable, because in 1986 the second largest line item in the BNI budget was the phone bill, believe it or not. The phone bill was incredibly expensive, but over the years, I don’t even know where it is in our budget now. So technology has really evolved a lot, and of course, us going online. Roughly 16% of our organization is still completely online. About 16% are hybrid, and the balance have gone back to meeting [in-person].

Priscilla:
Yeah, yeah. Okay, great. What role has BNI played in shaping the global networking culture?

Ivan:
So I really believe that we’ve impacted that to some extent. Because, you know, when I started BNI… networking, we don’t teach this in colleges and universities — we still don’t, but networking was almost entirely face-to-face cold calling. People didn’t understand that networking is really about building relationships. And I think the one thing that has helped influence and played a role in shaping the global networking culture is our philosophy of Givers Gain®. And Givers Gain is more than a phrase to me. It’s a way of living one’s life. It’s a perspective to view and interact with the world. It’s an attitude, not an expectation, and when it’s applied properly, it will change your life. And when it changes enough lives, it will change the world. And I think implementing that philosophy into the organization, and trademarking it wasn’t a bad idea either… Doing those two things has helped to shape the global culture of networking to a very large extent.

Priscilla:
Great. Yeah, I think Givers Gain is incredibly unique in the business world, and I love that it’s there. Okay, well, how about what innovations or practices have been most impactful in keeping BNI relevant over the decades?

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve got a pretty short answer on this one, and that’s moving to online in 2020. It saved BNI. It saved many, many, many businesses that were in BNI. I had so many people come up to me after COVID and said, and they told me, BNI saved their business, and the ability to still do business while COVID was impacting people was amazing. And I mean, I think if COVID had hit in 1990 as opposed to 2020 it would have been completely different. BNI would have gone out of business. Hundreds of thousands of people would have gone out of business, and there would have been a lot more people who would die from it. And so I think we have really remained relevant by our integration of technology. And we now have 40 years of consecutive growth, year-on-year-on-year. Very few companies can say they’ve had 40 years of consecutive growth, but we have. Thanks.

Priscilla: Yeah. Okay, so now we’re going to move into a section on lessons learned. 

Ivan:
Yeah.


Priscilla:
And the first question is, what are the key lessons that you’ve learned as a leader during BNI’s journey?

Ivan:
So I think there’s a number of things. One of the first ones I learned was that ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice. I’m gonna repeat that: ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice. And when I was starting a BNI, I didn’t know how to network. I had no idea how to network. I was method-acting my way through the process and trying to figure out how do you do this? How do you do it right? What is effective? I realized pretty quickly that being transactional was not the way to do it, but you really had to be relational in the way you network. And so I was the poster child for ignorance on fire being better than knowledge on ice.

And you know, as BNI was starting, I couldn’t afford really expensive employees, and so I looked for people who were on fire, who were excited, who loved the concept of BNI and our philosophy of Givers Gain, and they were trainable. They were willing to be trained and taught. And I think that’s a lesson that is useful for all businesses, that sometimes ignorance on fire is better than knowledge on ice. Now the best is knowledge on fire, but you have to have a good payroll to be able to hire knowledge on fire. And that’s why you know, in a BNI chapter, if you have somebody who really doesn’t know how to network, but they’re willing to learn, that can make a great member. So you want to look for people who are willing to learn, even if they don’t have lots of experience in networking.

The second thing is stick to the fundamentals. Don’t reinvent the wheel. Oh my goodness, if I had to pick one thing that has been a problem since chapter number two, it would be that. And we now have over 11,300 chapters as of this day, and it has always been an issue. People want to reinvent the wheel. They start making up stuff, and go back to things that I did 20 years ago. “Hey, I have a great idea. Why don’t we do this?” And I did that 20 years ago – don’t do that, and here’s why. So stick to the fundamentals. Don’t reinvent the wheel. Don’t mess with the system. And if you really, really, really want to be successful in business, do six things a thousand times, not a thousand things six times. And so many people do a thousand things six times, and it doesn’t have to be six – it could be five, it could be seven. You want to do six or seven things a thousand times. And that’s one of the reasons why BNI works, is that we do it every week. We get together. It’s the same kind of system, different presentations, but the same system over and over and over again. And I would say the last thing… And it looks like we’re going to go into three parts [episodes] here.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
So the last thing would be work in your flame and not in your wax. And I know that in different podcasts over the years, I’ve mentioned these. I’m not sure I’ve put them all in one podcast, but I think one of the most important things is to work in your flame, and not in your wax. And by that, I mean when you’re in your flame, you’re on fire, you’re excited, you’re passionate about what you’re doing. People can see it in the way you behave. They can hear it in your voice. When you’re working in your wax, it just takes all your energy away. People can hear that in your voice, and they can see that in the way you behave. And your flame and your wax can change over time. You know, when I started BNI, I was King Arthur leading the charge, building the company for 30 years. About 10 years ago, I kind of stepped out of the role of King Arthur and moved into the role of Colonel Sanders…

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
Which is as the spokesperson for the organization, but that’s my flame now, and I love it. I love pouring into people and I love doing these podcasts and sharing information. I love when BNI members come up to me and say, “I listened to podcast number such and such, and I really learned something, and I’m able to put that to my business, and I really appreciate it.” And so these were some of the early lessons I learned and I’ve been applying them now for decades. And it looks, Priscilla, because I know there’s a few more questions, looks like we’re going to go into Part Three. Let me turn it back over for you today, unless you have any question for me, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s great. I think Part Three deserves its own podcast because it’s talking about the future.

Ivan:
Yeah, great, awesome.

Priscilla:
So that’s a little advertisement for the next podcast. All right, thank you so much for this, Dr Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Part 2 of this series answers questions about the evolution of BNI and shares lessons learned over the past 40 years.

title.rendered: Episode 908: Reflections on BNI’s Journey, Part 1

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI turns 40 this year, and in this first episode of a three-part series, Dr. Misner answers three questions about BNI’s history.

What inspired you to start BNI 40 years ago?

Necessity is the mother of invention. I’d just lost a big client and needed referrals, but all the networking organizations were either social hours or two transactional.

Can you share one or two defining moments that shaped BNI into what it is today?

  • Developing manuals for the leadership team and directors.
  • Creating the Board of Advisors.
  • Holding the first BNI Conference.
  • Launching BNI Connect.
  • Moving online during COVID.

What are you most proud of when you look back at BNI’s growth over the last 40 years?

In 2024 BNI generated over 25 billion–with a “B”–over 25 billion US Dollars in Thank You for Closed Business.

Stay tuned for more in Part 2 and Part 3.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 753: You’re Going to Need a Bigger Boat.
  • Listen to Episode 835: A Radical Shift to a Greater Success.
  • Listen to Episode 872: Ask Ivan about GDP and Morning Meetings.

Complete Transcript of Episode 908

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, it’s great to be on the BNI Podcast again, Priscilla; thanks for asking. This week, I am in Charlotte at the [BNI] Global Support Team headquarters in Charlotte, North Carolina. I just met yesterday with someone who was just on my podcast, Heidi – the editor for the next book, and Graham. He and I and Heidi are working on a book called Garage to Global. So we just met yesterday, and today is several meetings here at BNI. I love to walk around and meet all the employees and have a chance to talk to other [BNI] Board Members.

Priscilla:
Great. So explain what we’re doing today.

Ivan:
Well, I did an interview not long ago where they – the host, asked a number of questions that I thought were really valuable questions, and I wanted all the members who… We have so many people that listen to BNI Podcast. It’s just, it’s amazing to me. I travel the world, and people just come up to me and say, “I recognize your voice, hi.” Or my favorite is, “Hi, Ivan. How are you and where are you?”

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
I get it all the time. And so I thought it would be really good to do this as a podcast and do it the same way that the interviewer did it. And that is, I’m asking you, if you’d be kind enough to ask the questions, and then I’ll give an answer on sort of reflections on BNI’s journey over 40 years. The organization is 40 years old now. I started BNI when I was 28 years old. You can do the math. I’m now 68 as of the time of this recording, and I still love what I do, Priscilla. I still absolutely love what I do, because it’s things like this, where I get to pour into BNI members and talk about what I did right and what I did wrong. And what they can do right, so they can learn from my mistakes and be successful. So with that, you’ve got the questions. I’ll let you lead.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. All right, then. This first section is going to be about reflections on BNI’s journey. So our first question is, “What inspired you to start BNI 40 years ago?”

Ivan:
So I’ve talked about BNI’s Origin Story on a couple of podcasts, and I invite people to go back and listen to some of them, but I’ll see if I can give you a slightly different spin. One of the podcasts that, I don’t know the number, is the Brody moment. I think the Brody moment is such a great podcast that talks about when I realized that BNI was going to be really incredibly large, and that’s a great podcast.

But I think overall that BNI is a great example of necessity being the mother of invention. I needed referrals. And again, I have talked about this in some other podcasts. I absolutely needed referrals. And I put together some people who… Well, before I put together anybody, I went to a lot of networking organizations, many different networking organizations. And I found they were either too mercenary, where it was very transactional and everyone was trying to sell to me, or I felt that it was totally social, where there wasn’t any business being done. And the mercenary groups, I felt like I needed to come back and get a shower because I’d been slimed.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
And the social groups were just… it was happy hour and hors d’oeuvres, and I had better wine at home than they were serving at the social hours, I would call them. And so I didn’t like either. I wanted something that was focused on business, but not mercenary, and something that was relational, but not totally social. And so I put together that first group, the BNI group, and I… you know, I’d like to tell you I had this vision of an international organization, but I just needed some referrals because I had lost a big client.

And I’ve talked about that a bit in some other podcasts. The Brody moment, by the way, I looked it up as we were talking, is episode number 753, 7-5-3. If you have a chance and you haven’t listened to episode 753, go listen to it. Because that was the point at which I said, you know, I started BNI, I opened a number of chapters without me really trying, but when I had my Brody moment, which is the name of the sheriff from the movie Jaws, when I had that; I realized that I had created something that was going to be way bigger than I ever imagined. And it was at that point that I started to create a plan to scale the business, to turn it into something much, much larger.

So it was – necessity was the mother of invention that led me to create an organization that I felt was relational and business-oriented. And it caught on so well that we just opened one chapter after another after another, until I had my Brody moment, and then decided to go all in. And again, the Brody moment episode is number 753.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. Okay, great. So here’s another question. “Can you share one or two defining moments that shaped BNI into what it is today?”

Ivan:
There were a few things that happened along the way. One was me recognizing that education is a leaky bucket process. That when I taught somebody how to do something, information leaked out, and when they taught somebody else, more information leaked out, and when they taught somebody else, and you know, you’d have a half a bucket of information, and then people would start adding their own stuff. So I realized early on in the process of BNI, within the first year, that I needed manuals, so that at least we were all singing from the same song book, right? We were all playing from the same playbook, and that if they were going to get off track, it was because they weren’t using the playbook, but that the playbook had to be there. And so creating manuals for the Leadership Team and for the Directors, that was the first defining moment, I think, for the organization.

The creation of our Board of Advisors was a defining moment. And anyone who’s listening to this podcast, if you’re a [BNI] member, you may have a copy of Givers Gain – The BNI Story, but you can also get a PDF of it on [BNI] Business Builder, which is a free website for BNI members. Just log in and there’s a PDF on Business Builder, and I tell the whole story of how I created the Board of Advisors; back in 1986 I created it. Today, it’s the International Board of Advisors, and they have been unbelievably beneficial to the organization in helping me build, especially in the early days. That was a defining moment for me.

1990 was a defining moment in that it was our first Conference, the first time we brought everyone together. And any guesses how many people we had at our first conference, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Let’s see, 60?

Ivan:
Twelve.

Priscilla:
How many?

Ivan:
Twelve, it was twelve. That was our first BNI Conference, and two of those people were me and my late wife. So there were ten Directors, myself, and my late wife were there. And, you know, maybe twelve and a half, because you could count one of my children [who] came in and out a lot; it was much more casual than today. But the idea of Conferences was a defining moment. And now our Conferences, we have them all over the world in different countries, and then we have a [Global] Convention every year where thousands of people come. Listen, if you’re listening to this podcast, and you have a chance to go to a [BNI] international convention, go. It will blow your mind.

It is incredible to walk in and see the flags of all the countries from all over the world. And it’s like going to a United Nations event where all the countries love each other. And it’s just fantastic that the different languages, the different accents, presentations from different people, all of the translators that are there, although more and more we’re finding technology is a pretty decent way to translate, and that’s improving. So these were all defining moments.

BNI Connect was a massive defining moment, and I’ve talked about it in episode number 835, 8-3-5. BNI Connect – the story of it is embedded in the book The 3rd Paradigm. And listen to episode 835, and then pick up a copy of The 3rd Paradigm. BNI Connect was a game changer. We were, prior to BNI Connect, doing business worldwide by paper, and we’re talking 2011 and earlier. And in 2011 we started to transition into BNI Connect, which was a complete game changer for us as an organization with over 75 countries and hundreds of thousands of members. So those were some of the defining moments of the organization.

And of course, how can I forget when COVID hit. I had talked in 2019 about the future of networking, including online. And people thought I was crazy. And I’m pretty sure, Priscilla, I heard somebody say in a corner, “He’s getting old, I think he’s losing it.”

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
But it’s like, “No, no, really. Technology is going to be a game changer.” Well, who would have thought that a year later, you know, we would be completely online because of COVID. We transitioned all of our chapters from in-person to online within just months worldwide. So those were some of the defining moments of the organization.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds good. Well, let me ask you another one here. “What are you most proud of when you look back at BNI’s growth over the last 40 years?”

Ivan:
Yeah, we’re running out of time. So let me be brief, as brief as I can. I am humbled by what BNI has become. I am truly humbled by how much it has grown, how many members that we have worldwide, what this organization has done. But the thing that has surprised me the most, and that I am the most proud of, is that last year, this is being recorded in 2025, in 2024 BNI generated over 25 billion with a “B”, over 25 billion US Dollars in Thank You for Closed Business. Thank You for Closed Business is the amount of business that our members have generated for themselves, and that, to me, is just mind-boggling.

I never would have thought that we would generate tens of billions of dollars in business. And in episode number 872, I talk about the GDP. And I talk about, according to the International Monetary Fund, Gross Domestic Product measures the amount of business generated in a country. Well, if you take a look at the United Nations estimates of GDP, [the] UN estimates of GDP, there are actually 100 countries in the world, in the world, that generated a lower GDP than what BNI did in 2024 in Thank You for Closed Business for its members.

Priscilla:
Is that just in one year?

Ivan:
That’s in ONE year!

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
In one year, BNI did over $25 billion. And Thank You for Closed Business measures… because I talk about this in the podcast because someone said, “Well, GDP is different than Thank You for Closed Business.” Yeah, GDP is about a country. Thank You for Closed Business is about a company but Thank You for Closed Business measures the amount of business generated for BNI members. And we need to embrace that analogy and celebrate the success that BNI has helped so hundreds of thousands of businesspeople all around the world, and so I think that’s what I’m most proud of. And we’re out of time, and we’ll cover the rest in Part Two of this, Priscilla. Thanks.

Priscilla:
Yes, definitely. I just wanted to throw that in there. Okay, thank you, Ivan, that was great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 907: GAINS Profile Updated

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The GAINS Profile is the basis of productive One-to-One meetings, and more One-to-Ones lead to more referrals. At your first One-to-One with another BNI member, you should take turns sharing your Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills. If you don’t get all the way through, continue at your next One-to-One with that member. Review any changes to your profile at future One-to-Ones with that member.

You can complete your profile on the BNI Connect app or download the PDF template below.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 5: The GAINS Exchange.
  • Listen to Episode 570: More 1-2-1s = More Referrals.
  • Download the GAINS Exchange Profile to complete on your computer.

Complete Transcript of Episode 907

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I get to have a few weeks here at my home office in Austin, Texas. I just love being home. People ask me, where’s my favorite place in the world? And I tell them it’s home.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Yeah.

Ivan:
So today we’re going to talk about the GAINS exchange. It’s also known as the GAINS profile online on BNI Connect. It’s called the GAINS profile. I first did it in a book that’s out of print now called Business by Referral, but you can find it in a number of my networking books, and certainly it’s a big part of BNI.

Ironically, the first time I ever talked about this, and really focused on this subject on a podcast, was episode number 005, Episode 5. Yeah, that’s a long time ago. I think I started these in 2007, so that would have been in 2007. And so I thought it was about time to update a couple of things with the GAINS profile.

And again, it’s… originally it was called the GAINS exchange because you exchanged profiles. So either term is fine, but the profile is about that acronym and GAINS stands for Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills. And if you go back to Episode Five, you’ll see the story of when I tested it. And I tested it in a chapter to see how it [would] go over, where I had one member, you know, share with the other member their Goals, Accomplishments, Interests, Networks, and Skills, and then the other member shared it with the first person.

And it was a couple of gentlemen that really didn’t want to do it, but they only got halfway through and found out they were both coaches for their sons’ soccer teams, which is called football in many countries. And that was it. They never finished the profile because they made such a great connection that the rest of their discussion was about the sport.

And what’s funny is they had never given each other a referral. They’d been in the group together for many months, never given each other a referral, but all of a sudden, after they made this strong connection, they really looked out for each other, and within just a few months, they were giving each other referrals. And it hit me just how powerful this is, because it’s about finding overlapping areas of interest. And so we’re going to include a PDF of the GAINS exchange profile in this podcast, but let me review with you what you want to do with each one.

First, you want to start with your Goals. Your goals are business or personal objectives that you want or need to meet for yourself or the people who are important to you. You need to define your goals. You need to have a clear picture of those goals, and you want to talk about how you want to build relationships with people to help achieve those goals. So talk about your goals, business and personal. It’s when you learn about someone’s personal interests, you really are able to make a better connection with that person, particularly if you’re talking about family, children, spouse, something like that, that’s very powerful.

The second is Accomplishments. And here, you know, it to some people, feels like a little bit of bragging. It’s okay, these are your friends; these are your associates in BNI. Sometimes people are really shocked at, you know, certain accomplishments that someone may have. Here you want to talk about the things that you’re proud of. Remember that some of your best insight into others comes from knowing what goals they’ve already achieved. So talking about your knowledge, your skill, your experiences, your values, are really important. So when I’m talking to people who don’t know me well, I talk about my interest in fine art and my interest in astronomy – astronomy, not astrology, my interest in astronomy and wines. I studied to be a sommelier, so I these are things that I love and talk about, and they overlap into. Is with the accomplishments you also want to talk about, you know, the things that you’ve actually achieved, or maybe your degrees, maybe awards that you’ve been given, a particular skill set that you may have.

Interests are next. Here you want to talk about, you know, if you’d like to play sports or read books, listen to music. For me, I have a boat. I love to go out on my boat on Lake Austin. I have a walking trail on my property, it’s a quarter mile walking trail. I go out there almost every day, and I love to do that. And as I mentioned, I love wine and astronomy. So these are interests of mine. And when you find overlapping areas of interest, and this is where the two men really connected was in their overlapping areas of interest of coaching a sport, that’s when you make a connection with someone.

And then Networks you want to talk about the networks that you belong to, both formal and informal. It can be an organization, an institution, a company, or some organization that you’ve been active in the past or for a long, long time, networks that you’re in. And you’ll be surprised to find out that certain people have also been in those organizations. For me, I was a Rotarian for 16 years. I was also, and this may surprise you a little bit, Priscilla, I was also a member of NAWBO for several years, which is the National Association of Women Business Owners. I was a member. I had a partner in a business years ago who was a woman but I was the member, and they allowed men to be members in the organization. And so I think it was like 5% of the organization were men, but I was in NAWBO. And so it’s always interesting when other people are in it, and you have a talking point. And then…. what’s that?

Priscilla:
No, I just was, you know, affirming, yes.

Ivan:
Yeah, it is. It’s interesting, and people are always surprised when they find out I was a member of NAWBO. Skills. The more you know about the talents and abilities of the people in your network, the better equipped you are to find and refer competent, affordable products and services that need to arise.

And so this is the GAINS profile, or the GAINS exchange. Now, I used to say, and I probably said it in the podcast from Podcast 005, one person should go through all five [parts of the GAINS [exchange], and then let the next person go through the five. Let’s say just for argument’s sake, [you’re] meeting for an hour so you have 30 minutes each. I no longer believe that. That is a bad idea, and I’ll tell you why. And I think I suggested it in Episode 005. It’s a bad idea, because what tends to happen is, if one person goes first through all five, they end up taking 40-45, minutes of the allotted hour. The person doesn’t have enough time to cover their GAINS exchange, their GAINS profile, and that’s a problem, that’s a mistake.

And so I now recommend that you go one at a time with each. So Priscilla, you would talk to me about your Goals, and then I would talk to you about my Goals. And then you would talk to me about your Accomplishments, and then I would go next; and then your Interests, and then I would go next. Your Networks, and I would go next. Then your Skills, and I would go next. That’s what I recommend now, otherwise, what happens is one person inevitably gets the majority of time. And I heard that over and over and over again. So one of the reasons I wanted to redo this podcast was to make this suggestion, because over many years, we’ve found that that was a problem in the way it was done.

Here’s another point on the GAINS profile that I think is critical, and that’s episode number 570. And 570 is “More 1-2-1s = More Referrals”. And I probably talk about this… I would say it’s in my top five or six topics that I talk about when I do live presentations, when people ask [during] Q&A, “How do I how do I increase the number of referrals that I get?” And this episode is probably in my top five or six topics that I address at some point when I’m doing an Ask the Founder session live somewhere, because it’s so incredibly powerful. And it shows, it’s a research study, a university study that was done in Europe in a region of BNI, and it shows very clearly that people… it compares people who do one 1-2-1 [meeting] a month, two a month, three a month, four a month. So if you look at the people who do one 1-2-1 a month, versus those who do four or more 1-2-1s a month, or basically one a week – so one a month versus one a week. The amazing thing is, and this is episode number 570, the amazing thing is, the people who do one a week instead of one a month give almost exactly 100% more referrals.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
That’s amazing, but that’s not the most amazing part. The most amazing part is they receive almost exactly 100% more referrals, almost double. So the thing I tell people when I’m doing live presentations is, you know, who wouldn’t like to get twice as many referrals? We all would, right? Who wants twice as many referrals? That everybody’s hand would go up and I’d say, “All you have to do is do one quality 1-2-1 a week, one a week, and the GAINS profile is a great format to do that with.”

Now, if you’re doing a GAINS profile, let’s say you’ve done a 1-2-1 with somebody before, and now it’s your second or third time. You may not have to do the GAINS profile, or you might just review it. Hey, this is what I had before. I would add this to it. And then what you really want to focus on is, how can I help you? How can I give you referrals? Here’s how you can give me referrals. It’s got to be reciprocal, but 1-2-1s are the single most powerful way to generate referrals in a Chapter, other than possibly doing a great Weekly Presentation. I would put them kind of head-to-head, but we have hard data on the One-to-Ones, so check out episode 570, and take the PDF that’s here on the GAINS profile, the GAINS exchange, and start using it with, certainly with BNI members and potentially with other people as well. Any thoughts before we wrap up, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No, I think it’s a great idea, and people should use the GAINS profile, you know, as often as they can, because it really, really lays everything out beautifully.

Ivan:
Yeah, it does. And, you know, it does both relational topics as well as sort of transactional kinds of topics, like Accomplishments – very transactional. Interests, though, are very relational. Networks are kind of transactional. You know, you’re talking about what groups are you in and what groups you’re in. Skills? You know, finding overlapping areas of skill and ability is also good, and overlapping Goals is good. So it has this really interesting mix of both transactional and relational, which goes back to the gender book that I did years ago. And so to me, this works both very, very well with men and women who are trying to network, because it covers both sides of that, particularly the relational piece. I think we’re out of time. Priscilla. That’s all I have for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was great, Ivan. Thank you so much. That’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
