title.rendered: Episode 906: You Have a Book Idea. Now What?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Heidi Scott Giusto from Career Path Writing Solutions explains how to know when your book idea is worth pursuing and what you need to do to get it written and published. Before you go any further, ask yourself:

  • Is there a business case for the book?
  • Who is your target audience?
  • Do you want to self-publish, find a traditional publisher, or take a hybrid approach?

Remember: writing a book is a marathon. Don’t give up at mile 25. It doesn’t matter how good your book is if no one ever reads it.

Be sure to check out Heidi’s website at www.careerpathwritingsolutions.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 906

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas today, and I have a guest who I’ve known for quite a few years. We haven’t actually talked about how many years, but it’s been a long time. Her name is Dr Heidi Scott-Giusto, and you’re going to look at the spelling and go, what it can’t be it, but that’s it. That’s how it’s pronounced. And Heidi has helped me edit, I don’t know quite a few books over the years, and was the co-author of one of my books. Heidi is a writer, editor, Communications Consultant. She owns Career Path Writing Solutions, which is a consulting firm that helps people communicate when it matters most. She collaborates with clients from diverse professional backgrounds, and has a specialty on working on business leadership and self-help books. She earned her PhD from Duke University and recently was a co-author with me and Dawa Phillips on The 3rd Paradigm, which I’ve done a couple of podcasts on The 3rd Paradigm that Heidi helped with. And she is working on a future book with us — us being Graham Weihmiller, the past CEO of BNI, and Heidi and myself. Heidi, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Heidi: Thank you so much for having me. I am thrilled to be here and to talk about this topic.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s a great topic. And if you’ve looked at any of the podcasts, you know this goes by in record speed, about 10 minutes. But let’s see if we can tackle all of the things that you want to talk about. So let’s say somebody decides they want to write a book, which I think is like the best way to create a brochure for your business. What are some of the considerations after you have a book idea for any of the BNI members listening?

Heidi:
Yeah. As you know, there’s a long list of these. But my top three considerations – number one is being really pragmatic right from the get go. Because sometimes people will say things like, “Oh so-and-so said I should write a book.” And I would always ask, “Is there a business case for your book?” You really need to have a purpose and clear reason for writing it, because rarely are you going to make a significant amount of money, you know, from the book itself.

Ivan:
Yep.

Heidi:
So you want to get clear on how the book is going to serve your business and goals and really, you know – why? Maybe writing a book… you and I worked on a book called Infinite Legacy. Maybe it’s part of your legacy project. Then you want to acknowledge that and then determine, you know, who do you want to have access to the book and what ideas. But just those really pragmatic considerations first, I think people should consider before they really dive in.

Ivan:
Yeah, you make a good point that you’re not going to make a lot of money in writing a book. Look some people’s books sell millions of copies and they make a lot of money. But the average business book, the last time I checked, and maybe you have more current information, but the last time I checked, the average business book sells about 7000 copies. That’s average.

Heidi:
Yeah, and I don’t have those numbers right off the top of my head, but that’s even a little more than I would have expected.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s an old statistic, and with people publishing books so much online now, that may be a high number, but it counts all of the you know, huge, big best sellers, along with the ones that sell, you know, 100 copies, like I think my first book did.

Heidi: (laughing)

Ivan:
So, yeah, you have to have a business case. That’s the first one. What’s your second point?

Heidi:
The audience – identify the target audience before you start writing. And you want to keep that reader in mind the entire writing and revising process, even as you consider the format. You know, do you want a traditional book? Maybe you want a workbook. Always think what will be most helpful and beneficial to that intended reader and work backward from that.

Ivan:
Yeah, and can you have more than one target audience? I know we’re talking about having a couple of target audiences with our next book.

Heidi:
I think you… I’d say you can, but they’re still going to be related, right? The book can’t be for everybody.

Ivan:
So, you know, sometimes a self-help book can be for a very broad audience.

Heidi:
That’s true, but I would say even then you are keeping in mind, someone reading this has bought into the idea that I can improve myself or help you know, so even that, there’s still some unifying theme around who you’re writing for.

Ivan:
Yeah, fair enough. Fair enough. You have a third point.

Heidi:
Yes, and you might be able to speak more to this even than I can. Publishing. Publishing is a mystery for so many people. So even when you have that idea, you’ve established your business case, you’ve identified your audience, you want to learn the basics of the differences between traditional, self-publishing, and hybrid publishing. I have had authors contact me about how to publish their book with a traditional publisher, and they’ve done so after they wrote and self-published the book on Amazon.

And those conversations often end up in disappointment for that author, because I am the one having to tell them that only very rarely is a publisher going to pick up a book that has already been published. Like some of the more well-known ones are, like The Tale of Peter Rabbit, a classic, and more recently, like the Fifty Shades of Grey series and The Martian. But those are very rare.

Ivan:
So those were self-published first and then went to a primary publisher.

Heidi:
Yeah, they became such hits, and I want to say the Harry Potter series may have been as well. They became such huge hits that then the publisher came on. And I think sometimes those get more attention, and so people don’t realize that they are really the outlier, they’re not the norm.

Ivan:
I would say an extreme outlier. It’s very rare that a publisher is going to pick up a book that’s been self-published already.

Heidi:
Yeah. And so that is just an unfortunate situation all around, because you have people who put all this effort into it and they didn’t realize that they are uninformed or misinformed of what a typical process is, if you want to go that route of traditional publishing.

Ivan:
Yeah, now you mentioned traditional publishing and hybrid. For those of you who don’t understand the difference traditional publishers, a publisher like Simon and Schuster, Entrepreneur Press published The 3rd Paradigm; they are a traditional publishing house.

A hybrid – and more and more traditional publishing houses are also doing hybrid. A hybrid is you’re basically paying for the book, and they’re doing all the distribution, and you get a larger percentage, but you also have to pony up a fair amount of money in a hybrid book.

The third option is total self-publishing. And the best, I believe the best approach for self-publishing is Amazon’s KDP. If you’re going to self-publish, that’s a great place to do it. So those are the three that are out there.

And if you’re going to go with a traditional publisher, be patient. I had 40 rejections, over 40, 4-0 rejections. And it was funny, because a few years ago I found the sheet of paper. It was before Excel, and I literally drew out on a graphic sheet of paper, a graph of every publisher I sent it to and when I got the rejection letter. And I had over 40 rejections before I was finally able to publish The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret, which has now sold over 100,000 copies and has been translated into maybe a dozen languages. So be patient if you’re going traditional publishing. Hybrid is a little quicker and a little easier, but it takes a lot of money, and self-publishing is pretty inexpensive through Amazon.

Heidi:
Yeah, but you have to have a marketing plan and your audience already in place, really for any of these, but especially with the self-publishing route as well. And you said you mentioned, I think, wasn’t it Dr Seuss who got rejected maybe close to 50 times.

Ivan:
Yeah. Dr Seuss and Jack Canfield, with Chicken Soup for the Soul, got rejected many, many, many times, and that series has sold three quarters of a billion, that’s a billion with a B – three quarters of a billion copies. Now the series has.

Heidi:
Yeah. So just persevere, right? You can’t, you know, it might not happen in the first try, or even the first couple dozen tries.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. Yeah, if you’re going to go self… it’s never been easier, though, to publish a book through something like Amazon and KDP. Even hybrid is easy, but it does take money. People ask me, How do I get almost all of my books to the best sellers list? And my answer is, it’s easy. What’s hard is getting a half a million followers on your social media. Ah, so if you have a good social media following, you can push a book to a best sellers list. If you don’t, don’t expect it to be a best seller. That’s really, really rare. You’ve got probably a better chance of winning the lottery than pushing a book to the best seller without a group of followers who are there to show interest in what you are doing.

Heidi:
Yeah.

Ivan:
We’re almost out of time. What are some of the real common roadblocks that an author faces?

Heidi:
Speaking of running out of time – finding time. So everyone probably who’s listening to this podcast right now would probably classify themselves within the category of busy professional. So if you’re serious about writing a book, then you need to diligently block time in your calendar, like your weekly calendar. Treat it as sacred. Treat it like a doctor’s appointment or a client appointment that you’d never just not show up to a doctor or for a client meeting. So you have to show up for that blocked time. And/or engage in writing retreats, which would be longer blocks of time for the writing. Some people will sequester themselves away for a few days or a week and do a lot of drafting in that really like sprint time. You have to get rid of the idea that you’re never… like you won’t find the right time. You really have to make it.

Ivan:
I totally agree. My Wednesdays are writing days, and I try not to let anything interfere with that, other than maybe an interview, a media interview. Otherwise, it’s time for me to write or research. We’ve done the writing retreat a couple of times now, and I find those writing retreats, especially with co-authors, a great opportunity to share ideas and to come up with some new concepts for the book. Listen, we’re way out of time. Do you have any last thought before we wrap up?

Heidi:
I would just say if you get to the point where you’re almost there, writing a book is a marathon. Don’t give up on Mile 25; don’t de-prioritize it. Because I’ve seen a lot of people, they’re so close, and then they de- prioritize it, and the book either never gets published, or it gets published three years later, and you know, that’s just not an ideal situation for many reasons. So keep that in mind.

Ivan:
And have the book edited. In some of your notes to me, you said one person had a great manuscript almost ready to go in the 20 years you’ve been editing.

Heidi:
Yeah, and it’s still, to my knowledge, isn’t finished, and it’s a great book. It is a great book. There’s a good case for it. But life gets in the way sometimes, and you’ve got to try to avoid that.

Ivan:
They’re going to shut our microphone off here in a second. So let me give your website out, Heidi. It’s careerpathwritingsolutions.com. That’s www.careerpathwritingsolutions.com. I thank for everything you have done to help my books be better, and I look forward to doing the next book with you, which is Garage to Global. And everyone will hear more about that over time. Over to you. Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. I just have one little thing to say, which is that my studio, Live Oak Studio, we specialize now in recording audio books. That’s what we do exclusively. So if you have any of those great authors out there that are thinking of another way to promote themselves, think about an audiobook.

Ivan:
What a great idea, and having it done professionally really makes a difference. No doubt.

Priscilla:
Yes, definitely. I just wanted to throw that in there.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Heidi Scott Giusto explains how to know when your book idea is worth pursuing and what you need to do to get it written and published.

title.rendered: Episode 905: Applying Givers Gain to Business (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

How and why do you apply BNI’s “Givers Gain” philosophy to business? Dr. Misner shares answers from his book Infinite Giving.

Why We Give

  1. We give because we understand that in our community, what we do, others will do, and we will all benefit, particularly in a BNI group.
  2. We give because we know that in the same situation, others would do the same for us.       
  3. We give because we want to give back where we have profited before.        
  4. We give because when we work together, we get bigger and better results than working on our own, and we’ve really seen that in BNI.    
  5. We give because we enjoy it.

How to Give

  1. Give a referral opportunity between two other businesses you know – maybe a supplier of yours and a client of yours.
  2. Give mentoring to a business that needs your expertise.
  3. Give your knowledge to local business groups.
  4. Give to local education and youth community groups.
  5. Give to elders in the community.

Practice Givers Gain in a way that’s right for your business. Once you make a commitment to using the power of this philosophy, it will fuel your business, and success will follow.

Complete Transcript of Episode 702/905

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve been traveling a lot via Zoom. And I’ve done several IFA presentations, which is the International Franchise Association. And I also spoke to cityCURRENT in Nashville, Tennessee. So still out there meeting lots of members and potential members out in the field.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. What are you going to teach us today about Givers Gain?

Ivan:
So today, I’m going to be talking about applying Givers Gain to your business. And this is based on content from the book Infinite Giving, which I’ve had both of my co-authors on, which is a great little book. I highly recommend it, if you have a chance pick it up; it’s about the Seven Laws of Givers Gain. And you know, I really believe that Givers Gain is not only a great way to get business; it’s an even better way to do business. And this is why BNI has made it our principle core value in our organization.

Giving has many cultural and legal differences around the world. In some cultures, giving is actually seen negatively, yet, it’s a positive force in society and this can be applied in every business regardless of location or culture, I believe. What’s important in acts of giving is our motivation. And here are some motivations that I think fit the philosophy of Givers Gain and help us maintain mental clarity around what our motivations should be.

  1. We give because we understand that in our community, what we do, others will do, and we will all benefit, particularly in a BNI group.
  2. We give because we know that in the same situation, others would do the same for us.       
  3. We give because we want to give back where we have profited before.        
  4. We give because when we work together, we get bigger and better results than working on our own, and we’ve really seen that in BNI.    
  5. We give because we enjoy it. I know I do. I love giving referrals, I feel great when I give a referral.

Having an ethical motivation is, I think, the key to giving. You can always give once and justify your motivation, but giving over and over again, I think, requires a motivation that’s understood not only by you, but also by the people that you surround yourself with. And adopting one or more of the motivations that I talked about above, or before, will help you stay grounded in your motivation. Here are some examples of where giving in business, I think, has really changed lives.

A member I know named Allen is a commercial real estate agent in Orange County. And throughout 2009 and 2010, Allen had met with as many business people as he possibly could doing one-to-ones and using his network to find these people and book a meeting with them. And they were not just from his local BNI group, but they were from members throughout the entire county. And the more people he met, the more he was able to become a super connector for the local business community. And he was generating a ton of business for the local economy simply by making connections and giving the gift of a referral. He even won awards for it and became the star in his group. He was recognized as having the most one-to-ones. He gave out the most referrals and he focused on building relationships. And once he was convinced that this was effective, he found that it was a good business opportunity for him as well, because he ended up getting a lot of business.

When I asked him about his activity, he told me that he was completing his 26th year in business and it was the best year he ever had. And he credited giving so many referrals as the reason for him getting so much business. Giving in business as in life, I think, works in a lot of different ways.

Here’s another example. David, another BNI member in the UK, runs a fantastic print business and he offers brilliant service, great value, and he delivers when he says he will, if not earlier. And if you ever tried to pay him a compliment, he’ll sort of give a sheepish smile and an explanation that he’s only “doing his job”. But just up the road from where his business is based, is the area’s largest independent hotel, which of course, he’s a trusted supplier for. And after turning around an order on a particularly short deadline, the hotel manager, showing gratitude, offered David a meal for two at the hotel’s restaurant as a way of saying thank you.

So David thanked his client and graciously declined the offer, and instead asked for something else. And that was for them to take the call of one of his other clients, which was a commercial designer and project manager by the name of Suzanne. Suzanne had recently asked for help from David, being a supplier of hers, and decided to use that opportunity to get an introduction to the hotel, which was what she needed. The introduction was made, business was done, and both of David’s clients were happy.

And in the true spirit of giving, the additional revenue has allowed Suzanne to take on larger development projects, which David now prints for. So it just goes round and round and round. And the local sub-contractors who are used on these projects have increased revenue, and now come to David when they’re in need of his products. And due to the quality of his work and products, they become ambassadors and referrers for his business.

David is really very selfless, humble, a wonderful networker because he embraces the principles of Givers Gain, and the cycle of giving continues to benefit him, and those around him, particularly his other BNI members. I believe that acts of giving change lives.

Acts of giving can be powerful in business, regardless of the size of business. David’s a great example of that. And we can give in many ways, and we can give many things. Here’s a list of some of the types of giving that I think makes a real impact in the business community.

  1. Give a referral opportunity between two other businesses you know – maybe a supplier of yours and a client of yours, like David did.
  2. Give mentoring to a business that needs your expertise.
  3. Give your knowledge to local business groups.
  4. Give to local education and youth community groups. I just, Priscilla, helped a young lady 17-years old, part of a Boys and Girls Club, in doing a speech contest. And I know a little bit about speaking publicly. And I helped her come in as one of the top two positions in her speech contest, and it was really gratifying to see how far this young lady came from where she started to where she ended up in terms of doing her presentation.
  5. Give to elders in the community.

I think all of this kind of giving activity as part of an eco-system that will come back and benefit you and those you care about in a really positive way. You choose how to practice Givers Gain that’s right for your business. And once you make a commitment to using the power of this philosophy, I believe it will fuel your business, and success will follow. I have seen it over and over again throughout BNI’s history. The more this happens, the more energy can be given to important global social concerns. And I think it’s all part of the infinite giving that we talk about in the book Infinite Giving. And it allows us to focus on a bigger picture, and the bigger picture is really living in a world of plenty, where resources are plentiful, and the struggle for life is not against each other rather, it’s against scarcity. And it’s about supporting each other. And to me, that’s what Givers Gain is all about. That’s my message for today. Priscilla, anything you want to add?

Priscilla:
That was great, Ivan. The one thing I thought of is, you know, the fact that Givers Gain is one of the guiding principles of BNI, I think that that makes it an organization where people want to stay for long periods of time. They don’t just come in, you know, take what they can get and leave. They really, you know, like I’ve been in BNI maybe 14 years or more, and there’s many more in my group that have been in BNI for long periods of time. And I think it’s because of Givers Gain, it’s just such a beautiful way to do business.

Ivan:
You know, there’s a handful of things that an entrepreneur does that you look back on and say wow, that was really good idea, and this was certainly one of them, for me. Coming up with that as our philosophy, our principle core value, I think, was a real game changer because there’s so many networks out there that are incredibly mercenary. And you know, I would go to those and felt like I’d been slimed and needed to get a shower because they were always selling to me. And then there were those that were just incredibly social, and no business was being done, and I didn’t like those. And I wanted something that was a focus on business and yet also it was relational but not totally social. And the glue that I felt that would hold it together is this core value of Givers Gain; and it really has kept it together. And I urge people – pick up a copy of Infinite Giving. It’s a quick read, and it really will give you all of the Seven Principles of what makes up the philosophy of Givers Gain.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
That’s it for today. Priscilla, thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much. That was really lovely. Well, we do have a new sponsor for our podcast, and it’s www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with tools and inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. Whether you are a new or a seasoned member, these audio programs will help you unlock the power of networking. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and also use the promo code Ivan50 and get 50% off of all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Once you make a commitment to using the power of Givers Gain, it will fuel your business, and success will follow.

title.rendered: Episode 904: Why BNI Has a Code of Ethics

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Misner explains why BNI’s Code of Ethics has 6 points, instead of the initial 5, and how the sixth, “I will live up to the ethical standards of my profession,” came to be.

The first five items in the Code of Ethics were added in 1986, not long after BNI was founded, and apply to all types of businesses and professions.

  1. I will provide the quality of service at the prices that I have quoted.
  2. I will be truthful with the members and their referrals.
  3. I will build goodwill and trust among members and their referrals.
  4. I will take responsibility for following up on the referrals I receive.
  5. I will display a positive and supportive attitude.

Item 6 was added because certain professions, like law and medicine, have their own ethical codes, and those have stricter requirements than BNI. In those cases, members need to follow both.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 285: BNI’s Code of Ethics
  • Listen to Episode 481: Referrals for the Difficult to Refer Business.

Complete Transcript of Episode 904

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am happy to say I am at the US and Canadian National Conference for BNI in Houston, Texas. And I love, I love BNI conferences and having the two countries get together to do their National Conference is always exciting.

Priscilla:
Oh, nice. That sounds like fun.

Ivan:
It is fun. Conferences and conventions are a blast; it always amazes me to see how this organization has grown over the years.

Priscilla:
Uh huh, it really has.

Ivan:
Yep. So today I’m going to be talking about BNI’s Code of Ethics, and why we have a Code of Ethics. For those of you listening, you can go back to episode number 285, #285, where I did talk about BNI’s Code of Ethics there. But I’m going to give some information that I didn’t share in that podcast back in 2012, that’s when I did that podcast, and I’m gonna give some information that I didn’t share there.

And the reason I’m doing this, it was actually recommended by my assistant, Dana Coyle, who was on a webinar that I did, and somebody asked me a question. And they said, “Why do we have the last item of the Code of Ethics, ‘I will live up to the ethical standards of my profession.’ Why do we have that?” And he said, “I’m a car salesman. We don’t have an industry-wide Code of Ethics. So why do you have that?” And so I want to answer that question specifically, which I didn’t do in 285 but before I do, I want to give a little bit of context first, starting with all of the six items in the Code of Ethics.

Number one is I will provide the quality of service at the prices that I have quoted. Two is, I will be truthful with the members and their referrals. Three, I will build goodwill and trust among members and their referrals. Four, I will take responsibility for following up on the referrals I receive. Five, I will display a positive and supportive attitude. And of course, six, I will live up to the ethical standards of my profession.

The first five were created back in 1986 with the Board of Advisors, and that was back when it was literally just the Board of Advisors, not the International Board of Advisors. We only had chapters, pretty much, in Southern California, and we had talked about the fact that we needed some kind of Code of Ethics. And so I sat down with the Board and literally around the dining room table of my home in La Verne, where I lived at the time and where I started BNI, we sat around the dining room table, and we started talking about what the Code of Ethics should be, because we needed something to hold people accountable for their performance as a business professional in BNI. And Priscilla, we were like into page two of all of the specifics of how people should behave in a BNI chapter. And one of the members is like, this is too much. Nobody’s going to ever read this. It’s just too much content. You know, it’s starting to feel like, you know, a tax code, U.S. tax code, instead of a Code of Ethics.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And we pretty much just, I mean, imagine a chess board. We just, kind of, you know, swiped the whole board across and wiped everything out and started over. And our idea was to just come up with a handful of general considerations to follow an ethical code and that’s where we came up with the first five. Good quality service, be truthful, Goodwill. That was an important one. And so many members over the years have actually been asked to step down because they just didn’t build goodwill in in the chapter or and trust.

Of course, following up on referrals. Oh my goodness. How could you get a referral and not follow up? But even in 1986 we’d seen that happen, and we’re like, No, you can’t do that. You got to follow up. And display a positive and supportive attitude, that was really important. So I mean, isn’t it amazing? It’s been almost 40 years since the Code of Ethics was done, yet those still apply just as strongly today as they did – in the 70 plus countries that we’re in.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
They still apply. Number six was added later. I don’t recall what year, but it would have been in the probably the late 90s or early 2000s and it came about because of certain professions, like medical professions, that had, they have a code where they can’t share their clients. They can’t say anything about who their clients are. But in particular, it was the bar associations in Northern California, the legal associations, who started to tell their attorney members that they could not be a member of BNI because there was nothing in there about not sharing their client database and confidentiality, you know, wasn’t in our Code of Ethics. And so not all professions have that requirement.

So we negotiated with, literally negotiated with, various bar associations to come up with number six, which is, “I will live up to the ethical standards of my profession.” And then in most of the manuals that members see, there’s a note at the end that says, “Professional standards outlined in a formal code of conduct for any profession supersede the above standards.” And we put that in, also at the request of a number of bar associations, to say, hey, look, if you’re an attorney, your Code of Ethics supersedes ours. Because generally speaking, professional codes tend to be more particular than BNI’s Code of Ethics. And so number six in that note at the end came about years later, because of various medical professions and legal professions.

And the Code of Ethics has been one of the things that we’re, I think, really proud of, and it’s also a great tool to use to have conversations with members when, you know, they may be bringing in referrals, but are they building goodwill? Do they have a positive attitude? You know, I’ve seen chapters where they sat down and like, you know you’re a good member, but… I remember, and I think I’ve said this in a previous podcast, Priscilla, we had one member, a woman who said to me, “We’ve got this guy in our chapter where it’s like, the best thing he can ever do is call in sick, because when he’s not there, we have a great meeting.” And she said, “He is a ‘fun sucker’. He just sucks the fun right out of a room,” and so, you know, that’s when I told them, “Look, you need to sit down with him and have a conversation about his attitude. And he needs to come in with a positive attitude.” So the Code of Ethics was really thought through thoroughly, and was multiple meetings of the Board of Advisors, many, many hours spent, and probably just as many hours combined for item number six in the Code of Ethics. So what are your what are your thoughts on all of that?

Priscilla:
Let me ask you — I think the number six applies a lot to like therapists.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
That’s what I always thought it was for. You know, we’ve had some therapists in our chapter, and they don’t get a lot of referrals, and I think that was mainly because it’s very private. There’s not a lot you can say about it, you know?

Ivan:
Well, I mean, I do see therapists that get referrals, but you just can’t give the name of the person…

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
…that you’re referring.

Priscilla:
Right.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
But there are a number of other ways that therapists can get referrals, and I think I’ve done a podcast on it, I’ll have to look – Referrals for the difficult to refer business. And I know I did an article on this years ago, and I bet I’ve done a podcast on it. I’m doing a search as we talk right now. Yeah, it’s episode number 481, Episode 481: Referrals for the Difficult to Refer Business. And I wrote this article and did this podcast because of therapists and because of the business that I was in when I started BNI, which was business consultant. And it was hard for people, you know, way before coaches, business coaches, I mean 1985 — way before business coaches. And so people didn’t know how to refer me. I was a difficult to refer business.

And therapists were even more difficult to refer, in my opinion. And so go listen to episode 481, and one of the things that I talk about is doing speaking engagements as a way of getting referrals. And you count that as a legitimate referral if you get a speaking engagement to a local Rotary, Lions, Kiwanas, women’s organization, any organization. Listen to episode 481 and that’s a great way for therapists to get referrals. That kind of sidetracks the topic but it’s an important question, and I’m glad you asked it.

Priscilla:
Yeah. But the rest of the ethics, I think one of the things I really enjoy about the Code of Ethics is it used to be when we were always in-person. Now I don’t know whether this still happens or not for different chapters, but we would all stand up and we would repeat the Code of Ethics in unison, [during a new member’s induction] and it really brought together the chapter and made the new member feel connected. I thought it was really a cool thing.

Ivan:
Yeah, a lot of chapters still do that. It’s something that’s part of the system with any new member, stand there and have the new member read the Code of Ethics and have the members read the Code of Ethics with it. It was something that I saw done many, many years ago and incorporated into the program as a result of watching how impressive it was to have everyone stand there and do the Code of Ethics together.

It actually, I think, started in Las Vegas of all places. The director there would have some fun with it. She’s still a BNI Director, Leslie Taylor, and Leslie would say, “Okay, you know, raise your right hand, lift your left foot, put your left hand on the BNI business card holder, and raise your right hand.” And she would have some fun with it. And they would all read the Code of Ethics together. And it started with that, and then we kind of implemented it worldwide.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think it’s nice.

Ivan:
Well that’s the history behind the Code of Ethics. A little more information than I did in the previous podcast. I hope that gives people some context. And be proud. Be proud to be part of an organization that has a Code of Ethics that we really want our members to follow. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. That’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner explains why BNI’s Code of Ethics has 6 points and how “I will live up to the ethical standards of my profession,” was added.

title.rendered: Episode 903: Three Keys to Mastering Online Reviews

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Rav Mendiratta joins the podcast this week to share three keys to mastering online reviews and BNI testimonials, plus three mistakes business owners make when asking for them. If you’re not having any luck with reviews, you may be making one of these mistakes:

  1. Asking without sending a link to the review site.
  2. Not following up on requests for reviews.
  3. Not automating the requests and follow-ups.

To increase the number of reviews you get by up to 50%, do these three things:

  1. Choose the right review platform for your business.
  2. Showcase your reviews on your website and in your weekly presentation.
  3. Choose a review automation service that works with your review platform.

Responding to reviews professionally can increase trust and loyalty. Thank customers for positive reviews and address negative reviews in a positive way.

Visit Rav’s review automation website: itspropel.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 903

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I have actually been able to be home for a few weeks. I’m still home in Austin, Texas, and it’s great to not have to get on a plane for a few weeks.

Priscilla:
Oh yeah, I bet.

Ivan:
Absolutely. So I have another guest today, and this gentleman was highly recommended by his [BNI] Directors for this topic. His name is Rav Mendiratta, and Rav is the CEO of SocioSquares, and he’s the Chief Product Officer at Propel, a SaaS platform that helps businesses shine through Google reviews and SEO. He’s got 15 years of expertise in online marketing and AI solutions, and he’s been recognized as LinkedIn Top Voice, an invitation-only recognition given to industry leaders. And I really thought that he would make a great guest to talk about mastering online reviews, because testimonials and endorsements are really important in BNI. So Rav, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Rav:
Thank you. Dr Misner, it’s truly an honor to be here.

Ivan:
Well, it’s a pleasure having you on, and please call me Ivan. So talk about three mistakes that business owners tend to make with reviews and particularly, and as you’re listening to this, [BNI] members, think about getting reviews that will help you in your BNI chapter. So what are the three mistakes people make, Rav?

Rav:
That’s a great question, Dr. Misner, and in my years of experience, I’ve seen business owners make these three mistakes every so often. First one is they ask and forget. Now, many business owners assume that just because a customer says they’ll leave a review, they actually will. It’s like a physical therapist treating a patient and saying, “Please stretch at home,” but never checking in or scheduling a follow up. Now, most customers have good intentions, but life gets in the way. They get in their car, drive home, deal with kids, emails, Netflix, and by the time they remember, the moment is gone.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Rav:
That’s why businesses need to make it effortless. Maybe send a direct link via email or text, so leaving a review is instant and hassle free.

Ivan:
And can I say the direct link concept is a great concept, and I’ve had people do that with me, and it’s nudged me more often than not. So that’s a great suggestion.

Rav:
Absolutely. The number two mistake I’ve seen is no follow ups. And no follow ups in my world means no reviews. Now, asking for a review once and never bringing it up is like a sales guy expecting a prospect to get back on the proposal on time without a reminder, right? Never happens. So most people won’t leave a review the first time you ask. But businesses that follow up increase review rates up to 50%. A few simple, personalized emails or text reminders can make all the difference.

Ivan:
Yep.

Rav:
And the third one is doing it manually instead of automating. Now, a well written review can be worth thousands of dollars over time, but many business owners spend hours manually asking for reviews instead of using a software to automate the process. It’s like a law firm handling every case with paper files instead of using a case management software, it’s inefficient and slow.

Ivan:
So let me ask you, Rav — there are software platforms that people can use, that would automate the request for reviews?

Rav:
Yes, absolutely. There are several of them out there, and we have one such SaaS platform as well.

Ivan:
Okay, all right, I didn’t know that. That’s good to know. So if they get these, if they follow up, and they do so several times, you’re going to increase the number of reviews by, you say, up to 50% is that correct?

Rav:
Yeah, it varies by industry, but here’s what the data tells us: without follow ups, only 5 to 10% of customers leave a review on their own. With proactive follow ups, that number jumps between 30 to 50%. But here’s an interesting challenge. Now, unhappy customers are much more likely to leave a review than happy ones.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Rav:
So if I were to take an example of, say, an auto body shop, let’s say they just finished a big job fixing a customer’s car. The customer is thrilled, no more dents, no more stress. But a week goes by, they don’t leave a review. Why? Because their problem is solved and they’ve probably moved on. But at the same time, if there’s another customer who experienced a minor delay, maybe in scheduling or a part got delayed, they are much more likely to leave a review about that frustration. So if the body shop is not actively collecting positive reviews, their online reputation could be shaped by a few unhappy customers rather than the many satisfied ones.

Ivan:
So listen, I couldn’t agree with you more. I first wrote about this back in 1994 in a book I wrote with Mike Macedonio, Mike and Dawn are your BNI Executive Directors. And Mike came on to one of the later editions, but I wrote about it in a book called The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. And I say that very thing, people are more likely to talk about you when they’re upset with you than they are when they’re happy with you. The only exceptions to that are restaurants and movies or TV shows where people will talk about that around the water cooler. But you know, the auto body shop, you’re right on the money that you have to really go after those reviews in order to get those positive reviews. So, any other suggestions on how to go about getting them?

Rav:
Absolutely, and you are way ahead of your time if you wrote about this 20 years back, thank you for doing that. I think there are a lot of review platforms where business owners can collect reviews so from Facebook to Yelp to Trip Advisor. So picking the right platform is a must. Showcasing the reviews is important, and then automating whichever platform they select, automating the request process, making it easy is really important for business owners.

Ivan:
Yeah, I have one other suggestion. And that is when you get a BNI member who does a review, and they do a good review on your business, in your weekly presentation, read part of that review. Thank the person for the review and remind other members to please do a review as well if they’ve used your business. So you know, when you show people in a BNI chapter what other BNI members have done, and ask the existing members to also follow suit, you’re more likely to get them to do the review. Would you not agree?

Rav:
Absolutely, 100% and for BNI members who rely on referrals as their primary source of generating new business. Even asking for a testimonial on BNI Connect could be huge.

Ivan:
Yes.

Rav:
Because a strong presence on BNI Connect builds trust with their BNI network.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you can use reviews like that, you know, cut and paste them with approval, to your own website, which you know leverages one review into multiple reviews.

Rav:
Absolutely, absolutely.

Ivan:
So, I know you want to talk a little bit, we’ve got a couple more minutes, on responding to reviews. So you have some ideas on that that I think are valuable.

Rav:
Absolutely. So in today’s world, more than 50% of customers, or potential customers, who are reading reviews, they are looking at the responses as well. So thanking customers for positive reviews builds loyalty, while handling negative reviews showcases that the business is calm and, professionally, can handle critical feedback as well, and it shows that you care. So responding is very important as well.

Ivan:
You know, I couldn’t agree more, again, responding, particularly to those negative reviews. When I’m looking at a review online, if I see a negative review and I see a response from the business that is, as you said, calm, cool, collected, or words to that effect, and addresses the issue in a in a positive way, rather than a defensive way, I kind of discount that negative review, and I take that that response as being more accurate to what the organization is all about. Would you not agree with that?

Rav:
Absolutely. And the interesting thing about negative reviews is that if they are pointing towards a specific type of service, like in the body shop, the delays. If a part is delayed and a customer is frustrated because of that, the body shop owner can’t do much over there, right? So people who are reading the reviews, they understand that, so responding to that professionally helps a lot.

Ivan:
Yeah, I agree. And it’s funny, I actually have a situation like that with one of my books, where somebody gave me a one star out of five stars, and they were upset at what was being charged for the Kindle version of the book, and they were upset that it was being charged so much. And I responded saying, “Hey, I’m really sorry, but I have no control on what the publisher charges for a Kindle book,” and I think the people that read that were like, “Okay, yeah, this is a lame review that this one star review gave.”

Rav:
You know, Dr Misner, it’s very interesting that a perfect five star rating is not always the best solution. People today intentionally go to the negative ones and see what is written. So like these, actually they help that. Okay, people are really happy with the content of the book. It was just the price.

Ivan:
Yeah, and it was out of my control.

Rav:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Listen, we’re almost out of time. Any closing thoughts, Rav? This has been good material, and if you’re listening to this, take these ideas and apply them to how you can do testimonials and endorsements in your BNI chapter. Any last thoughts, Rav?

Rav:
Dr. Misner, I believe that trust is the foundation of every successful business, and online reviews are the building blocks. The more you stack them up, the stronger your business stands in the eyes of potential customers. So asking for reviews, automating the process, showcasing your reviews in today’s day and world is a must. So I strongly recommend that to all BNI members.

Ivan:
I agree completely. And if you’re in BNI, you’re listening to this podcast, take these ideas and try to apply them into your chapter, and get your members to do reviews and endorsements, preferably in writing, so that you can use them on BNI Connect, on Yelp, on whatever platform, LinkedIn, whatever platform you would like. Rav, thank you so much for being here. For anyone that’s interested in connecting with Rav, you can reach him at www.itspropel.com. That is i, t, s, p, r, o, p, e, l, .com. Rav, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast.

Rav:
Thank you, Dr. Misner, it was an absolute honor.

Ivan:
Thank you. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Rav. It’s really interesting and wonderful to hear about that. Okay, I think that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
