title.rendered: Episode 902: Thirty-nine Year BNI Member

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Steven Marche joins Dr. Misner this week to share some of what he’s learned in 39 years as a BNI member.

  • When you make a referral, don’t just give the prospect a name and number. Have your referral partner call them and make the connection.
  • Instead of trying to explain BNI, tell people to go to the nearest meeting–and not to leave without an application form.
  • You need to show up. Steven has had only 5 absences in 39 years, and prepares his substitutes to represent him.

Visit Steven’s website at homeloans411.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 902

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great, and I am at home in Austin, Texas this week, and I’m really excited to have a guest on the podcast. He is the world’s longest-standing member of BNI. His name is Steven Marche, and Steven has been a BNI member for 39 years. Now, we have a couple of members whose companies have been in for 40 years, but it was their parent and then they handed [their membership] over to one of their children. But Steven himself has been a BNI member for 39 years.

He joined BNI when it was called The Network many, many years ago, in June of 1986, where he learned a lot of lessons, he has said, about networking and helping clients secure real estate loans, which is the industry he’s in. Steven is very detail oriented, and he loves to get loans closed. His passion, he says, lies in assisting people, especially the self-employed business owners. And he likes to say the only good loan is a closed loan. Steven, it’s been a long time since we’ve talked, and it’s really great to hear your voice, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Steven:
Thank you, Ivan, I appreciate that.

Ivan:
You’ve got a great voice for radio. You should do a radio show, Steve.

Steven:
I did, and it was phenomenal when the rates were in the twos and threes.

Ivan: (laughing)
Listen, it’s great to have you on the podcast. And I got to start by asking you, 39 years. I’m just so impressed with that. That is so incredible. How have you managed to be a member for so long? I know you’ve transitioned from one chapter to another, but, you know, talk about the long-term relationships being in that long.

Steven:
Well, you started me off. You stood there in that conference room [at] the Best Western [hotel] in 1986 and I was impressed. And I said, “Hey.” You taught me and told me what you learned and why you opened a few chapters at that point. And I learned it all. One thing that I did learn, and it’s been the best way to get people into the chapter and build it.

But here’s one that maybe you’ll share with everybody — referrals. When someone would call me, and especially realtors, they would always talk about, well, I have to give two people referrals or three referrals. And I said, “That’s not the way I do it. The way I refer somebody, if I trust [them] is I will make sure my good friend calls you, Ivan, and it’s up to them to call you and make that connection.” And I taught that throughout the chapters and been back to call. They’ve asked me to come in and speak many times in the different chapters which I have.

And another thing is about membership. I have always brought in members. And here’s how I taught them. I told the story about Copenhagen. We had a exchange student, and she came a few times back to California from Sweden, and she says, “My dad would like to talk to you.” I said, “Have him call me.” She says, “You leave here every Wednesday morning, very, very early before I get up, and your family wondered where you went.” I said, “To BNI, to network.” “Oh, okay, my dad liked to know.”

So I called him up, and he told me his name was Michael, and he said, “Well, what do you do and how do you do it?” And I explained. I said, “But you need to go to one.” He says, “Oh, I checked it before I called you, and it’s three hours away.” And I said, “Just a minute.” I said, “There’s one 10 minutes [away]. But here’s what you do. You go, don’t leave until you get an application and join.”

Do you know that he called me the next day and told me that he wouldn’t leave, and they couldn’t believe it, and he joined. Six months later, a year later, he became vice president.

Ivan:
Let me just comment on that, Steve. You know the idea of going to a meeting is so important, and you know, when I was starting BNI in 1985-86, and I did the kickoff that you were at. And very few people can say that I was there and did the kickoff when they when they started. But one of the things that I would tell people is, you got to come to a meeting. They’d ask… back in ’86 we had no materials, so they’d ask for brochures or whatnot. I didn’t have anything. There was no internet. And I said, “You got to come, you got to come to a meeting and experience it. Bring plenty of business cards.” And so that is the key — going to a BNI meeting to see it firsthand.

Steven:
Well, another one, very quickly, is Georgia [USA]. A gentleman lived in Chicago, then Minnesota, and he moved his CPA practice for whatever reason, to Georgia, a distant cousin. And he asked me, “What do I do?” And he knows that every Wednesday I had a meeting, and I said, “Find a [BNI] chapter in Atlanta, Georgia, and just go.” He said, “What do you mean?” “Call them up and tell them you’d like to be a guest.” And I said, “Do not leave until you get an application and join the chapter.” Well, within six months, he became Treasurer, and I believe he’s still Treasurer after all these years, best CPA in that area. And he loves it, simple as that.

Ivan:
And he’s a, did you say he is a CPA?

Steven:
CPA. Yes.

Ivan:
Yeah. So the one organization, the one company that is still a member after 40 years is a CPA organization, Denny & Company, and they were my CPA back in 1985. And the company is still members today, and it’s Carolyn’s son, Matt Denny, who is the [BNI] member. So CPAs have been very, very successful in BNI, as have loan people, home loan people such as yourself.

Steven:
Oh absolutely. Attorneys… we were at Baker’s Square, you were there. And sure enough, we had four different attorneys. I never saw so many pink [referral] slips being transferred between them and us. The four attorneys would refer back and forth. And for two or three years, there were four different type[s] of attorneys in our group.

Ivan:
Yeah. So for those of you who are wondering what a pink slip is because we do most everything on BNI Connect now, those are the referral slips that we used to have, and some chapters still use them and actually hand out the [paper] slip, which I think has some power to it, handing it out like that. So that’s what Steve is referring to. I’m going to do a podcast coming up soon, Steve, where there’s a chapter that has over 10 attorneys in the group, which blows my mind, but it shows how you can actually be supportive of different classifications versus competitive with them.

Steven:
That’s true. We have one in our group. He’s given me a grant for a $3 million loan, and we were able to get him approval in a matter of three days. But he does the conventional Sure.

Ivan:
Yeah. Excellent. So any recommendations for people, for all the people out there, that have not been a member for 39 years. What’s your words of wisdom for people to understand that networking is more… that BNI is more of a marathon than a sprint.

Steven:
Well, you’ve got to be there. And people say to me, “How many times have you missed?” I said, “Five – two kids, one sickness and two vacations,” and they don’t understand that you’ve got to show up.

Ivan:
You do. But have you had substitutes representing you beyond those five? Or no?

Steven:
Yes, I have. During the years when we were [meeting] at the golf course, I had Mark Bacharach entertainer and magician come and entertain, versus me speaking because I was [unable to attend].

Ivan:
And having a legitimate sub is totally acceptable, and that’s fantastic. So you’ve missed five meetings without a sub in 39 years. So you hold two records in the organization, Steve. I think that’s incredible.

Steven:
Well, I did have recently, I think last year or so, I did have a substitute. This guy, Pete, subbed for me, and they said he did a great job. Of course, I gave him the information.

Ivan:
Well, you know, that’s the key on a sub. And I’ve got some podcasts where I talked about regretting creating the substitute program, which was approved by the Board of Advisors. But we also found that the substitute program, when done right, is effective. And so you just said you prepared your sub, and that’s when it’s done right. It’s when you just hand over and say, “Hey, fill in for me,” and you don’t give them any information that it’s done wrong. Or when you have these ‘professional subs’, all they do is substitute in a Chapter, that’s when it’s done wrong, but it sounds like you did it right.

Steven:
I passed it on as a verbal statement and it seems to work very well.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, Steve, any closing thoughts before we wrap up here today?

Steven:
Well, my wife never really understood what networking was all about. I explained that you had said it was never taught in school or college. You were told never to talk to strangers, not to share things. And I get up and share things every day. I learn something every day. I teach my kids — it’s always learning something every single day to make us better.

And my wife always asks me, “Steve, why are you so dedicated? You get up and you solve problems for self-employed people that are turned down.” You know, people don’t understand why would I be turned down from banks that they’ve been with for 25-30 years. They come to me and I figure it out. I solve problems. I have a sign that my daughter gave me for the holiday and my birthday: “Dad fixes things.”

Ivan: (chuckling)
I love it.

Steven:
I’m the fixer when it comes to loans: estate loans, blanket loans, swing loans, bridge loans, commercial loans, residential. Hey, look, if you’ve got a simple guy that has a restaurant, he’s been turned down by three of his major banks that he runs millions of dollars through. They turn him down. He came to me, he wants to open up another restaurant two miles down the street. We got him a loan in a matter of a week.

Ivan:
What you’re talking about here, Steve, that I think it can apply to everyone, is that BNI members really tend to, from what I’ve seen, knock themselves out to take great care of the referrals that they get. And clearly you do that, or you wouldn’t have been in for 39 years now.

Listen, we’re out of time. For anyone that’s interested, you can see Steve’s website at www.homeloans411.com, that’s home loans, the number 411, dot com. Steve, thank you so much for being a loyal member of this organization for 39 years. I’m really proud to have you as part of BNI.

Steven:
Well, it’s been 39 years since we first talked, and we’ve talked many times thereafter.

Ivan:
We have, and we’ll have to talk again more. Thanks a lot, my friend. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you so much. That was some wonderful information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Steven Marche joins Dr. Misner this week to share what he’s learned about referrals and visitors in 39 years as a BNI member.

title.rendered: Episode 901: Misner’s Corollary to Murphy’s Law (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 703, originally released on April 21, 2021. Links and special offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

What is Misner’s Corollary to Murphy’s Law?

If you ask yourself “What could go wrong with this?” before you launch a new business idea, you can head off some potential problems and increase chances of a successful launch. But you also need to ask yourself about the possible unintended consequences of success. What if you fix one problem only to create several others?

Examples from BNI’s History

  • Establishing the Substitute System: Members were able to avoid being marked absent, but often sent low-quality substitutes who did a poor job of representing their businesses, defeating the real purpose of having a substitute.
  • Tracking Referrals Online: Despite the massively improved process of passing and tabulating the information digitally, there was a sense of loss by many members in the “physical passing of a referral” to another member.

Avoiding Unintended Consequences

If at all possible, test your ideas in advance on a small group of actual customers. They will help point out the things you didn’t think were possible or didn’t think were problems. Expecting potential pushback, BNI’s CEO started testing BNI Online in January of 2020, before the pandemic was even on the horizon in Europe or the Americas. The BNI chapters that participated in the tests uncovered many potential issues that BNI was able to eliminate before rolling the program out to its remaining 10,000 chapters worldwide.

What unintended consequences have you experienced from successful business initiatives? Share your experiences and your solutions in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 702/903

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
Still traveling the world via Zoom. I’ve been to Australia, I’ve been to Japan – did several presentations there, and Greater China, as well as a number of presentations throughout the US. So, you know, I don’t get very many frequent flyer miles right now, but I sure am Zooming around the world.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that’s great. Okay. Well, tell us about what you’re going to share with us today.

Ivan:
I have what I think is an interesting topic. And this one’s not about networking. It’s more about business and a way of looking at your business that I have found to be of value. I’ve called this Misner’s Corollary to Murphy’s Law. Now I learned about Murphy’s Law in graduate school. For those of you who don’t know what it is, it basically says that what can go wrong, will go wrong. And although this law feels very pessimistic, I believe there is value to it. It gives a framework for people to look for the flaws in their thinking. And when one does that effectively, it’s easier to address potential issues before they arise.

Now, this leads me to Misner’s Corollary. And here is Misner’s Corollary:  Sometimes, what can’t go wrong, will go wrong. Here’s what I mean by that. When I have introduced something new into BNI and I also own, as you know, property business and publishing business, I’ve often thought about what could go wrong with a new approach or idea that I’m doing. And if you do that in advance, you truly help to head off challenges. However, what I’ve learned over the years is that you also need to think about “What can’t go wrong”. I know this sounds crazy and even more pessimistic however, if you go about this in a thought-provoking manner, you can truly think through potential challenges before you proceed. In particular, you want to consider what I call the “unintended consequences of a seemingly good idea.” This tends to happen when you have a solution to a problem and almost everyone agrees that the solution will definitely help with the problem. You think about how you’ll roll it out and avoid all those things that could go wrong with that rollout and its implementation. You then roll out the idea and it all goes well. What we tend to forget however, is the unintended consequences of that new idea. In other words, the roll-out goes great but then you create a whole new set of problems that never existed and were never considered when dealing with the original problem.

Now, this has happened several times in my career. An example of this is something I’ve talked about on BNI Podcast before, and that’s the implementation of the “substitute program” relating to attendance in BNI. It took many months to figure out a program where BNI members can send a substitute in their place to the weekly chapter meeting. The idea was that the substitute would represent the member (employees and customers were the prime substitute candidates) and then the member would be well represented and wouldn’t have an absence. Sounds good, looked good on paper however, there was a long-term unintended consequence – some members would look for virtually anyone to be a sub. Anybody – you got a pulse? you’re in; breathe on this mirror, it fogs up? you’re in. This created a less than satisfactory situation for the group, especially when the sub basically just pitched their own business instead of representing the person they were supposed to be there for. And this was the unintended consequence of a seemingly good idea. And it’s taken years to address this issue and it’s still not perfected, but it’s taken a long time to address it.

Another example of the impact of unintended consequences was BNI’s transition from paper copies of referral slips (the small paper slips that included the name and contact information of a referral) over to digital online referrals. Now, despite the massively improved process of passing and tabulating the information digitally, there was a sense of loss by many members in the “physical passing of a referral” to another member. Priscilla, you and I have talked about this. Now, luckily, this lingering feeling was quickly erased with the outbreak of the COVID pandemic and the transition of all of our groups to meeting online. This meant the physical referral slips were no longer even relevant, and that helped with the transition. But we didn’t, it wasn’t on our radar to, think that people would go “No, I want to pass that physical referral slip out.”

And so, one of the problems with Murphy’s Law and Misner’s Corollary is that you never know for sure if it would have been an issue because the problem never surfaced, which is probably a good thing. In other words, if you really think through Murphy’s Law and you think through Misner’s Corollary, and you address all of those issues, you may not ever see the issue and you may think, well, I went through a lot of work, nothing happened. Well nothing happened, probably because you really gave it great consideration. And that’s the theme of this podcast – give change great consideration and look for things that can and can’t go wrong.

There have been some occasions where employing these strategies do have a clear-cut result and they can be identified. One such occasion was in January 2020, when BNI CEO, Graham Weihmiller, began to transition 10,000 BNI chapters meeting in-person to online meetings. He expected the pushback early on and started the transition where it was necessary (Asia) in January and experimented in areas where it wasn’t necessary yet (North America) in late January, February. By moving forward and experimenting with the program, the organization was able to prepare for much of the pushback that we expected, and the result was an incredible pivot. I mean, an incredible pivot of 10,000 in-person meetings to online in, literally, a matter of weeks as it was rolled out country by country across the globe.

And yes, we envision going back to in-person over time, as most people know. But we have many chapters who, even as of this podcast, have never met in-person because they started online. And so we now know that the online process can work when necessary. The lesson that I learned here is that when you have a good idea, think about what can go wrong with that idea, then spend some time thinking about what “can’t go wrong.” And this means you need to really think outside the box to consider the potential unintended consequences.

Most importantly, test the idea in some limited way to identify the things you thought couldn’t go wrong. And that’s what Graham did with flipping the BNI chapters to online, was he tested it to see what we didn’t even think of that could go wrong. And we were able to fix a number of things before the complete rollout. Believe me when I say you still might discover unintended consequences, but the experimental testing phase is critical to avoid Misner’s Corollary to Murphy’s Law. And that’s my message for today. Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Yeah, that’s a good one. It’s great; it’s like checking and double checking and triple checking and thinking of every possibility. I think that’s a good idea.

Ivan:
And you know, the bigger the challenge, the more important it is to check and double check, to look at what can go wrong and look at what can’t go wrong. And think those through so that you can be a little bit more prepared for a transition that you may have in your own business and what I’m talking about is really something that, I think, affects the BNI member’s business. This is a concept that you in business can apply to your own individual business and try to help transition change within your own organization more effectively. That’s my message for today Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. I just want to say it’s amazing that you were able to transition all of those chapters to online.

Ivan:
Do you know I do a lot of interviews with the media and they always have a double take when I give them those numbers and they just say, “Are you serious? You transitioned 10,000 chapters in weeks?” And that to me is one of the greatest pivots I’ve ever seen done in business. And Graham, our CEO, is really, and every time I talk about this, he points to everyone else – “well, it was because he had a great team.” Yeah, but it really started with him and he was the one that really made that happen and I’m very grateful for it.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I can see why. Well thank you so much for the great information. I want to tell you about our new sponsor for this podcast. It’s www.misneraudioprograms.com These audio programs are going to provide you with tools and inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you build your business. Whether you are a new or a seasoned member, these audio programs will help you unlock the power of networking. So check out the great material to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com, and then use the promo code Ivan50 and you can get 50% off of all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And just so you know, all the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. So, thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Even a successful business initiative can have unintended consequences. Learn how to identify them in advance.

title.rendered: Episode 900: AI and Networking (Part 2)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Spencer Reynolds from BNI Tampa Bay returns this week to explain how to use AI to find the best referral partners in your BNI chapter, identify visitors to invite, and follow up.

Start your conversation by providing information about yourself and your business so the AI model knows what you do and what kind of referrals you want. Next, upload the list of your chapter members and their professions and ask:

  • Who are the best referral partners on the list and what should I be talking to them about?
  • Can you help me draft invitations to One-to-Ones?
  • What local businesses that would be great referral partners are missing from the list?
  • Can you give me their phone numbers so I can invite them?

Follow the steps in Episode 899 for the One-to-Ones, then use the AI meeting summary as a starting point to ask “What would be a good follow-up email to send in two weeks?”

Links

  • Listen to Episode 899, AI and Networking (Part 1).
  • Listen to Episode 327, Your Business 101.

Complete Transcript of Episode 900

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas this week, and I just want to thank you, Priscilla, for helping us take this to Episode 900.

Priscilla:
(laughing) Isn’t that amazing?

Ivan:
It really is amazing. When I started this, I think it was in 2007 maybe… I just had no idea if it would last or work. And you’ve helped make it work for a long time. And I appreciate you.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Thank you, Ivan, I really appreciate you.

Ivan:
Well, it’s been great working with you, and I hope to continue to work with you for a long time.

Priscilla:
Yeah, we have to get to Episode 1800.

Ivan:
There we go. Well, I’m really excited about Episode 1000 for sure.

Priscilla:
(laughing) Okay.

Ivan:
So I brought back Spencer Reynolds. Those of you who listened last week know Spencer was talking about AI and Networking, using technology to deepen real connections. He’s been with BNI for nearly 18 years. He runs the [BNI USA] Tampa Bay franchise and generated more than 124 million [USD] in Thank You for Closed Business last year for the region. We had a really good conversation, and it’s rare that it’s so deep and meaty that I say, hey, come let’s do a second one. But that’s what I did last week. Spencer, do you want to kind of wrap up, kind of the spreadsheet idea that we were talking about before we go into the next topic?

Spencer:
Yeah. So just for your weekly presentation, preparing all 52 weeks so you’re ready to go. ChatGPT, AI for any of the models can help you do that by saying, Here’s my date, here’s my LCD that I want to focus on, my Lowest Common Denominator, one piece of my business, and the ask that I want, and then who I want to be connected to. And then let it come up with the words, and you can actually go through and prompt all those and it will output a spreadsheet at the end that you can download and go, “Wow, I’ve got my entire plan ready to go.” Now, of course, you’ve got to make sure that they all work for you, but that is an incredible opportunity to prepare that many all at once.

Ivan:
It is. And if you haven’t listened to last week’s episode, go back and listen to it. He goes into detail about it. I want to add one thing to that, and that is I talk about something similar to what he’s describing, but not with AI. It’s episode number 327, Your Business 101, where I talk about how I recommend that you sit down and do 52 weekly networking success strategies and write it out. But here ChatGPT helps you do all of that. So go listen to episode number 327. All right, Spencer, we still have a lot of content for you to cover. Talk about AI assisted introductions.

Spencer:
So how you can get connected to people? Now I’m going to use this in two different formats, one, how you can actually find the right people, and the second one, who to invite, especially inside of some of those connections. So as you have your chapter, you want to be able to find who are the people that you can do more business with. Now, one of the things you can do, maybe many people don’t know, is you can copy and paste anything into ChatGPT. You can use documents. You can do things. But if you go right to the public website for your chapter, which, if you go to your region, like our region is BNITampa.com, you go into the chapters, and you find your chapter, all your members are listed there with their names and their professions.

So one of the things you can do is copy that sheet, just copy, click, paste it into a ChatGPT window, and then say, here are the members of my chapter, because it is public information, you’re not disclosing anything out there. Here’s my chapter list. Who on this list are the best referral partners for me, and what identification can you do for what you know that I do?

Now, you want to prompt it beforehand so it does know who you are, so you need to know your AI voice knows who you are and what you do. So maybe have some conversations before then, if you’ve never done that. But then say who are the ones that I should be working on and what should I talk to them about? And now it comes up with brainstorming to be able to go, wow — these AI assisted introductions, like they’re telling you, these are people who normally work well together in BNI, because there’s a lot of people all over the world using BNI and using AI, so it knows a lot about what we’re doing with our weekly presentations, our feature presentations, our asks.

And so who are the normal people that I would normally do business with? Because you might not think it’s normal, and it comes up with a great idea, and you’re like, wow. Now you can say, help me write an email or a text message to this person asking for this and requesting a One-to-One to meet with them. And now it writes it for you. And now you go and have this meeting and this conversation, and it’s prepared by… Because some chapters may be small, some chapters may be large. We happen to have a 127-member chapter here, and a new member coming into that may be like, I have no idea where to start.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Spencer:
But you can have it find people inside your own group and give you some great lead-ins to start in that way. What do you have in that conversation?

Ivan:
I love that. Yeah.

Spencer:
The second one is that same list that you just put in…

Ivan:
By the way, on that list, make sure you don’t put in email addresses and phone numbers of people.

Spencer:
Oh, yeah, it doesn’t do any of that, right? It’s only the public information that’s on the public website. But imagine you use that same exact list, and now you can ask it another question. And by the way, this is an AI tool. When you’re using AI the same thread, meaning where you started this conversation and asked a lot of questions to get to where you got to, keep that same thread open, and now start another question. Regards to that, you can say now the list I gave you earlier of all my chapter members, who is missing that is a great referral partner, that one of our chapter members could do a lot of business, or multiple members can do business with.

Ivan, I just did this. It was mind blowing. I actually went into ChatGPT. I put this information in, and I asked the question, and I put it in, by the way, it came right back, and said it looks like, and it literally broke down my chapter that I typed in, and it said, “You have 10 members in construction and six in finance and four in legal and five in real estate and two in health and wellness. And here is who it came back saying, “You do not have a home inspector, a property inspector, in this chapter. And that would be a valuable connection.” I’m like, yes, it would. And so I said, in the Oldsmar, Florida area, because the BNI Business Connections Chapter is who I was using, can you help me find some. It came up with a list. And then I said, Can you please give me their phone number? And it gave me the seven companies and their website as well as their phone number for the home inspectors in the city that we’re looking for. Now, it’s shooting fish in a barrel.

I pick up a phone and have a have a very simple question. You know that we use inside of BNI the GRIP script: Are you interested in Growing your business? Are Referrals part of that? Can I Introduce you to some people who can give you business? And here’s the Place and time we meet. And I got seven names and numbers straight out of AI to invite to my chapter. That is a genius method on how to get more visitors to your chapter.

Ivan:
Yeah, it is a powerful method. And I think I did a previous podcast on helping a sedan service who wanted to figure out what their power team would consist of, and he came up with some professions that he had never thought of like a wedding planner in a hotel for, you know, special guests, and there were a couple of other people. So asking these kinds of questions, it’s amazing that you get great results to enhance your business.

Spencer:
It’s so smart. I mean, we had that keynote speaker in Madrid when I was with you, Ivan, at the Global conference, who got up and talked about the value, or the smart value, right? Like, what’s the IQ of AI? And it is advanced on that by many, many multiples since that time.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Spencer:
It is better at anything, reasoning and thinking, it’s way better than any of us could ever do on our own or even with a group of people.

Ivan:
We’re almost out of time. And I think one of the topics that members would like, and that is AI for Networking follow up or follow through.

Spencer:
Yeah. So how you’re looking at, you know, maintaining these relationships, growing these relationships, you really want to look at the opportunity of saying, Okay, how can I now make this connection continue to happen? So following up with them — for one we talked about last episode, about making sure you have an action plan from your One-to-One conversation. But now what about the next week? And the next week? That chat remembers what you did last time, and now you can say, “Write me an email that I can use two weeks from now.” Maybe you have a [Schedule] Send feature in your [email] account that you can send it two weeks out, and so that it’s actually following up with that person again, with the words that it knows it used last time. And that way, the people that you’re sending this to are amazed. They’re like, if you’re this good at doing One-to-Ones in BNI, how good must you be at your own business?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Spencer:
Right? Because what we see and do BNI, we think you do everywhere, and if you’re this good at the follow up game, wow, the value that brings to you and your credibility inside the chapter, and you’re just using a tool like AI that can be free for you to help you follow up with people and really take your game to another level. Even though you may not be good at doing the wording of those, this does it for you.

Ivan:
Yeah. Any closing thoughts before we wrap up, Spencer?

Spencer:
You know, I will leave you with this thought, Gary Vaynerchuk, or Gary V as he’s known online, I saw a little clip of a short video from him that said this, “If you’re not training your AI voice right now every day, so it learns who you are and what you do in your business, you’re going to be left behind like that executive who used to have his secretary using his AOL account to print out his email so he could respond to them.” I’m like, yeah, it’s time to step up if you’re not doing it yet.

Ivan:
Yeah, you got to be out there. You got to be using it. So let me share something embarrassing along that line. I wanted to see who it would name regarding an expert in networking. So I said, Talk to me about networking techniques that are valuable for small businesses and quote one of the world’s leading experts on the topic. And the thing scrolled down, and I’m like, Oh, please, oh, please, please, please. And there it was by Dr. Ivan Misner. I’m like, yes, okay, it’s got my voice there.

Spencer:
You’ve got to look for yourself. I actually asked it Ivan, I said, “What’s Ivan Misner’s best quote of all time?” It came back and said, “Success is the uncommon application of common knowledge.”

Ivan:
Yeah, out of the book, Masters of Success.

Spencer:
Love it.

Ivan:
Thank you. Thank you. That’s great to know. Spencer, thank you so much for being on the podcast. Podcast number 900. I really appreciate you.

Spencer:
Thanks for inviting me.

Priscilla:
Can I ask something before we go?

Spencer:
For sure.

Priscilla:
Tell me about the GRIP script. Where do you find that?

Spencer:
G, R, I, P, that acronym of grip. You can find it in BNI Business Builder, if you go type in GRIP, you will find that it comes back and trains you on Grow: are you interested in growing your business? Are Referrals part of that? Introduce, and the Place. [It’s a] very simple acronym to follow for inviting.

Ivan:
Inviting people. Yeah, GRIP. I should probably have somebody on to do the GRIP thing. But it is on [BNI] Business Builder, and it is in our manuals.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much. It was great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Spencer Reynolds returns to explain how to use AI to find the best referral partners in your BNI chapter, identify visitors, and follow up.

title.rendered: Episode 899: AI and Networking (Part 1)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Spencer Reynolds explains how you can use AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini to be more effective at BNI activities. You can ask AI to

  • Analyze two GAINS profiles before a One-to-One and suggest talking points and questions to ask.
  • Provide feedback on your weekly presentation.
  • Provide a summary and action steps from the audio of a One-to-One meeting.
  • Identify local companies that would be good referrals for members of your chapter.
  • Create a spreadsheet with a 52 weekly presentations based on the 52 most common LCDs for your business.

Stay tuned for more great AI tips in Part 2 next week!

Complete Transcript of Episode 899

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austin, Texas this week. I have some long-term friends and BNI Directors who are visiting Jody and I [sic] here in Austin.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
I also have a guest today. He is Spencer Reynolds. Spencer has been on the BNI Podcast before, Episode number 698, Referral Flow in Power Teams. He’s been in BNI for nearly 18 years. He has successfully launched 50, 50 – that’s 5-0 BNI chapters between Utah and Florida, and he now leads the largest single region in the USA, BNI Tampa Bay, with over 58 chapters and nearly 1700 members. The Tampa Bay franchise has generated $124 million [USD] in Thank You for Closed Business in the last 12 Months. Spencer’s expertise is obviously in growth and technology, and that’s what he’s going to be talking about today: AI and networking, using technology to deepen real relations. Welcome back to the BNI Podcast, Spencer.

Spencer:
I’m glad to be here, Ivan.

Ivan:
All right, so you’ve got several points. I’ll let you run with them. The first one is about it being a relationship tool.

Spencer:
Yeah, well, first and foremost, I think that we got to be clear that AI is here to stay, right? This is not some fad that was a good thing, and now we’re going to move away from it. This is going to be around for a long time. So I think anybody who is in business and serious about it, has got to start adopting that conversation of, like, How can I use it? What can I use it for? So I want to really apply this to every BNI member to say, How can I use this to actually do something in my life? Because I think a lot of people have played with it. It can be fun, but it doesn’t mean they’ve actually done something worthwhile with it yet.

So I want to just apply it and say, What are some of the activities we do in BNI, and how can we apply it? So first and foremost, obviously, ChatGPT is the large one that everybody gets to hear about. But there are other models out there, like claude.ai or gemini.google.com. So you can use any AI model in place of whatever we’re saying in ChatGPT. If you don’t like GPT or you like one of the other models, like Meta, there’s lots of them out there.

But here’s what I would say, first and foremost, one of the most important things that every BNI member must get really good at is having great, effective One-to-Ones. Would you not agree, Ivan?

Ivan:
Absolutely. Effective One-to-Ones are really important.

Spencer:
Yeah. And so how [do] you prepare for that One-to-One? Sometimes we don’t do much. We just show up, and that’s a real missed opportunity to really set us up for success inside of that. So we’ve taught for, I don’t know how many decades now, probably all of them, of BNI, that we really want to have our G.AI.N.S. exchange, where we talk about our Goals and our Accomplishments, our Interests, our Networks, our Skills, and having that prepared document that we share with each other. Here’s something you may not know. Most of our models out there with AI and specifically ChatGPT right now, is a multi-model, meaning you can do different things with it, like upload documents. You can talk to it with a voice. You can type in it like most people are used to.

But did you know that you could take a PDF document or a Word document that somebody sent you that is their G.A.I.N.S. profile and upload it to ChatGPT, then take yours and upload it to ChatGPT, and then let it say, Please analyze these two gains profiles and give me the top three things that we should talk about that we have in common. And now it gives you talking points, and you can say, give me two questions to ask for each one of them. And now it’s prepared you to have a relationship right off the bat based on your data and their data, without having to spend all the time reading through it.

Now, I’m not saying shortcut and don’t read through it. But when you can have something analyze it really quickly and come back with, hey, both of you like to do X or Y or Z, these are things you may want to talk about. That’s just one little tidbit to go — that’s a start to using AI.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve just heard about being able to upload PDFs. So that’s… I haven’t done it. I need to do it. Great suggestion.

Spencer:
Yeah, I’ll tell you there are some fun things that… I’m a bleeding-edge technologist and so I love uploading audio and video. Did you realize that you could take your weekly presentation, it’ll create it for you; I’ve got some great things about that, but then you can actually record yourself giving your Weekly Presentation, and upload it to ChatGPT and say, “Please give me feedback,” and it will tell you exactly what you can do to make it better, like a business coach, like a speech coach, like some amazing things, just for yourself to get better at your weekly presentation. So another one that I would…

Ivan:
By the way, I’ve done something similar with that when asking ChatGPT for suggestions on a book tour. I’ve done lots of books, but it gave me a suggestion I’d never heard of by doing something similar, asking about, you know, how do you improve my book tour?

Spencer:
Oh, that’s, that’s great. So I’ve done one as well, where I’ve actually recorded. Now, you want to do this with permission of the other person, so to carry along in that One-to-One concept. Imagine you now are sitting down. You’ve got your questions ready to have a great conversation starter with your One-to-One member that you’re going to be sitting down [and] have your meeting. And now I ask, before I start my meeting, would you mind if I hit my voice recorder and record our conversation, just the audio of it, because I’m going to upload this. And then it’s going to give us an action plan of what we talked about. In the action plan it will state the steps that we both agreed to take by the end of this conversation. Would you be okay with that? And people are like, “Oh, yeah, that’d be fine.” And then you get done, and you upload it and say, “Make me a meeting summary, and give me the action points,” and you copy and paste, put it in email, and voila, you now have just taken a lot of work that could take many minutes of summarizing and thinking through, and it just does it automatically in seconds.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve had people do that with me, and it’s very helpful. And in doing a summary, and you don’t have to spend time trying to catch everything they say, or you don’t have to go through and, you know, recording and write down everything. Yeah, it’s a tool.

Spencer:
So have you used the audio feature yet, Ivan?

Ivan:
I don’t use it regularly, but I have used it. That’s how I got that advice on a book tour.

Spencer:
Here’s my favorite part. Any of these things that we’re talking about, you can just use your voice to ask the same questions. And here’s the key in AI for all of you who are starting, it’s all about prompting. If you’re not good at the prompts, you’re not going to get great stuff out of it. So ask a question. Then when it gives you something back, prompt it back until you get what you want. I do that with my voice nine times out of 10. I have that little button and smartphone button on the side of my iPhone 16 that I can click, and it’s an action button. I actually have it do audio notes with chat GPT immediately so I can ask it questions. Because I do, I ask questions throughout the day.

My wife, she loves it, because now I don’t talk to her as much, I can talk like a lot and never stop talking. So she’s like, I’m glad that you have somebody to talk to and ChatGPT does a lot of that with me. I spend probably an hour or two every day, while I’m driving or doing different things, coming up with ideas or saying, “What can you come up with?” and it will give me incredible ideas to do. And then I say, “Remind me of that one.” “I want to do this.” “Keep this in your memory.” And then I go back and I look it up and copy and paste it. I don’t write emails anymore. I do it all through ChatGPT. It writes the email for me, so saving you time on the end of the activities you’re already doing. That’s one of the main uses I would use ChatGPT for. So setting up for a great One-to-One, recapping for your One-to-One of what it is that you’ve agreed to do. Great, great opportunities for using, using AI. Are you ready for another one, Ivan?

Ivan:
Yeah. Is next one personalized engagement?

Spencer:
Yeah. Because when you can personalize this engagement, right, you actually have the opportunity to take this into so many activities that we do in BNI. I’m going to take it into personalizing it for your Weekly Presentation. Now, I was doing an Advanced Training in one of our chapters, and somebody asked me a question, “How can I use AI to make a better Weekly Presentation?”

I said, “I’m so glad you asked.” And I did a live sequence. I actually pushed the button on the phone, and I asked, “Do you know this company?” And I said, the company’s name, and it got it wrong. And everybody laughed. “Ah, it got it wrong.” I said, “Okay, let’s try again.” And I tried again, and it got it wrong again. And the third time, it finally said, yes, it knew the right law firm in the city, and that the kind of law that they practice was personal injury law.

I said, “Great. Now I’d like a Weekly Presentation based on that for 30 seconds. What would you say for that?” And it gives me a presentation. Everyone said, “That was great.” I said, “Actually it wasn’t. It sounded good. But it didn’t give us a specific ask.” So then I prompted it again, “Give us a specific ask and make it around a motorcycle accident or an injury around a motorcycle accident.” And it gave it, and everybody’s like, “Wow, that’s amazing.” I said, “Not yet. We still need to get a specific ask inside of that.” I said, “Now I’d like a local motorcycle shop in the Clearwater area in Florida that would be the person that I could reach out to. Give me the name of that.” And it came back with the name of the motorcycle, and it actually said Harley Davidson of Clearwater. And it was amazing. The whole room just sat there and looked at me with these stunned eyes, and a girl on the other side, she goes, “And that’s real, because that guy is my friend, and I know him.”

Ivan:
Oh wow!

Spencer:
(laughing) And I said, ”We just generated a referral within about four minutes from asking questions to ChatGPT.” And people were like, “What? This is incredible!”

Ivan:
It is amazing that you can literally have a conversation with ChatGPT and it will give you information. Sometimes it gives you misinformation. So you have to be careful.

Spencer:
Oh sure.

Ivan:
It is really good at giving information that then enables you to, you know, do referrals, or, in one case, I needed it to help me produce a survey. And so you just, you ask it questions, and you’re right, you’ve got to dig deeper. So, you know, if you don’t have everything, then ask for a little more information and more specific information.

Spencer:
I probably asked six or eight questions just to get to where it got to. And then people were amazed. Here’s the fun part. I said, “You guys want to see one step further?” Now this goes into advanced AI crazy, but I said, “Can you now take this…” (and they happen to be a Friday afternoon chapter). I said, “Take this and make me a spreadsheet with the first column being every Friday, starting next week for the next 52 weeks. Then next to that, I want to have another column that is our LCD, like the motorcycle injury. Give me the 52 most common LCDs,” and it gave me all those. And then it gives me the person to ask for and it made the whole thing. And I said, “Make it a downloadable spreadsheet, and it did.”

Ivan:
You’ve got so much more content, and I want you to finish up on this, but I’ve never done this. I’m doing it twice in a row. Would you like to come back next week and finish the content that you’ve got on this? Finish your thought about that spreadsheet, and finish the rest of the information, and we’ll do a part two of AI and Networking.

Spencer:
I would love to.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Spencer:
The way that ended was very simple. I said, “Now put, you know, each one of these columns, so I want this one to be the dates of the Fridays going forward. Here’s my LCD, here’s my specific ask of who I’m looking for, and then give me the words for my actual presentation, so that I’m prepared and ready and can practice it every week.” And it literally generated a spreadsheet with 52 weeks. And everybody sat there scratching their head, going, you’ve got to be kidding me, it can actually do this. I’m like, now you’ve got to go through and make sure that those make sense. And the best way to do that would be do this yourself and do it on your own ChatGPT account so it knows it’s you, and prompt it through.

Ivan:
We have to pick this up next week, Spencer. We’re about out of time, but let’s continue this dialog about the spreadsheet.

Spencer:
Love it.

Ivan:
Okay, all right. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. That’s really exciting information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Spencer Reynolds explains how you can use AI tools like ChatGPT to be more effective at One-to-Ones and Weekly Presentations.

title.rendered: Episode 898: What Makes a Perfect Member? (Part 2)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Greg Davies-Polaine returns this week to expand on the qualities of members who score 100 points in BNI’s traffic lights system. Traffic lights are a measure of your engagement with your BNI membership. They track activities that have the potential to make you money–things you do to build your own business.

Use traffic lights to lean into your BNI membership. Don’t try to change or innovate. Follow the system. Ask members with high scores to share how they make the system work for them and learn to make it work for you.

Links

  • Be sure to listen to Episode 897: What Makes a Perfect Member? (Part 1).
  • Find out more about Greg at https://www.thestoryfella.com/.

Complete Transcript of Episode 898

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at home in Austin, Texas. And, you know, people think of Texas and all they think is hot, but the truth is, in the last couple of weeks, there’s been almost a full week below zero or below freezing in Fahrenheit, so below 32 degrees.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
Almost an entire week. So it’s been pretty chilly here. I’m glad I have a generator now for when the power goes out.

Priscilla:
Oh, right.

Ivan:
So I’ve asked a guest to come back, Greg Davies-Polaine. Greg has been to my house working on some of our books together, and we’re doing a new one that’s in the works — Infinite Leadership. Greg is known as the Storyfella, and he is an international inspirational speaker and award-winning speaker, and is a big Disney fan, which I should introduce you to a couple of BNI Directors that are big Disney fans. Greg, we’re finishing up on the podcast we did last week. I thought there was so much good, juicy content in it that I wanted to continue the discussion. Can you give like a 30-second overview of the value of a 100-point [BNI] member, and then we’ll do the rest of the questions.

Greg:
Absolutely. So thank you for having me on again, and we recognize that 100-point members represent less than 1% of the BNI membership each and every month. And why other members should be so focused on 100-point members is a riding tide really does lift at all boats. So their activities will positively influence those around you, and we can replicate the good habits that they have in place and put them in play into our own membership so that we can get more.

And the three main attributes that came back from the survey was 1: They are passing referrals with intent. So BNI is integrated in their day. They’re asking people how they can help at every single level, whether it be clients, suppliers, family, friends, how can I help you today? What can my network do for you?

2: They have an incredible mindset around visitors, realizing that there are so many wins in the visitor inviting process, both for them, personally, their business, for visitors that they are inviting, the chapter, the venue. So the whole mindset — it’s not about how I need to get a visitor along; it’s about everyone wins when we start inviting people.

3: And the big one, which I know is a passion piece of yours, Ivan, is they follow up. They follow up with absolutely everything. They follow up after One-to-Ones, referrals, presentations, open networking. They’ve just got the art of follow up down to just an absolute T.

Ivan:
And you know, sometimes members look at the kinds of things that it takes to be 100-point member, and we’re talking about traffic lights, for those of you who don’t know… and they think, well, this is stuff that I’m doing for BNI. No, this is stuff you’re doing to build your business, and to help other people so they help you build your business. So these are not things to do for BNI, these are your marketing techniques, your marketing activities to do through BNI, not for BNI.

Greg:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
With that said, that was a good overview. Why would you say that we need the traffic lights in general?

Greg:
So one of my go-to sayings is, what gets measured gets improved. And one of our [BNI] core values is Accountability, and a lot of that is personal accountability. And when we conceive our figures in black and white, we know where our own personal shortcomings are and where we need to work on. And what I love about traffic lights is if you passed a single referral to a member that was worth a million dollars, it wouldn’t get you 100 points on the traffic lights. Because whilst that is incredible for that member that you pass a referral to, it wouldn’t necessarily translate into business for you. I mean, hopefully they will be more motivated to pass you business in return. But there’s no guarantee.

So the traffic lights track a broad spectrum of activities, which, if you put in place consistently and persistently over time, you have a greater opportunity for success in your membership. So it’s just a beautiful tool to allow us to keep ourselves accountable to the activities that, when we put them in play, will give us the best opportunity for success.

Ivan:
You use the phrase doing things consistently over time. For years I’ve said, if you want to be successful, you’ve got to do six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times. And this is a great example of that. If you’re doing the things that will get you to score high in traffic lights, you’re doing six things 1000 times, and you will be successful as long as you continue to do those consistently.

Greg:
Absolutely. And what I adore about the traffic light system is we only ever score the stuff that has the potential to make you money. So all of the activities there are there because they have a chance of you getting a really strong return on investment from your membership.

Ivan:
Hey, here’s a trivia question for you, Greg. Do you know what country created the traffic lights system that is now used worldwide [in BNI]?

Greg:
No. I’m going to claim the UK and Ireland.

Ivan:
You’re correct. Tim Cook was the person that put it together. I’m sure it was with Martin and Gilly Lawson’s assistance and direction, but Tim put the program together in the UK and Ireland many, many years ago. And it’s now a system that, you know, we’ve tweaked it over time, but it’s a system that’s used worldwide to gauge engagement.

Greg:
And we’ve just completed a book on legacy. What an incredible legacy for Tim to leave. How many additional millions of dollars’ worth of business are now in our local communities because people understood the activities that they had to put in place. It’s a wonderful thing.

Ivan:
It is. It’s a great legacy. All right, so if you joined as a new [BNI] member today, what would you do differently given the amazing feedback that you got from this survey?

Greg:
Thank you for asking me that one, and I’m laughing because I was a terrible member when I started. I was the definition of a 90-minute member, and it was only because of the philanthropy of those around me who flung a BNI arm around me and said, “Do you realize what this could do if you did it right?” that I was able to continue and be successful on my journey.

So the short answer to that is, I would lean in. I would realize everything on the traffic lights that had a score against them were there because it gave me the opportunity to earn more money, rather than it being a tool to rank members or even a stick to beat people up with. It’s an engagement tool. It shows you how engaged you are with your membership. And even better than that, it shows you the other members that are, at the time, more engaged for you than you are, and I would seek those members out.

Ivan:
Yes.

Greg:
Ask them, “What are you doing? How are you doing this effectively?” And what’s incredible about our community is normally the first question out of people’s mouths when you sit down with them is, “How can I help you?” So not only will these people be very visual because of the traffic lights because they’re doing the right activity, but in my experience, they are tripping over their selves to share their knowledge, share their experience, and they genuinely want your success and then celebrate when you are successful.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Greg:
It’s just an incredible organization. And the traffic lights, our member traffic lights, allow us all to be successful just due to the collaboration that we have in place.

Ivan:
Yeah, and we know where we stand, and you’re right. It’s great to go to those people that are in the green, are doing really well, and ask for their advice. And we’ve been talking about 100-point, but let me just add, when you can hit 100 points, that is amazing. And it’s a celebration. It’s a dancing on the roof celebration. And what is, I think, even harder, is to be in the green for a year, even if you’re not 100 points for a year, but if you’re in the green for a year, you’re doing a great job. And, you know, shoot for that, and then shoot for the 100 points, because clearly, those people are really putting it together.

Greg:
It is about the very last quote that I took from the survey, and we mentioned it on the [previous episode], someone has just answered: Do it consistently.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Greg:
And it is about consistence. And the more we can level out our activities so we avoid peaks and troughs, the more compound effect kicks in, and we build upon success, upon success, upon success.

Ivan:
Yeah. Why reinvent the wheel? This program has been going for more than 40 years. You said you weren’t a good member. I’ll be honest. I wasn’t a good member when I was a member. I was method-acting my way through the process. I was young. I was trying to figure it out. I’ll tell you what I did do was I wrote things down that worked. Like you took this survey and found the things that work. I wrote these things down that worked, and I started creating manuals on what worked. I even had manuals on what not to do for my staff. You know, don’t do these things, make sure to do these things, and we figured it out. And it’s taken decades to figure this out. Follow the program. Don’t try to reinvent the wheel. Anything you want to add before we wrap up today, Greg?

Greg:
Just thank you very much for having me on, especially over Valentine’s Day. I know you must have been incredibly busy. And I would just reiterate the last thing that I would do, I implore everyone to do. Lean into the membership. Don’t try to change or innovate. Follow the system. Find a system that works, which is a wonderful BNI system, and then work that system to your benefit.

Ivan:
Okay, my friend. Thank you very much for being on, and I’m sure I’ll have you back. You know, we certainly, when we come out with Infinite Leadership, we should have you and/or Julian on as well.

Greg:
Thank you very, very much.

Ivan:
All right, take care. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, thank you so much to both of you, because that was some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 897: What Makes a Perfect Member? (Part 1)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Storyfella Greg Davies-Polaine (né Greg Davies) shares the common traits of perfect BNI members who score 100 points in BNI Member Traffic Lights. Their success has an impact on all the surrounding members and the surrounding BNI community. Best of all, their attributes can be easily replicated and obtained by anyone at any level.

  • They pass referrals as a normal part of their day, by asking their clients what other help they need whenever they talk to them.
  • They’re good connectors in search of referral partners, with incredible One-to-One strategies.
  • They make inviting visitors part of their business.
  • They follow-up on everything.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 404: Standing in the Middle of Referrals
  • Listen to Episode 517: Achieving the Power of One
  • Listen to Episode 693: Givers Gain and Storytelling
  • Find out more about Greg at https://www.thestoryfella.com/.
  • Read Networking Like a Pro, 2nd Edition.

Complete Transcript of Episode 897

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just did a live BNI Brazil event on Instagram, and they always have a huge turnout when I do these. So that was exciting. And I did, at the end of last week, something called Direct to Founder, which I opened it up for BNI Directors anywhere in the world to do a couple of webinars with me and ask me whatever question they have. So, those are great events. And I’m back in Austin.

Priscilla:
How many people were there in Brazil?

Ivan:
I don’t have the final count, but I know last year was around 1000.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow.

Ivan:
I’ll get the final number soon.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
So today, I have as a guest, somebody who has been on my podcast once before on storytelling. And [he is] mentioned many times because Greg has done some books with me. Greg was co-author of Infinite Giving and Infinite Legacy, and he and I are working on another book with [co-author] Julian Lewis. Greg has added something to his name. Greg Davies is now Greg Davies-Polaine, and I love that you added your wife’s name to your name, Greg. I think that’s unique and pretty cool.

Greg:
Thank you very much.

Ivan:
So for those of you who don’t know Greg, he has the best job in the world. He’s slightly embarrassed about calling it a job, because he tells stories for a living. He’s known as the Storyfella, and he uses that telling of stories to help communities and individuals, certainly in BNI. You know, he believes that working hard sometimes is unavoidable and necessary, but if we understand why we’re doing it and surround ourselves with people who inspire us to be better, it’s worth the effort. I couldn’t agree more. Greg is a bestselling author, multi-award winning director of BNI, and an international inspirational speaker, Greg still finds time to drum with the mighty Goat Patrol, which I’m assuming that’s a band, right Greg?

Greg:
That is a band, yeah. It’s not a collection of barnyard animals.

Ivan: (laughing)
Glad to hear that. All right, Greg, we’re going to talk about what makes a perfect member. So why should members care about being a perfect member?

Greg:
So let me just give you a little bit of background. But before I do that, thank you so much for having me on. I feel absolutely honored to be on for the second time. So really, really privileged to be on this. So thank you. We set up an event in the UK, and it was a brilliant member called Russell Frost down in BNI Berkshire, actually our co-author’s Julian’s region. He was 100-point member. And what that means is we track all of the attributes that give you the opportunity to earn money in your membership, and we put a score against them, and 100 is the very best score you could get. And he approached…

Ivan:
And that’s part of traffic lights, because we just did a podcast on this topic — traffic lights. And so you’d be top of the green with that number.

Greg:
Absolutely, yeah. So you’ve maxed out every single point that you possibly can and he came to me and said, “You know, can we do anything to let the 100 point members from across the UK and Ireland get together and network?” I mean, we’re talking about less than 1% of the membership every single month in the UK and Ireland hit 100 points. We’re talking about the elite, elite. And so I put together an event. I hosted it. We had members of the national team come on. We raised some money for the [BNI] Foundation, and I reached out to you and asked if you wouldn’t mind sending a message. And you said, “I’ll do you one better than that, Greg. If you ask them, What makes a 100 point member? we will record a podcast.” So we’ve now been running that event for over 12 months. We’ve raised over $1,500 for the BNI Foundation, and I have some tangible survey results. And here I am. I am back to tell you what they say makes a perfect member.

Ivan:
I am looking forward to hearing this. Go ahead.

Greg:
So first of all, why should other members care if they’re not at 100 points? Well, one of my favorite sayings by JFK [U.S. President John Fitzgerald Kennedy], or certainly been attributed to him, is a rising tide lifts all boats. These guys that represent less than 1% of the membership aren’t just passing business to the other one percenters. They are passing business and opportunities; they’re mentoring people. They’re upskilling their other members around them. They are increasing success within BNI exponentially, and their success will have an impact on all the surrounding members and the surrounding BNI community. So their attributes can be easily replicated and obtained by anyone at any level, and that’s why members should really care about what these guys are doing.

Ivan:
So what are the common traits?

Greg:
There’s three I’m going to talk about, and I’m going to kind of give a very quick summary and then a quote taken directly from the survey for each one. Now the first one you might press the podcast “end” button because it sounds so straightforward, you’ll probably both roll your eyes.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Greg:
But the first one they do, Ivan, is they pass referrals, and that does sound absolutely, you know, mind numbingly simple. And yes, they’re competitive, and yes, they see it as gamification, but they really do integrate the referral passing process into every facet of their life. So on multiple occasions, there were lines that came out in the survey, such as, “What’s a BNI Power Hour? BNI is part of my day every day.” And they do incredible things, like before they put their phone down with their customers, they say, “What else can I do to help you today?” And when their customers say, “Well, what do you mean by that?” They say, “Look, I meet with 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 local businesses. I’m connected to hundreds of local businesses in the local region. What do you need? How can I help you?”

I had a commercial landlord on and answered the survey, they actually go to their tenants and say, “What do you need to help you grow your business?” Can you imagine that? A landlord coming to you and rather than just holding their hand out and saying, “Give me some rent,” they’re saying, “How can I help you earn more money so you can get a bigger office and, you know, you enjoy seeing me once a month?”

They’re also really good connectors. They are looking out not just for single referrals, but referral partners, and they do that by having an incredible One-to-One strategy in place and really getting to know their members so that no buying signal passes them buy. And the quote for this one is, “My reticular activating system is on all the time, hence why I hit my target of giving at least one referral per day. And I’m currently sitting on 394 for the year.” And that answer was given in November. So that’s the first trait, they pass referrals, and it’s just a natural part of their day.

The second trait is their visitor mindset. They understand that by inviting visitors, it is a win-win-win-win- win… I mean, the wins just go on right away from the very initial invitation, even if that person says no, they then struck up a dialog and they could become their client. When they do attend, the visitor themself gets a huge amount of benefit. They realize that there are hundreds of like-minded, forward-thinking business owners, just like themselves in the local community. They might pick up business. They might pass business. The venues are happy because they’re paying for more for more covers than they normally would. They really understand the visitor mindset.

And the great quote from this one was, “I make visitor inviting part of my business. It’s important to broaden connections and speak to more people who can help your network. They can introduce you to potential referral partners, or even become referral partners themselves. And this is why I reach out to so many people about BNI as a lead-in and that allows me to get in front of them for my business and my members’ businesses at the same time.” Incredible.

And the last one, Ivan, I know you’re gonna love this one, is they follow-up, but they follow-up on everything. So they follow-up with visitors before a meeting, after the meeting; they follow-up on referral requests they’ve heard and say, “Can you explain a bit more about that? How do I introduce you? What do I say if they ask this question?” They follow-up on referrals they’ve passed. They follow-up on referrals they’ve received, and they do it consistently and persistently over time. The fortune really does come from the follow-up.

Ivan:
Yeah, the follow-up is really important, and I talk about it in a number of my books, including Networking Like a Pro, the Second Edition, the 24/7/30 Follow-up System. So, yeah, follow-up. But what good is going to these networking meetings if you don’t follow-up with people?

Greg:
It’s incredible, isn’t it?

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, listen. You’ve got so much content here, and I know you have more. I’m going to start winding it down here. And are you up to being on again next week and we’ll continue this conversation?

Greg:
I’d absolutely love to be. Yes.

Ivan:
Yeah, because I don’t want to cut you short. I do want to mention, you mentioned reticular activating system, and while you were talking, I looked up on BNI podcast. And if you’re interested in really understanding what the reticular activating system is, go to episode number 404, 4-0-4, called Standing in the Middle of a Referral. And it talks about the reticular activating system. And I think I tell a story there about how I slept through an accident in front of my house, but as soon as I had a baby, I woke up immediately when they whimpered. So you know, your reticular activating system changes when you’re thinking about something else or someone else. It’s a real powerful story. Episode 404.

Greg, I’m gonna invite you back next week to continue this conversation. We’ll maybe review the common traits and then talk about traffic lights in general. Are you good with that?

Greg:
I’m absolutely. What an honor. Thank you very, very much.

Ivan:
My pleasure. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Well, I think that’s it for this week. T

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
