excerpt.rendered: Greg Davies-Polaine shares the common traits of perfect BNI members and explains how to get the same results they do.

title.rendered: Episode 896: The 5 Secrets of Successful People

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Y.P. Lai, National Director for BNI Thailand and BNI Philippines, explains that the five key fundamentals that BNI asks members to do are actually fundamental business principles that follow universal laws of success.

  1. Turn up to impress.
  2. Continuous learning.
  3. Build connections and relationships.
  4. Create value for your business associates.
  5. Help your clients to grow their business.

If you plug into the five key fundamentals, you’ll be successful in your life and your business through BNI.

Complete Transcript of Episode 896

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I just did a Board Meeting in Charlotte where the BNI Global Headquarters is. And for everyone listening to this, this is International Networking Week®, the first full week of February. So welcome to International Networking Week.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
And I’m really excited to have somebody from our international team here today. His name is Y.P. Lai. Y.P. is an Amazon Best Selling Author, a master global trainer, a certified dream coach. I’m guessing that’s with Michael Gerber?

Y.P.:
That’s with Marcia, one of our keynote speakers at the BNI Global Convention in Poland.

Ivan:
Yeah, we’ve had some pretty amazing speakers at the Global Conventions. So Y.P. has trained thousands of business owners worldwide. He’s a National Director for BNI Thailand and BNI Philippines. He has over 30 years of corporate experience. I didn’t know this about you, Y.P. You trained as a civil engineer.

Y.P.:
(chuckling) Yes

Ivan:
Y.P. simplifies complex ideas into actionable steps. He is absolutely a mentor to many, many BNI Directors and members throughout the world. Y.P. welcome to BNI Podcast.

Y.P.:
Thank you. Thank you so much, Dr Ivan. And it’s a pleasure to be here and good to, you know, hear you in person, Priscilla. I’ve been hearing so many podcasts.

Priscilla:
Oh, thank you.

Ivan:
Lots and lots of podcasts. And Y.P., we’ve known each other for many, many years. Just call me Ivan, please.

Y.P.:
Okay, yeah, sure, Ivan.

Ivan:
So today’s topic is the Five Secrets of Successful People. So could you share with us the five key fundamentals that members often see as a BNI thing, but in fact, it’s really what successful people do.

Y.P.:
Okay, just before I share the five, you know, the five secrets, I like to say that, you know, if Y.P. says I don’t believe in gravity, I go to the tallest building anywhere in the world and I jump down, and what’s gonna happen to me? I’m gonna go splat. Because whether I believe in it or not, you know, gravity is a universal law, and it always works, right? So the thing about BNI is BNI uses the universal law of success and the universal law of business. And so I see that these five key fundamentals that we always ask members to do are actually fundamental business principles, you know. So what are they?

Number one: all successful people turn up to impress. They just don’t turn up and let things happen. They prepare to make sure that whatever meetings, whatever presentations, they’re always giving their 100%.

Second thing is that, you know, successful leaders are always learning. They’re always learning. You know, the business world is so competitive now we have got to be always upskilling ourselves to do things, you know, better, faster and cheaper than our competitors. Otherwise, we’re gonna get left behind.

And the third thing is building connections. You know, sometimes people think that building connections is all about networking to get clients. But I think it’s more than that. You know, when you talk about building connections, you got to build connections and relationship with authorities, the money people, your bankers, equity investors, you know, so that when you need money to grow your business, they are there. With your suppliers. Okay, we know that in the last couple of years, many businesses had to fold up because their suppliers went bankrupt and so on. So we’ve always got to be networking for plan B, plan C, you know, alternative suppliers.

Then the next one is, you know, the fourth level is your industry, people in your industry, because you’ve got to know where your industry is heading. Sometimes, you know, you got all these big brand names that when they were not, you know, in tune with what the changes in their industry, they got blindsided, and suddenly they become irrelevant, huge telco companies, you know, films. So all these are situations where they got blindsided, they were not in touch, you know, of the changes or the trends in the industry.

And the last one is, of course, networking to get clients. Okay, so these are the five things, which I call the five key fundamentals that we encourage BNI members to do.

Ivan:
Can I just review those real quick for everybody? Turn up to impress; continuous learning, what we call lifelong learning in BNI; build connections and relationships; create value to your business associates; and last, help your clients to grow their business.

Y.P.:
Yeah.

Ivan:
I know one of the things that you recommend on those five points is making sure to do One-to-Ones. You want to talk about that for a moment?

Y.P.:
Okay, sure One-to-Ones is actually, you know, a session where you get to learn more about the other person and find out what they are. It could be at different levels, okay. At initial level, find a bit about their interest, their family, you know, what’s their hot buttons? And later on, as the trust and relationship builds, then you continue on to focus a lot more about their business. You know, what are the target markets? What are the leading questions that you can use to ask them questions?

Ivan:
Yeah. So one of the things you mentioned related to turning up to impress, is to prepare for the meetings. There’s a couple of things that I have in mind. One is, don’t wing it with your weekly presentation.

Y.P.:
True.

Ivan:
Don’t just make it up as you’re speaking, but to come in prepared. What else would you recommend? I’m sure you recommend being prepared for that.

Y.P.:
Yeah. So there are various, you know, activities in BNI, so you mentioned the weekly presentation, there’s a feature presentation, even as you mentioned about One-to-Ones, you know, I always say, come prepared with questions. Okay, so for One-to-Ones always encourage members to exchange their profiles, you know, the speaker profiles, their G.A.I.N.S. profile, so that you can have a read of those questions. And then when we meet, it’s not about finding the details. It’s like asking relevant questions from what you’ve gleaned from reading the profiles, you know? So this is actually helping it to be even more productive as a One-to-One, rather than just basic fact finding.

Ivan:
Yeah, right. And as you do, if you do more One-to-Ones with somebody over the years, you really, you want to go deep. It amazes me how people don’t really know if someone has kids, how old their kids are, how long they’ve been in business. You know, when you know more things about somebody personally and professionally, you build a bond or a relationship with them, wouldn’t you agree?

Y.P.:
Yeah, definitely, definitely. You know, sometimes in a Chapter, you ask the members, do you know the chapter members well? And everybody will raise their hand and say, Yes. Then you ask them, do you know whether they are married? You know your members, are they married, with the kids and so on? And they go, “Ah, uhhh…”

Ivan:
Right. Yeah. And that’s where the One-to-Ones come in helpful and the [profile and G.A.I.N.S] exchange. One of the things that I’ve been saying in recent years that I didn’t say in the beginning is that I recommend, if you’re going to do the G.A.I.N.S. exchange, that you do your goals first, then I do my goals, then you do your accomplishments, then I do my accomplishments. One of the problems I ran into in the early days was somebody, if you have an hour, somebody takes 45 minutes to do going through their list and the other person only has 15. Love One-to-Ones, but share, go back and forth as you do it. Anything else you want to tag on to those five steps?

Y.P.:
Actually, you know, one of the good things about BNI is that now these are the five key things that all successful business owners do, and BNI has got the traffic lights, or we call the members traffic lights. It actually is a success indicator. I don’t see members traffic lights as a report card. I see that it’s a success indicator when I’m doing one of the five key fundamentals. Well, it gives me a green indicator to say that, hey, you’re doing well, Y.P., keep it up. And for those that I’m just started, not doing so well, I could get a red indicator. So actually, we’ve got a Success System, or success tracking system in BNI, to help us focus on the five key fundamentals to make sure that we are successful not only in BNI, but also in our business.

Ivan:
Yep. I mean the things that you’re talking about — turning up and lifelong learning and creating value, these are things that don’t apply just to BNI, as you said. They’re things that apply to any successful business. Here with traffic lights… the traffic lights measure things that will help you be very successful within BNI. And many of those things are, like, turning up, you know, showing up to the meeting. But there are other things, you know, green, you’re doing a great job if you’re in the green. Yellow, you’re doing pretty well, right? Red – you really need to start working on the fundamentals. And gray, Y.P., what do you think about the gray?

Y.P.:
I would say gray is showing that you haven’t even started on that. Okay, that is what area you got to start working on, yeah, and build yourself in that fundamental until you become green.

Ivan:
Yeah. So the things that you do to help move you up to traffic lights to certainly yellow and up to green are really important to help you be successful. I’ve had some people, maybe you’ve had this, too, Y.P., that have said to me, “Well, you know, I can just do things that manipulate it, and I can move myself into the green.” And yeah, that’s true, you can. You can do it for a month, maybe two, but you can’t do it for six months. You gotta actually be doing this stuff.

Y.P.:
Yeah.

Ivan:
We’re out of time. Y.P.. Any last thought before I hand it back over to Priscilla?

Y.P.:
Okay, I just say that for those BNI members listening, this is a success system. BNI is not plug and play, but it’s so easy to plug into the five key fundamentals and just do it and you’ll be successful in your life and your business through BNI.

Ivan:
Said by somebody who has had a lot of success in the organization. Y.P., thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast.

Y.P.:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Y.P. Lai explains that the five key fundamentals of BNI are fundamental business principles that follow universal laws of success.

title.rendered: Episode 895: Intentional Legacy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

History almost remembered Alfred Nobel as “The Merchant of Death.” His inventions in the field of explosives made construction safer and war more dangerous. When a mistaken newspaper obituary showed him his accidental legacy, he chose to create an intentional legacy by leaving his wealth to fund the Nobel Prize.

Even without Nobel’s wealth, you have the power to create an intentional legacy. True legacy is not in the success of your endeavors, but in the impact that you’ve made on others.

Read Infinite Legacy by Ivan Misner, Greg Davies, and Julian Lewis to find out how.

Complete Transcript of Episode 895

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. I just got back from Mexico, Puerto Vallarta area. I did a BNI event. I did a Corporate Connections event, which is a sister company to BNI. And then I did my annual TLC. Well, semi-annual TLC meeting, the Transformational Leadership Council.

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
It was a great week, got a lot done.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. And what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
I’ve kind of, I think, a fun story that I’m going to share. And this comes from my latest book, Infinite Legacy. And it’s a book that I wrote with Greg Davies and Julian Lewis. And you can see it on the right side of the podcast [website], where all the books are. And if you have a chance, check it out.

You know, as we have just had the 40th anniversary of BNI, started in 1985, I’m often asked about legacy and what I’d like to leave in life. And the question reminded me of a story of another businessman who almost left an “Accidental Legacy” instead of an “Intentional Legacy.” This is a long time ago, more than 100 years ago, sitting at a table in a café the middle of the 19th century in Paris, a successful inventor, industrialist, millionaire drank a coffee and set about updating himself on the news and current affairs.

And you know, while we benefit from notifications on our phone and streaming news 24 hours a day, back then, it was, you know, the newspaper — sitting there, drinking coffee and reading the news. And so he turned the pages, scanning different items, and got to the obituary section of the newspaper. And he was really shocked, because the headline read, “The Merchant of Death Is Dead,” and it was his name. It had his name and he read the obit to make sure that it was really him and not somebody else with his name. And, you know, he thought maybe it was just sloppy journalism, because his brother had passed away not long before that, and he realized it wasn’t.

And he was shocked at what the world would say about him as a successful chemist, engineer, inventor, businessman, and he held 350 patents, and this was back in the 1800s, he owned all those patents. And so the implication was clear, the person who he had become rich as, by finding ways to kill people faster… it actually said that in the obituary, “This person has become rich by finding ways to kill people faster, more [people] faster than ever before.” And as often is the case, inventions with noble intentions can sometimes lead to multiple purposes of the invention.

What he had invented was TNT, or dynamite, and he did it to safely mine resources from mountains and to help with construction. And this well-known story of mistaken reporting in the 1800s was the driving factor in his decision to change his legacy. So within a few years, he signed his last will and testament in Paris’s Swedish-Norwegian club. And he was 62 years old then, and he decided to use some of his personal fortune to support and encourage the work of scientists and inventors from around the world. And this document described a project that was far more ambitious than anyone could have imagined in a relatively short document.

It had less than 1000 handwritten words. It laid out a plan to devote a huge amount of his personal fortune, in fact, it was the majority of his estate, which was valued at almost a quarter of a billion dollars in today’s money, and he wanted to give them to these series of awards. These awards were dedicated to those who, during the preceding year, conferred the greatest benefit to humankind.

The awards consisted initially of five prizes. The first three were the greatest discoveries or inventions in a field of medicine or chemistry or physics. The fourth was a standalone prize in the field of literature, for the most outstanding work. And the final prize was awarded to the person who would have done the most, or the best, work for fraternity between nations and the abolition of reduction of standing armies. And in 1968 a sixth prize was added in the field of economics. So despite paying out multiple prizes annually, each with a current value of 1.1 million, so everyone who gets one of these prizes gets 1.1 million [US Dollars], the working fund, despite that, has grown to 570 million [US Dollars] and is used to recognize and achieve recognize some of the greatest achievements of humanity.

So the man’s name was Alfred Nobel. When you think of Nobel today, you don’t think of dynamite. As a matter of fact, most people don’t even know that he invented dynamite, but most people overwhelmingly know of the Nobel prizes, including the Nobel Peace Prize, which was one of those prizes.

And so he went from having this “Accidental Legacy,” this legacy of the creation of something that was used for good but was also used to harm people, and decided that he wanted an “Intentional Legacy”. Now I tell you this story to demonstrate that no matter where you are in your journey, no matter how you are currently perceived by those around you, you have the power and the opportunity to create the legacy that you desire. He did this in his 60s.

The notion of legacy is often entwined with ideas of wealth or historical impact. It’s deeply philosophical and in the book Infinite Legacy, we paraphrase Jean-Paul Sartre. I may have mentioned this in my previous podcast on legacy. We paraphrase Jean-Paul Sartre, who was a towering figure in existential philosophy, and he provided a compelling perspective on legacy through a poignant observation in his play, No Exit. He said, “One always dies too soon or too late, and yet their life is complete at that moment, with a line drawn neatly under it, ready for the summing up.” You are your deeds in life and little more.

It’s your contributions in life that make a difference. I’ve talked a lot in my podcasts about how we all have people that are in our story. Someone who has made a difference in our life, but the true legacy to leave is Whose story are you in? Whose life have you changed? Who have you helped in the world? And during our lifetimes, we all have the opportunity to create either an “Accidental Legacy” or an “Intentional Legacy.”

Nobel realized that he was about to leave an Accidental Legacy, one that he didn’t want. So instead, he determined to leave an Intentional one. It’s one that we all recognize today. We may not have the type of wealth that Nobel had for his legacy, however, we all have the power to make our own legacy intentional. True legacy is not in the success of our endeavors, but in the impact that we’ve made on others. And I say, leave an Intentional Legacy in the world by making a positive impact in the life of others before the line is drawn neatly under your life, ready for the summing up.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
And that is my message for today. If anyone is interested, that is one of the opening stories to the book Infinite Legacy: The Seven Principles of Creating a Lasting Impact with Your Life. And again, you don’t have to be rich to leave a lasting legacy. My parents had no money. My dad was a blue-collar worker. My mother had serious medical issues and was a stay-at-home mom. But my mother taught me… whatever people skills I have, I got from my mother, not my father. But I got my work ethic from my father; I got my work ethic from my father. And they left a legacy in that I learned to work hard, I learned how to connect with people, and I was able to build an organization that’s now in well over 70 countries around the world. And that’s a legacy that they left with the pebble that they dropped in the water and the ripples that come from that. And so leave an Intentional Legacy. Start thinking about what you’re doing to help other people in their life.

Priscilla:
Boy, that’s a powerful message. Really. I appreciate that so much. I’m going to have to get your book.

Ivan:
Yes, please. (laughing)

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
It really got me thinking, because, you know, the more gray hair I get, the more people ask me, you know, what’s your legacy? And my answer was probably one of the shortest answers I give to questions on stage, and that is, “Your life is your legacy.” And I started thinking about that. Well, you know, I need to explain that a little bit more. What does that mean? And this story about Nobel really clarified it for me. You want to want to leave an Intentional Legacy, not an Accidental one.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm. Amazing.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
Okay, Ivan, that was wonderful.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: You can create an intentional legacy without wealth. True legacy is the impact that you’ve made on others.

title.rendered: Episode 894: Your Business First

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI won’t work effectively unless you know what you want out of your business and how to translate that into the BNI process. Frank De Raffele explains how to lay the groundwork for success in BNI.

  1. Learn how BNI works.
  2. Make a one-page business plan.
  3. Develop a strategy for each of BNI’s tools every quarter.
  4. Embrace the Givers Gain mindset.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 673: Proactive Referrals.
  • Listen to Episode 581: What a Brigadier General Taught Me about Communication.
  • Listen to Episode 221: Your Business 101.
  • Visit Frank’s website, https://www.frankderaffele.com

Complete Transcript of Episode 894

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am near Puerto Vallarta, Mexico this week and doing three different organizational meetings. One is for Corporate Connections, a sister company of BNI. I’m doing another presentation for BNI, and then I’m attending the Transformational Leadership Council [TLC] meeting, which I’m a member of. And I always get great ideas for conventions from TLC. So I’m looking forward to this one in Mexico.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds like fun. And who do you have on the podcast today?

Ivan:
I have a guest who’s been on the BNI Podcast before, he was on episode number 673 “Proactive Referrals.” And his name is Frank De Raffele. Frank has been part of BNI for over 30 years. And 2025 is his 30th anniversary for BNI Mid-Hudson Valley region where he serves as the Executive Director. He is a New York Times best selling author of four books, an international speaker and MC, a workshop leader, he speaks on leadership, performance, and business development and success. And I consider Frank a really good friend. Frank, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Frank:
Thanks for having me. I appreciate it. So I know about every 250 I can be on. I’m excited about that.

Ivan: (laughing)
We can make it quicker than that if you like, that’s not a problem.

Frank: (laughing)

Ivan:
So your topic is “Your Business First,” and I love where you’re headed with this. Talk about the groundwork that’s needed as a BNI member to be successful in BNI, receiving profitable referrals.

Frank:
Well, I think there’s two things, Ivan. One is there’s got to be the groundwork of understanding how BNI works. When you get involved as a member, you’ve got to understand the tools, the resources that are available in BNI, and have that really taught you through the mentor programs, the trainings that we do. So that way, you understand the BNI concept and how it works. It’s not just a meeting to attend. It’s actually a way to develop it into a division of your business.

But it’s really not going to work effectively unless you have a very clear vision of your business, where you want to be in three to five years, and you have kind of the strategy set up of how you’re going to go about getting there. Basically having kind of your business plan together of really what you want to accomplish in your business in the next few years. Because once you have that clear on what you want to do, then you can say, “Okay, how do I want BNI to help me with that?” And then we can use BNI in its most effective manner.

Ivan:
So the second point you have, I think, is right on the mark, and that’s the business plan. You believe in starting off with a short business plan, correct?

Frank:
Yeah. I think it’s got to be a one-page business plan. You know, it’s something that’s practical, it is something that’s actionable, that you’re really take and go forward, and basically, it’s really knowing, I mean, we come down to a business plan in in this environment we’re talking about with BNI, is really about your marketing and sales. How are you looking to increase revenue over the next X number of years? What products or services do you want to offer to what target markets, and then how do you plan on reaching those target markets? Are there different verticals you want to go after? And once you know that type of detail, then that can be translated into BNI of exactly what you need to be asking for to make it work.

Here’s what I believe. I believe that BNI is about leveraging your time. It’s about leveraging your relationships, right? It’s about leveraging your knowledge. So it’s all about… when you hear a lot of people say, you know, how do I work less and make more? BNI is kind of the answer to that. You know, if you really know how to do it, you’re able to now, instead of me going out and making 30 sales calls in a month trying to reach all these different people, I now go to one meeting a month for an hour and a half, and I meet 30 or 40 people.

And those people, by me educating and training them, by developing relationships, they’re able to go out on a weekly basis and kind of be my part-time sales force. Go out there when they find the opportunity to refer me out, they can do that, but they’re not going to be able to do it unless I make it as easy as possible for them. And that means I have to do my homework. I have to know what I want out of my business and how to translate that into the BNI process.

Ivan:
Let’s go back to the one-page thing, because when I was a consultant, I worked with a number of companies, and they would want 20, 30, 40, 50, 100-page business plans. And those got put on a shelf and were almost never used. And I would always have them reverse engineer that. I had a professor, and I think I’ve talked about this in a podcast before, where they had us do, he had us do a 10-page paper, and then we had to cut that 10 page down to five, and then we had to cut it back down to two. And then he made us to a one-page summary.

And I got to tell you, it was although we wanted to just strangle this guy, because he did this over a period of weeks, it was the best exercise ever did, because that one-page summary of the 10 page paper we did, was what we would mostly utilize to be able to talk about that paper. And so that one page plan, it’s okay to have details, but that one page summary is what you’re going to mostly be working from, wouldn’t you agree?

Frank:
Yeah, I think 100%. I think the value of doing the 50-page business plan is there’s so much thinking that has to go into you have to do deep thinking going through it, and all of a sudden you’ve thought this all through. Pretty much once you’ve finished it, it’s obsolete, right? Because everything changes on a daily basis going forward. But because you’ve done that deep thought process, you can now start to cut it back, as you said, from that 50 to 25 to 10 to five to one.

And then it’s like, okay, and now that one page, when you read it, you know that it means 50 pages worth of stuff to you. So it’s very, very meaningful, but you’ve boiled it down to things that are the most important thing to do, and you can say, Okay, this is what I need. These bullet points are really what are going to make me happen. And you have the justification behind it, versus if you start out with just five bullet points, you’re not going to really know what that means.

Ivan:
Agreed.

Frank:
That deep thought process is important.

Ivan:
Alright. So what’s the third step?

Frank:
I think the key here with BNI is going to be so once I know my vision, I know where I want to go with my business, and then I learn that BNI process, then I have to say, Okay, what are the tools available for me in BNI so I can make it work? So if we look at this and say, Okay, well, I know we have the Open Networking portion of meeting — how do I use that most effectively? My weekly presentation, which I think is probably the single most important part of the meeting, because out of a 90-minute meeting, you have one minute to educate and train people on who you are, what you do, what are good referrals for you, how to recognize a referral, how to get into that conversation and how to get that person to actually say yes, they’ll take a phone call with me, if you will. Right? And there’s a lot in that one minute.

So how do you strategize that out to make it really work? And you’re not going to be able to do that unless you’ve thought out what you really want in your business. And there’s all different ways to do it. But a quick couple examples is, well, you know, let’s say you’re at the beginning of the year. Let’s say it’s January. So you could think of BNI as, Okay, what do I want to do for the first quarter of my business? And how do I want BNI to help me in that first quarter – January, February, March? And then you can put together a series, a 12-week series that you’re going to do in your weekly presentation. So each week it’s kind of like… here’s what I think about that. If I’m trying to get you to recognize a referral for me, I would imagine that in my head I have a very clear picture of what the perfect referral is for me. But my job is to take that post picture and put it in the brain of every single person in the room in my BNI chapter. I can’t do that in one week, but over, let’s say, 12 weeks. imagine it’s a puzzle, each week, I give them a different piece of the puzzle, both visually and in terms of knowledge of my business.

And week by week, they’re saying, Oh, I get it more. I see what Frank wants. Oh, I see what that means, because they’re tying together. And then by the end of those 12 weeks, they’re like, Yeah, I get what he wants. I fully understand now, because I’ve tied it together. So I’m actually doing one presentation, if you will, that is 12 weeks long, versus 12 different things that don’t connect to each other. And the only way I can do that is if I fully know what I want in my business, and that means my job is to make it as easy as possible for the people in BNI to get a referral for me. Not to make them work for it, but for me to make it easy for them, so when they leave that meeting and they go around during the week that all of a sudden, referrals for me start to pop out because I’ve given them visual and auditory cues to recognize things, saying, Oh, this would be a good referral for Frank. But again, you can’t do that unless you’ve really thought your business out and you know how to use those tools in BNI.

Ivan:
Yeah, there’s a variation of that that I did in a podcast. It’s episode 327, “Your Business 101,” episode 327. I’d recommend that people check that out. It’s similar conceptually to what Frank is talking about here. You also mentioned One-to-Ones being important. Anything you want to say about One-to-Ones?

Frank:
Yeah, I see One-to-Ones as being important in two ways. One is doing a One-to-One is about: 1) obviously, building trust and confidence in the relationship for us to get to know each other better. And 2) is to get a deeper understanding about each other. So one of the things that we do is what’s called the “Triple Play One-to-One.” We recognize every member… We, I’m sorry, educate every member to look if you’re gonna do a One-to-One with somebody, do a triple play. Make the first One-to-One a GET TO KNOW you one. So you and I have to get together and say, what do you do? What do you enjoy? What are your hobbies? That type of stuff. And I walk away and I say, “Hey, I like this guy. He’s really nice.” Because if you walk away and go that guy Frank, he’s kind of a jerk, then that means I’m not getting referrals. We’re not taking that next step.

Then the second One-to-One that we do is about, let me UNDERSTAND MORE ABOUT YOUR BUSINESS. What is your business? What is your service, your products? Who do you help? What problems do you solve? When do people need you, etc. Now I understand more about your business. So I like you. I understand your business.

And now the third One-to-One we do is what I call the REFERRAL COMMITMENT meeting. We’re going to get together and I’m going into the One-to-One with a mindset of, I’m committing to getting you a referral by the end of this One-to-One. Which all that really means is not that I’m going to have a referral, because referral means I’ve already spoken with the person, but I may walk away with two or three connections or leads for you that I can say, “Okay, I’m gonna reach out to these people now. I’m gonna follow through with it and see if I can get them to talk to you.”

Ivan:
So you’re recommending multiple One-to-Ones with somebody. It’s an interesting approach. We’re almost out of time, Frank. I know you want to talk about mindset. We’ve only got a few seconds left. You want to hit on that topic?

Frank:
Yeah, I think the greatest thing about mindset is understanding that I believe when you come into BNI, it’s perfectly fine and what you should do is focus and come in on a very selfish reason. You come in to make more money at your business. It’s very selfish why you’re in BNI. However, the process, the way that we do that, is very unselfish. It’s called Givers Gain. And you have to have that mindset of, look, I want to make money, but the only way I’m going to do this, we all have to contribute. We all have to kind of put it all on the table in front of us. So if I have the mindset of giving to other people, and everybody in the room is doing that, that’s pretty much the definition of synergy. We’re all coming in to help each other. And that’s, I think, one of the main things that’s really made BNI grow so much over the years, is that mindset of Givers Gain.

Ivan:
Yeah. Thanks, Frank. I really appreciate you. And of course, you’re in the Hudson Valley, you’re the Executive Director there. You also have a website, FrankDeRaffele.com. That’s Frank De Rafelle – that’s D, as in, dog, D, E, R, A, F, F, E, L, E, FrankDeRaffele.com, Frank, thanks for being on BNI Podcast again.

Frank:
My pleasure. Good to be here with you.

Ivan:
Thanks. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. That was wonderful. Thanks, Frank.

Frank:
Yep.

Priscilla:
I think that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI won’t work unless you know what you want out of your business and how to translate that into the BNI process. Frank De Raffele explains.

title.rendered: Episode 893: Lasting Relational Leadership

content.rendered:
Synopsis

As BNI UK & Ireland National Director Mike Holman explains, our foundations as leaders and as BNI members come through the relationships that we build. Ask yourself these questions:

  1. Do people really know me?
  2. How deep are my relationships?
  3. Whose story are you part of?

Solid business relationships go beyond the transactional. Your referral partners need to know your personal goals and the “why” of what you do. Take time to learn about them and show you care.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 112, Networking Lessons from Nature.
  • Listen to Episode 637, The Networking Mentor.

Complete Transcript of Episode 893

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I am in Austin this week at home. I just spent a better part of a week with my co-authors of another book I’m working on called Garage to Global: How do you take your business from your garage to a global enterprise? So that was an interesting week.

Priscilla:
That sounds interesting.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s gonna be a great book, I think.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that sounds like an interesting topic.

Ivan:
I have another interesting topic today. Someone whom I’ve known a very long time. My guest today is Mike Holman. Mike has been involved with BNI for 25 years. He started his journey as a BNI member first and then became a member of the [BNI Chapter] Leadership Team. And then two years into the journey, he was given the opportunity to start a brand-new territory in the United Kingdom and went on to actually own his own BNI franchise there. In June of 2022, he was given the honor and opportunity to become the BNI National Director for the UK and Ireland. And Mike says he proudly adores that role. Mike, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Mike:
Thank you, Ivan, pleasure to be here.

Ivan:
Good to have you. So we’re talking about relational, lasting relational leadership. Explain that.

Mike:
Yeah, there’s many structures around the world that I actually admire, and you look at these buildings and these structures, and they’ve been there for hundreds or thousands of years sometimes, and every one of these structures has an incredible foundation beneath it that supports it. Well, I truly believe, as a leader and as a member, we also need to have those foundations in place. And those foundations as a leader or member, really come through the relationships that we build. And if we want something that’s going to last a long time, then we invest into those relationships.

Ivan:
Yeah, so one of the first things on this topic that you wanted me to talk about was, “Do people really know me?” Tell me about that.

Mike:
Yeah, I learned a lot over the years, personally myself, and I remember a breakthrough moment when I really felt I developed as a leader. It was on the back of a lot of learning. It was on the back of Simon Sinek’s Start with Why. And I remember standing up in front of a membership; I was the [BNI] Executive Director, and I was trying to explain to the leaders of the region what we should be doing, the direction we should be going, and it was falling on deaf ears. People were looking at me thinking, “Yeah, all you care about is growing your BNI franchise. You don’t really care about us.”

And I stopped, and I just basically told them what my purpose was, and I told them, really from the heart, about why I cared deeply about their success. And it completely changed the environment in the room. People come up to me afterwards and said, “I really understand what’s driving you,” and I’ve used that ever since, really, to try to inspire other people to share their story. I read a title or a quote in a book many years ago now. I can’t remember the person who said it, but it was “Leaders are leaders, not due to their rank or title, but their ability to influence people to want to change.” And that always stayed with me. And I also remember a quote of yours, Ivan, which is, if you want to influence people, inspire them and transform them, tell great stories, and there’s no greater story than our own story when we’re building relationships.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think a lot of people in leadership roles are so focused on the How, they forget about the Why. Not only their personal why, which is what you’re talking about, and I think that’s important. And I found that when I was visiting individual chapters, I was basically a Director, I would always go there and say, “Look, I care about your success. I want you to be wildly successful in this organization. And that’s why I’m here, and that’s why I’m offering the suggestions that I am.”

Because people do naturally go to, “Well, you just want to make more, sell more, whatever business you’re in.” Everybody always thinks that about you. But if you start with your personal Why… and also, I would add to that, the Why related to the topic that you’re discussing. So if you’re talking about attendance, why is attendance important? What’s the data behind it? What does it show? But people don’t care about How until they understand the Why, and when you can show that attendance absolutely increases the number of referrals in a [BNI] Chapter, then people are willing to listen to the How. So the Why factor, to me, is critical.

Mike:
I remember, Ivan you said, I think I must have heard it 20 years ago for the first time, and thankfully, many times since, you’ve always said people don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care. And that goes through every part of what you’re saying. Their understanding of why we encourage people to do the things that they do in any aspect of their membership, to help them get the best out of their membership.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know, you can talk about how you care, but you also need to show them that you care. There’s a an old quote, let’s see if I can remember it. I cannot hear the words you’re saying because of the behavior you’re showing, or something like that. It’s a phrase like that, in how you behave, how you act, shows whether you care or not.

Mike:
Yeah, and I’d really encourage all our members to really tell people their story, [their] Why. And we have an amazing tool in BNI, Ivan, and I’m sure you created the G.A.I.N.S profile.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
And I think sometimes we get a little bit too transactional with that G.A.I.N.S. profile. We look at it and we think about the goals of our business, the competence in our business, the interest. But taking that to a more personal level really helps people connect with one another. So what are your personal goals? What do you want to achieve for yourself, your family? What’s your ambitions? And when we get into that personal level with our fellow members, that’s when people, I truly believe, truly open up to one another, because we may be the expert at what we do in our industry, but if people don’t genuinely connect with us because they don’t really know us, then they can be reluctant to be part of us, those referrals that we all truly would love to see our members get.

Ivan:
Yeah, and I think a lot of people bypass that in the G.A.I.N.S. exchange. The G.A.I.N.S. exchange is written in a way that talks about some transactional issues, like, you know, what are your Goals and what are your Accomplishments? But your Interests are really relational. And we tried to break it up so that, you know, people were trying to focus on both. And it’s very important. I found that quote, by the way: What you do speaks so loudly I cannot hear the words you’re saying. And so, you know, it’s important to say these things and then behave that way, walk the talk in what you do. So talk to me about the depth of relationships, depth versus breadth of relationships. I know that was one of your topics.

Mike:
Yeah, absolutely. And again, about relationships in BNI, I encourage all our members think about the room of people around you. We think about the depth of relationships you have with people. We have a great opportunity to get lots and lots of relationships in BNI through our chapter, beyond our chapter, but I encourage people to really invest in those key relationships in their room that they have around them. It’s amazing. We have chapters at 100+ members all around the world, but every single member probably needs, I’m not sure, what do you think, Ivan, probably 8 to 10 really good, strong relationships they can manage and focus on and get the most out of.

So are we actually really investing into those people, getting to really, truly know them, helping understand the referrals they want, how we can educate and train them how to find the referrals that we want? So I think that’s really important that people do invest if they want to build a relationship with people, that they invest deeply into those relationships and not have the scattergun approach to relationship building.

Ivan:
Yeah, two things on that. One is, if your network is a mile wide and an inch deep, it will never be very powerful. So it needs to be both wide and in places deep, which is what you’re saying. You need to have some relationships that do, in fact, go deep and you know them well, and they know you well, and that’s the only way you’re really going to build a powerful personal network.

Mike:
And you reminded us of that great story of your own where you talked about the roots of a tree.

Ivan:
Yes.

Mike:
How there was a storm. Do you want to share that one again? I love that.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve talked about it on a [BNI] podcast before. When I lived in Southern California, there are these eucalyptus trees that were brought from another country into Southern California. And in Southern California we have these Santa Ana winds that were really, really high winds. And it’s oftentimes in the news, you’d see these trees that had fallen over. And they’re almost always eucalyptus trees. And the reason they’re eucalyptus trees is that those trees have a a root system that spreads very wide but doesn’t go very deep. So when it gets really tall and the wind hits it, it knocks it over. And when I saw that when I lived in Southern California, I thought of members who when they were going through difficult times, they had this wide network, but it didn’t go very deep, and so their business would just fall over because they didn’t this wide network beneath them, which I think is absolutely critical.

Mike:
I love that story.

Ivan:
Yeah. So your last topic that you wanted to talk about was, whose story are you part of. Talk about that a little.

Mike:
Yeah, again, it’s really… I used to say all the time, BNI changes people’s lives. And actually, no, it doesn’t. People change people’s lives.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mike:
BNI is the operating system, and we have the ability to bring people together, and it is people that change people’s lives. So I’d ask everybody to think about, whose life could they be impacting this week, next week, next month? Who is the person you see the greatest potential in? Who is the person who’s behavior may have changed and you little bit concerned about them? Who is the person you’re indebted to? Who is the person you’d really like to see be a success? And just invest into those people. We talk about the principles of BNI, Givers Gain, but I truly see the Givers [who] give are the ones who are the true leaders and the people that other people admire and respect in BNI. And I would just encourage everybody just to reach out to one person, because they can really influence that person and change somebody’s life by being in their story.

Ivan:
I agree completely. It’s not who’s in your story, but whose story are you in? that I think makes a difference in people’s lives. We are literally almost out of time. But I know you had a short… maybe you can make it a short story about your childhood.

Mike:
Well, I mean, a lot of people asked me this and said, “Well, what was the story you told your members that influenced them?” And it’s simply this. “When I was growing up, I came from a very working-class background. My father was a bus driver, and as he was a bus driver in the 70s, he’d be working lots of shifts at the weekend. He could earn more money doing that, and he’d work double shifts even at the weekends. The result was I didn’t get to spend much time with him.

“So growing up, me and my dad didn’t have a great relationship. He was never a bad dad. I was never a bad kid. We just didn’t get much time together. But it did mean that relationship was a little bit soured. And then I realized, only later in life, the reason he did it. He did it. He worked those hours, he sacrificed time with the family because he needed to put the clothes on the back, he needed to put the roof over our head, he needed to put the food on the table.”

And I turned around to our members and said, “I never want any of our members to have to sacrifice their family for their business or their business for their family. I truly believe BNI can help you in all aspects of your life. My father had no choice. You do.”

Ivan:
Yeah, what a great story. And it’s good that you shared that with your members, and that kind of personal connection can make all the difference. Mike, I appreciate what you do, and thanks for your many years in BNI. I really, I really do appreciate it.

Mike:
Thank you, Ivan, it’s been an absolute pleasure all those years.

Ivan:
All right. Well, we’ll need to have you back on BNI Podcast.

Mike:
Thank you, sir.

Ivan:
Priscilla, back over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was wonderful. Thank you. That’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. title.rendered: Episode 892: Infinite Legacy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In honor of the 40th Anniversary of BNI, Dr. Misner shares the 7 principles for leaving an intentional legacy from his new book, Infinite Legacy.

  1. Be proud of the legacy that you leave.
  2. Give what you can afford when you can afford it.
  3. Be guided by your passion.
  4. Connect with your community.
  5. Act with urgency.
  6. Think like a stonemason.
  7. Have an infinite mindset.

Links

  • Get your copy of Infinite Giving.
  • Get your copy of Infinite Legacy.

Complete Transcript of Episode 892

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin, Texas this week, and this is a special week, Priscilla, because it is the 40th anniversary, January, 8, 2025 is the 40th anniversary for BNI.

Priscilla:
Wow, that’s impressive, Ivan. It really is.

Ivan:
You know, when I started BNI as one group in Southern California, I honestly didn’t think at that time that this was going to be a global organization. That hit me later in the year, as I saw the chapters just increased like crazy.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
I decided a couple years ago, with my co-authors of Infinite Giving to do a book and have it come out right around the time of the 40th anniversary for BNI, and the book would be about legacy. And so I just, a couple of months ago, came out with a book called Infinite Legacy. I absolutely love the opening of this book. It’s fantastic, and I’ll share the opening of the book, maybe in a future podcast. I think it’s probably one of the strongest openings of a book that I’ve done. But here I want to kind of do an overview of the principles of leaving a legacy in life.

The opening of the book does talk about whether we leave an accidental legacy or an intentional legacy. Whether we’re just kind of going through life without thinking about the impact we’re making, or whether we’re leaving an intentional legacy. And I think more and more as, certainly as people age, they are motivated to shape their legacy. And I started thinking about legacy a few years back.

As I seem to get more gray hair, people would ask me what do you hope your legacy will be? And my answer has always been, “Your life is your legacy. How you live your life is the legacy that you leave behind.” And with this book, we wanted people to shape the legacy they leave behind. We wanted people to leave an intentional legacy and not an accidental legacy.

And so we talk about in the book The Seven Principles of leaving an intentional legacy. And the first is to be proud of the legacy that you leave. Everyone leaves a legacy, so be aware of that and create one that allows you to be proud of yours. And legacy is a conscious choice. And by the way, a lot of people think, well, legacy, you know, you don’t really leave a legacy unless you’re a multi-millionaire and you’re leaving a lot of money and yada, yada, yada. And that’s just not true. We all are able to leave a legacy.

I look at my parents, who were, you know, we grew up in a blue-collar family. My dad worked multiple jobs. My mom had health issues, but they left an amazing legacy. Whatever people skills I have I got from my mother. My mother was a people person, and she would just talk to everybody. And I got my work ethic from my father. My dad was a hardworking man and a family man too, and he spent time with us. And so I feel like my parents left a legacy. Even though they didn’t have money, they left a legacy, and their legacy was how they raised their children, how they raised me, and they raised me so that I could do the things that I have been able to accomplish in life. And so you don’t have to think of a legacy as being financially based.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
It’s what you leave in other people that can be your legacy. And along that line, the second principle is, give what you can afford when you can afford it. And from here, we mean give from your saucer, not your cup. I know somebody who wanted to start a nonprofit, they were very entrepreneurial, but they wanted to start a nonprofit organization and commit a lot of their time to this nonprofit.

I said, you know, “Do you have the financing to start this nonprofit?”

He said no. I said, “Man, you are so entrepreneurial. Go start the business you want to start, make a lot of money, and then financially support the nonprofit that you want to do. You know you’re doing it in reverse. You’re going to put in tons of time, and you may not have the success that you want, because you’re not going to have the money that you need.”

And he didn’t do it. He just went with the nonprofit, and the nonprofit unfortunately failed. And so now he’s doing the entrepreneurial thing. So he couldn’t afford to do what he did.

Give what you can afford, when you can afford it. As your cup gets bigger, it will overflow, and it will overflow into a bigger saucer. And so as you’re more successful, it can overflow into… and whether we’re talking about money or time. As you, as you have an opportunity, maybe you can give more time; It’s not just money.

The third is to be guided by your passion. And we have Mike Holman coming in a week or two, who’s going to be on [the BNI Podcast] and he’s going to talk about the importance of your Why. And I’ve talked about why many times, and you’ve got to understand your why in order to align with your with your legacy. Just know that I believe in passion. It’s very important to be passionate about what it is that you’re doing, and you have to have commitment. Passion without commitment is just an interesting idea. You have to have that commitment and the passion. And most of my career, I’ve talked about it’s the importance of following your passion, and I haven’t always talked about the commitment. But what I’ve discovered over time is that you’ve got to really make a strong commitment to that passion in order for it to be successful.

The fourth principle is connecting with your community. These are all the principles that we talk about in Infinite Giving. It’s not who’s in your story, it’s whose story are you in? It’s about the people that you’ve made a difference with in your community. And your community starts with your family, and it goes to the street, your neighborhood, through to the larger community, to your BNI community – the people that you know in your BNI organization, and then beyond that, you know, the greater area that you might live in. Connect with the community and make a difference. Richard Branson talks about this with circles. You know, you start with a very tight circle, and the circle gets bigger, and the circle gets bigger and the circle gets bigger, and as you become more successful, and again, not just with money, but maybe in what you do professionally, then your community can broaden.

Act with urgency is the fifth principle; act like someone is watching. We will be judged on the legacies we leave, not the ones we intended. And so you want to act with urgency on how you show up in the world.

The sixth one is an interesting one. I love this one. It’s to think like a stonemason, and in the book, we tell a story about a nobleman walking up to a stone mason and asking the stone mason what he’s doing. And he said, “Sir, my lord. I am shaping the stone, chipping it off on the sides so that it can be handed over, sanded down a bit, and then given to another stone mason, who will place it into the wall.” And the nobleman goes to a master stone mason, and he says, “May I ask what you’re doing?” And the master stone mason says, “My lord, I’m building a cathedral.” The same exact job, but with a bigger vision. And so part of having an infinite legacy is to have that big vision of building a cathedral, not just chipping away the stone. And I think all too often in our life we’re chipping away at a stone as opposed to thinking about the larger vision that we have for the impact that we want to have.

And that’s the seventh principle is to have an infinite mindset. True legacy is not the success of our endeavors, but in the impact that we’ve made on others. And so the importance of legacy is evident in the way it inspires and motivates future generations, and it can provide a sense of continuity and a connection from the present to the past to the future, giving people a sense of belonging and identity.

And we thought that this is the perfect topic for our 40th anniversary, and hoping that BNI is a legacy that will change, continue to change lives. To me, it’s not just a business, it’s a movement in changing the way people do business. And that’s the reason why we came out with the book Infinite Legacy, which, by the way, hit one of the best sellers lists on Amazon recently.

Priscilla:
It sounds great.

Ivan:
So that’s my message for today, Priscilla, if you have anything you want to add.

Priscilla:
The only thing I want to say is that the podcast itself has been ongoing for quite a while. I think it’s 17 years.

Ivan:
17, 18 years, yeah.

Priscilla:
18 years?

Ivan:
Yeah. I think we started in 2007.

Priscilla:
Yeah, yeah. So that’s kind of impressive, too. I think.

Ivan:
Yeah, it is. There aren’t too many podcasts that have lasted over a decade, and we’ve certainly done that. And you’ve been there since the early, early parts of it. In the first 50 [episodes]…?

Priscilla:
No, even, I think it’s even like in the 40s [episode numbers].

Ivan:
40s, yeah. Well, and you’ve now been a BNI member for 21 years. You started, I think you started BNI when you were like 17.

Priscilla:
(laughing) Oh, yes, I was just a teenager at the time.

Ivan:
Well, I invite people to check out the book Infinite Legacy and think about the legacy that you want to leave in life, and the biggest legacy that you can leave is in the people around you. And I can say that certainly about my parents. They didn’t leave a financial legacy, but they left a legacy in me, in how I should show up in the world. And I’m very grateful for the legacy that they left.

Priscilla:
That’s nice.

Ivan:
Thanks, Priscilla,

Priscilla:
Oh, you’re so welcome, Ivan. Okay, well, that’s it for today. Thank you so much for that great podcast.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 891: Be Prepared, Always Win

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The prepared always win when it comes to networking. Guest host Tim Roberts explains how to prepare for networking at holiday social events and make your conversations meaningful.

  1. Start with three questions you’d like to be asked to start a memorable conversation.
  2. Prepare meaningful answers that show your passion and practice them.
  3. Ask these questions of the people you meet at the event.
  4. After they answer, they’ll ask you the same questions.
  5. When you follow up, they’ll remember your questions and your answers.

Complete Transcript of Episode 891

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by Tim Roberts, who’s standing in today as a guest host for Dr. Misner and will be joining us from time to time. Hello, Tim. Welcome to the podcast.

Tim:
Hello. Thank you again for having me. And yeah, today we’re going to talk about how the prepared always win when it comes to networking. And I think a lot of times when people hear that title, they may think about BNI and the importance of preparing our Weekly Presentations and preparing our Featured Presentations. And those are absolutely true. We want to be prepared in those moments. We want to have a clear plan of when and what topics we’re going to talk about; you know, build a marketing plan for the year. We’re gonna take a little different approach today. Talk about networking in general and in BNI, but how to take advantage of the fact that most people never truly do prepare for networking.

As we enter in the holiday seasons, we’re getting busier; there are more and more events coming up, and a lot of those events will be social in nature, whether it’s a Chamber [of Commerce] after hours, a holiday party, a company holiday party, family, friends, whatever it may be, we have an amazing opportunity to really be out, be engaged, to be networking. Unfortunately for most, present company included, I would say all of us in general, we never really know how to take advantage of that situation.

We might know that we need to be there. We might know we want to be active. We might know that we know that if we don’t go, we won’t get anything. But how do you actually prepare for a social networking opportunity? How do you actually make it meaningful? How do you actually make it valuable to you? I think what happens to a lot of us is we’ll show up to those events, and we will naturally gravitate to those we already know. We’ll find ourselves talking to the same people. We’ll find the conversations to be kind of… they’re great, but they’re not really valuable. They’re not really purposeful in helping us accomplish any kind of goal, and how do we change that? And it takes just some simple preparation. And realizing that you can really take advantage, for lack of a better term, of a lot of other people in that situation, by knowing they didn’t prepare; that they’re feeling very similar to you. They don’t really know how to get something out of it. They’re there. They want to be there, but they haven’t really prepared for that moment.

So, this is a very simple tip I’ve been teaching for many years, and I wanted to share across here with everybody else. What you want to do is first, think selfishly. Think about what kind of conversation could you have at this event that you know, based on your answers, it would be an impactful, meaningful, valuable conversation for you. Meaning, what questions would you want to be asked that you know, based on your answers, would provide value. Value to me in these kind of moments isn’t a tangible referral at that time. I would hardly suggest anybody have that as a goal. You should not be going to a Chamber [of Commerce] after-hours [event] with the intent of getting a referral that day.

The intent is to start a relationship. The intent is to be remembered after that event. So when you follow up with them and want to schedule a one-to-one, or schedule a meeting and schedule the further development of that relationship, they remember you and that the conversations you had were rememberable [sic] and positive. So under that kind of guideline, what questions would you want to be asked that you know, based on your answers, would make that conversation valuable to you? I would think of strong business, yet also personal type of questions. So not a personal question; it has to be business founded, but is not just “Tell me what you do. What product do you offer? Who do you work with?” Something that has a little bit more meaning, something that maybe others are not asking or you’ve never been asked before at this type of event.

Some examples of these things would be, “What’s the most common misconception about your business?” “What’s your favorite part of your profession, or least favorite part of your profession?” “What’s the one job or client that really sticks out in your mind? And why does that?” “How did your past experiences lead you into the profession that you’re doing today?” “What’s your why? What’s your purpose behind what you do? Where does your passion come from?” “If you had to start all over again, what would you do differently?” “What’s your biggest challenge?”

These type of questions are again, founded on business but really kind of personal, right? They’re not fluff. They’re not just the same question everybody else is going to be asking them. They take thought and they trigger emotion. Now, the best conversation anybody ever has is the one where they do the most talking. So the best way to be most memorable is to get other people talking.

So what you want to do is prepare. What are the three questions you would really want to be asked? Really think about your answers. How would you answer a question like “What past experiences led you to do what you’re doing?” Or “What is your passion behind your profession, and where does that come from?” Really think deeply on how you would answer that. Besides, yeah, I just really like what I do, or, you know, I really like helping people. Those are fluff, generic, surface level answers. Really think about those questions. And part of the preparation might be to have somebody, a spouse, a friend, a colleague, ask you these questions and dig for some meaningful answers. Think about them, prepare them.

And then what you do is you simply go to that event and you walk around asking other people those questions. And what this is going to do for you, that little bit of preparation is going to make you stand out amongst everybody else at that event. You are going to be the one who asked questions nobody else was asking. Questions that, again, triggered some kind of emotion, questions that led to a meaningful conversation for the other person, questions that are going to make you memorable.

And what happens after they answer that question? When they’re not prepared with their own list of questions, they naturally regurgitate the question right back to you. And now you can give a meaningful, well-thought, well-prepared answer to the question that you wanted to be asked at that event.

This is the same thing and the same strategy you can take into your open networking at your BNI meeting. You won’t have time to do three of them, and maybe you change the question each week. Can you change who you’re talking to each week? It’s why open networking is so valuable in our meeting. It’s not a buffer zone to show up whenever you feel like or you know you got that 15 minute extra window. This is the only time at the meeting you can have these types of conversations. Great way to approach a brand new member. Great way to approach somebody you haven’t talked to in a while. A great way to set up that next one-to-one. You can ask a question. Say, I want to learn more. Can we schedule that next one-to-one? And just that little bit of preparation is truly going to make you win in the game of standing out and being remembered after that meeting. That’s all I’ve got today.

Priscilla:
Wow. Well, that’s really interesting. Thank you. I appreciate that. That’s it for this week. Thank you for joining us, Tim, and thanks so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
