title.rendered: Episode 890: Finding the Good in Bad Times (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 695, first released on February 24, 2021. Some links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Finding the positive in difficult situations is hard, but not impossible. Dr. Misner shares 7 takeaways from 2020 to get you started reflecting on the good that came out of a difficult year.

  1. Micro-dose the news to avoid negativity.
  2. Look for opportunities, like a chance to spend more time with your children.
  3. Hope + a plan + action will lead you successfully through challenges.
  4. During difficult times, your network will be there for you.
  5. If you wait until tough times are over, your business will be over.
  6. You can travel the world without getting on a plane.
  7. Friends are more important than ever.

So, what are your takeaways from 2020? What was something positive that you learned or took away from the most challenging year in recent history? Share your observations in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 890/695

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I have recently been to BNI Mid America, that is the central part of the United States, where I did a large online event. So, still visiting regions all around the world.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Well, tell us a little bit about this topic.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, as you might suspect it, I came up with this content because of 2020, the year 2020. And it’s all about really looking for the good in a bad situation.

I ended 2020 with a humorous graphic on my social media that said this, it said, “Dear 2021: I don’t want any trouble from you. Just come in, sit in the corner, don’t touch anything, and keep your mouth shut.”

You know, that was pretty, I thought it was pretty pithy, and kind of funny. But there’s an important flip side to this sentiment. And that flip side begs the question, “what did we personally learn from 2020?” And I think that’s an important question to consider.

Complaining about things that go horribly wrong is easy. Finding the positive in difficult situations is not. And I believe that finding the gems in life is the best way to move forward successfully.

I learned a great deal from 2020. And I have a lot of valuable takeaways for me out of 2020. And here are seven takeaways that I got from last year.

  1. I’ve talked about this in a podcast, so I won’t go into much detail, and that is micro dose the news. I learned that to get what I need to know from news apps and not to obsess about the problems by watching the news hour after hour. Negative news sells and the media has become a master of sharing negative news. Technically, I think it’s opinions much more than actual news, but they’ve become a master of it.
  2. A really valuable lesson I observed involved a man that told me that he was able to keep his job, and I think I may have mentioned this in a podcast, but that he had to work from home and he also needed to care for his children and help them do their schoolwork. And he told me, he was really stressed out about doing that double duty. And during our conversation, he came to the realization that this was actually an opportunity to spend much more time with his children. And although it was challenging, it might be a good time period where they all look back with fondness because of the experience they had together. And when he embraced that idea, his vision of the situation completely changed. And that was a lesson that I hope that many others with children experienced.
  3. Third is I witnessed focus beating out fear. And I know I talked about this in podcasts and a lot of my writings, focus beat out fear during 2020 for many entrepreneurs. When people are frozen by fear, they had no hope. But when that fear focused them, they had hope and they were much more likely to weather the storm. Hope is more powerful than fear. Hope is that little voice inside you whispering to you about what “can be” when everyone around you is screaming “what can’t be.” Hope plus a plan, plus action, will lead you successfully through challenges.
  4. Fourth, I learned that during difficult times your network will be there for you. I saw people support each other emotionally and professionally. I witnessed people give one another ideas that kept their business alive and thriving. Two of the best examples included the furniture re-upholstery shop that became a COVID mask manufacturing company with all of the material they had, and a brewery that became a COVID hand sanitizer company with the alcohol that they had for the brewery.
  5. I learned that if you wait until tough times are over, your business will be over. During difficult times you need your network more than ever. You need BNI more than ever, Social distancing is the wrong phrase. Physical distancing is what’s necessary at this time, and hopefully, this will change this year. We need to be more social than ever, though. And I saw successful businesses activate their network and not abandon their network.
  6. I learned that I could travel the world without getting on a plane, I love that. I have 2.3 million miles on one airline alone. And it was a gift to be able to spend months with my wife at home last year, particularly last year. It was the longest period of time that we had together in our 31 years of marriage, and I’ll forever be thankful for that.
  7. I learned that friends are more important than ever; that the quality of the people in your life, or in your room as I call it in one of my books, will determine the quality of your life. And the quality of the people determine the quality of your life.

For me, that last lesson was profound. It was a tough year for me as it was for many people. But I came out of 2020 with a clearer vision of who my friends are. And I understand who truly cares about me as an individual, and as a person, as a result of 2020. It was something that I learned. And I will forever be grateful for that.

So I suggest that if you’re listening to this podcast, you don’t completely write off 2020 and erase the year from your memory banks. Instead, I suggest that you consider what you learned from last year and what your positive takeaways are from that experience. You’ll be a better person for it.

So, what are your takeaways from 2020? What was something positive that you learned or took away from the most challenging year in recent history? Give some thought to this question and write down your observations here on this podcast. I’d love to read the things that you come up with, that were takeaways for you in 2020. Positive takeaways, it’s easy to come up with the negative ones, we all have a big lesson, just watch the news. The positive things that you came out of 2020. And I believe that reflecting on this, that kind of reflection, will help you recognize the good that took place during one of the toughest years on record. And so Priscilla, I’m gonna put you on the spot here. What good came out of this for you? Anything come to mind?

Priscilla:
Well, I have to go along with what you said in terms of how people were really there for each other. And I saw that in my BNI group. I saw that in our Nextdoor neighbors group, you know, there’s a online Nextdoor group that you can be a part of. I saw people helping each other through that. And then you know, just in my own circle, you know, people being extremely kind and loving, even though we were maybe, had to be on Zoom or whatever. And that’s a fun thing. Being on Zoom with your friends, and having birthday parties on Zoom, and being able to connect all over the country and pull in your friends and your family to situations that before you would never think of doing that.

Ivan:
Right.

Priscilla:
And so that was something really positive.

Ivan:
Yeah, listen, that’s exactly what I’m talking about. And so if you’re listening to this podcast, I’d like to ask you to do what Priscilla just did. And name a few things that were positive that came out of 2020. Because if all we do is look at how bad 2020 was, and it was bad for all of us, it certainly was for me, I lost my wife last year. And so, it was tough on all of us. It’s still good to reflect on what we got out of 2020 moving forward. And I think that’s the best way to be successful in life. So thanks for your suggestions, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it’s so nice to look at the positive. I always love, I always try to do that.

Ivan: Yeah, as do I. Because it’s so easy, we live in such a negative world, that it’s so easy to just obsess about the problems and challenges, but generally, the people I’ve met in life, who are really successful, tend to be, not everyone, but they tend to be a lot more positive and look for a positive future, as opposed to obsess about a negative past. So that’s everything I’ve got for today, Priscilla.

Everyone, make sure to post your comments. I promise you, I will read them all. Thank you very much. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla: Oh, perfect. Thank you so much, Ivan. I really, really love this podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares 7 takeaways from 2020 to get you started reflecting on the good that came out of a difficult year.

title.rendered: Episode 889: Taking a Leadership Role in Your Chapter

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Serving on the leadership team in your BNI chapter gives you an opportunity to increase your visibility and therefore credibility. By making your chapter more successful, you make your own business more successful. Plus, you develop both life and business skills that many people pay to learn, as guest Phil Berg explains in this episode.

Links

  • Find Phil online at https://linktr.ee/PhilBergCoach.
  • Listen to Episode 404: Standing in the Middle of Referrals.
  • Listen to Episode 689: The Only Difference Between Online and Offline Is the Spelling.
  • Listen to Episode 888: How to Educate Others to Refer You.

Complete Transcript of Episode 889

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin this week, but I’m doing lots of online programs, including the Founder’s Education programs, which I do every month. And [BNI] members can find them up on [BNI] Business Builder if you don’t drop in live. So I’m getting out there these last couple of weeks, but I’m doing it through Zoom.

Priscilla:
Perfect.

Ivan:
I have a guest again this week on our program. Last week, he’s been on BNI Podcast before, and that’s Phil Berg. Phil is consistently invited all over the world to share his knowledge and expertise on topics such as effective networking, leadership, team development, management, effective communication and goal setting, amongst other things. I consider Phil a good friend. He’s been to my home. We’ve worked on a book together. We’ve got a book coming out next year, and I’m excited to have him on my podcast with this topic in particular, which is about taking a leadership role in your chapter. Phil, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Phil:
Thank you so much, and it’s lovely to be here again.

Ivan:
So Phil, how specifically can being on the [BNI Chapter] Leadership Team benefit a person in business, a member of BNI?

Phil:
I think first and foremost, there’s a difference between being seen and being known. And I think a lot of people get seen, but they don’t necessarily get known. And I think there’s limited opportunities to increase your visibility and increase your credibility. And I often think that if you’re unknown, then by default, you reduce the opportunity to be remembered when someone has a need. So for me, being on the Leadership Team gives you a beautiful opportunity to kind of show who you are, show how you are, etc.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s funny, isn’t it? How if you’re really good on the Leadership Team, that kind of rubs off on your credibility in whatever business you’re in. It’s like, well, they’re good at that, and there’s kind of a natural assumption that they must be good at their business as well. Would you agree?

Phil:
I think so. You know, the old saying, as I said on a previous podcast, is, you know, the way we’re seen as doing anything is the way we’re perceived as doing everything.

Ivan:
Right, exactly. And the exposure is, I think, sometimes not completely understood. I remember once a person got in the newspaper for being the new president of a chapter, and an old friend reached out to him because he saw this newspaper photo, and it was like, “Hey, I saw I saw you in the newspaper. I moved out of town. I’m back here on business.” They had breakfast, and they hit it off so well, he ended up hiring him–he was a contractor–to do a multi-million-dollar building, a commercial building, in the city, and it happened because he was in a photograph as the new president of a [BNI] chapter. So, you know, taking on the Leadership Team role gives you exposure within the chapter, but it also gives you exposure outside as well.

Phil:
Yeah, and to the visitors, you know, I mean, you know, I was a member for a long time, happily, until I sold my business. And the reality is, you know, when you think of a visitor’s experience, you know, it’s amazing the visitor’s experience, and you’ve got to be one of these people that when they leave, you’re remembered. And what a wonderful opportunity to create that.

Ivan:
So some people are concerned about the time it takes to do the role properly. What are your thoughts on that?

Phil:
Well, I always say, “Are you doing a role? Or are you using the role?” If you think of BNI as an income strategy, then it doesn’t become an inconvenience. It actually becomes a strategy, as I just mentioned, for you to have that opportunity to increase your visibility and therefore credibility. I also think you have the opportunity to influence the continued growth and success of your chapter and to leave a legacy. And how amazing is that?

Ivan:
Well, I think it’s amazing. I just did a book called Infinite Legacy. So, I think legacy is pretty important.

Phil:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And you know what? Your life is your legacy, the things that you do and how you show up in the world is an important part of your legacy, and being on the Leadership Team is, I think, also an important part of that. Is it developing a BNI chapter, or is it about developing a business group, this thought that you had?

Phil:
Yeah, when you when you think that the goal of the BNI chapter is to help all the members increase their business, increase the quality of their clients, you know, earn more money using BNI as a strategy, then ultimately, you want to be thinking that continually having a direction to make the chapter even more successful. And when you can do that, that’s more participating members looking out for each other, you know, we make a difference. And how amazing to lead the group in making a difference, not just to ourselves personally, but to our businesses, our families and the community. I think it’s often more than people realize. You know, we sometimes just look at the people around us, but it’s the knock-on effect, and I think we make an amazing difference. And how fantastic would you be when you are leading that?

Ivan:
Alright, so knock-on effect is a British term. You want to explain that for those of us not from the UK?

Phil:
Sorry. So I would say the ripple effect. In other words, when… there’s something called velocity of money, so when a transaction is made, the person receiving the money, they may receive 100% of the money, but they have to redistribute a percentage of that money to other people. So for example, when I was in the carpet and flooring business, you know, you may give me, say, $2,000 to put some carpet down for you, but of that $2,000 I’ve got to give some of that to my installation team. I’ve got to give to some of the manufacturers who pay their staff to make the carpets. The money gets re-spread around the community. So we make an amazing difference.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. The ripple effect is important. And by the way, don’t worry about using British term. I’ve used American terms in the UK and gotten myself in trouble. So… (laughing) Yours won’t get you in trouble. So did you ever take a Leadership Team role in BNI?

Phil:
I did. And you know, if I’m honest, I never joined BNI to be the chapter president. In fact, I’ll never be the chapter president. And then you have to realize that, you know, part of being a giver is to be part of the team. You can’t just turn up to the meeting, receive the referrals, give a few out and just think that you’re a good member. There’s a difference between a good member and a good person. And I realized that to have the opportunity to work with some great people and continue the development of the chapter to even more success. You know, how wonderful to have that opportunity. And as I understood more and more that we are a business group, it’s nice to be able to not control it myself, but it’s nice… You know, it’s very difficult to moan about somebody else if you’ve never done it yourself, so you have to experience it.

Ivan:
So this is a question that I’m just going to throw at you. Do you feel like you learned some life skills or business skills by being on the Leadership Team that you maybe wouldn’t have learned otherwise?

Phil:
What a brilliant question. Without doubt and put on the spot, I learned communication skills. My communication skills and the way I delivered the messages were far in advance of, if you like, the way I would have delivered the messages to my own — I don’t like the word staff, but it is the word staff. So, you know, in my own company, I realized that I might have unintentionally delivered a request if, you like, more as a “Hey guys, we need to do this.” Whereas when you are delivering this message to your peers who don’t work for you, I definitely learned to deliver messages in a far more effective way. I definitely learned a lot more about seeing things through, accountability. And you know, I took those skills into my own business. So superb question, without doubt.

Ivan:
And so it benefited you to be on the Leadership Team. This is one of the things that I tell people, and they don’t quite understand it, but it really does benefit you, if you take it seriously and do the work seriously.

Phil:
Well, think about like this. This has just entered my head. And it has just entered my head. How much would anyone, me included and any of our listeners, how much would anyone pay to learn the leadership skills that you can naturally get being a leader within your group? People would pay 10s and 10s of thousands for that skill.

Ivan:
Yes.

Phil:
So, you know, yeah. I mean, yeah, I’ve just thought of that. I mean, definitely, without doubt, a benefit.

Ivan:
And certainly, if you immerse yourself in the content. There’s so much content available for BNI members, and particularly for Leadership Teams: online Business Builder and, of course, this podcast, and our books and our manuals, there’s so much that if they immerse themselves in it, they’re going to be better businesspeople as well as better members and leaders.

Phil:
Yeah. And also, you have opportunities being on the Leadership Team that you may not actually have, respectfully, being a member. For example, there will be Leadership Team events. So you know, you have an opportunity to do what you can’t just do without being in that position.

Ivan:
Well, we’re out of time, my friend. Is there any last comment before we wrap?

Phil:
Yeah, just think of it as personal development and continuing to build your reputation, rather than the time.

Ivan:
Yeah. Yeah, being on the Leadership Team will make you a better businessperson and possibly a better person in general. So, yeah, I agree. Great stuff. Phil, thanks for being on the podcast. Phil’s website [Coming Soon!] is PhilBerg.coach. That’s Phil: P, H, I, L, B, E, R, G, dot coach, C, O, A, C, H. PhilBerg.coach. Phil, thank you so much.

Phil:
Thank you very, very much. Be well.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, that was great. Thank you both so very much.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Serving on the leadership team in your BNI chapter gives you an opportunity to increase your visibility and therefore credibility.

title.rendered: Episode 888: How to Educate Others to Refer You

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Coach Phil Berg returns to the podcast to explain how to build relationships in BNI to empower people to want to help you, and then give them the tools they need to have the ability to help you. When crafting your weekly presentation,

  • Go deep when you identify your ideal client: industry, specialty, company size, position.
  • Be specific about your value to clients and how you help them.
  • Provide a line of entry–an example of what to say to prospects.
  • Explain what makes you better than your competitors.

Links

  • Find Phil online at https://linktr.ee/PhilBergCoach.
  • Listen to Episode 404: Standing in the Middle of Referrals.
  • Listen to Episode 689: The Only Difference Between Online and Offline Is the Spelling.

Complete Transcript of Episode 888

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am home in Austin, Texas, to celebrate in the U.S. the U.S. Thanksgiving. So I’m going to be with family this week and celebrate the Thanksgiving holiday.

Priscilla:
Oh, great. Okay, and who do you have with us?

Ivan:
I have a really good friend of mine. He’s been a friend for a long time, Phil Berg. Phil and I and Bharat Daga are working on a book called Networking is a Marathon, Not a Sprint. You’re gonna hear more about it next year. It’s a great book, and [I’m] looking forward to it coming out. Phil has been on the BNI Podcast before, it was at least once. Episode 689, “The Only Difference Between Online and Offline Is the Spelling,” which I love that title. So he’s been on before. Check that out. His business career started in the retail industry in the 1970s and then in parentheses, he says, “Yes, I’m old.” He started by selling carpets and flooring on a market stall, leading to building and a well-reputed retail flooring company. And he was a member of BNI from 1997 until a transaction that he had in 2007, so he’s been in BNI a long time. He specializes in helping companies, and his expertise really is in effective networking and leadership. Phil, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Phil:
Thank you so much, and happy Thanksgiving.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate that. So you’ve got a couple of things here that I think are really interesting in terms of educating others how to refer you. And I think this is critical. I think this is the thing we really get wrong as members, is that we just do our shtick instead of educating people how to refer them. So you have three tips that you want to share. One is that it’s not about you. Talk about that.

Phil:
Yeah, I think the reality is, because, you know, public speaking, etc. is, you know, a very big fear, I think people need to become better and better educators, become more confident. And I think one of the things they need to start understanding is that it’s what people need to hear as against what we fill the time in. So people tend to stand up and say, “I do this. I do that. I want this. I want that,” instead of thinking, “What does the other person need to hear to be able to refer me?”

Ivan:
Yeah, and that’s an educational process. You got to teach them how to think about you, isn’t it? Right?

Phil:
Correct. Yeah, it’s, you know, I always say, “Don’t teach people to be you. Teach people to connect you.” Build those relationships to empower people to want to help you, and then give them the tools they need to have the ability to help you.

Ivan:
So how can they be specific, not only about who they want, but how the referrers can approach them?

Phil:
Yeah, I think there’s ultimately, once I know who you want, really, the missing piece is to understand your value to the other person. I will be able to refer you so much easier when I’m doing the other person a favor by giving them you. So if I understand your value to the other person, then I’m going to actually be excited to introduce you, because they have a need and you have a solution, and then it simply becomes a good deed. But I need to know what their need is and what your solution is. If that makes sense.

Ivan:
Yeah, it makes sense to me. Can you give an example? Do you have any idea of an example that might resonate with our listeners?

Phil:
Yeah. So for example, you know, as we get older in our years, there may be a few aches and pains. And therefore I know somebody that is a qualified chiropractor, for example, that has specialized in, if you like, easing the pain of an older, more mature person. So it’s understanding that, you know, I really have someone that can make a difference to you, and therefore I can introduce you. So we need to start educating the people around us of our value to the other person.

Ivan:
The chiropractor really needs to talk at some point, obviously not every week, but about being able to help aging individuals with their aches and pains. So it’s really reliant on the chiropractor to educate, right?

Phil:
Correct, and it is totally the responsibility of the educator. It’s you know… I remember you gave me a fantastic line once, and it was years and years ago, and you said, “It’s not my responsibility to refer you, Phil.” And I remember thinking, “Well, yes it is.” And then you said this, and it was amazing. You said, “No, it’s your responsibility to teach me how to refer you,” and that stuck — big time.

Ivan:
Yeah, thank you. And I really still believe that with members, it’s the members’ responsibility to teach people how to refer them, because we’re not in that business, you’re in that business, they’re in that business. And they have to learn how to teach people to think about them. I’ve got a really good podcast on “Standing in the Middle of Referrals” which talks about that [Episode 404]. And go back and find that podcast if you’re listening to this one, because it talks about that at length. All right, so we know you want them, but why do they want you?

Phil:
So ultimately with that, we should think of ourselves as solution providers, if you like. I remember my young kid brother used to work for my flooring company, and he just said to me one day, he said, “You know, so why are we good?”

And when I look back at this, I can’t believe I answered it, but I said this very sincerely. I went, “Because we are.” And he went, “Well, I understand that, but why?” And I actually got a bit annoyed with him and said, “Well, I’ve just told you we are.”

And the reality is I learned to actually start searching, actually, “Why am I good? If I would like an introduction to somebody that’s likely to already be using my competitor, what compelling reason could I teach the people around me to give them the tools to say, “Hey, but does your existing supplier do this? Or would this be an added benefit?”

And I really learned to look into my own business and instead of taking it for granted, without being arrogant, but I just knew I was good, but when push comes to shove, I really didn’t know why, and I had to search for those answers.

Ivan:
Yeah, and then share that information. Because one of the things I say is, it’s not your confidence that counts. It’s my confidence in your ability to provide a quality product or service. And how do I gain that confidence? Well, I have to gain that from you. You have to show me why I should be confident in your ability to provide a quality product or service.

Phil:
Yeah. And as we know, the way we’re seen as doing anything is the way we’re perceived as doing everything. So the way we appear, the way we behave, etc., that’s going to give people confidence in kind of who we are, our values, etc. But then they simply need the tool. So a great one, for example, is a line of entry. So me, for example, this would help everyone listening. It’s teaching your referrers a line of entry. And a line of entry for someone like me, for example, as a sales trainer, “If you meet somebody that sells motor cars, you’ve only got to say to them, ‘Do your team sell as many cars as you as you like?'” The answer will always be, “We can always sell more.” or “No.” And then it’s simply saying, “Hey, you know, I’ve got someone that I truly believe could be a value to you. Would you trust me to take the call?” So there’s a line of entry if, if people could learn that it makes it easier for us to talk about you.

Ivan:
Yeah. So a lot of members feel they are being specific, and yet they still don’t feel they receive the referrals they want, or enough of them. I ran into somebody like that just a week or so ago. What are your thoughts on that?

Phil:
Well, I think people think they’re being specific because no one has told them they’re not.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Phil:
And we only know…

Ivan:
I told him he wasn’t because he shared with me what he did. And I’m like, “That’s not specific enough.” So, I agree completely, but I cut you off. Go ahead.

Phil:
No, no, exactly. I’m laughing with you. So I think being specific is going as deep as you can. You know, I always say, “Don’t give people around you the brains.” I mean, you know, compliments to you as well. You once said to me, “If I’ve got to do… if you can’t be bothered to do the research to teach me, then don’t ask me to be bothered.” And I think we’ve got to make it simple for the other person to understand who you want.

And the way to do that is to simply incorporate maybe a few things: “I’d like an introduction to this person and their name, ideally. This is the position in the company, or if it’s business to consumer, this is the age group. This is where they would live. This is the type, you know, they’d have two children at this age.” So it’s making me think of someone that you want. So ideally, if it’s business to business, name of the person, ideally the position they’re in, ideally the name of the company, and certainly the industry. Because I may not know that person, I may not know that company, but I do know somebody else in the industry.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s counterintuitive to people. They think that they need to be broad, but the truth is, the more specific they are, the more likely they are to get a referral. We’re almost out of time. Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Phil:
I think, do your research everybody. You know, the world has changed, and it is a lot easier to do your research on Google, etc. And just remember this, if you ask for small, you’ll get small. If you ask for big and use the same strategy, then everyone knows people you want.

Ivan:
And when you say, “Do your research,” you mean, “Learn how to educate people to refer you,” correct?

Phil:
Learn how to educate but also understand who you want. Many people don’t actually know who they want. They fall into the trap. “I want an interior designer.” Which one?

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. I love it. Really valuable information, Phil. For anyone who wants information, more information about Phil, go to PhilBerg.coach. That’s P, H, I, L, B, as in boy, B, E, R, G, dot coach. C, O A, C, H, PhilBerg.coach. Phil, thanks for being on the BNI Podcast again.

Phil:
Love it and Happy Thanksgiving to you and the family.

Ivan:
Thanks. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, that’s it for this week. Thank you both so much for that great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Phil Berg explains how to empower people to want to refer you and give them the tools they need to make the referral.

title.rendered: Episode 887: Integrating AI into Your Company

content.rendered:
Synopsis

AI integration in your company isn’t the domain of IT. AI adoption is about human behavior change. It’s a people issue. It’s a change management issue that you have to lead.

  • Model use of AI, which gives your team permission to use it.
  • Hold upskilling training workshops to help your team understand how AI can be used in different elements of your business.
  • Pick one or two use cases and run a pilot.
  • Ask the early adopters to share feedback with the rest of the company informally.
  • Measure the success of that pilot after 12-18 months.

Not sure where to start? Find something that you love researching or thinking about or doing, and see how AI can spark your imagination around that topic. Once you have an “a-ha” moment, it will be easier to get your company started.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 886: The ABCs of AI.
  • Listen to Episode 750: Competing in the New World of Work.
  • Visit Kian’s website, LearnABC.ai.

Complete Transcript of Episode 887

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am on vacation in Hawaii, a secret, secret location, and my wife doesn’t know that I’ve slipped away to do this podcast, so let’s keep it just between us if we can.

Priscilla: (chuckling)
Okay, that sounds great. And who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
I have my friend, Kian Gohar, back on today. And we hadn’t actually planned on doing two podcasts back-to-back, but his content is so amazing, I asked him if he could come back. He is an AI researcher, best selling author, and CEO of Geolab. He’s a former executive director of the XPRIZE. You can hear more about him in my previous podcast, the last podcast [episode], and also he was on podcast number 750, “Competing in the New World of Work”. Kian, welcome back to the BNI Podcast.

Kian:
Hey, Ivan, good to be back with you again.

Ivan:
You want to give a, just a quick 30-second, overview of what we talked about last week, and then I have a quick story I want to tell, and then I have a couple questions for you.

Kian:
Yeah, we talked a little bit about, what are the fundamentals of AI, what I call the ABCs of AI, and how do you learn to pack some of your workflow and behaviors to be able to get more creative answers with AI to solving some of your business problems.

Ivan:
Yeah, and I think more and more BNI members are very concerned about how AI is going to impact their business. And in some cases, I’ve seen [members] excited about what AI could do, and they’re also confused by it. And I’ll give you a great example, it was literally on the ride back from the time that you and I… go back to the last podcast, everyone, if you’re listening to this, and I talk about how Kian and I did a discussion about a marketing book, and the AI gave me an idea that I never thought of.

And so I was talking about that with a BNI member who has a sedan service, and they were driving me home, and he said, “You know what? I have no idea what my Power Teams are in BNI now.” Just so you know, Kian, a Power Team is a group of… it stemmed from Contact Spheres. They’re a group of business professionals who have a symbiotic relationship. They’re in compatible, non-competitive professions, like a caterer, florist, a photographer, a travel agent. I call that the wedding mafia. Once they bring you in, they don’t let you out, right?

So he’s a sedan service, and he said, “I don’t know what all my people would be.” So I took my definition of what a Contact Sphere and a Power Team [are]. The Power Team is a group of Contact Sphere people that are actually working together. They’re not just in symbiotic professions; they’re actually referring each other.

And I put a definition in, and I asked ChatGPT, what would be some good Power Team members? And it gave me a great list. And it gave me a list of people that I hadn’t thought of. And when I talked to him, he said, “Oh my goodness, I never thought of them.” Like event planners, wedding planners, hotel resorts, travel agencies, concierge services. These are a number of things where he hadn’t thought of, but by having this conversation with AI, with ChatGPT, I was able to give him professions that he could then work with to increase his referrals. And that’s the beauty of what I think — that’s one of the beautiful things that I think — we can do with AI. What do you think of that?

Kian:
I love that, Ivan, and I have an idea now from this conversation that we’re having right now is — I’m going to go into ChatGPT and ask it: I have this dilemma around creating partnerships for my own company here at Geolab. How would Ivan Misner think about who my Power Teams are? (laughing)

Ivan: (laughing)
Oh yeah, I gotta hear the answer to that. I will tell you…. okay, so this is a secret… I can’t believe I’m saying this. I did ask ChatGPT a few months back: describe, give me the definition of networking by one of the world’s leading experts on the topic. And I had my fingers crossed and [it gave me] my definition with my name, and I’m like, Alright. I feel pretty good. This is great.

Kian:
That’s awesome. I mean, that’s also one of the hacks that your listeners can think about is like, pretend you’re expert XYZ on this topic. Let’s say you want to create a new product or service, and you give that context to AI about your new product or service, then you can ask, how would Steve Jobs, the world’s greatest innovator, think about this new product idea that I’m having? What are some of the assumptions he might make and some ways he would go around it? So think about pretending like you’re the world’s expert in marketing or sales or negotiation or networking, like Ivan Misner. That’s one of the beauties of AI, it can help us get out of our head and really come up with creative solutions.

Ivan:
I love this. And listen, if you’re listening to this, it isn’t about him. This isn’t about Kian. It’s not about me. It’s not about Steve Jobs. It’s about you. Take the ideas that he’s talking about and apply it to you. Now you might ask it, you know, what would this person say or do? But use your needs in the discussion with the AI. Well, look, I don’t want to kill the whole time on this story. There are a couple more things that we didn’t get to in the last podcast. One is that AI is not the domain of IT. What does that mean?

Kian:
You know, we think of AI as like a technical thing, but the reality is, we interact with generative AI in the workplace in a non-technical manner. And a lot of people think that they have to be really technical to use AI, or maybe their head of technology at their company, or their IT director is responsible for integrating AI. But the truth is AI integration in your company isn’t about IT. It’s really a people issue. We do not believe that AI will displace human work. However, humans with AI skills will displace humans who don’t have AI skills.

So it’s critical that you don’t approach this like technology adoption, like upgrading your servers or whatever system you have, or your software. That’s not what AI adoption is about. It’s about human behavior change. It’s a people issue. It’s a change management issue. How do you think about learning, and training your teams, changing their workflow, integrating AI into their work stack? All of this is about humans, and it’s critical that your listeners think about who’s involved in designing AI experiments and leading that change, and it should be a mix of people, and definitely not just in the domain of IT.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know, last week I said that paralegal is in trouble. And I think I misspoke to some extent. I think paralegal without AI implementation is in trouble. I think paralegal will be around, but it’ll be the people who understand how to utilize AI that will continue to have a career in paralegal. And I think that that will end up applying to a lot of professions. Wouldn’t you agree?

Kian:
Absolutely. And I think, you know, your listeners are probably thinking, Okay, I buy into the top level hypothesis of AI can help me. But really, what can it do for me? Maybe… How can it impact my sales? Or how could it improve my customer service or improve my marketing? There are a bunch of domains that you can apply AI in your company to make it more effective, and there’s tons of low hanging productivity gains you can have. One of the things I’d recommend is I want you to think holistically about what use cases you’re going to start tackling first? You’re not going to make your company AI ready overnight. It’s going to be a process.

So you want to have a change management program in place, and what that means is you as the leader, want to role model the behavior to your teams that you’re using AI as well, so that gives them permission to use it. You want to have some upskilling training workshops to help your team also understand how AI can be used in different elements. And then you want to pick maybe one or two use cases, narrow it down, and then run a pilot, and then measure the success of that pilot. Have some early adopters on your team who can be AI champions, who can experiment and then share this feedback in a casual environment, like a lunch-and-learn so that they can help others on the team learn about what they experimented with.

And then over time, you’ll see that this use case has been integrated, and now you can work on another use case and another use case. I want to just… So this idea for your listeners: we’re going to be using AI forever on end in our workplaces, and so we don’t need to solve everything urgently in the next six months. But I want you to begin, and start with one use case, and then within 12 to 18 months, you will have been able to transform a bunch of your functions with AI. And you’ll look back on the last 18 months and you’ll say, “Wow, I can’t believe I did all that work before without AI.”

Ivan:
Well, any last thoughts? And while you’re thinking about that, I forgot to mention your website on the last podcast. Your website is LearnABC.ai. That’s learn L-E-A-R-N-A-B-C dot A-I. LearnABC.ai. Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Kian:
I would just say, have fun with it. You know, when we think about AI, we think, Oh, my God, is this going to solve some of these business problems? But I would also say, start personally and start interacting with it about a personal dilemma or something that you’re really curious about, like travel or cooking or reading or whatever.

And the reason I suggest this is because I want to get you excited about using AI, and until you get excited about how it can make you more intrigued about something you care about, you’re not going to really integrate it into your workflow, because then it’ll be like work. So start on a personal level, find something that you love researching or thinking about or doing, and see how AI can spark your imagination around that topic. And then you’ll have an Aha. It will be like, “Oh, if it can do this, I wonder if it can do that for me at work.” So start personal.

Ivan:
Kian, my friend, thank you so much for being on two episodes back-to-back, and you are welcome anytime you’ve got some great ideas on AI. I’d love to have you back.

Kian:
Oh my God, what an honor. Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
All right. Over to you, Priscilla,

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. That’s it for this week. Thank you both so much for the great information. T

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: AI adoption is about human behavior change. It’s a people issue. It’s a change management issue that you have to lead.

title.rendered: Episode 886: The ABCs of AI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Special guest Kian Gohar explains that AI can help us to be more creative in the workplace and can help us come up with better solutions to problems we face at work. If you really want to use AI to be creative in your problem solving, you have to learn how to interact with it differently than we have been doing for the last 20+ years of the Internet era.

Try having a voice conversation with AI. Ask it to interview you for context before giving answers. Tell it what you like and don’t like about its ideas, and ask for more suggestions. Don’t settle for the first answer.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 750, “Competing in the New World of Work,” with Kian Gohar.
  • Visit Kian’s website: LearnABC.ai

Complete Transcript of Episode 886

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at the BNI international [Global] Convention this week in Hawaii. It’s been a long time since I’ve been in Hawaii. And you know, I love BNI conventions because it’s like having a United Nations meeting where all the countries love each other.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
I really enjoy it and we’re going to be celebrating our 40th anniversary! We’re gonna do it a couple months early, but our 40th anniversary in BNI! So I’m excited to be here.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds great. And who do you have today?

Ivan:
I have a great guest with us today. I consider him a personal friend. He’s a fellow member of the Transformational Leadership Council (TLC). Kian is an AI researcher, best selling author, and he’s the CEO of Geolab. He’s a former executive director of the XPRIZE Foundation. And when we first met, he was talking about the XPRIZE; it was really interesting. And he was also with the Singularity University.

Kian has coached the leadership teams of dozens of Fortune 500 companies on innovation, AI and the future of work. And I have a lot of BNI members asking me about what’s the future of AI. So I couldn’t think of anyone better to invite than him. He’s the co-author of the globally bestselling book, Competing in the New World of Work, and his latest AI research has been featured in The Wall Street Journal, the Harvard Business Review, Financial Times and NPR. He’s a graduate of Harvard Business School, and he helped me with AI and marketing one of my books, which was a great experience. Kian [Gohar], welcome to BNI Podcast.

Kian:
Ivan, it’s so wonderful to be back with you here again. Always a pleasure.

Ivan:
It’s a pleasure to have you. So you’ve got a number of topics that I think are really valuable for our members. One is that AI is not just a search engine. Talk about that.

Kian:
Yeah. So we wanted to begin with this idea. We believe, with this hypothesis, that AI can help us be more creative in the workplace and can help us come up with better solutions to problems we face at work. And so we had this natural hypothesis that AI is — they make us more creative. And we wanted to experiment and find out how much more creative can it make us be? Two times? Five times, 10 times, 100 times? If I had to ask you, Ivan, how much as a percentage you think AI can help you be more creative in your problem solving at work, what number do you think?

Ivan:
Oh, geez, I don’t know. But I can tell you that it saved my work on doing a survey from three or four months down to three or four days.

Kian:
Amazing, incredible productivity gains. So we ran this experiment last year with real teams at real companies all around the world who had real problems, and we gave half them access to AI, and the other half did not have access to AI. And what we found was that the teams who had access to AI actually only had modest gains in creativity at best. And this totally blew our mind.

And as we dug into it, we found it’s because we as humans are biased to settle for the first acceptable answer that we get, and AI is really good at giving us an acceptable answer. It’s like the world’s greatest golden retriever that it just wants to make you happy, so it’ll give you an answer. But that doesn’t mean it’s the best answer.

And this was a notion that won a Nobel Prize last century by a researcher called Herbert Simon. It’s called satisficing. We satisfice ourselves that an acceptable answer is the best one. But what we found was that there were some teams who were really, really creative when they were using AI, and they were totally different than all the other teams who had access to AI, so we wanted to find out why. And what we discovered is that these teams changed their workflow and their behavior of how they interacted with AI.

So instead of it being a search engine when you ask a question like you do on Google or Bing, these teams who are really creative with AI, actually had a back-and-forth conversation with AI, just like you and I are having, and that led them to deeper, creative solutions to the problems that they were facing. And this was the big ‘aha’ that blew our mind, which made us realize that if you really want to use AI to be creative in your problem solving, you have to learn to use it as a thought partner, as a conversation partner, and learn how to interact with it differently than we have been doing for the last 20+ years of the Internet era.

Ivan:
So you and I did that at a TLC event, and I’ve written 30 books, so I know how to market a book. But if you recall, we kept asking it, “Well, what else might you do to market the book? What else might you do?” And it came up with an idea that I had never thought of, which was: do a virtual book tour. And I did that with my last book, and it was a big success.

Kian:
How did it do?

Ivan:
Yeah, it went over really well. And it was through this dialog that you’re talking about right now, which is interesting, because… what do you think is the best way to interact with AI?

Kian:
Yeah, so we are used to sitting behind a laptop or a computer and going on to Google or whichever search engine, and there’s this box, and we think about formulating the perfect question to put in the box to get the exact answer that we think the search engine is going to give us. And the user interface for AI, whether it’s ChatGPT or cloud, or any of the other platforms, they kind of look the same. They kind of look like, you know, Google. It’s like that box. And we, we have this, you know, we’re stuck in the tyranny of the search box. And so we go into AI, and we start like, trying to figure out the exact prompt to then be able to get the right kind of answer. And the truth is, it almost doesn’t matter.

And what I would love for your listeners to think about is to have a conversation using their voice with AI, rather than typing it into the app. And so avoid this tyranny of the search box and have a conversation like you and I are having. Ramble, be unstructured, just like any kind of normal conversation. And AI has the capabilities to be able to understand that extremely well.

And the way you do that is download either the app on your phone or a desktop app. And there’s a button at the very bottom right-hand corner that might look like a headphone, might look like a speaker, depending on which app you have, and that starts the conversational AI feature of it. And when you start having conversation back and forth, you start having significant more insights. And it’ll give you some answers, and you might like some of the answers.

And what I would suggest is to talk back to AI, just like a teenager would talk back to a parent. It gives you some ideas. Tell it maybe some of the things you like about those ideas, maybe some of the things you don’t like about those ideas. And then ask the AI to regenerate or come up with a whole new set of ideas. Just kind of like you and I did when we were having that conversation about your book launch; and that’s how we get to significantly better, more creative ideas.

Ivan:
And we did, on that particular day, you kept prompting it, and it kept responding. It literally was a dialog. It was like there was a third person in the room and answering the questions. It was really quite amazing.

Kian: (chuckling)
Yeah. And I think one of the things that’s really important for your listeners to get fluent in the fundamentals of AI – we call it the ABCs of AI, sort of like anytime you learn a new language, you learn the alphabet. And here we have AI as a new copilot in the way we will always be working forever on end. So we got to learn some of the basics, the fundamentals, the ABCs.

One of the most important lessons I can share is that it’s critical when you’re having a conversation with AI to give it context for whatever dilemma you’re trying to solve for. And you might say, I don’t know what kind of context to give. And so the context, the example that you and I had was you’re launching a new book, you’re trying to figure out unique marketing channels and experiences that might help disseminate the knowledge. And so that’s context. Maybe you might say, I don’t know what context to give. Maybe you just draw a blank.

So let’s hack the behavior of how we interact with AI, and ask AI to interview you for the context it needs to get you the right kind of answer. So you might say, “Hey, AI, ask me five questions about this situation or topic before you give me any answers.” And that gives an opportunity for AI to ask you questions, interview you, for enough context that it needs to then begin a conversation. And then you can go back and forth, you can push, you can challenge. You can ask it to move in a different direction, assess some of your assumptions. And just like you and I are having a conversation right now, or you would with a friend in-person, that’s how I want you to engage with AI to get to significantly better answers.

And the beauty of this is that we all have this capability of speaking (for the most part), and it comes natural to us as humans, and it helps demystify how we interact with AI, rather than thinking that we have to be technical experts or have capabilities or have the exact perfect prompt. Because the truth is, this is all about how humans and AI interact in the future, rather than us thinking about this as a technical solution.

Ivan:
Again, this is such an important topic, and we could, I think, go another 10 minutes pretty easily. Are you willing to come back again next week and continue this conversation? Because I think this is really valuable for members.

Kian:
Of course, I’d be more than happy to come back and share some more lessons on the ABCs of AI.

Ivan:
All right, well, we’re going to have you back next week, and for those of you who listen to BNI Podcast often, Kian was on podcast number 750, “Competing in the New World [of Work],” back in March of 2022. He’s here on session one of The ABCs of AI, and I’m gonna have him back again next week to talk further and maybe go even a little bit deeper on this topic, because I think it is absolutely the wave of the future. Again, thank you so much for being here today.

Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 885: The Connector Effect (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 697, first released on March 10, 2021. Some links and offers may no longer be valid.

Synopsis

Why would you join BNI? To become a Master Connector. A Master Connector is the person that people come to when they have a problem.

This week Robert Skrob, who is also the author of Retention Point, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to talk about the new BNI book the two of them wrote with Graham Weihmiller. It’s called The Connector Effect and its purpose is twofold:

  1. To help your chapter grow by improving retention because members have a better expectation of what BNI membership is all about.
  2. To help business owners grow their business, because BNI is the single most effective business growth strategy out there today.

The Connector Effect is your ability to attract people to you. It grows in proportion to your referral network and your ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers by referring those people to members within your network.

The more members that you have within your network, the more problems you’re able to solve. The bigger your network gets, the more referrals everyone in that network generates. The Connector Effect is “Givers Gain” in action.

Get your copy of The Connector Effect at www.theconnectoreffect.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 885/697

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hi Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m still doing, I’m still Zooming around the world like most other people are, but I’ve been to a number of countries including the United Kingdom and Singapore for big events. And I’m happy to be able to talk to so many people around the world using Zoom. It’s at least one of the benefits of this crazy world we live in right now.

Priscilla:
I know. And that’s great. It sounds like fun.

Ivan:
It is, actually it is fun. So I’ve got a guest today, Priscilla, it is a good friend and co-author of a book. His name is Robert Skrob. Robert is a marketing expert, and he’s been in the field for more than 20 years. He’s helped thousands of businesses attract and retain their best target customers. Robert’s superpower is simplification. I love that, Robert. I tell people my superpower is persistence. So good combination.

He simplifies people’s messages to attract customers, simplifying what you deliver to improve long- term retention, and simplifying how you deliver it to increase your profits as you scale. And while he’s worked with entrepreneurs in more than 100 business categories, today he specializes in helping businesses grow recurring subscription revenue.

And Robert is the co-author with our CEO of BNI, Graham Weihmiller, and myself for a book called The Connector Effect. And we’ll have a link here in this podcast. I believe it will be theconnectoreffect.com, but it will also be here, you can click on it. Robert, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Robert:
Thank you so much. I’m honored, Dr. Misner, been a longtime fan and absolutely it’s a pleasure to have the opportunity to chat with you again and be a guest on your show. Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Well, listen, it is great having you here. And just for full transparency, Robert did the heavy lifting for this book. He deserves most of the credit. And you know, we certainly contributed a lot. But Robert, this is something that Robert really took the ball with and ran. And it’s a brand new book. And it’s really aimed at not just BNI members, but people that are thinking about joining BNI. Why would I want to come to BNI? and the reason is to become a Master Connector. So Robert, what is The Connector Effect? And why did we create the book?

Robert:
Yeah, I thank you for that. The Connector Effect is a fresh look at BNI, [which] has, you know, been around for 36 years now. And being able to look at it with a new prism of how, really, BNI chapter membership is actually kind of like a disruptor for a small business because it changes everything about how they generate customers, how they connect with other entrepreneurs, how they get information about growing their business. And The Connector Effect was a way of showing Givers Gain in action, that by passing referrals, is the secret key to growing your business.

The Connector Effect, specifically, is your ability to attract people to you. It grows in proportion to your referral network and your ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers by referring those people to members within your network. And that as your network grows, so does your ability to solve problems and attract customers to you.

Ivan:
Yeah, someone told me once, it’s like a force multiplier, belonging to BNI, which I think is a great term. I know it’s a military term, but I think it’s really true. It’s a force multiplier.

Robert:
Totally. And there’s so much that goes along with BNI membership that we really don’t think about, and me coming from the outside, looking at it and going “you know what?”

You know, there’s a huge movement in the tech world today to try to create gamification. And everybody’s trying to figure this out, you know, similar to Waze, [which] you know, helps you find your particular destination and connects you to other users of the app along the way. So does BNI with your scoreboards, and your referral counting, and your being able to track, it makes making money fun. And so that, not only are you doing it in order to grow each other’s business, but there’s a friendly competition. And the winner is everybody because we grow our businesses. And so being able to bring forward these sorts of, you know, ways that in the systems that BNI gives you as a small business, I was really thrilled and excited about bringing that forward in this book.

Ivan:
You mentioned gamification. And you mentioned Waze, and I think that’s a great example. It’s, I’m almost embarrassed to admit this, I’m admitting it on recording here. On Waze, I wanted to be Waze Royalty. There’s Waze Baby, and there’s Waze Knight. I have three college degrees, but my big goal was I wanted to be Waze Royalty.

Robert: (Chuckling)

Ivan:
I’m very happy to say I am now. But the gamification really is effective, because it’s about, you know, kind of competing with yourself to be amongst the best.

Robert:
Of course. And when, within your BNI chapter of course, the score is based on how many referrals you pass, and the BNI systems that, for somebody coming from the outside, watching members of the chapter kind of keep score by passing referrals, that can feel kind of mercenary. Because outside of BNI, that’s very uncommon. And so really framing something that might turn somebody off, instead really helping to illustrate that that’s a real benefit to them, and that you can enjoy it, and it’s fun, was really the kind of the key of taking each of these elements of this ingenious system that together is worth well more than the sum of its parts, and helping illustrate how they work to somebody that’s new, so that they can really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and measuring things, you know, we measure things in addition to the referrals, thank you for closed business, and of course One-to-Ones. But these are all measuring things that make a difference in the success of membership. So it’s not just measuring things to measure things, it’s measuring the right things to get great results.

Robert:

Well, gamification for a purpose, rather than simply becoming the king of Waze.

Ivan:
Yes.

Robert:
Well, that certainly is important, it will not diminish your status, your highness.

Ivan:
Thank you, thank you.

Robert:
But if, when you’re the king of thank you for closed business, you have changed some lives in your chapter. And so having a friendly competition of who gets to wear that crown, everybody in that chapter wins.

Ivan:
You know, that’s so important, what you just said there, I want everyone to listen to that, again. When you [compete] based on, you know, keeping track of thank you for closed business and referrals and One-to-Ones, that as you do those things, you’re changing people’s lives. And that’s the goal here, it’s to transform lives, to change the way the world does business, and to do that through our referral marketing platform. And that’s really what I see as the benefit of being a great connector. So can you explain a little bit for us, Robert, what is The Connector Effect as we describe it in the book?

Robert:
Yeah, absolutely. You know, the kind of the key point of the book is your BNI chapter gives you a network that you can trust to refer your friends, family, and best customers. A group of people who trust you to do what they promise. And because they trust you to do the same, they pass referrals of their friends, family, and customers to you. One of the things that I heard from a lot of BNI members was that it can take a couple or three years before you fully experience the benefit of receiving referrals.

And what I thought is that really discounted a tremendous amount of value that you can get from your BNI chapter, the very second that they accept you into your business category in order to join. And that is the ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers. And that is really what becoming a, what we talk about in the book, becoming a Master Connector is all about. The person that people come to when they have a challenge, when they have a problem. And many of them, you’re going to be able to refer to yourself and be able to help them within their own business and provide your services.

And other times you’re providing your referrals to your BNI chapter members. And the key is that the more members that you have within your network, the more problems you’re able to solve. And because if you don’t have a category taken within your referral network and within your chapter, that means that there’s problems that you’re encountering, that you don’t even know that you can solve. And once you have those folks in those slots, and giving weekly presentations, it creates in your mind an open loop that you can close when you encounter individuals with those types of problems. And the bigger your network gets, the more referrals everybody generates. It’s really, The Connector Effect is Givers Gain implemented.

Ivan:
You know, the technology that exists today is just mind boggling to me compared to what it was when I started BNI. And when I started BNI, I wanted to be a Master Connector. And I don’t know if I ever told you this, Robert, but this is what I did. Once every two or three months, I would send a letter to like hundreds of people that were in my database, which was mostly a Rolodex, and for those of you who are Gen Xers, I’m not talking about an expensive watch. It’s a little paper document that, you know, that you turn and you get these cards with all the addresses.

And anyway, I would mail to all of my contacts, a list of business professionals that I did business with. That’s it; not their names, not their phone numbers, and then I’d have a cover letter that said, “You know, I really believe in connecting people who need referrals. And so here’s a list of professions, if you need one of these professions call me.”

And, you know, it took about six months or a year, but people started calling me, Robert, and asking, “Hey, do you know this person? Do you know that person?” I didn’t give the phone number because I wanted to make the connection personally. And that was sort of a, you know, paper-based Master Connector. But now with technology, we can just do so much more, wouldn’t you agree?

Robert:
Absolutely. And I think that’s the real power. You know, whereas you had to assemble all of those names yourself and vet those individuals and have meetings with them and really work to curate that list that you were, you know, that you were making available. Your BNI chapter is a system that plugs in to your existing business that gives you access to that power, all together really at the power of your BNI Connect app. And so, it’s fast, it’s effective, and makes you a Master Connector, so much easier than it was for you out on your own 36 years ago.

Ivan:
Absolutely. Any last thoughts before we wrap up? We’re over time. Any last thoughts before we wrap?

Robert:
Yeah, the reason why we built the, or we created, The Connector Effect were two things. To help your chapter grow by improving retention because members have a better expectation of what BNI membership is all about, and really the most important, helping business owners grow their business. BNI is the single most effective business growth strategy out there today. And I want to do everything we can in order to help business owners around the world appreciate it, use it more effectively, and grow their business for the benefit of their families.

Ivan:
Robert, thank you so much for doing this book with me. I really appreciate it. Thanks for being on the podcast today. You’re helping to make a difference in a lot of people’s lives. The book is called The Connector Effect. It’s written by Robert Skrob, Graham Weihmiller, and myself. You can go to www.theconnectoreffect.com or find it on Amazon. Thank you, Robert.

Robert:
Absolute honor to be here. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you both. That was some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
