excerpt.rendered: What are the odds of connecting with another BNI member just by dialing a number incorrectly? That exact thing happened to Dr. Misner.

title.rendered: Episode 878: Failing to Follow Through

content.rendered:
Synopsis

One of the biggest networking and referral mistakes that people make is failing to follow through. Networking without follow-through is a waste of your time and money. If you’re not going to follow up, don’t bother showing up.

If a BNI member fails to follow up on a referral you give them, you’ve got to talk to that member immediately about following up.

When you get a referral, follow up immediately. When someone asks you for a quote, send it to them. If you don’t think the referral is a good fit, let them know. Never leave anyone hanging.

If you need more help following up, listen to Episode 623: The 24/7/30 Follow Up System.

Complete Transcript of Episode 878

Priscilla:  
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am happy to say I’m at my home in Austin. It’s one of the first times in many years that I’ve been home for six weeks in a row, or I will be home for six weeks in a row. So…

Priscilla:  
Oh, that sounds nice.

Ivan:
Working from home.

Priscilla:  
Uh huh.

Ivan:
So today’s topic is about failing to follow through, and it really involves a story, which I’ll get to pretty quickly. But let me start by saying that I really think that successful businesspeople know the importance of networking and how it can be a huge opportunity to gain referrals. However, one of the biggest mistakes that people can make is failing to follow through. And this story that I’m going to share with you today came from one of my employees several years ago, and I think it serves as an important lesson to all of us on how networking without follow through is nothing more than a waste of time.

So this situation I’m going to describe happened before she came to work for BNI. And for the record, the names in this story have been changed to protect the innocent and the guilty both. So here’s the story. My employee, who I’ll call Winnie, needed the services of a graphic designer to assist her with the creation of a website for her father’s business. And she attended a local networking mixer before she was employed by BNI. It was another organization; it was actually, it was a local chamber event that she went to. And she met a gentleman by the name of Blake. That’s the guilty and so the name has been changed. He was a graphic designer who seemed excited about the project and claimed that he could accomplish exactly what she needed at a very reasonable price.

And so they talked and exchanged contact information, and they connected the next week by phone to discuss the project in more detail. And Winnie was pleased with Blake’s ideas and liked the examples that she’d seen of his work, and she told him he seemed like the perfect person to help her with the project, and she’d like him to send her a price quote as soon as possible. So a week passed, and Winnie heard nothing from Blake, and when she called him to ask about it, he said he was working on a quote and gave her, what sounded to me like a lame excuse about being busy, which like… Have you ever met anyone, Priscilla, who hasn’t said, “Oh, I’m really busy.”

Priscilla:  
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
Everybody says they’re busy. It’s not an excuse to not follow up, especially for business. So another week went by, and again, nothing from Blake. And while Winnie was frustrated, she was willing to give Blake another chance because she really she did like his work and the conversation. So she sent him an email and left him a voicemail saying that she would love to give him her business, and was anxious to hear back from him. Well, after two weeks had gone by without any response, Winnie found another designer, and to this day, as far as I know, Blake never responded to her.

And yet, here’s what blows my mind. I know for a fact — I know this person. I know for a fact this guy was still going to local networking mixers, which cost him money and time to attend, and he was trying to drum up more business. Yet when he had money practically sitting on the table in front of him, he failed to follow through. No matter what his reason was for not getting back to Winnie — being too busy, too lazy — my words, not his, or whatever else, he shouldn’t be out there networking if he can’t follow through on what he claims to be able to deliver. He’s just wasting his time and money. And more important, he’s wasting other people’s time, which is only earning him a bad name and a bad reputation.

And you know, I’ll be honest, I don’t see a lot of BNI members who do this, but I have run into BNI members who’ve gotten a referral and failed to follow up on the referral. And when that happens, if that happens to you as a BNI member and somebody fails to follow up, you’ve got to talk to that member immediately about following up. Because when you give a referral, you give a little bit of your reputation away. And if you give a good referral, it enhances your reputation. You give a bad referral, it hurts your reputation.

I just gave a referral to a good friend of mine, and I reached out to the [BNI] Director, asked for a [BNI] member who was a contractor, got a name, reached out to the contractor directly. Within 30 minutes, he reached out to me. I made an introduction. Within two hours, he was at the woman’s house to talk to her about what she needed to have done. She texted me back and said, “Wow, what a great organization this is.” She said, “You know, I’ve known about you and BNI for many, many years, but to see it in action is incredible.”

That’s what should be happening when you get a referral. You should be reaching out to the person, and that person should be responding immediately. And when they respond immediately, it enhances your reputation. When you give a good referral and that person follows up, it enhances your reputation. And if you give a bad referral, it hurts your reputation. So to me, I wonder — who spends countless hours networking, hoping to fail and see zero results from their efforts? No one, right? Nobody wants that. So I find it astounding that people single-handedly sabotage their success. They guarantee their own failure by failing to follow up with the contacts that they make. So if you don’t plan on following up, don’t plan on showing up. Don’t join BNI.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
If you’re not going to follow up, don’t join. Don’t show up. The moral of this story is, if you aren’t prepared to follow through, networking is a complete waste of your time and other people’s time. If you have a Blake, I call it Blake-the-flake story. If you have a Blake-the-flake story of your own, I’d love to hear about your experience. If it’s about BNI, don’t throw BNI under the bus, but feel free to share your Blake-the-flake story in the comments section here on BNI Podcast.

Also, in terms of follow up, and I’d love your feedback here in a moment, Priscilla. In terms of follow up, I recommend episode number 623, episode 623, it is the “24/7/30 Follow up System.” It’s a classic broadcast of an original podcast, 24/7/30, and it talks about following up. What it doesn’t talk about is giving that bid, you know, giving your quote. But that to me, I didn’t include it in the follow up because it just seemed so obvious. If you get the “Yes, I want a quote from you,” that you actually give them a quote.

And you know what? If you feel like it’s not a good fit, then tell the potential customer, “I don’t think it’s a good fit for these reasons.” But don’t leave them hanging thinking that you just forgot them. That’s the worst scenario, and that’s where the Blake-the-flake phrase came from for me. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:  
Well, exactly what you said. The thing that hit me is there must have been a reason why he did not follow through, and maybe it was just an intuition on his part that this wasn’t going to work out with this person. There was something about her that bothered him, and he just wasn’t able to say it. He wasn’t able to be honest.

Ivan:
I’ve also met people that just kind of screwed up. They get so busy being busy that they don’t do the hard work necessary to close the deals.

Priscilla:  
Yeah.

Ivan: 
I mean, I’ve experienced that. And knowing this guy, because I did know who he was, I think he was the latter, because he kept going out there. He was constantly looking for business, but when it came down to, you know, putting together the quotes, he wasn’t doing it, which made no sense to me. That’s why, you know, I know another person who was great at getting business, but really bad at doing quotes. And so he brought in a business associate, not a partner, not an owner, but a business associate who actually put together the quotes, which he would review and approve and then send out.

Priscilla:  
Yeah.

Ivan:
And he did great, because he was the rainmaker, he brought in the business, but he let somebody else do the quote. And of course, he had a crew that did the work that he helped oversee. But look, what you’re saying could be right. But what I know of this person, I think he was just busy being busy.

Priscilla:  
Mm hmm. Yeah, that’s too bad.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:  
What you said, it really does reflect back on the person who gave the referral.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. In this case, she met him personally. But for us in BNI, it reflects on us.

Priscilla:  
Exactly.

Ivan:
Yeah, and look, you know, let’s say you’re right and he just didn’t think it was a good fit. Then he had an obligation to say to her, “I don’t think it’s a good fit for this reason.” I think maybe he could even say, “I underpriced what I thought this would cost.” And then it would give her the choice of saying, “Okay, what do you think it would cost?” Or he could say, “I don’t think it’s a good fit, because I’m not sure I could really help this particular business.” Whatever the reason was, he had an obligation to get back to the person.

For many, many years I handled… I don’t handle complaints anymore. So you guys, if you’ve got a problem with your local director, don’t email me, because I’m just going to forward it over to the [BNI] National Director or Executive Director. But for many, many years, I would… problems that were going on in the region, I’d get contacted. And as you know, Priscilla, I travel a lot, and I never, ever waited a week or two weeks or three weeks to respond, at least not on purpose, ever.

I almost always responded within 24 hours, 48 hours at the most. And I’d respond and say, “Hey, look, I’m traveling.” Even though they got an auto reply, I’d give a personal message. “I’m traveling. I won’t be in town for a week, two weeks. I will get back to you as soon as I get back in town.” So at least people know what’s going on and why there’s a delay. At the very least, she deserved that. I think we’re out of time, Priscilla. If you have any last thought, feel free to share it. Otherwise that’s it.

Priscilla:  
Okay. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Networking without follow-through is a waste of your time and money. If you’re not going to follow up, don’t bother showing up.

title.rendered: Episode 877: Solutions Focus–Think Big, Act Small

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The bigger the issue, the smaller the step that is going to unlock it and start to reorient things and move things in a useful direction. Special guest Mark McKergow explains how to identify tiny signs and take small steps to make your BNI chapter successful.

Start with the goals you identified in Episode 876: what you want to have happen and what’s already working. That’s the “Think Big” part of a solutions-focused approach.

To put “Act Small” into practice, ask the group to imagine some tiny signs of progress toward that goal. For each of those signs, identify a small step members can take toward achieving it. Agree as a group on which step to take first. If your tiny sign is having the meeting start on time, a small step might be for members to leave the house 10 minutes earlier than usual the next week.

If it works, keep doing it and move onto the next step. If it doesn’t work, ask the members to come up with alternative suggestions. Over time, the small steps build momentum toward the larger goal. Returning to the group to check in on progress empowers everyone to get un-stuck.

Visit https://thesolutionsfocus.com/ to pre-order your copy of the fully-revised 3rd edition of The Solutions Focus.

Complete Transcript of Episode 877

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at my home office in Austin, Texas. I’m actually in town for almost six weeks which is really rare that I’m not on the road. I just finished part of the Founder’s Education Series for this month called “Effective Presentation Skills.” I would urge you, if you’re a member of BNI, to go to [BNI] Business Builder, if you weren’t there live, go to Business Builder in BNI and check out [the recording of] my Effective Presentation Skills presentation.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds good.

Ivan:
So I have back this week, Dr. Mark McKergow, who’s an author and consultant and speaker. As we talked about last week, he is the co-author of the book The Solutions Focus – the Third Edition. And Mark has been a friend for many years. I mentioned him in my latest book, The 3rd Paradigm, where we talk about co-creation and the importance of working together and holding the vision, not the obstacles. And The Solutions Focus book was really an important aspect of helping to hold the vision in a number of projects that I’ve had over the year. And we’ve got Mark on today to talk more about some of the material from his book. And I didn’t mention last week, but it’ll be in the transcript – go to www.thesolutionsfocus.com to get copies of the book, or to local bookstore, and of course, on Amazon. Mark, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Mark:
Hi Ivan. Great to be with you again. I’m calling in from Edinburgh, Scotland today, and we’re having a great late summer here, and it’s great to be hearing about all you folks in in the U.S.

Ivan: 
It’s beautiful here in Austin, it’s actually in the 80s, which is kind of rare for this time of year, but it’s great. So I’ve been to Edinburgh. Loved it. As a matter of fact, I think one of the best parties I’ve ever been to was a Bobby Burns supper in Edinburgh, which was crazy.

Mark:
Burns Night is a great thing. In January, we all have haggis and whiskey, and everybody loves it.

Ivan: (chuckling)
Yep, it was a very notable evening. Well, we’re here today to talk more about The Solutions Focus – Third Edition. Love the book. And let’s start by talking about working with tiny signs of progress and small steps being the key. And this is important for you listening to this, in terms at your chapter level and making tiny signs of progress, you know, just little things to do, to adjust, to move the chapter in the right direction. Over to you, Mark.

Mark:
So there’s two things here, Ivan. There’s tiny signs, and then there’s small steps. And people think, they have the idea that if you have a big problem, you need a big plan or a big step to do something about it. But it turns out that’s not the case at all. And my experience is very much that the bigger the issue, the smaller the step that is going to unlock it and start to reorient things and move things in a useful direction. May not solve the whole thing. But the temptation is to think big, when what you really want to do is think big at the beginning: What do we want to have happen here? What’s working already? And then get really small.

So I love to ask people, “What would be the first tiny signs that things were getting better here, the first tiny signs?” And then they give me a tiny sign, which is somebody would write a report about something. And I say, “No, that’s a big sign. Give me a tiny sign. What’s the first tiny sign that you might see, like tomorrow morning or this afternoon, that would let you know that something was happening, that was moved, starting to move things in the right direction?” So that’s a sign that hasn’t happened yet, but it’s something that would be useful.

And the curious thing I have found over many, many years of working with Solutions Focus is that if you get people talking about these tiny signs that haven’t happened yet, they start to get the idea somehow, that if some of those signs happen, that would be a good thing. And these tiny signs are all actionable, that’s the wonderful thing. So you talk about a tiny sign that somebody would come into the office and smile at the other people. And that, while it’s not yet an action, it’s something that could be done. And so these very tiny signs can easily turn into small actions. And that’s one of the chapters in the book, it’s all about the value, the huge value, of small actions. And this is about how we go from kind of stuck to starting to move forward.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Mark:
This is the really exciting part, because stuckness is frustrating. People hate being stuck. They feel trapped. Things are wrong. They know things are wrong, but they can’t figure out what to do. Whereas, if we start to talk about small, tiny signs that things were getting better, and then putting some of those signs into action, people start to get the idea that we’re moving forward, and then once you’re moving, you can start to move a bit faster and a bit faster, and build on it, a bit faster.

It’s a little bit like getting an automobile or a train moving. The hardest part is the first little bit actually starting to move. That’s the point where you need to put the most oomph in. And then as things begin to turn, the wheels begin to turn, you can add more and add more, and you learn more about how to move forward. And then you can kind of begin to make more and more progress with more and more confidence and more and more enthusiasm and more and more pulling the group together.

So take this idea, if you’re really, really stuck, don’t look for a big thing. Think about a really small thing that would help to move things forward, and then go with that and see what happens. See what happens. Most of the time, you’ll find it will help, and things will start to move in a little useful direction. And hey, even if it doesn’t, you only did a small thing, right? You didn’t invest a lot in this. So you can go back and have another think, and, you know, learn from your experience, and try another small step. So this is a really efficient way of working as well as an effective way of making progress when things are really tough, things are really stuck, and honestly, you’re at your wit’s end. That’s when Solutions Focus works really, really well.

Ivan:
I remember you telling a story at one Transformational Leadership Council [TLC] meeting where you were working with a client and they had a whole list of problems, and they created the list, and you told them to triage it as I recall, you know, what are the top three issues? And as I remember the story, you said, “Work on these top three issues, take small steps on these three issues, and that they didn’t call you back, because once they started fixing those top three issues, everything else kind of fell into place. Am I getting that story right?

Mark:
That’s right, absolutely. And it happens again and again. It’s not very good for my consulting practice, but it’s really good for my clients, that everything is connected. And this is not a hippy dippy thing about, you know, connections. This is about you change one thing, and that changes something else, and that knocks onto something else, and then it affects something else.

And when people are starting to succeed at work, hey, guess what? They go home, and they’re in a better position when they go home. And so their family life maybe starts to take a little upturn. And their relationships with their kids might start to improve, and whatever. And they’re working, you know, their connections with the community might improve. So there’s all sorts of knock-on effects. So one thing leads to another, leads to another, and you just can’t tell at the beginning quite how this is going to pan out, which is one of the wonderful things about Solutions Focus.

So it’s not a kind of guaranteed “Do this and that will happen.” It’s “Do this and then watch to see what happens.” And if you’ve done the first conversation right, the first thing will be something useful, but then other things will happen, too.

And as I said, when people are stuck, they are frustrated and angry. When things are starting to move, you’ll say, “Okay, let’s go with this. Come on.” And that’s where I want people to be. It’s feeling empowered, feeling that they have some kind of, you know, control, or influence at least, over what’s happening in their lives and in their businesses and in their work and in their relationships, and at that point they think, okay, okay, maybe I have got this after all.

And that’s the difference that Solutions Focus can make you go from really stuck, focused on the problem, to thinking about what you want, thinking about what’s working, thinking about tiny signs and small steps, and within literally minutes sometimes, you have some kind of connection and useful idea that things can move forward, are starting to move forward, and we’re off again and life has begun again.

Ivan:
So if you’re listening to this, I want you to really think about how this applies to your BNI chapter, and, of course, your business, but your BNI chapter, because there are oftentimes just some small steps that you can take to really start turning around a BNI group. And what Mark is talking about is really powerful. So Mark, we’re almost out of time, but there are a couple of other quick things I wanted to talk about. One was doing this in a rapidly changing world. Any quick thoughts on that?

Mark:
It really fits really well with our rapidly changing world. Problem Solving was made for a very steady, static world where things don’t change very much. But if things are changing, and goodness knows they are at the moment for all of us in many ways. Then Solutions Focus – what do we want? Ask, where do we want to go? Not what’s wrong, or where have we come from. Such a powerful way of starting and then, and you’re taking account of all the changes that are happening already, because where we want to go tomorrow might be different to where we wanted to go yesterday, and you just take account of that.

Ivan:
Well, the last thing I just want to touch upon, you’ve covered a little, but I think it’s a great thing to end on, and that’s what’s more helpful: to ask what’s wrong or to ask what’s wanted? And man, this is needed. When you get a group of people together, 30, 40, 50, 60 people, it’s really easy to focus on all the negativity, and not what’s wanted. And can you just tell us one more time about that?

Mark:
Clearly, I think it’s more valuable to ask what’s wanted. But here’s the thing, it’s good also to agree that there’s some we need to do something about.

Ivan:
Yes.

Mark:
You know, there’s a thing here that we need to tackle. Okay, we all agree that this is a thing that’s worth tackling. Yes, okay. Now, what do we want to have happen here?

Ivan:
And that’s where most of the time needs to be spent on.

Mark:
That’s right, that’s right. But it’s good… I’m not saying you should never ask what the problem is ever again, you know. Even I do that right at the start, just to make sure that we’re kind of focusing on the right thing to begin with. And then when you start talking about what do we want? It’s not just about the thing that’s wrong; it’s about all sorts of other things too. And it’s such a richer and wider and more empowering and embracing conversation when you talk about, how could things be here if things were going really well?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mark:
It’s a marvelous thing, and it frees us up to think about all kinds of things, including things maybe we don’t have yet, we haven’t invented yet, we don’t know how to do yet, but we can imagine, start to imagine that they might be useful.

Ivan:
Mark, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast again. For those of you listening, I have recommended Mark’s book for many years. You can get a copy, and view his website, www.thesolutionsfocus.com. The book is available online and in most major bookstores. It is coming out this month, but you can pre-order it if you’re early.

Mark:
And there’s also an audio book too. For the first time, for this third edition, we have an official audio book, which you can order through all the usual channels if you’d rather listen to it than read it.

Ivan:
I urge people to get this book. It’s made a big change in the way I work in the organization, and I think it can make a big change for you. Mark, thanks for being on. Let me hand it over to Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, that that was wonderful. Thank you both for that.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The bigger the issue, the smaller the step that is going to unlock it and start to reorient things and move things in a useful direction.

title.rendered: Episode 876: Solutions Focus — A Better Future

content.rendered:
Synopsis

A problem-focused approach works well if you’re dealing with a broken washing machine or automobile, but when people are involved, you need a solutions focus. Dr. Mark McKergow joins Dr. Misner this week to explain the first two steps in the process.

  1. Instead of asking “What’s wrong?” ask “What do we want to have happen here? Where do we want to go?”
  2. Ask “What’s already working that we want to keep in the future?”

The answers to those two questions provide the source material you can turn into action. Learn how in next week’s episode.

Links

  • Listen to Episode 396: Host Leadership.
  • Listen to Episode 790: Why So High?
  • Visit https://thesolutionsfocus.com/ to pre-order your copy of the fully-revised 3rd edition of The Solutions Focus.

Complete Transcript of Episode 876

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I just got back from vacation in Santa Barbara [California] and had a wonderful time. I was able to attend a birthday party at Jack Canfield’s house. He just turned 80.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow.

Ivan:
Went there. Yeah. It was amazing to see Jack and all of my friends, and friends, many friends of the guest that I’m about to introduce. I [also] did an online training with BNI Canada Directors, and I have spent a couple of days with the CEO, the new CEO of BNI, Mary Thompson, who just took over about six weeks ago. So keeping busy and staying out there.

Priscilla:
Great. So, tell us a little bit about this particular podcast.

Ivan:
Well, I am really happy to have on this podcast a longtime friend, Dr. Mark McKergow, and I have mentioned Mark and one of his books, The Solutions Focus, nine times–I checked on my podcast, nine times over the last several years. Mark is an author, consultant, speaker, an inimitable blend of scientific rigor and performance pizzazz, which is an interesting combination, but it works. I’ve seen it. He has pioneered the development of solutions-focused change models in business with his book, The Solutions Focus.

And he also founded SOLWorld. I think that’s how you pronounce it, S-O-L W – World international [network]. And he edits the Journal of Solution Focused Practices. He’s worked on every populated continent in the world, just like BNI is on every populated continent. And he developed the Host Leadership approach, which, by the way, we did a podcast on, podcast number 396, a long time ago. And he’ll play jazz, the jazz saxophone in the 2024 Edinburgh Fringe Festival. And he is great on the saxophone. Mark, welcome to BNI Podcast.                                                                            

Mark:
Hi there, Ivan, and hi everybody. It’s great to be with you. And I’m speaking from Edinburgh, where we’ve just finished the Edinburgh Festival and the Edinburgh Fringe Festival. And both my shows were sellouts, which was fabulous.

Ivan:
So you just did it – good for you. You are a great musician, and you’re a past TLC [Transformational Leadership Council] member. And whenever you come to TLC, they ask you to bring your saxophone so that you can play and you’re truly amazing. I’m in awe of people who can play musical instruments. I can barely play a kazoo.

Mark:
Well, it’s great fun, and it’s a great way to connect with people, both other musicians and people who might want to listen or get drawn in by the music. So it’s been a very good friend to me over many, many years.

Ivan:
So a lot of people don’t know this, and I’m getting off script here, but in in my book with Stewart Emery, “Who’s in Your Room?” we talk about resonance and dissonance. And I always think of the thing that you did with Fred Johnson about resonance on hitting the right key or chord. I’m not a musician, I don’t know what it was. And then he hummed into it. I don’t know if you remember doing that at a TLC event.

Mark:
Absolutely. Yes, I remember it very well, and it’s such a crucial thing, because you can be resonant, resonating with somebody or something. And then if you change it a little bit, it can become very like alarming and uncomfortable and dissonant, as you say. And the difference between those two things is really small. It’s one of the curious things about music and musical tones but you have to be sensitive to it, and it takes two to do that. It takes two to resonate.

Ivan:
So anyone who’s read my book, “Who’s in Your Room?” the idea of resonance and dissonance came from this gentleman who’s on our podcast right now, Mark McKergow. He is the one that gave us the idea. So thank you. But we’re here to talk about your new book, Mark, and a book that I have recommended, as I mentioned nine times over the last several years, “The Solutions Focus.” Third or fourth edition?

Mark:
So it’s The Solutions Focus. It’s the third edition that’s just about to appear in the US and worldwide on the 17th of September this year.

Ivan:
2024. But people can order it in advance. You can do advance orders.

Mark:
You certainly can on all good book shops, as they say. And I was really excited to get the invitation to write the third edition, because the first edition came out — get this — in 2002. 2002! We didn’t even… we hardly had the internet then. We didn’t have smartphones or anything. So the world has really changed. But the solution focused approach is to building useful change, have been going around the world. And I’m so excited to have the chance to revisit all that great stuff. Add some new stories, add some new connections, and show how people are using these ideas of solution focused change around the world.

Ivan:
Well, it’s a fantastic book, and I definitely recommend it now. With “solutions focus,” it really talks about building on what you hope for and what’s working or what’s not working. And we are very accustomed to problem solving, which works well in a stable or fixed setting, but less so where people are centrally involved or where there’s conflict. So, do you mind talking about that a little bit?

Mark:
Yes, that’s right. So we think about problem solving as being a kind of necessary thing. If something’s wrong, we have to solve the problem. And of course, sometimes that’s true. And if you have a broken automobile or a broken washing machine, then solving the problem is a good thing. But hey, when you shout at your washing machine, it doesn’t answer back. But when you’re working with people, however, each time you say something or do something, things change around slightly. And so how you tackle the thing matters. So here’s the thing with solutions focus. It’s not at all about asking what’s wrong. It starts off by asking, “What do you want to have happen here?”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mark:
And you think those are kind of similar questions, but actually they’re really fundamentally different. Because when you ask what’s wrong, you get people focusing on all the bad stuff. Whereas if you ask, “What do you want?” then people start to talk about what would be better than what we have now. And there’s a curious thing happens when you ask, “What will be better than what we have now?” It tends to draw people together, in my experience.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Mark:
Because you say, “Imagine, you know, a kind of perfect world here. Well, how would we want this to work?” And people start talking about that and contributing. And this is so much more valuable than talking about what’s wrong, what’s broken, what’s not working; and that sort of conversation tends to lead to dissatisfaction, punch-ups, people getting angry and stuff. And I don’t know about you, but I don’t want that in my meeting room or my workshop. I want people to be coming together around what they want. And so it’s such a fundamental thing, and it’s so tempting to slip back into problem solving sometimes.

Ivan: 
I don’t know if I got this exact phrase from you, but I got the concept from you, Mark, and I have been talking about this for many years in BNI. If all you do is focus on the problem, you become an expert at that problem.

Mark:
That is absolutely right, and you did get that from me.

Ivan:
Yes, I always quote you. And if you focus on the solution, then you can actually come to the solution. And I see that in BNI all the time, where people just, they just, all they do is focus on the problem and it becomes the downfall of a chapter.

Mark:
Yeah.

Ivan:
When a chapter focuses on, okay, how do we fix this?

Mark:
How do we want it to be? How do we want to work together? These are the good questions, I think, because that starts to give you something to move towards. Whereas, if you start talking about what’s wrong here, you’re trying to build something to move away from. But hey, if you say, “We’re going to move away from this, but where are we going to go? Where are we going to go?” So, “Where do we want to go?” is such a much better question than, “Oh, we don’t like this place. There’s something wrong about this. But hey, you know, let’s just talk about it anyway. Let’s sit around and complain.” This does not lead, in my experience, to rapid, positive change. Whereas talking about the better future, the future we all want.

And then, another key piece of it is talking about what’s working already. That’s part of that better future. Because often, in hard times, I find that even when things appear to be going wrong, there’s a lot that’s working. There’s a lot of good stuff. And the other piece of this is to keep that good stuff, hold on to that good stuff, build on that good stuff, add to it, amplify it.

And so we take those two pieces side by side, what do we want to happen in the future and what’s already working in the past and the present, and that then gives us some really interesting things to work with. And I think in the next podcast, we’re going to be talking about some of the things you can do to actually turn that interesting sort of source material into action.

Ivan:
So one of the podcasts that I did in the past where I mentioned your content was podcast number 790 and the title of that is “Why So High?” And it was a technique that you taught me about asking people what the problem is, and they give it a score from 1 to 10 and when they’re upset, they give a low score. And you taught me to say, “Why so high? You want a low score.” And they look at you confused, “Well that’s a low score.”  “Yeah, I know. But why? Why didn’t you say it was even lower?”

Mark:
It’s all about… if the thing you want to have happen is 10 on a scale, and the absolute absence of any of that is like 1, then where are you now? And people say, ‘Oh, about three.” And they think that’s a bad answer, but it’s a great answer, because, hey, it’s not a one or a two. So what’s making it a three already? And this is how we get to this piece of the what’s working already stuff.

Ivan:
Exactly. And so I urge everyone pick up a copy of the Third Edition of Solutions Focus. It’s a great book. Mark was kind enough to put me on the front cover with a quote that I totally, absolutely was ecstatic to do, which was that “This book will help deal with the challenges of our times in intelligent and innovative ways.” It is in a sense, and I know we need to wrap up. And I don’t know if you know this, Mark, but I quoted you in my latest book, The 29% Solution, and I recommended The Solutions Focus as part of holding the vision and not the obstacles. And that was a key element of The 29% Solution.

Mark:
That’s right. The obstacles will only matter if they arrive, and most obstacles never actually appear. So the thing is moving forward, and we’re going to talk about that in the next podcast.

Ivan:
Outstanding. Well Mark, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast, and we’ll have you again next week. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. That was some wonderful information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: A problem-focused approach works if you’re dealing with a broken washing machine. When people are involved, you need a solutions focus.

title.rendered: Episode 875: We Don’t Pay You to Think (Classic)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is a rebroadcast of Episode 685, first released on December 9, 2020. Links and special offers may no longer be valid.

This week on the podcast, Dr. Misner shares a story from his college days, when he had a night-shift job stocking shelves at a grocery store. He had an idea for making it easier to get the shelves stocked, but when he mentioned it to his manager, the response was “We don’t pay you to think.”

But paying people to think is exactly what entrepreneurs and managers should always be willing to do. At BNI, Directors, Executive Directors, National Directors, Members, and Dana have all made observations that have changed the way Dr. Misner thinks about things.

The things we say to people can mean a lot. If you’re going to end up in someone’s story, you want to be in it for a positive reason. One of the ways you can do that is to listen to people’s advice and opinions as much as you can. (You don’t have to act on it, but listen to it.)

If you have a story, good or bad, share it in the comments—but don’t name names, either individuals or companies.

For more on the topic of “Whose story are you in?” check out Episode 637, “The Networking Mentor.”

Complete Transcript of Episode 875/685

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I’m doing okay, Priscilla, thanks for asking. That’s an unusual title, isn’t it? You’ve got to be wondering what this is about.

Priscilla:
Definitely.

Ivan:
Well recently, my assistant, Dana, who’s amazing, made a couple of suggestions to me. And afterwards, she asked if I minded her giving these suggestions to me. And I immediately said that not only did I not mind her ideas, I actually wanted her to share those ideas with me. I then told her the story that I’m going to share with you today, and with everyone listening to this podcast, about an experience of mine many, many years ago.

I was 21 years old. I was finishing up my bachelor’s degree in California and I had scholarships to help but I still needed to work a job to pay for my living expenses. And I found a good paying position working for a really large chain grocery store, stocking shelves from midnight until 7:00 a.m. four days a week. And then I went to school. So I would go home, get a shower, and go to school. It was brutal, absolutely brutal. And on some days, I would work all night and go get that shower, and then go straight into classes by 9:00 a.m. Even then, I believed that sometimes you got to do what you got to do, to get to do what you want to do. And early on I knew one thing for certain, and that was that I did not want to work at a grocery store stocking shelves at any hour of the day for a career.

And I came to that realization because of one conversation that I had with the early-shift Assistant Manager. In other words, he was not one of the higher-level assistant managers. And I had this conversation soon after I started working there. The night crew had some serious quotas for boxes that had to go up on the shelves each and every night. While it didn’t sound very hard, the truth is it was back-breaking work and it was probably the most physical job I’ve ever had. And one morning as I was coming off a break, I had a conversation with the Assistant Manager. I made a suggestion to him that I thought would help in moving the many pallets, I mean pallets, of boxes that had to be taken by dolly to every aisle in the store and then put up on the shelves, you know, one item at a time. It was a small suggestion. I don’t even remember what it was, but I thought it would help. And that’s when the Assistant Manager gave me a life lesson that I would take with me for the rest of my career. He said, “Ivan, we don’t pay you to think. We pay you to get lots of boxes on lots of shelves every single night. Now go back to work.”

Priscilla:
Wow.

Ivan:
Yeah. And I remember so vividly standing there thinking, someday, I’m gonna own my own business. And I promised myself that I will never, ever say that to anyone who ever works for me, ever. In fact, I’ll tell them the opposite. I pay you to think. I want ideas. I want input. I want engagement. And that’s the philosophy that I’ve tried to bring to BNI, and it’s certainly the philosophy I’ve tried to bring with Directors, as well as Director Consultants and Ambassadors and Members. I want to hear your thoughts and ideas. Now I have no idea where this manager is today, but if I ever met him again, I’d actually tell him that I appreciate that comment. Because it cemented my belief that managers and entrepreneurs need to do the opposite of what he said to me.

You see words have power. And that’s not the kind of power I want to impart on other people. Managers need to listen to the ideas, entrepreneurs need to listen to the ideas, that employees have. They may not all be gems but listening shows you care about them and you care about their ideas. It also gets engagement, and possibly, even a certain amount of loyalty because the employee or the Director, in our cases many times, feels that their input matters. And I may not have played this perfectly over the years. But it’s something I truly strive to always do with people who have worked for me. I kept track of this Assistant Manager for about 10 years after I left the company. At that point, he had been promoted to the main-shift Assistant Manager.

And I was well on my way to building a global enterprise by that time, that now has operations in more than 70 countries. I believe that paying people to think is exactly what entrepreneurs and managers should always be willing to do. Sometimes, we get our life lessons from people who give us great advice. And sometimes we get our life lessons from people who give us horrible advice. By applying a little discernment, they can actually both be a gift. His was certainly a gift for me. I did my best to never, ever follow it. So, did it go in a slightly different direction than you thought?

Priscilla:
Yes, it certainly did. And that’s a great takeaway for some pretty bad advice.

Ivan:
Yeah, it was pretty bad advice. And you know, I think sometimes managers, entrepreneurs, just get so frustrated with all the things that have to be done. They just don’t want people talking, they just want them doing. And I get that. But at the same time, I think it’s really important to pay attention to the people that work with you and for you and hear them out. Because sometimes they do, in fact, have a gem. And I can’t tell you how many times the people who’ve worked for me gave me advice that changed what it was I was doing. Whether you’re talking about Dana, my assistant, who has many suggestions that I have, like, oh, wow, I didn’t think of that, thank you; to Directors, Executive Directors, National Directors, Members, who have made observations that have changed the way I’ve thought about things. And I don’t know if you remember, Priscilla, on one of our podcasts, I don’t remember the number, I talked about, and I may have done this more than once, how we have people in our story, that we share our experiences, and we tell about people that have been in our story, that are in our life, and that they’ve made a difference in our life, somehow, some way. And generally, these people we talk about are people that have had a positive difference. And one of the things, I know I’ve said this in a previous podcast, is that what’s most important is not who’s in your story, because we all have that. The question is, whose story are you in? And why are you in their story? Words have power. And the things we say to people can mean a lot. And you can be in someone’s story in a negative way, as this Assistant Manager was, or you can be in someone’s story in a positive way. And what I’m suggesting is that we all try to be in someone’s story in a positive way. And one of the ways you can do that is to always, as much as possible at least, listen to people’s advice and opinions. Because they, too, matter. And that’s my message for today. Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I like that message, Ivan. It shows a great deal of respect for the people that  work for you and that you work with. And I think everybody benefits from that.

Ivan:
Yeah. And listen, if you have a story, good or bad, put the good ones, the good, the bad, the ugly, but if it’s bad, don’t name names, okay. Or name the companies. But if you’ve got a story, good or bad, I’d love to see it here on the BNI podcast. That’s it for today. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great, Ivan, thank you so much. Well, that’s it for this week.

All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 874: Small Acts Equal Big Results

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This episode is hosted by Guest Expert Tim Roberts, introduced in Episode 866.

Chapter growth through strategic visitor invitations is the Third Pillar of Success in BNI. Your BNI chapter could double in size in the next month if you take a strategic approach to inviting visitors. Tim Roberts explains how.

At the Meeting: Identify Professions

This should take about 30 seconds per member.

  • Share the number one profession missing from your contact sphere right now.
  • Take responsibility for inviting someone in that profession.
  • Have someone write down who is responsible for which invitation.

After the Meeting: Do Your Research

Search members of your target profession within a 30-mile radius and rank them based on reviews and recommendations. You should be able to list at least 20 different individuals or companies. Here are some good places to search. When searching social networks, be sure to ask for recommendations.

  • Google (Maps, Business Profiles)
  • LinkedIn
  • Facebook
  • Yelp
  • Chamber of Commerce

Start Inviting

Invite each person on your list to the next BNI meeting or Visitors Day. Tim recommends making a personal phone call.

If you can get your chapter to start creating the culture of accountability to some simple activities, the cumulative results will be astounding.

Links

  • Learn more about the Three Pillars of Success in Episode 873.
  • Listen to Episode 566 of BNI The Power of One.

Complete Transcript of Episode 874

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by Tim Roberts, who’s standing in as a guest co-host for Dr. Misner. He was introduced previously in episode 866 and was a co-host in episode number 873. So welcome Tim, thank you for joining us.

Tim:
Thank you Priscilla and thank you everybody for joining me again this week. I’m very excited to be here to be able to help co-host BNI podcast. Last week, I talked about the importance of mindset and understanding that we own our own results when it comes to any networking, but BNI in particular.

And I want to build on that this week, talking about how small acts can equal really great results when each of us, as part of a team, does our part. Last week, I talked about while nobody can network for you, we’re not networking alone either. And we do have this team around us, and the results our team is seeing can really be expanded when each individual person takes ownership of their part.

So in one example last week, I talked about the importance of finding and bringing in those professions that are the most strategic for you as a member. So what professions are going to find you the most results based on opportunity, because they’re in your Contact Sphere and potentially [your] Power Team? Well, if everybody in your chapter were to take that kind of approach, the results are quite astounding.

I talk to a lot of my chapters about how growth is really a tool to success. It’s one of the pillars to our success that we control, right? So again, just for those who may not have heard last week, the Three Pillars to our Success are our activity – how active are we? Our effectiveness – how skilled are we in that activity? And the third one is, how many people are we doing that with? Meaning, what size is the opportunity presented to us based on our network?

And a lot of chapters will set goals for growth throughout the year. They want to grow from 20 to 30, or 30 to 40, or, you know, whatever number it may be. And I’ll ask them, “Why did you pick that number?” And there’s never really an answer. It’s just more of a “seems like a good benchmark to reach, it seems like a good number.” When reality is that chapter could, and should, double within the next month.

I believe your chapter could double its size in the next four weeks. And the way we do that is by each and every member in the group thinking about picking what is the one profession that they need in that group, selfishly, strategically, that’s currently not there, right? So what is the number one profession missing from your contact sphere right now?

Now I’ve seen chapters do this kind of exercise where people will go around the room and share their top-wanted professions, and chapters have boards of their top-wanted professions – “These are the Top 10 professions we want in this chapter.” And they’ll get a few of them from time to time and may be able to check off one on the list, but they hardly ever complete the list. And they hardly ever, even more infrequently, complete the list in a timely manner.

And the reason for that is because it’s just a wish list. It’s a bunch of professions on a piece of paper or on a board up somewhere, saying, “Hey, we all want this. So if you see one…” Versus people taking ownership of the profession that’s going to drive their results the most.

So in a [BNI] chapter, you can go around the room and have everybody share “What is the one profession you need?” Have everybody go around the room and then take ownership of that. Okay, Bob, you said you need a painter – you own getting this chapter a painter. And Sue, you said you need an accountant? You own getting this chapter and accountant. And have everybody take that ownership and that accountability.

If everybody were to run through a very simple exercise (I think it takes about two hours worth of work) of taking that profession go to three different websites – go to Google, go to LinkedIn, go to Facebook, and do some research on, let’s just say you need a painter. I’m going to Google every painter within a 30 mile radius of my chapter, and I’m going to rank them based on reviews and recommendations and their website, etc.

I’m going to go to LinkedIn, I’m going to do the same thing, and I’m going to go to Facebook, and I’m going to literally type in, “Hey, who knows a great painter?” and I’m going to take a list of those recommendations. At the end of that exercise, you should have at least 20 different names, company names, to be inviting for that one profession.

If you were to do that, and then personally invite every single one of them – and my favorite invite is a phone call. I’m a big believer that it’s the most effective way of doing it. If you ever want to hear my language on how to invite, I did a podcast on it, on “BNI The Power of One,” episode 566, you can hear how I would invite. But I think the personal phone call is the way to do it.

If you were to invite all 20 of those people in an effective, you know, confident manner, I believe you’ll get about seven or eight of them to say yes, they want to come to your meeting. Of [that] seven or eight, you’ll get four or five of them to show up because we all know that everybody we invite doesn’t necessarily show up to the meeting. You’ll get about four or five of them to show up. That means you got four painters now in the room at the same time, looking at the one seat your chapter has. I hope that good money, you’re going to get at least two different applications, and now your chapter gets to pick the best painter, and you strategically got the painter that you needed.

Now, if everybody in the chapter just did that simple exercise this month, about two to three hours’ worth of work, your chapter doubles. It doubles, which we know quadruples the opportunities and the referrals generated, just by the fact that we added that many people. Not even getting into the fact of getting them active and getting them skilled and all of the other aspects that will make us even more effective with those people. Just by simply getting that amount of people in, we’re going to quadruple, if not more, the results we’re seeing.

And it’s a very small act individually. It’s not a lot of work, it’s not a lot of effort. It does take some time, and most often, that’s the thing that we miss out on. We have great intentions, but intentions do not equal results. But if you can own that, be accountable to that one small activity, and every member in the chapter does that, the cumulative results are amazing and huge.

And it’s the same thing with some of our other activities. If we can take ownership of better preparing our weekly presentations, you will get greater results if everybody does that. If everybody’s not doing that, even if you prepare your weekly presentation, you spend a bunch of time practicing it, you get some coaching on it, you go and present it. But if everybody else is thinking about what they’re going to talk about, they’re not listening to you, it’s not going to be very effective. But if everybody takes ownership of the small activity of just spending some time preparing, you will produce more results from your current chapter.

If everybody took a little bit more time to put some strategy to their One-to-Ones, you will produce more results than you currently are. It does not take grand leaps, you know, crazy amounts of effort or time individually, when we work effectively as a team. When it’s only 10 or 20% of the chapter trying to do it for everybody, which in the case of growth is statistically true. It’s the 80/20 rule, a hundred percent. About 20% of your chapter is actively trying to grow your chapter; the other 80% are letting it happen to them. Then, yeah, it’s a lot of work. It takes a lot of time. It’s a slow process. It’s probably not super effective for a lot of people.

I’m a big believer that while bigger chapters make more money, statistically, they don’t necessarily make more money for everyone. Because a lot of people are just again, letting the chapter grow around them and not strategically for them, because they’re not engaged in that process what we talked about last week. But if you can get your chapter to start creating the culture of accountability, one of our core values, and in being accountable to some simple activities, some small activities when it’s really broken down individually, the cumulative results will be astounding. That’s all I’ve got this week, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh. Well, I think that’s great. I really appreciate that. We just did a visitor day in my chapter, and we all invited many people, and we tried our best, and we got 30 acceptances, and only 10 showed up.

Tim:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
And we weren’t really very successful. It was kind of strange, but people are still visiting. They’re just… they haven’t joined yet. So…

Tim:
Part of that is because it’s…  if you create some competition, your conversion will go up.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Tim:
And the whole point of a visitor day is to get a lot of visitors there, because it increases conversion, increases competition, and it increases the energy. So visitor day is nothing more than that collaborative, let’s pick a day just to further increase our effectiveness in closing visitors to new members. But the most effective way is, instead of just coming up with a list of 30-40 people to invite, is by inviting 20 people from the same profession, and everybody pick a different one [profession to invite], you’ll be far more effective.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think that’s really great advice. So thank you for that.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 873: You Own Your Results

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This episode is hosted by Guest Expert Tim Roberts, introduced in Episode 866.

If you’re not getting the quality or quantity of referrals you want from BNI, ask yourself “What have I been doing to dictate the results I want to achieve?” At the end of the day, you are the one in charge of the results you get from your BNI membership.

Here are Tim’s Three Pillars of Success in BNI.

Your Activity Level

  • Show up at every meeting.
  • Engage in Open Networking.
  • Prepare your Weekly Presentation.
  • Pay attention to what other members are asking for.
  • Do One-to-Ones consistently.
  • Take leadership roles.
  • Invite visitors.
  • Take trainings.

Your Effectiveness

Learn the skill sets you need to network effectively. BNI offers training on these skill sets–including this podcast.

  • Weekly Presentations
  • Featured Presentations
  • One-to-Ones
  • Inviting Visitors
  • Making Referrals
  • Leadership Roles
  • Regional trainings.

The Visitors You Invite

Invite prospects who help create more opportunity for you and the chapter.

  • Who is missing from the room?
  • Who is missing from your Contact Sphere?
  • Who is missing from your Power Team?
  • What are you doing to grow your chapter?

Complete Transcript of Episode 873

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by Tim Roberts, who’s standing in today as a guest co-host for Dr. Misner. He was introduced in episode 866 and will be standing in as the guest host on the BNI Podcast from time to time. Hello, Tim. Welcome to the podcast.

TIm:
Hello, Priscilla. Thank you for having me and thank you for everybody for joining us in this week’s BNI Podcast. I am humbled and honored to be a co-host here. For those who may not have ever met me before or heard my name, my name is Tim Roberts. I’m the Executive Director for BNI New Hampshire, Northeast Florida, and Central Florida here in the U.S. I also host “BNI The Power of One” Podcast, and we talk more about my background in the episode Priscilla mentioned.

Today, I want to talk about the importance of mindset and owning your own results, and realizing that in BNI we do own our own results. I’ve been in BNI now for 23 years. I’ve had the great pleasure of meeting tens of thousands of members over that time, and one commonality that I’ve seen over the years is how easy and natural it can be to deflect blame on a lack of either total results, or even quality of results, that we might be getting in our BNI membership.

You know, I talk to a lot of members who feel like they’re not getting either the quantity or quality of referrals, and it’s things like, “My chapter’s not big enough.” “I don’t have the right people.” “My members aren’t willing to do One-to-Ones.”  “My members are not engaged.” “My members don’t take it serious.” The other members, the other members, the other members…

And I’ve always challenged people to step back and think about: what have you been doing to dictate the results you’re looking to achieve? And at the end of the day, we truly are in far more control of our results from our networking efforts than many people even think that they are or look at.

There are three pillars to your success in BNI, three things that you control that will dictate your outcomes. Number one is your activity level. So let’s start there. Have you been active enough in your membership to deserve the results you say you desire? Activities in BNI are things like attendance. Are you regularly attending your meeting with great frequency? There will be times when you miss a meeting, but are you habitually missing meetings and pushing the envelope on what is allowed with our attendance policy? When you are at the BNI meeting, are you actively engaged in every aspect? Are you engaged in Open Networking?

Are you actively engaged in listening to what other members are asking for during their weekly presentations? Meaning, are you fully prepared for your weekly presentation so that you can do that? I’ve seen a lot of chapter meetings where you can tell a lot of members are winging their weekly presentation, for lack of a better term. Which means that while everybody else is doing theirs, they’re sitting back thinking about, “Man, what am I going to say this week? What am I going to talk about?” Which makes the entire experience ineffective for everybody in the room.

Are you actively involved in the chapter? Are you taking roles in leadership? Are you getting involved in areas to make an influence, or have an influence, on parts of the chapter, the meeting, the experience that you think might not be going the way you think it should be going. Are you just quick and easy to blame those who are in those roles? Are you active in the meeting after? Are you quickly running out as soon as the meeting ends, or are you hanging around to ask questions, to get more details on people’s weekly presentations, to do some more networking, to connect with a member, to schedule One-to-Ones?

Are you active in your One-to-Ones? Are you regularly doing One-to-Ones consistently? Are you properly prepared for those One-to-Ones? You can do a One-to-One to get credit on a PALMS report, but are you actually going to a One-to-One with a true purpose, with a true strategy, with proper preparation beforehand, with proper follow up afterwards? Or are we just doing an activity to say that we did it?

Are you engaged in inviting? Are you engaged in taking trainings? Are you engaged in all of these activities that, at the end of day, you control? While it’s true that we don’t network alone, what is also true is nobody can do the networking for you. You have to own, and be disciplined enough to put in, the upfront activity.

The second pillar to our success, then, is how effective are we being in that activity? This is, again, something only you can control. Effectiveness means your skill sets. Are you actively pursuing education onto ensuring that when you do a weekly presentation, it’s being done effectively? That you’re saying the right things, that you’re targeting the right types of referrals you’re really looking for? Which is something we’re going to talk about in a future episode. Are you skilled in the activities of networking?

Networking is paradoxical in the sense that we all know we need to do it, thus we all assume we know how to do it, and unfortunately, it’s just not true. BNI is the only organization that I’m really aware of that properly trains and gives a plethora of opportunity for you to learn the different skill sets needed. Weekly presentations being one, featured presentations be another. Are you taking trainings on how to ensure your One-to-Ones are really being effective? Are you taking trainings on how to invite and who you should invite for you strategically? Are you taking trainings on how to find referrals for other people? Are you taking trainings on how to be effective in a leadership role, whether you’re in the role now or in the future? Are you engaged in your region and outside activities, like roundtables that some regions are doing? Trainings that regions are doing? Other networking events, speed networking, these kind of things.

The third pillar, which is probably the most impactful one when it comes to producing your results, is the one that most people are passive about. And if you were to look at a Power of One report that’s run nationally in the U.S. and I know in many other countries, [it is] the one column that people are the least active in. For those who aren’t familiar, the Power of One is a simple report that just is your PALMS report, but scores it on five activities you’re in control of, that you’re responsible for. So it’s attendance, referrals you’re giving to other people, One-to-Ones you’re doing, visitors you’re bringing, and CEUs that you’re earning. The one column that the high majority of members score the fewest points in, if any at all, is visitors [the third pillar].

You are in control of who’s in the room, way more than you may believe you are. Most understand that more members will create more opportunity for them. But where they lack strategy is thinking about what exact members will create the greatest opportunity for them. Who’s missing from the room, from their contact spheres, and potential power teams, that will actually give them consistent results just based on the fact that they’re looking for the same opportunities you’re looking for each and every week.

They become passive in this third lever to their success. They wait for the chapter to grow around them, which it may very well do, but it may not do it in a way that’s most strategic and effective for you. If you really want to increase your consistency of referral generation, you need to be consistently asking yourself who’s missing from your contact sphere and take a very proactive approach to bringing those people into your network yourself. You own that result.

So if you’re sitting there and saying, “Oh, my chapter’s not big enough, my chapter doesn’t have the right people in it,” I always ask, “What are you doing about that?” And oftentimes I’ll get met with a very blank stare of like, “What do you mean – what am I doing about it?  The chapter needs to do it,” which is just not true.

So these are the three pillars to your success. I know as I get the opportunity from time-to-time to be on here, we’ll dig into some of them, or all of them, hopefully in more detail. But at the end of the day, I think it’s important to realize you are in more control of your outcomes than maybe you even think you are.

I often ask a lot of my members, why are they in BNI? And I tell them the answer is not referrals or money, that’s a tangible result. But what are the referrals [and] the more money going to allow you to do? I use the word success. I love that word because it’s a word you get to define for yourself. Everybody has a different definition and a different purpose for what they’re really there for.

My follow up question recently has been, who else is reliant on you achieving that success, and who else knows you’re coming to your weekly meeting each and every week? It could be a spouse, a partner, kids, employees, whoever it is, and almost every time everybody’s hands go up, we all have somebody that knows we were part of BNI, knows we’re going to this each week, knows we’re doing it in attempt to grow our business and reach our desired level of success.

The question I ask them then is, if I hooked you up to a lie detector, if you had to be 100% honest, how proud would you say they are of your efforts? Or how proud would they be of your efforts if they were to see what it is you are or are not doing with your time in your chapter? I think it’s important to think about.

We’ll dig into each more, but at the end of the day, again, I reiterate, you control your own destiny. I think it’s key to end with a quote that I love, which is from Napoleon Hill, that says: “Both success and failure are largely the results of habits.” Priscilla, thank you.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great, Tim, thank you so much. I think that’s really, really useful information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
