title.rendered: Episode 872: Ask Ivan about GDP and Morning Meetings

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI members want to know: Is TYCFB (Thank You for Closed Business) really the BNI equivalent of GDP (Gross Domestic Product)? And why do most BNI meetings take place early in the morning? Dr. Misner answers in today’s episode.

TYCFB and GDP

According to the International Monetary Fund, Gross Domestic Product GDP measures the amount of business generated in a country. Thank You for Closed Business measures the amount of business generated in BNI for its members. Embrace the analogy and celebrate the success of BNI.

Early Morning Meetings

Dr. Misner is not a morning person. When he started BNI, he had to set three alarm clocks to be able to show up on time. But scheduling the meetings so early meant they wouldn’t interrupt meetings with his clients or the regular business hours of BNI members. There are some BNI meetings that take place at lunchtime or in the evening, but all of them take place outside of regular business hours.

Links

  • Learn more about measuring GDP (IMF website).
  • Listen to Episode 860 for the original analogy between TYCFB and GDP.
  • Listen to Episode 866, where Dr. Misner introduces next week’s co-host, Tim Roberts.

If you have a question for Dr. Misner, fill out the form at the bottom of the page.

Complete Transcript of Episode 872

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, I’m in Austin still, but I’m headed to Santa Barbara [California] next week to work from Santa Barbara and have a little bit of time off. And I just want to let everyone know that Tim Roberts, who I introduced in Episode 866, will be my guest co-host. For the first time, I’ll have a guest co-host. He’ll be running the next two podcasts, and I introduced him as the guest expert or guest co-host in episode 866, so please welcome Tim next week on his first two episodes of the BNI Podcast.

Priscilla:
Great. I look forward to it.

Ivan:
So today I’m doing an Ask Ivan session, and so I’ve got two questions. One is about GDP, and the other is about morning meetings — in particular, why are most of our [BNI] groups, not all, but why are most in the morning?

The first is, actually, was not a question, but it was a statement that was given to me, which I thought was kind of interesting. I received a message from a [BNI] member regarding episode number 860 which was another Ask the Founder, Ask Ivan, and it had several topics. One of the topics was, Thank You for Closed Business, or TYFCB, as we call it sometimes, Thank You for Closed Business. In that podcast, I said that based on the United Nations’ estimates, United Nations’ estimates of GDP — Gross Domestic Product, there were 100 countries in the world with a lower GDP than what BNI generated for its members in Thank You for Closed Business last year.

Priscilla:
Mm hmm.

Ivan:
The member scolded me for comparing GDP to Thank You for Closed Business, telling me that they are not the same. Okay, so from the perspective that one is a nation’s Gross Domestic Product and the other is BNI’s business generated for members, there is obviously a difference between a country and a company.

However, let’s look at the definition of GDP, Gross Domestic Product. The link for the definition that I’m about to give is going to be in the transcript. This is the definition: “GDP measures the monetary value of final goods and services, that is, those that are bought by the final user produced in a country in a given period of time, (say a quarter or a year).” That’s a parenthetical comment in the definition. “It counts all of the output generated within the borders of a country.”

That’s the definition according to the International Monetary Fund [IMF] website of what GDP is. Now let’s replace the words GDP in this definition. Let’s replace the words ‘GDP’ and ‘country’ with the words ‘Thank You for Closed Business’ and ‘BNI members.’

Ivan:
If we do that you get “Thank You for Closed Business measures the monetary value of final goods and services. That is, those that are bought by the final user produced by BNI members in a given period of time, (say a quarter or a year). It counts all the output generated within BNI.”

It’s the same thing. Okay, one’s a company, one’s a country. But if you just replace country, if you just replace GDP and country with Thank You for Closed Business and BNI, it’s the same thing, word for word, same definition, simply replacing country information with BNI information. The comparison that I gave between GDP and Thank You for Closed Business holds up as a very reasonable analogy. GDP is the amount of business generated in a country. Thank You for Closed Business is the amount of business generated in BNI for its members. It’s a fair comparison.

This member’s comment to me is a great example of how people pick apart an example instead of embracing the general concept it represents. Things like this happen in business and in BNI chapters all the time. There are people who look for problems where none really exist. BNI passes millions of referrals a year, and our members generate tens of billions of US dollars’ worth of business for each other. Embrace that incredible statistic. Celebrate that amazing number. I am humbled by the business that BNI members generate for one another, and I like to explain things like that in a way that gives us all a unique perspective on what an amazing job the members are doing and the impact that the organization is having all around the world, in local communities all around the world.

Most people don’t know this, but when I published my doctoral dissertation at the University of Southern California in 1993, the thesis related to the impact that groups like BNI could have on local communities around the world. Well after almost 40 years in this organization, and over 30 years since I wrote my dissertation, I can categorically state that the impact has met or exceeded my wildest imagination. We are positively impacting local communities all around the world, providing a system, a process and the training to generate referrals and create, as the IMF says, monetary value for goods and services. We are changing the way the world does business, and we’re doing that through our philosophy of Givers Gain. I stand by my analogy; I think is outstanding. And stop being so picky.

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
That’s my opinion of that. Okay, the second question, and I think we have time for two. The second question is a pretty quick answer. And the second question was from somebody in Arizona [USA], and she asked, “Why did I start BNI with morning meetings?” Now granted, not all of our chapters meet in the morning. We do now have groups that meet in or around lunch, or in the afternoon. We have a few, not a lot, that meet in the evening. But the overwhelming majority of chapters still meet in the morning. And she asked the question, and she loved the answer, and so I thought I would share it, because I talked to her directly, and here’s the answer.

I started BNI in the morning because [for] most companies, their regular business hours are later. You know, they open after BNI meetings are done. And I started BNI in the morning. And here’s the irony, I’m a night owl. At least I used to be a night owl. I did late hours and did not get up early, but I started BNI early in the morning because none of my clients would meet with me at 7:00 or 7:30, or an early morning meeting. They weren’t interested in meeting with me in the early morning. They wanted to meet after business hours started, and so none of my clients would meet at that hour.

And so I figured, what the heck — that’s a great time to do business. Yeah, okay, I don’t like really early mornings, but I’m gonna start… and the first chapters started at 7:00 am, and it varies a little bit from country-to-country, because in some [BNI] countries the normal start time is at 10:00 not 9:00. And so chapters do meet at different times in the morning, but the first chapters I started were 7:00 am which is ironic, because I don’t like, or didn’t like, early morning meetings.

Ivan:
I used to have to set three alarm clocks, Priscilla, to get up.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
Seriously, if I had to get up at 5:00 am so I could leave at 6:00 to get to a meeting, I would set my alarm next to me twice, and the first time it went off, I shut it off, but I had it set to go a second time. And I’d set it off like an hour in advance to just start waking up. And then it would bring a second time, like 15-20 minutes before I had to get up. And then the third alarm clock I would set in the bathroom, so I’d have to get up and walk to the dressing area in the bathroom to wake up. That’s how I hated early mornings.

But I knew that was the best time for me to meet to network, because I wouldn’t miss any business. And so that’s the reason why the overwhelming majority are in the morning. And so when people say to me, I’m not a morning person…. no, don’t. Nobody was less of a morning person than me.

And the irony is, of course, that once people started asking me to open a second or third or fourth of fifth chapter, I was doing at least four morning meetings a week for 15-20 years! (laughing) I would do three or four a week, and I’d keep waking up going, ‘I hate mornings. Why did I start this in the mornings?’ But I did it because my clients didn’t want to meet in the morning, and that’s the reason why most of our [BNI] chapters are morning meetings, and that’s the reason why I started the organization as a morning meeting organization.

Those are my two Ask the Founder… well, one was ‘give my opinion to the Founder,’ and the other was an Ask the Founder. Those are my answers. Any thoughts, Priscilla, before we wrap?

Priscilla:
I’m in favor of morning meetings, that’s what I want to say. And the reason is because if you start, I think, an afternoon meeting, it’s really hard to get back to work after that, and not just miss your clients, but actually try to refocus.

Ivan:
It is, and I agree. One of the reasons why we did afternoon, and there’s much less of them, is childcare, getting kids to school, that was an issue.

Priscilla:
Oh yeah, right. That’s a big issue.

Ivan:
And so that’s why we eventually started doing, you know, lunch or afternoon, but they are still a minority of the chapters. What do you think about the GDP thought? Am I off-base with comparing GDP to Thank You for Closed Business?

Priscilla:
I don’t think so. I think you explained it really well.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think it was just whining. People complain as though it’s an Olympic event sometimes. It’s not, I’ve checked.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Yeah, I like that you said, “Don’t be so picky.”

Ivan:
Yeah, really. All right, well, that’s it. That’s all I have for today. And remember everyone, next week Tim Roberts is going to do two co-host podcasts. Thank you. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you. Ivan, well, that’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

This entry is part 33 of 33 in the series Ask Ivan

Is TYCFB really the BNI equivalent of GDP? And why are most BNI meetings early in the morning? Dr. Misner answers in this week’s episode.

title.rendered: Episode 871: When Conversation Stops, Conflict Escalates

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Conflicts in BNI chapters and business relationships don’t just escalate out of nowhere. They start small and grow bigger as conversations break down.

Two common reasons that conversations break down are a lack of active listening and a focus on being right.

  • Reflect what the other person says back to them to be sure you understand and they know you’ve heard them.
  • Approach conversations with a mindset of collaboration rather than competition. Focus on finding a solution that works for everyone.

The next time you find yourself in a difficult conversation, remember to listen actively, focus on solutions, and build trust. By doing so, you can create a better outcome for everyone involved.

Resources for Better Conversations

  • Crucial Conversations: Tools for Talking When Stakes Are High, by Joseph Grenny, Kerry Patterson, Ron McMillan, Al Switzer, and Emily Gregory.
  • The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success, by Ivan Misner, Dawa Tarchin Phillips, and Heidi Scott Giusto

Have you applied these techniques successfully in your own relationships? Share your experience in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 871

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, I feel like I finally have a week at home. I’m in Austin, Texas this week, and enjoying having a week working from my home office.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great.

Ivan:
I think I mentioned a previous podcast that I was in the Philippines and in Vietnam. They were both great [BNI] events, and so I’m home this week.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Oh, wonderful. And what are you sharing with us today?

Ivan:
My topic today is about conversation and conflict. And when rational conversation stops, conflict escalates. Whether it be in our personal relationships, BNI, or general business dealings, when communication breaks down, things tend to go from bad to worse. As someone who has spent a considerable amount of time studying human interaction and communication, I can tell you without a doubt, the conflicts don’t just escalate out of nowhere. They often start small and grow bigger as conversations break down. And I’ve seen that in BNI over and over again.

One of the most common reasons for conversation breakdowns is a lack of active listening. Active listening is an essential part of communication that involves fully understanding and empathizing with the other person’s point of view. And when someone feels like they’re not being heard or understood, they’re likely to become defensive and resistant to what the other person is saying. That all sounds simple, but the truth is that when you’re talking to someone and they’re speaking to you, shut up. Listen, be quiet. Pay attention to what they’re having to say. If you don’t, it’s one of the ways that the conflict escalates.

To avoid that kind of conflict, it’s essential to practice active listening. This means that instead of just waiting for your turn to speak, you’re fully engaged in the conversation, and you’re seeking to understand the other person’s perspective. And by doing so, you can create a more productive dialog that can lead to a resolution of the conflict.

Another common cause of conversation breakdowns is when one or both parties are focused on being right, rather than finding a solution. And this is a common problem in business settings, where individuals may be more concerned with protecting their own interests than finding a mutually beneficial solution… certainly true in BNI situations.

If all you do, in any scenario, if all you do is focus on the problems, you become an expert on problems. However, when you focus on solutions, you can become an expert on solutions. And in a few weeks, I’m going to have a guest on, Mark McKergow, who is the author of the book, “Solutions Focus,” and we’re going to talk further about this in an upcoming podcast. When people are more focused on being right than finding a solution, they tend to become very rigid in their thinking and less willing to compromise. And this can quickly escalate a conflict as both parties become more entrenched in their positions.

One way to avoid this is to approach conversations with a mindset of collaboration rather than competition. By focusing on finding a solution that works for everyone, rather than just [for] you, you’re more likely to create a conversation that leads to a successful outcome. One of the most significant causes of conversation breakdowns, just to cover an important topic here, is the lack of trust. Trust is essential in any relationship, whether it’s personal or professional, and when trust is broken, it’s difficult to establish a productive dialog, as each party is likely to be skeptical of the other’s intentions.

So to build trust, it’s important to be transparent and honest in your communications — tactful, diplomatic, but honest. This means being open and upfront about your intentions, your expectations, and your goals, again, diplomatically. And by doing that, you create a foundation of trust that can help sustain productive conversations and prevent conflicts.

In addition to the causes of conversation breakdowns that I’ve just mentioned, it’s important to recognize the role that emotions can play in escalating conflicts. This is so true in BNI scenarios. Emotions can be powerful drivers of behavior, and when individuals feel angry, frustrated, or resentful, it can be difficult to maintain a productive dialog. Therefore, it’s important to manage your emotions during a conversation, especially if you’re feeling upset or defensive. One way to do this is to take a step back, take a few deep breaths, before you respond, and this can help you to calm down and approach the conversation in a more rational, objective manner.

So to review what I’ve said already, when conversation stops, conflict escalates. Clear, open, honest, and direct communication is the way to resolve most conflicts. And to prevent this from happening, it’s important to practice active listening. Focus on finding solutions rather than being right, building trust in your relationships. And by doing these things, you create a more productive and successful dialog.

There’s a book that I highly recommend on this topic, probably one of the best books I’ve read about dealing with conflict between individuals, and that book is “Crucial Conversations.” It is a fantastic book, “Crucial Conversations.” I’ve used it many times, and it helped me through a really serious problem that I talk about in the book I wrote called “The 3rd Paradigm.” And here are a few of the things that I learned out of the book, “Crucial Conversations.” One is be aware of feelings, which I already mentioned. Second is acknowledge their anxiety — “I can see you’re upset. This must be really difficult. Or “You sound very frustrated.” Be quiet and listen, listen, listen, listen.

Ask questions, restate the concern. These are all topics that came out of “Crucial Conversations.” Focus on a mutual purpose. Focus on a mutual purpose. And to aggressive statements, ask, “Is there another choice than that?” Probably the single most effective technique that I have found in the book is called “the feasible and.” That’s the phrase “the feasible and.” Here’s how it goes: Is there a way to do X and be able to accomplish Y? That’s “the feasible and.” Is there a way we can do X and still accomplish Y? And that is sort of a mindset reset that gets people to think about both perspectives in a very, very diplomatic way.

When one of the potential problems is that when people purposely withhold meaning or information from one another, it undermines the organization. So that’s why it’s important to communicate. Otherwise, what you have happening if you’re not communicating is you get people who are quietly criticizing and passively resisting. [That’s] another topic out of “Crucial Conversations.”

At the end of the day, communication is an essential part of not only our lives, but in particular, BNI meetings, and conflicts are inevitable. However, by focusing on improving our communication skills and building stronger relationships, we can minimize the impact of conflicts, and we can create more productive and successful outcomes.

As the old saying goes, it’s not what you say but how you say it. By focusing on building positive relationships and improving our communication skills, we can create conversations that lead to positive outcomes and prevent conflicts from escalating. So the next time you find yourself in a difficult conversation, remember to listen actively, focus on solutions, and build trust. By doing so, you can create a better outcome for everyone involved.

In running an organization, Priscilla, with hundreds of thousands of members, tens of thousands of locations, I really learned that effective communication is the key. I’m not always a master at it, but I find that when I’m at my best at listening and communicating diplomatically using some of the techniques that I’ve just talked about, magic happens. And it happens with a conscious effort to hear the other party communicate and always look for that ‘feasible and.’ That’s my message for today, Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Oh, I think that’s great. I think it’s really useful — all of the different aspects of it, and especially the listening part.

Ivan:
Yeah. And for me, I found that the active listening works best when you restate the statement. “Let me understand what you’re saying. You’re saying this…” And you’ll find that sometimes they’ll say, “No, that’s not what I’m saying.” “Okay, I’m sorry. Explain it to me again.” Or they’ll say, “Yeah, that’s exactly it. I’m really ticked off over that.” And then, okay, so now you know where it’s going, and that leads you to the place where, “Okay, I see where you’re coming from. How can we accomplish what you’re concerned about, and be able to do this?”

And so you look for a mutual objective and a focus on solutions rather than the problem, because if all you do is focus on the problem, you become an expert at the problem. Whereas if you focus on solutions, you can become an expert on solutions. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Thank you. Dr Misner. Well, that’s it for this week. Thanks so much for that great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Prevent conflicts in your BNI chapter and business by approaching conversations with a mindset of collaboration, not competition.

title.rendered: Episode 870: Co-opetition

content.rendered:
Synopsis

BNI Global Director of Learning and Development Steve Renz explains how specialization allows BNI members to collaborate with their competitors to capture value within their markets. Co-opetition offers an avenue for growth that a lot of business owners aren’t pursuing but should. You can find co-opetive partners both inside your own chapter and in neighboring chapters.

The Four Phases of Co-opetition

  1. Initiation: Identifying gaps in your services and discussing what your potential partner can bring to the table.
  2. Development: Establishing trust, developing a relationship, and deciding how to work together.
  3. Assessment: Are you getting the value out of this relationship that you need to? Is it time for improvement? Is it time to separate?
  4. Maintenance: Continued communication and identifying additional opportunities for each other.

Complete Transcript of Episode 870

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. And I am in Vail, Colorado [USA] this week for the Transformational Leadership Council meeting. I’ve been a member… this is our 20th anniversary for TLC.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
And so I’ll be spending some time with Jack Canfield, [author of] “Chicken Soup for the Soul,” and a number of other top names in the transformational field.

Priscilla:
That sounds wonderful.

Ivan:
I don’t know why they let me in. I’m a business guy.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
But, you know, they do let thought leaders in. So they brought me in as a thought leader.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s good. So can you explain what we’re doing here with co-opetition?

Ivan:
Yeah. Co-opetition is the podcast today, and I have my friend, Dr. Steve Renz. Steve serves as the BNI Global Director of Learning and Development. He is a US Marine veteran and a former BNI Executive Director, and co-owner of GovCon Dynamics. Steve holds a PhD in Organizational Leadership with a focus on Entrepreneurial Leadership from Regent University, and an MBA from Saint Leo University.

He’s a Six Sigma Green Belt, and Graham Weihmiller, the past CEO [of BNI], says those kind of belts are great, but they won’t defend you in a street fight, but they will help you manage projects effectively. So that’s what Six Sigma is all about.

He holds a master’s certificate in Project Management from Villanova University, and he served as an adjunct professor in the Schools of Business and Leadership in the MBA and PhD programs at Regent University, South University, and Carolina University. Steve, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Steve:
Thank you so much, Ivan. It’s a pleasure to be here.

Ivan:
It’s really good to have you on. We have an interesting topic today. It’s co-opetition, and sometimes people are not familiar with that term. It’s sort of a combination of cooperation and competition, I think. But from your perspective, what is it, Steve?

Steve:
You’re exactly right. Co-opetition really deals with organizations that compete but collaborate simultaneously to capture value within their markets.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, I see that a lot in BNI Chapters that have people that at first glance are competitive, but as you dig deeper, they realize that specialization is the key — most particularly attorneys. And I’ve talked about this in previous podcasts, where there are multiple attorneys in a Chapter, each with a specialization. And at first glance, you think they compete, but in reality they cooperate very effectively,

Steve:
Absolutely yes. And I think it ties in well with your book, “The 3rd Paradigm, A Radical Shift To Greater Success.” And I think this could be a radical shift that could be used by small business owners, large business owners, those that may not view this as a strategic initiative.

Ivan:
Yeah, I totally agree. So why should firms seek cooperative partners?

Steve:
Yeah, I think, you know, that’s a great question. You know, traditionally, in traditional business schools, it teaches it’s competition, it’s Sun Tzu, it’s art of war and things like that. But, you know, with almost half of all businesses failing within the first five years, with economies being a little bit more turbulent, with our customers’ expectations changing right now, markets becoming more complex, I think co-opetition offers an avenue that a lot of business owners aren’t pursuing but should. So there’s a lot of reasons, I think, for entrepreneurs, small business owners in particular, to really look at this as an effective strategy. I’ve seen it. I’ve utilized it in the path for my own success.

Ivan:
Yeah, I have, too, and it goes back 41 years for me. When I was a management consultant, I worked with other management consultants who had different areas of expertise. You know, mine was mostly in strategic planning and HR, and I worked with a number of management consultants that could have been competitive, but their focus was more in finance, which was not really my love, and I would work with them, and we had an agreement that I don’t take your clients, you don’t take my clients, and we worked together in a very cooperative way, although technically we were competition.

Steve:
Right. I really… my background in it really came from the government contracting perspective, when the government will often mandate co-opetition.

Ivan:
Mm hmm.

Steve:
They don’t call it co-opetition, but you are forced, you know, in a lot of regards, on large government contracts, where, through small business preference programs, the government mandates that a certain portion must go to the small businesses — the women owned, the veteran owned, HUBZones, the 8(a) candidates. Even the entire 8(a) program is set up around the mentor protégé program, to where those large businesses must cooperate with the small businesses to help them succeed.

Ivan:
Well now, there’s two things there I want to dig a little deeper. What did you call it, “8H”?

Steve:
8(a). It’s actually a tax designation, but it’s one of the [U.S.] federal government’s Small Business preference programs.

Ivan:
All right, I’m not familiar with that. Can you give us 30 seconds on what that is?

Steve:
Sure it’s reserved for socially and economically disadvantaged small businesses. So, it’s traditionally minority owned businesses who can… The 8(a) program is incredible because it allows those firms to acquire sole source contracts with the government.

Ivan:
All right, 8(a). I had not heard that expression before, and I’m guessing a lot of members maybe haven’t as well. So how do small business owners and entrepreneurs identify with co-opetition, and partners in co-opetition?

Steve:
Yeah, the part of the study that I did with this was a qualitative study, and what really came out with it, and you know, this ties in so well with BNI. And identifying it starts with, you know, your relationships, whether they were personal or professional. Most of those that I studied who were successfully engaging in co-opetitive partnerships, they looked to the relationships that they had previously, friends, people that they worked with in the past. But the other thing was, is they utilized their networks in identifying. When a small business owner is looking, they’re asking, “Hey, I need…”

I’ll use an example: We had three members in our region, three BNI members, and it was John, Dallas, and Matt, who were landscapers; all small businesses, relative startup, new businesses. And they would get large contracts, or large projects that they couldn’t take on themselves. So they could share equipment between each other. They could share staff [if] one person couldn’t show up for work, or, you know, they were down a lawnmower or two, they would call on the others, and together, all of them really grew their business. So I saw that play out.

I saw it with our chiropractors, who would refer patients depending on the specialty of that chiropractor, or maybe a another chiropractor had a certain piece of equipment that would be better for a particular patient, so they were referring patients back and forth amongst each other.

Ivan:
Yeah. So what you’re talking about is sort of co-opetition across chapter lines. Can you give an example of potential co-opetition within a chapter, besides attorneys, which was an example I gave.

Steve:
Yeah. A lot of our tech firms, our IT firms, we have firms who do software development, some are focused on cyber security, some are focused on hardware and installation. All of those, it’s that Power Team that comes together to help not only support each other’s businesses, but to satisfy the requirements of the customer.

Ivan:
The first one I saw in BNI was life insurance and property/casualty insurance [P&C], because the P&C agents often do life [insurance] but they work with a life [insurance] agent very effectively, and that was the first competition that I saw work in BNI, literally 40 years ago. So we’re coming up on time. Give me some of the qualities that are sought in co-opetition and partnerships.

Steve:
Yeah, the number one quality, and I think you mentioned this, it was trust.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Steve:
Because your reputations are linked, but you also have to have those proven abilities. You know, if you’re going to enter into a co-opetitive partnership, the trust has to be there. But they’ve got to be able to perform and execute to your standards, and really the standards of the customer. And then there’s also that mutuality. It helps when they have a shared culture, shared values, that there’s mutual respect, but there also has to be that expectation of reciprocity – that you’re going to work together to help each other, that it’s not just going to be a one-sided relationship.

Ivan:
Yeah, of course. Are there any phases that you would identify?

Steve:
Yeah. I think it’s really similar to what we call VCP [Visibility, Credibility, Profitability] is going through these phases. It’s an Initiation Phase, is what I call it. It’s when you’re first starting to feel out your potential competitive partner. You’re looking at your internal gaps, you’re looking at what this potential partner can bring to the table. And it starts with the communication process and an assessment of their capabilities. And here’s where you just kind of start, as you’re opening discussions of forming those expectations.

Then it moves into that Development Phase, where now we start establishing what is this trust and commitment level we’re going to engage in? How are we going to work together? We need to provide some feedback. And what was found in the study is, in this phase, if it was a personal relationship in the beginning, it starts developing into more of a professional relationship. If it started as a professional relationship, it really starts developing into a more personal relationship.

And then probably one of the most important phases that came out of this study was the Assessment Phase. You know, as you’re going along, you’re working together, this is where we start, we need to start questioning: Are we getting the value out of this relationship that we need to? Is it time for improvement? Is it time to separate? Or should we continue it?

And then the next most important phase is that Maintenance Phase. You know, it’s continued communication, identifying additional opportunities for each other, and that relationship continues to develop if you determine that it is profitable and we should engage further.

Ivan:
Well, we’re out of time, Steve. Any last thought before I turn it back over to Priscilla.

Steve:
Well, I highly recommend as a business strategy, really take a serious look at co-opetition, especially as a small business, a startup business, if you’re entrepreneurially oriented, I think co-opetition provides an avenue for you to really grow and develop.

Ivan:
Yeah, this is really an abundance mentality perspective as opposed to a scarcity mentality, and I see that almost always work better within BNI. Dr. Steve Renz, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast, and thank you for everything you do for BNI.

Steve:
Thank you, Ivan, I really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both for such great information. T

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Steve Renz explains how specialization allows BNI members to collaborate with their competitors to capture value within their markets.

title.rendered: Episode 869: Is There Room for Intuition in Business? (Classic)

content.rendered:
Note: This is a rebroadcast of Episode 686, originally broadcast on December 16, 2020. Offers and links may have changed.

Synopsis

Some people don’t think that intuition has a place in business. But what is intuition, really? The dictionary defines intuition as

The direct perception of truth or facts, independent of any reasoning process; it is an immediate apprehension; or a keen and quick insight into something.

But is intuition really independent of reasoning? Dr. Misner shares the story of how he came to learn that intuition is not about instinct but about quickly using reasoning given your amassed understanding of a particular issue.

(The fun part is that he learned this by reading Arthur C. Clarke’s 2001: A Space Odyssey.)

Your “gut feelings” won’t always be right, but don’t ignore them. Dr. Misner’s experience has shown him that intuition definitely has a place in business.

Do you have story about how intuition has served you well in business or in life? Or about a time you did not follow your intuition and regretted it? Share it in the comments below.

Complete Transcript of Episode 686/869

Ivan:
I am doing great. And I’ve been doing video messages all over the world. I’m, you know, basically traveling by video to many, many regions around the world. I miss the handshakes, Priscilla. I think I’ve probably shaken more hands than most politicians. I miss meeting people. I’m looking forward to the day that I am released to go out and meet people again.

Priscilla:
Yeah, we all are.

Ivan:
Yes, indeed. So, interesting question: what do you think, Priscilla? Is there room for intuition in business?

Priscilla:
I love the idea of using intuition for business. So I’m looking forward to hearing what you have to say about it.

Ivan: Well, my answer to the question is yes, most definitely. Now, decades ago, I may not have thought that, but over the years, I’ve really changed my opinion. And I believe that intuition can be another tool in the business tool belt. Now the definition of intuition is “The direct perception of truth or facts, independent of any reasoning process; it is an immediate apprehension; or a keen and quick insight into something.”

So years ago, I read a science fiction book—and there’s a somewhat unknown fact about me: I love science fiction books. I really started that love when I was a teenager, and my mother bought me 2001: A Space Odyssey by Arthur C. Clarke. And I just loved that book, and I read it long before the movie came out. So I enjoy science fiction, that’s my guilty pleasure, that and some television shows, but anyway, science fiction.

I read this book, and it talked about, “intuits”, I-N-T-U-I-T-S. These are people in their society, in this sci-fi book, who seemed to understand things instinctively. And the intuits were thought to have this incredible ability to have immediate cognition of a situation. But the truth was that the intuits developed the skills to quickly assemble the facts, analyze the data, and predict the probabilities based on their field of expertise. And yeah, the book was science fiction but it made a statement that really resonated with me. It said something that totally flies in the face of the definition that I read.

It said that intuits trained for many years in a specific field; they trained in different fields, and that it wasn’t just an instinctual understanding of an issue, but in fact it was about quickly using reasoning given their amassed understanding of a particular issue. And in the real world, that’s basically what predictive analytics does using computers today.

So, having reconsidered my opinion on instincts, I noticed that as I acquired more experience in my field, both in terms of networking and running a global business, I found myself better at assessing issues quickly and having a “gut feeling” about the direction I should go. And what I had presumed was instinct was, at least in part, quickly assessing the situation given my amassed knowledge of a particular subject. What some people think of as instinct might, in fact, be this amassed knowledge applied rapidly.

In fact, I recently had someone drop me a note about a particular challenge he was having. It was complex and he laid it out for me, and I gave him some advice. And in an email response discussing the resolution of the issue, he said to me that I had assessed a particular problem accurately and concluded by saying “Your gut instinct is amazing!”

But truth be told, it was maybe partly intuition, but substantially it was my years and years of seeing situations just exactly like this or very, very similar to this, and quickly assessing the problem and offering a solution. So, in my very own narrow field, I looked like an intuit to this person, it looked like the answer just came to me. So I’ve discovered that as I’ve acquired more experience, I learned to follow my intuition or my “gut” more and more. But trust me when I say it’s not infallible.

But I no longer take it for granted. I listen to my gut feelings far more today than I did when I was in my 20s or 30s. And I assess things based on how I feel about them in many ways, in addition to the hard facts, or the point of view that is being pitched to me or presented to me by someone else.

Haven’t we all been in situations where we had a feeling, just a feeling we should do something, but then we didn’t? And when that creates a problem, we look back on it. And we clearly see why we should have followed our intuition. I believe our intuition, at least in part, and I’d love to talk about this for a moment or two, Priscilla, but I think our intuition, at least in part, is probably related to the experiences we’ve had. And what we’re doing is quickly processing an interpretation to a situation based on those experiences.

Now, on the other hand, I suppose I could have a heightened sense of ESP, but I think it’s the former. That was a joke, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
(Chuckling) Oh, well I was just thinking, Ivan, often people talk about emotional intelligence. And I think that certain people have a stronger sense of those qualities than other people. And sometimes people are very analytical. And other times, people are really sensitive emotionally, and empathy, they have empathy.

You know, I’m very strong in the emotional side of things. And oftentimes, when I’m meeting somebody for the first time, they may be telling me their name, but I’m not really listening to their name, so I immediately forget it. But it’s because I’m getting their vibe, I’m thinking about who they are. I’m looking in their eyes, I’m feeling their energy; I’m analyzing it from a whole other perspective. And I think that intuition comes from that side of knowledge.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, that’s a whole segment I could have added into the podcast on emotional intelligence, because I think intuition and emotional intelligence do, in fact, come in hand to hand a little bit, certainly intuition and emotional intelligence, no question about it. That’s a really good observation.

So you know, the more I think about intuition, the more I think about it from a more esoteric perspective, not just from a business perspective. I read a quote recently that really resonated with me and you know, my intuition tells me that when a quote really resonates with you, you need to pay attention to it. And the quote was “That thing we call intuition…It’s your soul. You can trust it.” And I think that’s a powerful phrase. And it’s something that we can apply both in business and in our lives. And I’m inclined to agree with it.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan actually.

Ivan:
It does indeed. So, for those of you listening to the podcast this week, I’d love for you to share any story you have about how intuition has served you well. Or, where did you not follow your intuition and maybe you regret it. I’d love to hear your experience on intuition and whether it has a fit in business, and in life. That’s everything I’ve got for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, thank you so much, Ivan. That was really a kind of a deep podcast. I really enjoyed it.

All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 868: 10 Ways to Create Referrals for Life

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Establishing lasting referral connections is absolutely essential for any sustained growth in business. Generating referrals takes more effort than other marketing tactics, but it’s also more cost-effective and far more trusted. Here are 10 ways to create referrals for life, which is what BNI is about.

  1. Cultivate a mindset centered on referrals.
  2. Build strong relationships.
  3. Educate your network.
  4. Deliver exceptional service.
  5. Ask for referrals.
  6. Give referrals.
  7. Stay connected.
  8. Leverage social media.
  9. Attend networking events.
  10. Show gratitude.

Complete Transcript of Episode 868

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Vietnam this week, visiting for BNI [National] Conference in Vietnam. It’s my second visit there, and the first one was truly, truly amazing, a great experience. As a matter of fact, I have a photograph, literally, just above my computer here of me at the Vietnamese [BNI] Conference the last time I was there, about eight or nine years ago. So I’m excited to be back here.

Priscilla:
Yeah, is it a big group in Vietnam?

Ivan:
Oh, yeah, there’s lots and lots of members, very successful BNI chapters there. They’re doing a great job.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s cool.

Ivan:
So I’ve got a lot of content in this presentation. Let me jump in. It’s, as you said, 10 Ways to Create Referrals for Life. And so for those of you listening, pull out your pencil and paper and take notes, because I’m going to give you lots of content here to share with your fellow [BNI] members. We know that referrals play a role in the success of every business, and establishing lasting referral connections is absolutely essential for any sustained growth in business. Referrals tend to yield much better results in bringing in business to a company compared to other lead-generation methods or marketing strategies. And when someone refers your business services or products, it comes with a recommendation that boosts the likelihood of converting connections into sales. That’s really important. That’s one of the reasons why referrals are so valuable.

So generating referrals requires more effort, though, than some marketing tactics, you know, and it’s also more cost effective than a lot of marketing tactics. According to a Nielsen.com global survey of 29,000 people, 84% of those responding said that they prefer recommendations from people they know. That was the number one selection relating to the question: To what extent do you trust the following forms of advertising? And so referrals from people they know was number one. So building referrals for life, that’s what BNI really is about. And it’s not an event. It’s not a quick solution. When it’s done right, it should be a process rather than a one-time endeavor. So networking is an enduring investment that demands effort and dedication to nurturing relationships with your referral partners. This is important in understanding in a BNI group. It’s a journey, not a destination. It’s something that you’re working on to create referrals for life. So there are 10 recommendations that I’ve got to do this.

One is to cultivate a mindset centered on referrals. It’s really crucial to adopt a mindset that’s focused on generating referrals when networking and fostering connections. And when you prioritize assisting others over focusing on your immediate personal gains, you develop a relationship-building mindset that values helping and supporting people. And by being attentive to others’ needs and seeking ways to aid them in reaching their objectives, you can establish the kind of trust that allows people to feel comfortable referring business to you. That’s number one.

Number two: build strong relationships, which is one of our core values. Establishing connections is essential for fostering referrals. And to build these connections, it’s crucial to embody authenticity, honesty, reliability, being a good listener – a good networker has two ears and one mouth and uses them both proportionately. Demonstrating interest in others, following through on your promises. These are key aspects of nurturing strong relationships.

Three: educate your network. This is really important in BNI. It’s about, you know, training a sales force, not closing a sale each week. So educating your network is often overlooked as part of the referral process. In order to cultivate referrals, it’s important to educate those within your network about the business and your offerings that you have in your business. So this involves more than making a sale. It entails equipping your network with the knowledge they need to refer your business to other people by helping them grasp what sets your business apart and how you can benefit others. You’re effectively conveying the value that you provide to your clients or customers.

Number four is deliver exceptional service. Now this one’s clearly obvious and very important. So delivering great service plays a vital role in generating long-lasting referrals. When you provide exceptional service, your clients or customers turn into your advocates, and they’re more inclined to refer you to others because they have faith in what you do and the value that you provide. So providing service involves exceeding expectations and anticipating clients’ needs and, of course, fulfilling your commitments to them. You know, in BNI, there’s no place to hide. You’re going to see them every week, so you better do a good job for the people that are giving you referrals.

Number five is to ask for referrals. Now, isn’t it interesting that that’s not number one? It’s number five, because there are other things you got to do before you’re asking for referrals. So once you’ve established credibility with people, which takes time, it’s important to ask for referrals. And while this may seem straightforward, many business owners overlook asking referrals from individuals that they have a relationship with. It’s beneficial to make it a routine to seek referrals from clients or customers. We know that in BNI, but you got to build that relationship before you really start asking for referrals, even within the BNI context. Now, when you’re requesting referrals, be specific about who you would like to be referred to and why. Specific is terrific when it comes to doing your weekly presentation. And it may sound counterintuitive, because we like to say everything we do in the time we have. But the truth is, the more laser specific you can be, the more likely you are to strike a chord with somebody where they remember something about you that allows them to refer you more effectively.

Number six is give referrals. One way to get referrals is by giving referrals yourself, and when you refer someone, it creates a quicker potential relationship for both parties involved. It demonstrates your willingness to assist others, and it also enhances the chances of receiving referrals in return. So make it a practice to actively look for opportunities to recommend businesses that share your values and standards. That’s an important aspect of creating referrals for life.

Number seven: stay connected. Touch points are critical. It’s one of the reasons why we have weekly meetings in BNI, is that regular connection. So keep connected with your network by following up with them. This could be done through emails, phone calls, social media interactions, of course, face-to-face meetings or Zoom calls, one or the other we do in BNI. And by staying connected, you keep your business at the forefront of their minds. It shows that you value the relationship beyond just business transactions. I find it interesting. Sometimes people, you know, they go, “Well, I’ve made these connections in BNI. I’m going to step down, and I don’t need to go to the BNI meetings anymore, and people will just give me referrals.” They don’t, they don’t. If you stop going to BNI meetings, there’s somebody in your profession that’s going to join that chapter, and they’re going to start giving referrals to that person. So don’t do that. Stay connected. Keep going to those meetings.

Ivan:
Number eight is leverage social media platforms like LinkedIn, Instagram, X – previously Twitter, and Facebook. The, all serve as tools for maintaining existing relationships and sometimes even establishing new ones. I mean, listen, I’m very active in social media. I believe in social media, I don’t think it’s necessarily the best way to build your business, but it’s a great way to stay connected with people. So using social media is a way to connect with potential clients and existing clients and referral partners, share valuable content, engage in industry specific groups, stay updated to the latest news. I recommend being active in social media, and I certainly am.

Number nine: attend networking events. What a shock. Attend networking events is an opportunity to meet people, establish and nurture relationships, generate referral possibilities, look for events tailored to your industry. Of course, things like BNI meetings. When you attend these meetings, you know, remember to go there prepared to talk about what it is that you do, and to do it concisely, so that people can remember who you are and what you do.

And number ten: show gratitude. Expressing gratitude when someone refers you to a client or a customer is essential. You can show appreciation by sending a thank you, note a gift, or offering a discount on your services. Demonstrating gratitude strengthens the relationships and leaves an impression that may result in more referrals in the future. It always surprises me when members tell me that they’ve given referrals to somebody and they’ve never shown any gratitude for those referrals that they’ve sent their way. That’s a mistake. You want to create referrals for life. You want to show gratitude when people give you referrals.

Ivan:
So by incorporating these strategies into your networking efforts over time, you can cultivate lasting relationships that contribute to sustainable business growth. And keep in mind that building relationships is a commitment that pays off over time. This is not a rich quick scheme. It’s a way to build a solid foundation for a long-term successful business, and it demands ongoing efforts. Lifetime referrals go beyond just endorsements. They entail nurturing enduring connections within your network, delivering top notch service, and consistently enriching the lives of others. It signifies a level of approval and plays a pivotal role in fostering lasting growth for any enterprise.

Let me give you the 10 items again: cultivate a mindset centered on referrals. Number two, build strong relationships. Number three, educate your network – really important in BNI. Number four, deliver exceptional service. Number five, ask for referrals. Number six, give referrals. Number seven, stay connected. Number eight, leverage social media. Number nine, attend networking events, and number ten, show gratitude. That was a lot of information in 10 or 12 minutes today, Priscilla. I know. I hope the members found this of value.

Priscilla:
It’s very interesting, and it’s a great list. I wrote it all down. Ivan.

Ivan:
There’ll be a test next week for you.

Priscilla: (laughing)
Okay, great. Thank you. I think that’s it for this week, Dr Misner. Thank you so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
